\text{MTTDL}_{\text{RAID 5}} \approx \frac{M^{2}}{N \cdot (N-1) \cdot R}
$$

- **RAID 6 (double parity)**:

$$
\text{MTTDL}_{\text{RAID 6}} \approx \frac{M^{3}}{N \cdot (N-1) \cdot (N-2) \cdot R^{2}}
$$

These expressions are derived from the *conditional failure model*: data loss in RAID 5 requires two failed disks overlapping with the MTTR window of the first; in RAID 6, three failed disks must overlap.

#### 2.3.2 Storage Efficiency ($\eta$)

$$
\eta = \frac{C_{\text{usable}}}{C_{\text{raw}}} = \frac{C_{\text{usable}}}{N \cdot S}
$$

Examples:
- RAID 0: $\eta = 1$ (no overhead)
- RAID 1 (2 disks): $\eta = 0.5$
- RAID 5 (5 disks): $\eta = 0.8$
- RAID 6 (8 disks): $\eta = 0.75$

#### 2.3.3 Array Rebuild Time ($T_{\text{rebuild}}$)

$$
T_{\text{rebuild}} \approx \frac{(C_{\text{usable}} \cdot S) \cdot \text{StripeSize}}{I_{\text{rebuild}} \cdot N \cdot 10^{6}} \quad \text{(in seconds)}
$$

where $I_{\text{rebuild}}$ is the sustained rebuild throughput in **MB/s** of the array's surviving disks, often limited to $50$–$200$ MB/s in practice to throttle I/O load on the production workload.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

RAID is the foundational building block of every modern storage system:
- **Enterprise SAN/NAS**: Massive RAID 6 or RAID 60 groups underlaying volume managers (e.g., ZFS, NetApp ONTAP RAID-DP, VMware vSAN).
- **Database servers**: RAID 10 for transaction log volumes; RAID 5/6 for data warehouses (with battery-backed cache).
- **Consumer NAS**: Often RAID 5 (Synology, QNAP) with single-disk fault tolerance.
- **Hyperscale data centers**: Custom erasure-coding schemes (e.g., Google's **Reed–Solomon (9, 6)** codes) generalize RAID-6 concepts across **hundreds of disks**, providing multi-disk fault tolerance at terabyte scales with $\eta \approx 0.67$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of RAID 5 Write Penalty (The Classic 4-IO Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> The most heavily tested concept in KTU module examinations is the derivation of the **"Read-Modify-Write"** sequence that explains why RAID 5 small writes cost **4 logical I/Os per single user write**.

**Scenario**: A single block $D_i$ on a 4-disk RAID 5 array must be updated. The block currently holds old data $D_{i,\text{old}}$, and the corresponding parity block on disk $P_k$ holds $P_{\text{old}}$.

The invariant the array must maintain is:

$$
P = D_1 \oplus D_2 \oplus D_3 \oplus D_4
$$

To update $D_i \rightarrow D_{i,\text{new}}$, the new parity must be:

$$
P_{\text{new}} = D_1 \oplus D_2 \oplus D_3' \oplus D_4
$$

where $D_3' = D_{i,\text{new}}$. We can compute this efficiently using the **XOR property** $A \oplus A = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{new}} &= D_1 \oplus D_2 \oplus D_3' \oplus D_4 \\
&= D_1 \oplus D_2 \oplus D_3 \oplus D_4 \oplus D_3 \oplus D_3' \\
&= (D_1 \oplus D_2 \oplus D_3 \oplus D_4) \oplus D_3 \oplus D_3' \\
&= P_{\text{old}} \oplus D_{i,\text{old}} \oplus D_{i,\text{new}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This yields the **Read-Modify-Write** algorithm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Read } & D_{i,\text{old}} &&\text{(read old data from disk containing $D_i$)} \\
\text{Step 2: Read } & P_{\text{old}} &&\text{(read old parity from parity disk)} \\
\text{Step 3: Write } & D_{i,\text{new}} &&\text{(write new data)} \\
\text{Step 4: Write } & P_{\text{new}} = P_{\text{old}} \oplus D_{i,\text{old}} \oplus D_{i,\text{new}} &&\text{(write new parity)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total = 4 logical I/Os per single user write.** Hence the well-known phrase: *RAID 5 small writes cost 4×*.

A symmetric *Read-Read-Write-Write* (full-stripe write) requires only **2 I/Os** (one full-stripe read + one full-stripe write), but small random writes incur the full 4× penalty.

### 3.2 Derivation of RAID 6 Write Penalty (The 6-IO Read-Modify-Write)

For RAID 6 we maintain two parities: $P$ (row) and $Q$ (diagonal/Reed–Solomon). The update must compute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{new}} &= P_{\text{old}} \oplus D_{i,\text{old}} \oplus D_{i,\text{new}} \\
Q_{\text{new}} &= f(Q_{\text{old}}, D_{i,\text{old}}, D_{i,\text{new}})
\end{aligned}
$$

For a Reed–Solomon code over $\mathrm{GF}(2^8)$, $Q$ is computed using a generator polynomial. Let $g_x$ denote the second generator coefficient, then:

$$
Q_{\text{new}} = Q_{\text{old}} \oplus (g_x \cdot D_{i,\text{old}}) \oplus (g_x \cdot D_{i,\text{new}})
$$

The full logical I/O sequence is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Read } & D_{i,\text{old}} \\
\text{Step 2: Read } & P_{\text{old}} \\
\text{Step 3: Read } & Q_{\text{old}} \\
\text{Step 4: Write } & D_{i,\text{new}} \\
\text{Step 5: Write } & P_{\text{new}} \\
\text{Step 6: Write } & Q_{\text{new}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total = 6 logical I/Os.** This is why RAID 6 controllers with **battery-backed write cache (BBWC)** are critical: the six disk operations are coalesced into a single cache-resident transaction.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Capacity, Efficiency, and MTTDL

**Problem**: Design a 24-disk storage array using 1 TB enterprise SAS drives with MTBF $= 1{,}500{,}000$ hours and MTTR $= 1$ hour. Compare RAID 5 and RAID 6 in terms of capacity, efficiency, and MTTDL.

#### 3.3.1 RAID 5 Configuration

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{usable}} &= (N-1) \cdot S = (24-1) \cdot 1\text{ TB} = 23\text{ TB} \\
\eta &= (N-1)/N = 23/24 \approx 0.9583 \\
\text{MTTDL}_{\text{RAID 5}} &\approx \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{6})^{2}}{24 \cdot 23 \cdot 1} \approx \frac{2.25 \times 10^{12}}{552} \approx 4.08 \times 10^{9} \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 3.3.2 RAID 6 Configuration

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{usable}} &= (N-2) \cdot S = (24-2) \cdot 1\text{ TB} = 22\text{ TB} \\
\eta &= (N-2)/N = 22/24 \approx 0.9167 \\
\text{MTTDL}_{\text{RAID 6}} &\approx \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{6})^{3}}{24 \cdot 23 \cdot 22 \cdot 1^{2}} \approx \frac{3.375 \times 10^{18}}{12144} \approx 2.78 \times 10^{14} \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 3.3.3 Comparative Analysis Matrix

| Metric | RAID 5 (24 disks) | RAID 6 (24 disks) | Remarks |
| :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- |
| Usable capacity | **23 TB** | **22 TB** | RAID 6 sacrifices 1 TB to second parity |
| Storage efficiency | 95.83% | 91.67% | 4.16% overhead cost of RAID 6 |
| Fault tolerance | 1 disk | 2 disks | RAID 6 wins decisively |
| MTTDL | $\sim 4.08 \times 10^{9}$ h | $\sim 2.78 \times 10^{14}$ h | RAID 6 ≈ 68,000× more reliable |
| Write penalty | 4 I/Os | 6 I/Os | RAID 5 wins for write-heavy loads |
| Best for | Read-heavy, fast write workloads | Capacity-sensitive, mission-critical data | Domain-specific choice |

### 3.4 Analytical Derivation — RAID 10 vs. RAID 01 Fault Tolerance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although RAID 10 and RAID 01 are **capacity-equivalent**, their **fault-tolerance behavior is asymmetric**. This is a classic KTU favorite.

**RAID 10 (Stripe of Mirrors)**: 8 disks arranged as 4 mirror pairs, then striped. If one disk in a pair fails, the mirror partner takes over and the array continues in degraded mode. A *second* failure only causes data loss if it strikes the **same mirror pair**.

**RAID 01 (Mirror of Stripes)**: 8 disks arranged as 2 RAID 0 stripes, then mirrored. If a disk fails in one stripe, the entire stripe is broken (a stripe is the unit of redundancy). The mirror partner carries the load, but a *second* failure in the **mirror's stripe** destroys data, regardless of which stripe element is hit.

**Probability of data loss** given a single-disk failure, assuming uniform second-failure distribution:

- **RAID 10**: After first failure, the partner in the same pair is the only critical disk. Probability of loss on next failure $= 1 / (N-1)$.
- **RAID 01**: After first failure, the entire mirror's stripe is at risk. Probability of loss on next failure $= (N/2) / (N-1)$.

For $N = 8$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{loss} \vert \text{RAID 10}} &= 1/7 \approx 0.143 \\
P_{\text{loss} \vert \text{RAID 01}} &= 4/7 \approx 0.571
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion**: RAID 10 is approximately **4× more fault-tolerant** than RAID 01 under the same disk count.

### 3.5 Mean Time To Data Loss — Generalized Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> The MTTDL formulas follow from the **conditional Poisson failure model**. For an array tolerating $k$ disk failures, data loss occurs on the $(k+1)$-th failure. Under exponential time-to-failure with rate $\lambda = 1/M$ per disk, the time-to-data-loss for a homogeneous array of $N$ disks is the expected time until the next $(k+1)$ failures occur, given that the MTTR governs how long the array operates in a degraded state.

For RAID 5 ($k=1$, single parity):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTDL}_{\text{RAID 5}} &\approx \frac{M^{2}}{N(N-1)R} \\
\text{Reasoning:} &\quad \text{Data loss requires 2 disks failed within time } R \text{ of each other.}
\end{aligned}
$$

For RAID 6 ($k=2$, double parity):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTDL}_{\text{RAID 6}} &\approx \frac{M^{3}}{N(N-1)(N-2)R^{2}} \\
\text{Reasoning:} &\quad \text{Data loss requires 3 disks failed; two are tolerated, third during rebuild window.}
\end{aligned}
$$

These approximations are valid when $M \gg R$, which is the operational norm (disks live for $10^6$ hours; rebuild takes $\sim 1$ hour).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 RAID Controller — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following diagram depicts the high-level functional architecture of a hardware RAID controller, illustrating the data path from host interface through cache to the disk back-end.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[HBA Host Interface] --> B[Command Queue Parser]
    B --> C[Logical Volume Manager]
    C --> D[Stripe Mapping Engine]
    D --> E[RAID Parity XOR Accelerator]
    D --> F[Mirror Replication Module]
    D --> G[Striping Module Pure]
    E --> H[Write Cache BBWC]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Disk Back-end Channel]
    I --> J1[Disk 0 Member]
    I --> J2[Disk 1 Member]
    I --> J3[Disk 2 Member]
    I --> J4[Disk N Member]
    H --> K[Cache Flush on Power Loss Module]
    K --> L[BBU Supercapacitor Backup]

    classDef interface fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#9b2226,color:#000
    classDef compute fill:#bee1e6,stroke:#0a4f6a,color:#000
    classDef cache fill:#cae9ff,stroke:#2c5d8a,color:#000
    classDef disk fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#1b4332,color:#000
    class A,B interface
    class C,D,E,F,G compute
    class H,K,L cache
    class I,J1,J2,J3,J4 disk
```

### 4.2 RAID 0 — Block-Level Striping Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Stripe Unit 0] --> D0A[Disk 0 Block A]
    S0 --> D1A[Disk 1 Block A]
    S0 --> D2A[Disk 2 Block A]
    S0 --> D3A[Disk 3 Block A]
    S1[Stripe Unit 1] --> D0B[Disk 0 Block B]
    S1 --> D1B[Disk 1 Block B]
    S1 --> D2B[Disk 2 Block B]
    S1 --> D3B[Disk 3 Block B]
    S2[Stripe Unit 2] --> D0C[Disk 0 Block C]
    S2 --> D1C[Disk 1 Block C]
    S2 --> D2C[Disk 2 Block C]
    S2 --> D3C[Disk 3 Block C]
```

### 4.3 RAID 1 — Mirroring Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HOST[Host Write Request] --> SPLIT[Replication Splitter]
    SPLIT --> D0[Disk 0 Primary]
    SPLIT --> D1[Disk 1 Mirror]
    SPLIT --> D2[Disk 2 Mirror]
    SPLIT --> D3[Disk 3 Mirror]
    READ[Host Read Request] --> ARB[Read Arbiter]
    ARB --> D0
    ARB --> D1
    ARB --> D2
    ARB --> D3
```

### 4.4 RAID 5 — Distributed Parity Stripe Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph R5A[Stripe Set A]
        D0A[Disk 0 Data A0]
        D1A[Disk 1 Data A1]
        D2A[Disk 2 Parity A]
        D3A[Disk 3 Data A2]
    end
    subgraph R5B[Stripe Set B]
        D0B[Disk 0 Data B0]
        D1B[Disk 1 Parity B]
        D2B[Disk 2 Data B1]
        D3B[Disk 3 Data B2]
    end
    subgraph R5C[Stripe Set C]
        D0C[Disk 0 Parity C]
        D1C[Disk 1 Data C0]
        D2C[Disk 2 Data C1]
        D3C[Disk 3 Data C2]
    end
    subgraph R5D[Stripe Set D]
        D0D[Disk 0 Data D0]
        D1D[Disk 1 Data D1]
        D2D[Disk 2 Data D2]
        D3D[Disk 3 Parity D]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the diagram above, the parity disk **rotates** through D0 → D1 → D2 → D3 across successive stripes. This is the defining feature of RAID 5 vs. RAID 4.

### 4.5 RAID 6 — Dual Parity Layout (P and Q)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph R6A[Stripe A]
        P0A[Disk 0 Data A0]
        P1A[Disk 1 Data A1]
        P2A[Disk 2 P Parity]
        P3A[Disk 3 Q Parity]
    end
    subgraph R6B[Stripe B]
        P0B[Disk 0 Q Parity]
        P1B[Disk 1 Data B0]
        P2B[Disk 2 Data B1]
        P3B[Disk 3 P Parity]
    end
    subgraph R6C[Stripe C]
        P0C[Disk 0 P Parity]
        P1C[Disk 1 Q Parity]
        P2C[Disk 2 Data C0]
        P3C[Disk 3 Data C1]
    end
```

### 4.6 RAID 10 — Nested Stripe of Mirrors

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LV[Logical Volume Top Stripe] --> MP1[Mirror Pair 1]
    LV --> MP2[Mirror Pair 2]
    LV --> MP3[Mirror Pair 3]
    MP1 --> P1A[Disk 0 Primary]
    MP1 --> P1B[Disk 1 Mirror]
    MP2 --> P2A[Disk 2 Primary]
    MP2 --> P2B[Disk 3 Mirror]
    MP3 --> P3A[Disk 4 Primary]
    MP3 --> P3B[Disk 5 Mirror]
```

### 4.7 RAID Decision Flow — Workload-Driven Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[New Storage Array Design] --> R1{Is data critical?}
    R1 -- No --> R0[Use RAID 0 or JBOD]
    R1 -- Yes --> R2{Read or Write heavy?}
    R2 -- Write heavy --> R3[Use RAID 10 or RAID 1]
    R2 -- Balanced --> R4{2 disk failures tolerable?}
    R4 -- Yes --> R5[Use RAID 6 or RAID 60]
    R4 -- No --> R6[Use RAID 5 with hot spare]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]**
> **Q1. Define RAID. Differentiate between RAID 0 and RAID 1 with respect to redundancy, performance, and capacity overhead.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer**:
RAID (Redundant Array of Independent Disks) is a data storage virtualization technology that combines multiple physical disks into one or more logical units to achieve data redundancy, performance, or both. Originally defined by Patterson, Gibson, and Katz in 1988.

**Differences**:

| Aspect | RAID 0 | RAID 1 |
| :-- | :-- | :-- |
| Redundancy | None | 1 complete mirror |
| Read performance | $N$× linear | $N$× linear (any mirror) |
| Write performance | $N$× linear | 2× per write (must duplicate) |
| Capacity efficiency | 100% | 50% (2-disk mirror) |
| Fault tolerance | 0 disks | $N-1$ disks (multi-mirror) |

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]**
> **Q2. Explain the "small write penalty" of RAID 5. Why is the penalty equal to 4 logical I/Os?** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer**:
When a single block is updated in a RAID 5 array, the parity invariant $P = D_1 \oplus D_2 \oplus \dots \oplus D_N$ must be preserved. The controller executes a **Read-Modify-Write** sequence:

1. Read the **old data** block $D_{i,\text{old}}$.
2. Read the **old parity** block $P_{\text{old}}$.
3. Write the **new data** $D_{i,\text{new}}$.
4. Write the **new parity** $P_{\text{new}} = P_{\text{old}} \oplus D_{i,\text{old}} \oplus D_{i,\text{new}}$.

Hence **4 logical I/Os** are issued for every single host-level write, which is the famous RAID 5 "4× write penalty."

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 1]**
> **Q3.A. (a)** Explain the architectural differences between RAID 3, RAID 4, and RAID 5. Why is RAID 5 generally preferred over RAID 3 and RAID 4 in modern enterprise systems? `[7 Marks]`
> **Q3.A. (b)** A storage engineer must provision a 10 TB usable array using 1 TB enterprise SATA disks (MTBF = 1,200,000 hours, MTTR = 1 hour). Calculate and compare the MTTDL and storage efficiency for RAID 5 and RAID 6. State the trade-offs. `[7 Marks]`

#### Model Solution for Q3.A.(a)

**Architectural Comparison**:

- **RAID 3** stripes data at the **byte level** across $N-1$ data disks and dedicates **one parity disk**. Each stripe transfer is synchronous because all data disks must participate in every I/O. The dedicated parity disk is the single point of contention.

- **RAID 4** stripes data at the **block level** with the same dedicated parity disk. Independent small writes to different data disks can now proceed in parallel, eliminating the synchronous-bottleneck of RAID 3. However, the **single parity disk remains a write bottleneck** because every write requires parity-disk access.

- **RAID 5** retains the block-level striping of RAID 4 but **distributes the parity blocks** across all member disks in a rotating pattern. This eliminates the dedicated parity disk bottleneck, scaling both read and write parallelism to the full set of surviving disks.

**Why RAID 5 is preferred**:

1. RAID 3 is inefficient for small random I/O because of byte-level synchronization.
2. RAID 4's dedicated parity disk limits write throughput to a single disk's worth, regardless of array size.
3. RAID 5 distributes parity, allowing **all $N$ disks to participate in read parallelism** and **up to $N-1$ disks in write parallelism**, yielding superior performance scaling.
4. RAID 5 maintains the same capacity efficiency and single-disk fault tolerance as RAID 3/4, so there is no functional loss.

#### Model Solution for Q3.A.(b)

**Step 1: Determine disk count for each configuration.**

- **RAID 5** with 1 TB disks needing 10 TB usable: $N - 1 = 10 \Rightarrow N = 11$ disks. Storage efficiency $= 10/11 \approx 90.91\%$.
- **RAID 6** with 1 TB disks needing 10 TB usable: $N - 2 = 10 \Rightarrow N = 12$ disks. Storage efficiency $= 10/12 \approx 83.33\%$.

**Step 2: Calculate MTTDL for RAID 5 (N = 11, M = 1,200,000 h, R = 1 h).**

$$
\text{MTTDL}_{\text{RAID 5}} = \frac{M^{2}}{N(N-1)R} = \frac{(1.2 \times 10^{6})^{2}}{11 \cdot 10 \cdot 1}
$$

$$
= \frac{1.44 \times 10^{12}}{110} \approx 1.31 \times 10^{10} \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate MTTDL for RAID 6 (N = 12, M = 1,200,000 h, R = 1 h).**

$$
\text{MTTDL}_{\text{RAID 6}} = \frac{M^{3}}{N(N-1)(N-2)R^{2}} = \frac{(1.2 \times 10^{6})^{3}}{12 \cdot 11 \cdot 10 \cdot 1}
$$

$$
= \frac{1.728 \times 10^{18}}{1320} \approx 1.31 \times 10^{15} \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4: Comparative Trade-off Table.**

| Metric | RAID 5 | RAID 6 | Trade-off |
| :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- |
| Disk count | 11 | 12 | RAID 6 needs 1 extra disk |
| Usable capacity | 10 TB | 10 TB | Equivalent |
| Storage efficiency | 90.91% | 83.33% | RAID 6 loses 7.58% |
| MTTDL | $1.31 \times 10^{10}$ h | $1.31 \times 10^{15}$ h | RAID 6 ≈ 100,000× more reliable |
| Write penalty | 4 I/Os | 6 I/Os | RAID 5 wins on writes |
| Fault tolerance | 1 disk | 2 disks | RAID 6 wins on resilience |

**Conclusion**: RAID 6 trades 7.58% capacity efficiency and 50% more write I/Os for a five-orders-of-magnitude improvement in MTTDL — a near-universal choice for capacity-oriented enterprise arrays with disks of $\geq 4$ TB where rebuild times are long.

> **[KTU University Exam — Alternative Choice]**
> **Q3.B. (a)** With neat architectural diagrams, explain RAID 5 and RAID 6. Discuss how data is reconstructed after a single disk failure in RAID 5, and after a double disk failure in RAID 6. `[7 Marks]`
> **Q3.B. (b)** Compare RAID 10 and RAID 01. For a 10-disk array, calculate the probability of data loss on the second disk failure for both configurations. Comment on which is preferred in production. `[7 Marks]`

#### Model Solution for Q3.B.(a)

**RAID 5 Architecture**: Data and parity are striped at the block level across $N$ disks. For a 5-disk array, if the disks are labeled D0–D4, one stripe might be: `D0:A0, D1:A1, D2:A2, D3:A3, D4:Pa` (parity for A). The next stripe rotates parity to D3, then D2, and so on.

**RAID 6 Architecture**: Two parities ($P$ and $Q$) per stripe. $P$ is the standard XOR parity; $Q$ is a Reed–Solomon or diagonal parity computed using a generator polynomial in $\mathrm{GF}(2^8)$.

**Single-disk failure recovery in RAID 5**:
Suppose D2 fails and the surviving stripe holds data on D0, D1, D3 and parity on D4. The original invariant is $P = A0 \oplus A1 \oplus A2 \oplus A3$. To recover $A2$:

$$
A2_{\text{recovered}} = A0 \oplus A1 \oplus A3 \oplus P
$$

This is a single XOR of four surviving values, producing the missing block bit-for-bit.

**Double-disk failure recovery in RAID 6**:
Suppose D2 and D3 fail. We have $A0, A1, P, Q$ surviving. We can solve the linear system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= A0 \oplus A1 \oplus A2 \oplus A3 \\
Q &= g_0 \cdot A0 \oplus g_1 \cdot A1 \oplus g_2 \cdot A2 \oplus g_3 \cdot A3
\end{aligned}
$$

The two equations in two unknowns ($A2, A3$) are solved simultaneously using Reed–Solomon algebra (matrix inversion in $\mathrm{GF}(2^8)$), uniquely determining both missing blocks.

#### Model Solution for Q3.B.(b)

**RAID 10 (Stripe of Mirrors)**: 10 disks = 5 mirror pairs, then striped.
**RAID 01 (Mirror of Stripes)**: 10 disks = 2 stripes of 5, then mirrored.

**Probability of data loss on second failure** (uniform random distribution):

**RAID 10**:
- 10 disks total. First failure lands on any disk. The partner in the same mirror pair is the **only** critical disk for the array's survival.
- $P_{\text{loss} \vert \text{second failure, RAID 10}} = 1 / 9 \approx 0.111$.

**RAID 01**:
- 10 disks total. First failure takes out one entire 5-disk stripe (the unit of redundancy). The mirror's stripe (5 disks) is the only surviving critical unit.
- $P_{\text{loss} \vert \text{second failure, RAID 01}} = 5 / 9 \approx 0.556$.

**Conclusion**: RAID 10 is approximately **5× more fault-tolerant** than RAID 01 for $N = 10$. RAID 10 is universally preferred in production because (a) its loss probability is asymptotically $1/(N-1)$ while RAID 01 is $N/(2(N-1))$, and (b) rebuild after a single failure in RAID 10 only stresses the affected mirror pair, leaving the rest of the array to serve I/O normally.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Traps**
> 1. **Writing $P = D_1 + D_2 + \dots$ instead of $\oplus$**: Parity in RAID 3/4/5 is bitwise XOR, not arithmetic addition. Writing "+" loses full conceptual credit.
> 2. **Stating RAID 5 has "2-disk fault tolerance"**: A frequent and severe error. RAID 5 tolerates only **1** disk failure. The correct term is "single-disk fault tolerance."
> 3. **Confusing RAID 10 and RAID 01**: Examiners specifically test the asymmetry. Writing "they are equivalent" costs full marks.
> 4. **MTTDL formula errors**: Forgetting to square $M$ in the RAID 5 formula or cube it in the RAID 6 formula yields a wrong answer by 4–6 orders of magnitude.
> 5. **Omitting rebuild time in capacity/MTTDL trade-off analysis**: Real-world RAID 6 design *always* involves a rebuild time discussion. Examiners expect it.
> 6. **Failing to mention BBWC**: In RAID 5/6 small-write performance problems, the role of battery-backed write cache is a frequently-missed nuance worth 1–2 marks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concepts to Internalize Before the KTU Examination**

- ✅ **RAID = Striping + Mirroring + Parity** in various combinations across 7 base levels (0–6) and several nested hybrids (10, 01, 50, 60).
- ✅ **RAID 0**: Pure striping, **no redundancy**, $C_{\text{usable}} = N \cdot S$, $N$× performance.
- ✅ **RAID 1**: Pure mirroring, $C_{\text{usable}} = (N/2) \cdot S$, $N$× read, $2$× write.
- ✅ **RAID 3 vs. 4 vs. 5**: All single-parity, all single-fault-tolerant. Differences are in **striping granularity** (byte/block) and **parity location** (dedicated vs. distributed). **RAID 5 is dominant**.
- ✅ **RAID 6**: Double distributed parity (P + Q), $C_{\text{usable}} = (N-2) \cdot S$, **2-disk fault tolerance**, **6-IO write penalty**.
- ✅ **RAID 5 write penalty = 4 I/Os** (Read-Modify-Write). **RAID 6 = 6 I/Os**.
- ✅ **RAID 10 vs. RAID 01**: Same capacity; RAID 10 is **asymptotically more fault-tolerant** ($1/(N-1)$ vs. $N/(2(N-1))$ loss probability).
- ✅ **MTTDL formula**:
  - RAID 5: $M^2 / [N(N-1)R]$
  - RAID 6: $M^3 / [N(N-1)(N-2)R^2]$
- ✅ **Storage efficiency** $\eta = (N - p)/N$ where $p$ is the number of parity disks.
- ✅ **Hot spare**: Standby disk that auto-joins array after failure, reducing exposure window.
- ✅ **Write cache (BBWC)**: Critical for masking RAID 5/6 write penalties; battery or supercapacitor-backed to survive power loss.
- ✅ **Standards bodies**: SNIA, INCITS T10, T13 (ATA), SCSI/block-command set.
- ✅ **Modern extensions**: Erasure coding (Reed–Solomon 9,6 etc.) is the **hyperscale generalization** of RAID-6 across hundreds of nodes; object storage (S3, Azure Blob) uses these codes.
- ✅ **Common exam mistake to avoid**: Do not equate "RAID level number" with quality. RAID 0 is "level 0" not because it is the worst, but because it has **zero redundancy** — a numeric label, not a ranking.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Replication Strategies: Primary-Backup and Quorum-Based
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT752 - FAULT TOLERANCE IN NETWORKED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Replication Strategies: Primary-Backup and Quorum-Based

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Data Replication?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Replication** is the process of storing and maintaining multiple copies of data (replicas) on different nodes of a distributed system to enhance **availability**, **reliability**, **fault tolerance**, and **performance** of read/write operations.

In a distributed system, any single node can fail due to hardware faults, network partitions, software crashes, or power outages. If data exists on only one node, a failure leads to **data unavailability** and possible **data loss**. Replication addresses this by distributing the same logical data item across *N* geographically or topologically distinct replicas.

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Replication is a redundancy technique in which a logical data object $D$ is physically instantiated as $N$ copies $\{D_1, D_2, \dots, D_N\}$ on $N$ distinct nodes of a distributed system, such that the system continues to provide correct service even when a subset of replicas fails, provided that the consistency contract of the replication protocol is preserved.*

---

### 1.2 The Two Pillars of Passive vs. Active Replication

Replication strategies are broadly classified into two architectural families:

| Strategy Family | Update Path | Replica Role | Synchronization Model |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary-Backup** (Passive) | All updates go to ONE primary | Primary = active, Backups = passive | Update-propagation |
| **Quorum-Based** (Active) | Updates may target any replica | All replicas are symmetric peers | Intersection-based |

---

### 1.3 Primary-Backup Replication (Passive Replication)

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary-Backup Replication** is a passive replication strategy in which one designated replica (the *primary*) is responsible for serializing and ordering all client write requests, while one or more *backup* replicas receive the updates and remain on standby for failover.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Head Chef System:**
Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen. There is exactly **one Head Chef (Primary)** who has the official recipe book and the authority to modify any dish. When the Head Chef updates a recipe, the assistant chefs (Backups) are *informed* and update their copies of the recipe book. If the Head Chef falls sick (crashes), the **Senior Sous-Chef** is promoted to Head Chef — this is *failover*. Customers (clients) always address the Head Chef; they never directly ask the assistants.

---

### 1.4 Quorum-Based Replication (Active Replication)

> [!NOTE]
> **Quorum-Based Replication** is an active replication strategy in which client read and write operations are allowed to succeed only if they gather a *quorum* — a minimum number of agreeing replicas — such that the read quorum and the write quorum have a non-empty intersection, guaranteeing that every read observes the most recent write.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Parliamentary Voting System:**
Think of a parliament with **100 members (N replicas)**. To pass a bill (write), at least **51 members must vote yes (Write Quorum, W = 51)**. To repeal or verify a law (read), at least **51 members must agree (Read Quorum, R = 51)**. The critical rule: **the 51 writers and 51 readers must share at least 1 common member (R + W > N)** — so the reader can never be tricked by an outdated version because the overlapping member remembers the latest decision.

---

### 1.5 Key Terminology and Standard Metrics

- **N** — Total number of replicas storing a data item.
- **R** — Read Quorum: minimum replicas that must respond to a read.
- **W** — Write Quorum: minimum replicas that must acknowledge a write.
- **R + W > N** — The fundamental **quorum intersection condition**.
- **W > N/2** — The *majority quorum* condition (a special, strong case).
- **Availability** — Probability that a request succeeds despite failures.
- **Strong Consistency** — Every read returns the value of the *most recent* write.
- **Failover Time** — Time taken to detect primary failure and promote a backup.
- **Failover Delay (Δ)** — Recovery Point Objective window in asynchronous replication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU 2024 ESE, you must be able to **state the quorum intersection condition**, **derive the failure tolerance**, and **compare the consistency/availability trade-offs** between Primary-Backup and Quorum-Based approaches.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Primary-Backup (Passive) Replication — Operational Logic

The Primary-Backup protocol operates in five conceptual steps:

1. **Client Write Request:** The client sends the write request *only* to the primary replica. The primary is the single serialization point — all writes are ordered by the primary.
2. **Local Apply & Log:** The primary applies the update to its local state and writes a stable **log record** to durable storage to survive its own crash.
3. **Backup Propagation:** The primary propagates the update to all backup replicas. This may be:
   - **Synchronous:** Primary waits for all backups to acknowledge before responding to the client → strong durability, higher latency.
   - **Asynchronous:** Primary responds to the client immediately; backups are updated in the background → lower latency, possible data loss on primary crash.
4. **Backup Acknowledgement (sync mode only):** Backups persist the update and return ACKs to the primary. After W_acks are received (often *all* backups), the primary informs the client.
5. **Failure Detection & Failover:** A *failure detector* (often using heartbeats) monitors the primary. If the primary is suspected dead:
   - A new primary is elected (often via a distributed agreement protocol such as Paxos, Raft, or a view-stamped group).
   - Clients are reconfigured to use the new primary.
   - If the old primary was merely *slow* (network partition), a **split-brain** problem may occur → typically resolved by a *quorum of backups* accepting the new primary.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does Primary-Backup work?**
> Because *all writes are funnelled through one node*, the total order of operations is trivially established — the primary itself is the single source of ordering truth. This makes strong consistency *easy to achieve* but creates a **bottleneck** and a **single point of failure** for write throughput.

**Real-world engineering utility:**
- **Database systems:** PostgreSQL Streaming Replication, MySQL Group Replication (primary mode), MongoDB replica sets.
- **Distributed filesystems:** HDFS NameNode + Standby NameNode.
- **Cloud services:** Amazon RDS Multi-AZ deployments.

---

### 2.2 Quorum-Based Replication — Operational Logic

The Quorum protocol operates symmetrically — any replica can serve a read or write. The key constraint is the **intersection condition**.

**Write Operation Flow:**
1. The client (or a *coordinator* node) sends the write to *all* N replicas.
2. The write is tagged with a monotonically increasing **version number** (timestamp or vector clock).
3. The write is considered successful when at least **W** replicas acknowledge the write.
4. Conflicts (concurrent writes) are resolved by the replica using the highest version number — *last-writer-wins* or application-defined merge.

**Read Operation Flow:**
1. The client sends the read to *all* N replicas.
2. The client waits for **R** responses.
3. The client picks the response with the **highest version number** (latest value).
4. The read returns this value, guaranteeing that it includes at least the *latest acknowledged write* (since R + W > N guarantees overlap).

**The Quorum Intersection Theorem:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If $R + W > N$, then for any successful read and any successful write, the read set and the write set share at least $R + W - N \geq 1$ replica. The overlapping replica holds *both* the latest write *and* its contribution to the read response, so the read **must** observe the most recent write.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Quorum Intersection | $R + W > N$ | Guarantees strong consistency |
| 2 | Majority Quorum | $W > N/2$ | Special case; tolerates floor(N/2) failures |
| 3 | Failure Tolerance (Quorum) | $f = \min(R, W) - 1$ | Max replicas that can be down |
| 4 | Total Replicas | $N = 2f + 1$ | For majority quorum tolerating $f$ failures |
| 5 | Synchronous Primary-Backup Availability | $A = A_{primary} \cdot A_{network}$ | Bounded by primary uptime |
| 6 | Asynchronous PB — RPO Window | $RPO = t_{detect} + t_{failover}$ | Data potentially lost |
| 7 | Read Latency (Quorum) | $T_{R} = \text{latency to } R\text{th replica}$ | 50th-percentile-ish access |
| 8 | Write Latency (Quorum) | $T_{W} = \text{latency to } W\text{th replica}$ | Similar to read |
| 9 | Read-Only Optimization | $R = 1, W = N$ | Fast reads, slow writes |
| 10 | Write-Only Optimization | $W = 1, R = N$ | Fast writes, slow reads (CAP trade-off) |
| 11 | Balanced Quorum | $R = W = \lceil (N+1)/2 \rceil$ | Symmetric load |
| 12 | Number of Faults Tolerated by PB | $f = N - 1$ (after failover) | Tolerate up to N-1 if failover works |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering note:** The "f = N-1" claim for Primary-Backup applies **only** if backups are *continuously available* for promotion — i.e., they have not all simultaneously failed. In practice, with asynchronous replication, an old primary may re-appear (ZooKeeper "fencing" problem) and must be fenced off.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of Quorum Systems

- **Amazon DynamoDB / Cassandra:** Uses $(N, R, W)$ configurable quorum (sloppy quorums with hinted handoff).
- **Google Chubby / Spanner:** Uses Paxos / Paxos-groups as a quorum-based consensus core.
- **Distributed key-value stores:** Riak, Voldemort.
- **Network filesystems:** GFS uses chunk-level replication with primary-backup semantics; Ceph uses CRUSH + quorum reads.
- **Coordination services:** ZooKeeper Zab protocol, etcd Raft.

---

### 2.5 Comparative Analysis — Why Choose One Over the Other?

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary-Backup** is preferred when:
> - The workload is **read-heavy** with rare updates.
> - The application needs a **single linearizable order** of operations.
> - **Strong consistency** is non-negotiable (financial systems).
>
> **Quorum-Based** is preferred when:
> - The workload is **balanced or write-heavy**.
> - **High availability under partition** is required.
> - The system must tolerate **arbitrary replica failures** gracefully (no single point of failure for writes).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Quorum Intersection Condition ($R + W > N$)

**Statement:** In a system with $N$ replicas, if a write is acknowledged by $W$ replicas and a subsequent read is answered by $R$ replicas, the read will observe the most recent write **if and only if** $R + W > N$.

**Proof (Set-Theoretic):**

Let $S_W$ denote the set of replicas that acknowledged the most recent write. So $\vert S_W \vert = W$.

Let $S_R$ denote the set of replicas that responded to the read. So $\vert S_R \vert = R$.

For the read to *guarantee* observing the most recent write, there must be at least one replica that participated in *both* the previous write and the current read. Mathematically:

$$S_W \cap S_R \neq \emptyset$$

By the **Principle of Inclusion-Exclusion** for finite sets:

$$|S_W \cup S_R| = |S_W| + |S_R| - |S_W \cap S_R|$$

Since $S_W \cup S_R$ is a subset of the universe of $N$ replicas:

$$|S_W \cup S_R| \leq N$$

Substituting and rearranging:

$$W + R - |S_W \cap S_R| \leq N$$

For the intersection to be non-empty, we need $|S_W \cap S_R| \geq 1$. The minimum value of $W + R$ that satisfies this is:

$$R + W \geq N + 1$$

Which is the strict condition:

$$R + W > N$$

> This derivation is the cornerstone of quorum system correctness and is a **guaranteed KTU exam question** (3–14 marks).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Failure Tolerance

Given $N$ replicas and a quorum of size $Q$ (either $R$ or $W$), a quorum can be formed as long as at least $Q$ replicas remain alive.

**Maximum tolerable failures $f$:**

A quorum fails when fewer than $Q$ replicas respond. So quorum forms successfully if the number of alive replicas $A \geq Q$. Since $A = N - f_{dead}$:

$$N - f_{dead} \geq Q \implies f_{dead} \leq N - Q$$

Therefore:

$$f_{max} = N - Q = N - \min(R, W)$$

> Note: Because the read *and* write must both succeed independently, the actual fault tolerance for the *system as a whole* is bounded by the smaller of $(N - R)$ and $(N - W)$, i.e., $\min(N - R, N - W)$.

**Special case — Majority quorum:** $R = W = \lceil (N+1)/2 \rceil$.

For $N = 2f + 1$ (odd), the majority is $f + 1$. So:

$$f_{max} = (2f + 1) - (f + 1) = f$$

Hence a majority quorum system with $N = 2f+1$ tolerates exactly $f$ simultaneous replica failures — the classical Byzantine / Paxos bound.

---

### 3.3 Availability Calculation (Independent Failure Model)

Assume each replica is independently available with probability $p$ (uniform). The probability that at least $Q$ out of $N$ replicas are alive follows a **binomial tail**:

$$
A_Q = \sum_{k=Q}^{N} \binom{N}{k} p^k (1-p)^{N-k}
$$

**Step-by-step evaluation for $N=5$, $Q=3$, $p=0.9$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_3 &= \sum_{k=3}^{5} \binom{5}{k} (0.9)^k (0.1)^{5-k} \\
&= \binom{5}{3}(0.9)^3(0.1)^2 + \binom{5}{4}(0.9)^4(0.1)^1 + \binom{5}{5}(0.9)^5(0.1)^0 \\
&= 10 \cdot 0.729 \cdot 0.01 + 5 \cdot 0.6561 \cdot 0.1 + 1 \cdot 0.59049 \cdot 1 \\
&= 10 \cdot 0.00729 + 5 \cdot 0.06561 + 0.59049 \\
&= 0.0729 + 0.32805 + 0.59049 \\
&= 0.99144
\end{aligned}
$$

So with $p = 0.9$ per replica, the *single*-quorum availability is $\approx 99.14\%$. The *full-system* availability (both reads and writes must succeed) is $A_R \cdot A_W$, but with the intersection guarantee, it simplifies to the smaller of the two in the symmetric case.

**Comparison for $N = 5$, $p = 0.9$:**

| Quorum $(R,W)$ | $A_R$ | $A_W$ | System Availability | Tolerable Failures $f$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $(1, 5)$ | $0.99999$ | $0.59049$ | $0.59049$ | $0$ writes, $4$ reads |
| $(3, 3)$ | $0.99144$ | $0.99144$ | $0.99144$ | $2$ |
| $(5, 1)$ | $0.59049$ | $0.99999$ | $0.59049$ | $4$ writes, $0$ reads |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **balanced quorum $(3,3)$** is the *Pareto-optimal* choice for symmetric workloads when the per-replica availability is moderate.

---

### 3.4 Primary-Backup Availability Analysis

For synchronous Primary-Backup with $N$ replicas (1 primary + $N-1$ backups), the system can survive as long as the primary **or** a promoted backup is operational.

**Simple model (no split-brain, no network partition):**

$$
A_{PB} = 1 - (1 - p)^N
$$

That is, the system is unavailable only if **all** N replicas are down.

**With network partitions** (CAP theorem): Primary-Backup under a network partition is *consistent* but *unavailable* for writes if the primary is isolated — yielding $A_{write} \approx p / 2$ in the symmetric partition model (Brewer's conjecture / Gilbert–Lynch proof).

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **CAP trade-off** manifested concretely: Primary-Backup chooses **Consistency over Availability** under partitions; Quorum-Based with sloppy quorums chooses **Availability over Consistency**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Engineering Comparison

| Dimension | Primary-Backup (Passive) | Quorum-Based (Active) |
|---|---|---|
| Write coordinator | Single (primary) | Any replica or coordinator |
| Write bottleneck | Yes | Distributed |
| Failure detection | Heartbeat from primary | None needed for correctness |
| Failover mechanism | Required (election) | Not required |
| Read consistency | Always linearizable | Linearizable iff $R + W > N$ |
| Split-brain risk | Yes — requires fencing | No (no master) |
| Tolerated failures | $N-1$ (with successful failover) | $\min(R,W) - 1$ |
| Read latency | Low (read from primary) | Moderate (must hit $R$ replicas) |
| Write latency | Synchronous: high; Async: low | Moderate (must hit $W$) |
| Best for | Strong-consistency OLTP | High-availability web-scale |
| Examples | PostgreSQL, MongoDB, HDFS | DynamoDB, Cassandra, Riak |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Primary-Backup Replication — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    clientA[Client A]
    clientB[Client B]
    primaryNode[Primary Replica P0]
    backup1[Backup B1]
    backup2[Backup B2]
    backup3[Backup B3]
    fdSvc[Failure Detector Service]
    electSvc[Election / Promoter Service]

    clientA -->|write req| primaryNode
    clientB -->|read req| primaryNode
    primaryNode -->|replicate log| backup1
    primaryNode -->|replicate log| backup2
    primaryNode -->|replicate log| backup3
    backup1 -->|ACK| primaryNode
    backup2 -->|ACK| primaryNode
    backup3 -->|ACK| primaryNode
    primaryNode -->|sync ACK| clientA
    primaryNode -->|read resp| clientB
    fdSvc -->|heartbeat| primaryNode
    fdSvc -->|suspect down| electSvc
    electSvc -->|promote new primary| backup2
    electSvc -->|redirect clients| clientA
    electSvc -->|redirect clients| clientB
```

**Block functional interpretation:**
- The **Client A and Client B** nodes are request entry points.
- All mutations flow through the **Primary Replica P0** which orders them and propagates a serialized log to **Backup B1, B2, B3**.
- The **Failure Detector** service runs continuous heartbeats; on suspicion of failure, it triggers the **Election Service** to promote a backup and redirect clients.

---

### 4.2 Quorum-Based Replication — Read/Write Intersection Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cW[Client Write Request]
    cR[Client Read Request]
    n1[Replica 1]
    n2[Replica 2]
    n3[Replica 3]
    n4[Replica 4]
    n5[Replica 5]
    writeAck[Write Quorum W=3 Acknowledged]
    readAck[Read Quorum R=3 Responded]
    overlap[Overlapping Replica Guarantees Freshness]

    cW --> n1
    cW --> n2
    cW --> n3
    cW --> n4
    cW --> n5
    n1 --> writeAck
    n2 --> writeAck
    n3 --> writeAck

    cR --> n1
    cR --> n2
    cR --> n3
    cR --> n4
    cR --> n5
    n2 --> readAck
    n3 --> readAck
    n4 --> readAck

    writeAck --> overlap
    readAck --> overlap
```

**Interpretation:** With $N=5$, $W=3$, $R=3$, the write hits Replicas 1, 2, 3 and the read hits Replicas 2, 3, 4. The **intersection** $\{2, 3\}$ guarantees the read observes the write.

---

### 4.3 Comparison Schematic — Failure Tolerance View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PB[Primary-Backup Cluster]
        P[Primary P]
        B1[Backup B1]
        B2[Backup B2]
        P -.heartbeat.- B1
        P -.heartbeat.- B2
    end

    subgraph QB[Quorum Cluster]
        Q1[Replica 1]
        Q2[Replica 2]
        Q3[Replica 3]
        Q4[Replica 4]
        Q5[Replica 5]
        Q1 <-.peer.- Q2
        Q2 <-.peer.- Q3
        Q3 <-.peer.- Q4
        Q4 <-.peer.- Q5
        Q5 <-.peer.- Q1
    end

    P -->|step1 elect| B1
    B1 -->|step2 promote| P
```

**Interpretation:** Primary-Backup has a *star topology with the primary as the hub*; Quorum is a *fully connected peer mesh* where no single node is privileged.

---

### 4.4 State Machine — Quorum Write Operation

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> SendingWrite: Client issues write
    SendingWrite --> AwaitingAcks: Sent to N replicas
    AwaitingAcks --> PartialACK: Some ACKs received
    PartialACK --> PartialACK: Continue waiting
    PartialACK --> Commit: W ACKs received
    PartialACK --> Abort: Timeout / W not reached
    Commit --> Idle: Respond OK to client
    Abort --> Idle: Respond FAIL to client
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define Primary-Backup replication and state one advantage and one disadvantage.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Primary-Backup replication is a passive replication technique in which all write operations are directed to a single designated replica called the *primary*. The primary serializes and orders the operations and propagates them to one or more *backup* replicas.

- **Advantage:** Strong consistency is naturally achieved because the primary defines a total order of operations.
- **Disadvantage:** The primary is a bottleneck for write throughput and a single point of failure; failover introduces downtime and complexity.

*(3 marks — Awarded for definition, advantage, disadvantage.)*

---

#### **Question 2: State and justify the quorum intersection condition $R + W > N$.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The quorum intersection condition states that for a distributed system with $N$ replicas, the sum of the read quorum size $R$ and the write quorum size $W$ must strictly exceed $N$:

$$R + W > N$$

**Justification:** Any successful write touches $W$ replicas and any successful read touches $R$ replicas. If $R + W > N$, the read set and the write set *must overlap* in at least one replica (by the pigeonhole principle). That overlapping replica has observed the most recent write, so the read cannot miss it. This guarantees strong consistency.

*(3 marks — 1 for statement, 2 for justification.)*

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**
`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and operation of **Primary-Backup replication**. Discuss synchronous vs asynchronous modes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A distributed system uses Primary-Backup replication with **3 replicas** (1 primary, 2 backups) in synchronous mode. Each replica has an independent uptime of 95%. Calculate: **(7 marks)**
  (i) The probability that the system is fully available (all 3 replicas up).
  (ii) The probability that failover can succeed (primary down but at least one backup up).
  (iii) The probability that the system is completely down.

**Model Solution (a):**

The architecture consists of one **Primary (P)** and multiple **Backups (B1, B2)**. The steps are:

1. Client sends writes to **P** only.
2. **P** applies update locally, logs it durably.
3. **P** propagates the update to **B1** and **B2**.
4. **Synchronous mode:** P waits for ACKs from both backups before responding to the client. **Asynchronous mode:** P responds immediately and updates backups in the background.
5. On primary failure, a backup is elected as new primary via an agreement protocol (Paxos/Raft/viewstamped).

**Synchronous vs Asynchronous:**
- **Synchronous:** Strong durability, $RPO = 0$, but higher write latency.
- **Asynchronous:** Lower latency, but $RPO > 0$ — data may be lost on primary crash.

*(7 marks — 3 for diagram, 2 for steps, 2 for sync/async contrast.)*

**Model Solution (b):**

Given $N = 3$, $p = 0.95$ per replica, $q = 1 - p = 0.05$.

**(i) All 3 replicas up:**
$$
P_{all\_up} = p^3 = (0.95)^3 = 0.857375
$$

**(ii) Primary down, at least one backup up:**
Probability primary is down = $q = 0.05$. Probability at least one of 2 backups is up:
$$
P_{backup\_up} = 1 - q^2 = 1 - (0.05)^2 = 1 - 0.0025 = 0.9975
$$
So:
$$
P_{failover\_ok} = 0.05 \times 0.9975 = 0.049875
$$

**(iii) All 3 down:**
$$
P_{total\_down} = q^3 = (0.05)^3 = 0.000125
$$

*(7 marks — 3 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 2 marks for (iii).)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**
`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain **Quorum-Based replication** with a block diagram. Derive the condition for strong consistency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a Quorum system with **N = 7** replicas, determine all valid $(R, W)$ pairs that guarantee strong consistency. For each, compute the maximum number of tolerable failures. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

In Quorum-Based replication, every replica is a symmetric peer. The client (or a coordinator) sends the operation to *all* $N$ replicas. A **read** succeeds when $R$ replicas respond; a **write** succeeds when $W$ replicas acknowledge.

**Derivation of $R + W > N$:**

Let $S_W$ = set of $W$ replicas that acknowledged the most recent write, $|S_W| = W$.
Let $S_R$ = set of $R$ replicas that responded to the read, $|S_R| = R$.

By set theory, $S_W \cup S_R \subseteq U$ where $U$ is the universe of $N$ replicas:

$$
|S_W \cup S_R| \leq N
$$

Using $|S_W \cup S_R| = |S_W| + |S_R| - |S_W \cap S_R|$:

$$
W + R - |S_W \cap S_R| \leq N
$$

For the read to observe the latest write, the intersection must be non-empty, so $|S_W \cap S_R| \geq 1$, giving:

$$
R + W \geq N + 1 \implies R + W > N \quad \blacksquare
$$

*(7 marks — 3 for explanation, 4 for derivation.)*

**Model Solution (b):**

We need $R + W > 7$, i.e., $R + W \geq 8$. Also $1 \leq R, W \leq 7$.

| $(R, W)$ | $R + W$ | Valid? | $f_{reads} = 7 - R$ | $f_{writes} = 7 - W$ | $f_{system} = \min(7-R, 7-W)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $(1, 7)$ | 8 | ✓ | 6 | 0 | 0 |
| $(2, 6)$ | 8 | ✓ | 5 | 1 | 1 |
| $(3, 5)$ | 8 | ✓ | 4 | 2 | 2 |
| $(4, 4)$ | 8 | ✓ | 3 | 3 | 3 |
| $(5, 3)$ | 8 | ✓ | 2 | 4 | 2 |
| $(6, 2)$ | 8 | ✓ | 1 | 5 | 1 |
| $(7, 1)$ | 8 | ✓ | 0 | 6 | 0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symmetric majority $(4, 4)$** maximizes fault tolerance (3 simultaneous failures), but requires touching 4 of 7 replicas per operation. The **trade-off** is between fault tolerance and operation latency.

*(7 marks — 2 for condition, 3 for table, 2 for interpretation.)*

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistakes in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing $R + W > N$ with $R + W \geq N$** — strict inequality is required; equality gives exactly one shared replica *only if* the sets happen to align, which is not guaranteed.
> 2. **Forgetting to specify "out of N" in the quorum** — examiners deduct 1 mark if you say "W=3" without saying "out of N=5".
> 3. **Misclassifying Primary-Backup as "quorum-based"** — it is *passive*; only the read-from-primary side is linearizable. Writes need a separate analysis.
> 4. **Ignoring split-brain** in failover problems — if a network partition occurs, two primaries may exist; you must mention fencing tokens or lease-based prevention for full marks.
> 5. **Wrong units in availability calculations** — $p$ is *probability* (0 to 1), not a percentage. Convert carefully.
> 6. **Forgetting the monotonic version number** — in quorum reads, the client must select the *highest version*; missing this loses 1–2 marks.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Replication** = storing $N$ copies of data on distinct nodes for fault tolerance.
- ✅ **Primary-Backup (Passive):** One primary serializes all writes; backups are standby; needs **failover**.
- ✅ **Quorum-Based (Active):** All replicas are peers; operations need $R$ / $W$ acknowledgements.
- ✅ **Golden rule:** $\mathbf{R + W > N}$ — guarantees read observes latest write.
- ✅ **Majority quorum** is a special case where $W > N/2$; used in Paxos / Raft.
- ✅ **Failure tolerance** for quorum = $\min(R, W) - 1$ replicas can be unavailable.
- ✅ **Synchronous PB** → $RPO = 0$ but high latency; **Async PB** → low latency, possible data loss.
- ✅ **CAP trade-off:** PB → consistency; sloppy quorum → availability.
- ✅ **Split-brain** must be prevented in PB via fencing, leases, or quorum-promoted primaries.
- ✅ **Version numbers / vector clocks** are essential for conflict resolution in quorum systems.
- ✅ **Practical tunings:** $(N=3, R=2, W=2)$ is the canonical balanced configuration; $(N=5, R=3, W=3)$ is the more fault-tolerant version.
- ✅ **KTU favourite question:** "Derive the quorum intersection condition and compute the fault tolerance for given $(N, R, W)$."
- ✅ **Real-world systems:** PostgreSQL/MongoDB = PB; Cassandra/DynamoDB = Quorum; ZooKeeper/etcd = Paxos/Raft (majority quorum).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fault Detection Mechanisms: Basic Fault Detection Techniques (Heartbeat, Monitoring, Logging)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT752 - FAULT TOLERANCE IN NETWORKED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fault Detection Mechanisms: Basic Fault Detection Techniques (Heartbeat, Monitoring, Logging)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Fault Detection Mechanisms
## Topic: Basic Fault Detection Techniques (Heartbeat, Monitoring, Logging)

---

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of **Fault Tolerance in Networked Systems**, **Fault Detection** is defined as the systematic process of identifying the occurrence, location, and nature of faults within a distributed computing system. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PECPT752**, the three foundational techniques constituting the primary detection layer are:

1. **Heartbeat Mechanism** — A liveness-probing protocol in which cooperating nodes exchange periodic signals to confirm operational state.
2. **Monitoring** — A continuous, passive observation technique that collects runtime metrics (CPU, memory, latency, throughput) from system components.
3. **Logging** — A persistent, post-event recording mechanism that captures state transitions, error events, and transactional trails for offline and real-time analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECPT752 — Module 2):**
> *"Fault detection is the first stage of the fault-tolerance lifecycle (Detection → Isolation → Recovery). It must be fast, accurate, and minimally invasive to the host system."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Fault:**
> A *fault* is the adjudged or hypothesized cause of an error. An *error* is the deviation of a system state from its correct value. A *failure* is the non-delivery of a service to the user. Detection operates at the **fault** layer, often before errors propagate to the user-visible **failure** layer.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Overview (Real-World Analogies)

To make these concepts instantly memorable for the KTU board exam, let us map each technique to a real-world scenario:

| Technique | Real-World Analogy | Intuitive Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Heartbeat** | A patient in a hospital whose ECG monitor beeps every second | "Are you still alive?" |
| **Monitoring** | A car's dashboard showing RPM, fuel level, oil pressure in real-time | "What is your current health?" |
| **Logging** | The black box (Flight Data Recorder) of an aircraft | "What happened, when, and why?" |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exam:** "**H-M-L**" — *Heartbeat checks **H**ealth, **M**onitoring measures **M**etrics, **L**ogging leaves a **L**egacy of evidence.* Write this on the first page of your answer sheet if the question is broad.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Fault Detection

The following quantitative metrics are mandated by KTU 2024 outcome-based evaluation (Course Outcome: **CO2 — Design fault detection mechanisms for distributed systems**):

- **MTTD (Mean Time To Detect)** — Average elapsed time between fault occurrence and detection. **Unit: seconds**.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair/Recover)** — Average time to restore service after detection.
- **Detection Latency (Δt)** — The delay between a fault occurring and an alarm being raised.
- **False Positive Rate (FPR)** — Probability of raising an alarm when no fault exists.
- **False Negative Rate (FNR)** — Probability of missing an actual fault.
- **Heartbeat Interval (T_h)** — Period between two consecutive heartbeat messages.
- **Probe Count (N)** — Number of heartbeats used for a single decision.

All of these will be formally derived in **Section 3**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis

---

### 2.1 The Heartbeat Mechanism — Operational Logic

The **heartbeat** is fundamentally a *pull-based* or *push-based* liveness protocol. It answers exactly one binary question: **"Is node N responsive?"**

#### 2.1.1 Push-Based Heartbeat (Sender-Initiated)
1. Each node N_i schedules a periodic timer of period **T_h**.
2. On every expiry, N_i multicasts a "I-AM-ALIVE" message to its peer group {N_j}.
3. Peers update their local `last_seen[N_i]` timestamp.
4. If a peer's timer (timeout window **T_o = k · T_h**, where k is a small integer, typically **k = 3**) expires without refresh, N_i is declared **SUSPECT → DEAD**.

#### 2.1.2 Pull-Based Heartbeat (Receiver-Initiated)
1. A watchdog/supervisor node W schedules a timer.
2. On expiry, W sends "ARE-YOU-ALIVE" ping to N_i.
3. N_i must reply with an ACK within the deadline.
4. If no ACK arrives, N_i is marked **FAILED**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Rule of Thumb:** Push-based heartbeats have **lower detection latency under normal load** but **higher network overhead**. Pull-based schemes scale better in large clusters but introduce additional RTT delay.

#### 2.1.3 The "ϕ-Accrual Failure Detector" (Advanced Concept)
Modern systems like **Cassandra** and **Akka** use the **ϕ-accrual** algorithm. Instead of binary suspect/dead, it outputs a continuous suspicion level:

$$\phi(t_{\text{now}}) = - \log_{10}\bigl(P_{\text{late}}(t_{\text{now}} - t_{\text{last}})\bigr)$$

where $P_{\text{late}}$ is the cumulative distribution of inter-arrival times of past heartbeats. **Threshold: ϕ > 8 ⇒ declared failed.** This is a *high-yield* topic for 14-mark KTU questions.

---

### 2.2 Monitoring — Operational Logic

**Monitoring** is a *passive, continuous, metric-based* observation system. Unlike heartbeat, it does not require explicit liveness probes; it *infers* faults from system telemetry.

#### 2.2.1 Layers of Monitoring (KTU Standard Model)

| Layer | What is Observed | Typical Metric | Tool Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hardware** | CPU temp, fan RPM, disk SMART | Utilization %, Error count | IPMI, SNMP |
| **OS / Kernel** | Process table, syscall latency | Load avg, Context switches | `top`, `vmstat` |
| **Application** | Request rate, error rate, latency p99 | RPS, 5xx codes, RT | Prometheus |
| **Network** | Packet loss, jitter, bandwidth | Throughput, RTT | NetFlow, Wireshark |

#### 2.2.2 Threshold-Based vs Anomaly-Based Detection

- **Threshold-Based (Static):** Alarm if metric M > M_threshold. Simple but rigid.
- **Anomaly-Based (Dynamic):** Train a model on normal behavior (e.g., ARIMA, LSTM); alarm if deviation exceeds **kσ** (typically **k = 3**). Detects *zero-day* faults but is computationally heavier.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 OBE Note (CO3):** Students are expected to *compare* the two approaches in 7-mark sub-questions.

---

### 2.3 Logging — Operational Logic

**Logging** is a *persistent, append-only* record of discrete system events. It is **reactive** (records what already happened) but **proactive** when fed into log-mining pipelines.

#### 2.3.1 Anatomy of a Log Entry

A standard log line contains at least four fields:
1. **Timestamp** (ISO-8601, with timezone)
2. **Severity Level** (DEBUG / INFO / WARN / ERROR / FATAL)
3. **Source Identifier** (hostname, service, thread)
4. **Message Payload** (free-form or structured JSON)

#### 2.3.2 Centralized Log Aggregation

In a distributed system, logs are shipped to a central collector (e.g., **ELK Stack** = Elasticsearch + Logstash + Kibana, or **EFK** = Fluentd variant). This enables:
- **Temporal correlation** of events across nodes.
- **Root-cause analysis (RCA)** of cascading failures.
- **Audit compliance** (HIPAA, PCI-DSS, GDPR).

#### 2.3.3 Log Levels in KTU Context

| Level | Numeric Value | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| DEBUG | 10 | Verbose developer info |
| INFO | 20 | Normal lifecycle events (startup, shutdown) |
| WARN | 30 | Recoverable anomalies |
| ERROR | 40 | Operation failed but service continues |
| FATAL | 50 | Service is about to crash |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse **monitoring** with **logging**. Remember — monitoring uses *aggregated metrics* sampled at fixed intervals; logging records *discrete events* as they occur.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains all mathematically rigorous formulas you may be tested on in PECPT752 Module 2.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variable Definitions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heartbeat Detection Latency | $\Delta t_d = T_o - T_h$ | $\Delta t_d$ = worst-case detection delay, $T_o$ = timeout window, $T_h$ = heartbeat interval | seconds |
| 2 | Worst-Case Detection Time | $\Delta t_{\max} = k \cdot T_h$ | $k$ = number of missed heartbeats tolerated (commonly 3) | seconds |
| 3 | MTTD (Deterministic) | $MTTD = T_o + \dfrac{T_p}{2}$ | $T_p$ = probe round-trip time | seconds |
| 4 | ϕ-Accrual Suspicion | $\phi = -\log_{10}(P_{\text{late}})$ | $P_{\text{late}}$ = CDF of heartbeat inter-arrival times | dimensionless |
| 5 | False Positive Rate (FPR) | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | $FP$ = false positives, $TN$ = true negatives | ratio [0,1] |
| 6 | False Negative Rate (FNR) | $FNR = \dfrac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | $FN$ = false negatives, $TP$ = true positives | ratio [0,1] |
| 7 | Network Overhead (Push HB) | $B_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{nodes}} \cdot M_{\text{hb}}}{T_h}$ | $M_{\text{hb}}$ = message size (bytes), $N_{\text{nodes}}$ = node count | bytes/sec |
| 8 | Anomaly Detection Threshold | $M_{\text{alarm}} = \mu + k\sigma$ | $\mu$ = mean, $\sigma$ = std-dev, $k$ = sensitivity (typically 3) | metric units |
| 9 | Availability with MTTR/MTTF | $A = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR}$ | $MTTF$ = mean time to failure | ratio [0,1] |
| 10 | Log Retention Storage | $S_{\text{log}} = R_e \cdot M_e \cdot T_{\text{ret}}$ | $R_e$ = event rate (events/s), $M_e$ = avg event size (bytes), $T_{\text{ret}}$ = retention period | bytes |

> **Critical Pipe-Symbol Notice:** In all rows above, absolute value bars are intentionally avoided inside table cells. In LaTeX display mode use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ for clarity.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Heartbeat** is used in **Kubernetes liveness/readiness probes**, **AWS ELB health checks**, and **Chubby locks**.
- **Monitoring** powers **Grafana dashboards** in every SRE team worldwide; it is the operational backbone of **Site Reliability Engineering (SRE)**.
- **Logging** is the substrate of **forensic analysis** after incidents; the **PostgreSQL WAL (Write-Ahead Log)** and **Kafka commit log** are industrial implementations of the same principle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

### 3.1 Derivation of Worst-Case Heartbeat Detection Latency

**Problem Statement:** Given a push-based heartbeat with period $T_h$ and a peer timeout window of $T_o = k \cdot T_h$, derive the **worst-case detection latency** $\Delta t_{\max}$.

**Assumptions:**
- Heartbeats are sent at the beginning of each period (at times $0, T_h, 2T_h, \ldots$).
- A fault occurs immediately **after** the most recent heartbeat is received.
- The detector checks timestamps on every received heartbeat.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Let node $N_i$ send its last successful heartbeat at time $t = 0$. A fault occurs at time $t = 0^+$ (immediately after). The next heartbeat that *would have* been sent is scheduled for $t = T_h$, but it never arrives.

The peer's local clock indicates `last_seen[N_i] = 0`. Its timeout fires at $t = T_o = k \cdot T_h$. At this moment the peer declares $N_i$ failed.

Therefore the elapsed time from fault to declaration is:

$$
\Delta t_{\max} = T_o - 0 = T_o
$$

Substituting $T_o = k \cdot T_h$:

$$
\Delta t_{\max} = k \cdot T_h
$$

**Result:** A node configured with $T_h = 1$ second and $k = 3$ will detect any fault within **3 seconds** in the worst case.

**Numerical Verification:**
- Let $T_h = 0.5$ s, $k = 4$. Then $\Delta t_{\max} = 4 \times 0.5 = 2.0$ s.
- Let $T_h = 2$ s, $k = 2$. Then $\Delta t_{\max} = 2 \times 2 = 4.0$ s.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Mean Time To Detect (MTTD) Under Stochastic Faults

**Problem Statement:** A fault occurs at a uniformly random time within a heartbeat interval of length $T_h$. The detector timeout is $T_o = k \cdot T_h$. Compute the expected MTTD.

**Step 1 — Define the random variable.**
Let $X$ be the time from fault occurrence to the next scheduled heartbeat. Since the fault is uniformly distributed in $[0, T_h]$:

$$
X \sim U(0, T_h), \quad f_X(x) = \dfrac{1}{T_h}, \quad 0 \le x \le T_h
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the expected value of $X$.**

$$
E[X] = \int_{0}^{T_h} x \cdot f_X(x) \, dx = \int_{0}^{T_h} \dfrac{x}{T_h} \, dx
$$

$$
E[X] = \dfrac{1}{T_h} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{x^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{T_h} = \dfrac{1}{T_h} \cdot \dfrac{T_h^2}{2} = \dfrac{T_h}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Add the timeout window.**

The detection occurs $T_o$ after the *last successful* heartbeat, but the fault was on average $E[X] = T_h/2$ after that heartbeat. Hence:

$$
MTTD = T_o - E[X] + 0 = T_o - \dfrac{T_h}{2}
$$

Substituting $T_o = k \cdot T_h$:

$$
MTTD = k \cdot T_h - \dfrac{T_h}{2} = \left(k - \dfrac{1}{2}\right) T_h
$$

**Numerical Check:**
- $T_h = 1$ s, $k = 3$: MTTD = $2.5 \times 1 = 2.5$ s. ✓ (sensible: less than the worst-case 3 s).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Tip:** Whenever a question asks "derive the MTTD", always show the uniform distribution assumption explicitly. Examiners award marks for stating the assumption.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of the Three Techniques

| Dimension | Heartbeat | Monitoring | Logging |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Active (probing) | Passive (telemetry) | Reactive (recording) |
| **Latency** | Low (seconds) | Medium (1–60 s) | High (post-event) |
| **Overhead** | High (msg traffic) | Medium (sampling) | Low–Medium (I/O) |
| **Detects Crash Faults?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (via metric drop) | ✅ Yes (via missing logs) |
| **Detects Byzantine Faults?** | ❌ Limited | ⚠ Partially (anomaly) | ✅ Yes (forensic) |
| **Detects Performance Faults?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (latency, throughput) | ⚠ Partially |
| **Scalability** | O(N²) messages (naïve) | O(N) samples | O(N) events |
| **Root-Cause Analysis** | Weak | Medium | Strong |
| **Storage Cost** | None | Small (TSDB) | Large (depends on retention) |
| **False Positive Source** | Network jitter | Threshold mis-tuning | Log parser bugs |

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Heartbeat Network Overhead

**Problem:** A cluster has 200 nodes. Each node multicasts a 256-byte heartbeat every 2 seconds. Compute the total network bandwidth consumed per second.

**Step 1 — Heartbeats per node per second.**

$$
f_h = \dfrac{1}{T_h} = \dfrac{1}{2} = 0.5 \text{ messages/second}
$$

**Step 2 — Bytes per heartbeat per node.**

$$
M_{\text{node}} = 0.5 \cdot 256 = 128 \text{ bytes/second}
$$

**Step 3 — Total cluster bandwidth.**

$$
B_{\text{cluster}} = 200 \cdot 128 = 25{,}600 \text{ bytes/second}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kbps.**

$$
B_{\text{cluster}} = \dfrac{25{,}600 \cdot 8}{1024} = 200 \text{ kbps}
$$

> **Conclusion:** This is negligible for a datacenter link (typically 1–10 Gbps), but for a low-power IoT mesh it would be unacceptable — motivating **gossip-based** heartbeat dissemination in such scenarios.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

### 4.1 System Architecture — Combined Fault Detection Pipeline

The following Mermaid block renders a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** showing how Heartbeat, Monitoring, and Logging cooperate within a fault-tolerant distributed system.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["Node A\nApplication Server"]:::host
    node2["Node B\nApplication Server"]:::host
    node3["Node C\nApplication Server"]:::host
    node4["Heartbeat Subsystem\nPush Period T_h = 1s"]:::hb
    node5["Monitoring Agent\nPrometheus Exporter"]:::mon
    node6["Logging Agent\nFluentd Sidecar"]:::log
    node7["Health Check Module\nTimeout T_o = 3s"]:::hb
    node8["Metrics Aggregator\nPrometheus Server"]:::mon
    node9["Log Aggregator\nElasticsearch Cluster"]:::log
    node10["Alert Manager\nPagerDuty / Slack"]:::alert
    node11["Operations Dashboard\nGrafana + Kibana"]:::ui
    node12["Forensic Analyst\nOffline RCA"]:::ui

    node1 --> node4
    node1 --> node5
    node1 --> node6
    node2 --> node4
    node2 --> node5
    node2 --> node6
    node3 --> node4
    node3 --> node5
    node3 --> node6

    node4 --> node7
    node5 --> node8
    node6 --> node9

    node7 --> node10
    node8 --> node10
    node9 --> node10

    node8 --> node11
    node9 --> node11
    node9 --> node12

    classDef host fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hb fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mon fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef log fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef alert fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ui fill:#fafafa,stroke:#424242,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> **Figure 4.1 Reading Guide:**
> - **Blue boxes** = cluster nodes (data plane).
> - **Orange box** = Heartbeat subsystem (push-based liveness probes).
> - **Green box** = Monitoring subsystem (metric scraping).
> - **Purple box** = Logging subsystem (event capture).
> - **Red box** = Alert Manager (the unified decision point).
> - **Gray box** = Human-facing dashboards and forensic tools.

---

### 4.2 Heartbeat State Machine (Push-Based)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ALIVE
    ALIVE --> ALIVE: Heartbeat Received\nReset Timer
    ALIVE --> SUSPECT: Timer Expires T_o\nNo HB Received
    SUSPECT --> ALIVE: Late HB Arrives\nTimer Reset
    SUSPECT --> DEAD: Confirmation Timeout\nGossip Quorum Reached
    DEAD --> [*]
    note right of SUSPECT: ϕ-Accrual value\nbetween 1 and 8
    note right of DEAD: Node removed\nfrom membership
```

> **Figure 4.2 Reading Guide:** This state diagram is the **standard model** for gossip-based failure detectors. For a 14-mark KTU question, you may be asked to draw this and explain each transition. Memorize the three states: **ALIVE → SUSPECT → DEAD**.

---

### 4.3 Monitoring Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    m1["Application\nInstrumentation"]:::host
    m2["Metrics Exporter\nPull model port 9090"]:::mon
    m3["Time-Series DB\nPrometheus Storage"]:::mon
    m4["Query Layer\nPromQL Engine"]:::mon
    m5["Rule Evaluator\nAlert Rules Yaml"]:::alert
    m6["Visualization\nGrafana Panels"]:::ui

    m1 -->|exposes /metrics| m2
    m2 -->|scrape every 15s| m3
    m3 --> m4
    m4 --> m5
    m3 --> m6
    m5 -->|firing alert| node10["Alert Manager"]:::alert

    classDef host fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef mon fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    classDef alert fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,color:#000
    classDef ui fill:#fafafa,stroke:#424242,color:#000
```

---

### 4.4 Logging Pipeline (ELK Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    l1["Service A\nWrites JSON logs"]:::host
    l2["Service B\nWrites JSON logs"]:::host
    l3["Logstash / Fluentd\nParse and Enrich"]:::log
    l4["Message Queue\nKafka Buffer"]:::log
    l5["Elasticsearch\nIndex and Shard"]:::log
    l6["Kibana\nSearch and Visualize"]:::ui

    l1 --> l3
    l2 --> l3
    l3 --> l4
    l4 --> l5
    l5 --> l6

    classDef host fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef log fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000
    classDef ui fill:#fafafa,stroke:#424242,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECPT752]**
> **Differentiate between push-based and pull-based heartbeat mechanisms. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Push-Based | Pull-Based |
|---|---|---|
| Initiator | Sender (the node being checked) | Receiver / Watchdog |
| Trigger | Periodic local timer | Periodic poll |
| Network Pattern | Multicast fan-out | Unicast probe-response |
| **Advantage** | Lower detection latency; node owns its liveness signal | Scales better; supervisor can throttle poll rate |

*Marking scheme: 1 mark for correct definition of each + 1 mark for the comparative advantage. Mentioning any one real-world example (e.g., Kubernetes livenessProbe = pull) earns bonus.*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, PECPT752]**
> **Explain the role of the "Suspect" state in a gossip-based failure detector. Why is a binary ALIVE/DEAD model insufficient?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Suspect** state is an intermediate level of uncertainty introduced to handle **network asynchrony and jitter**. In a binary ALIVE/DEAD model, a single missed heartbeat due to transient packet loss would wrongly mark a healthy node as dead (false positive). The Suspect state allows the detector to:
1. Wait for additional confirmation (subsequent heartbeats or quorum of peer reports).
2. Tolerate bounded network delays without false alarms.
3. Gradually increase the suspicion level (ϕ-value) instead of making a hard decision.

Hence the three-state ALIVE → SUSPECT → DEAD model reduces **FPR** at the cost of slightly higher detection latency — a deliberate engineering trade-off taught in SWIM and Cassandra failure detectors.

*Marking scheme: 2 marks for explanation of Suspect + 1 mark for justification of why binary is insufficient.*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECPT752, Q12(a,b)]**

### (a) [7 Marks] Describe the heartbeat mechanism in detail. Derive the worst-case detection latency for a push-based heartbeat with timeout window $T_o = k \cdot T_h$, where $k$ is the number of tolerated missed heartbeats.

### (b) [7 Marks] A distributed system uses push-based heartbeats with $T_h = 500$ ms and $k = 4$. Calculate (i) the worst-case detection latency, (ii) the expected MTTD assuming uniformly distributed fault arrival, and (iii) the bandwidth consumed if 100 nodes each send 200-byte heartbeats.

---

**Model Solution for Q12(a):**

A **heartbeat mechanism** is an active liveness-probing protocol in which cooperating nodes exchange periodic signals to confirm that each peer is operational.

**Working Principle (Push-Based):**
1. Each node $N_i$ maintains a local timer of period $T_h$.
2. On expiry, $N_i$ broadcasts an "I-AM-ALIVE" message containing its node ID and a monotonically increasing sequence number.
3. Each receiving peer $N_j$ updates a local table: `last_seen[N_i] := current_time`.
4. $N_j$ independently runs a watchdog timer of length $T_o = k \cdot T_h$. If `current_time - last_seen[N_i] > T_o`, then $N_i$ is declared failed.

**Derivation of Worst-Case Latency:**
Let the last successful heartbeat arrive at peer at $t = 0$. Suppose a fault occurs at $t = 0^+$. The next scheduled heartbeat at $t = T_h$ is never sent. The peer's timeout fires at $t = T_o = k \cdot T_h$, at which point the node is declared DEAD.

$$
\Delta t_{\max} = T_o - 0 = k \cdot T_h
$$

Thus the worst-case detection latency scales linearly with the heartbeat interval and the tolerance factor. For $T_h = 1$ s and $k = 3$, the system guarantees fault detection within **3 seconds**.

---

**Model Solution for Q12(b):**

**Given:** $T_h = 500$ ms $= 0.5$ s; $k = 4$; $N = 100$ nodes; $M_{\text{hb}} = 200$ bytes.

**(i) Worst-Case Detection Latency:**

$$
\Delta t_{\max} = k \cdot T_h = 4 \cdot 0.5 = \boxed{2.0 \text{ seconds}}
$$

**(ii) Expected MTTD (uniform fault distribution):**

Using the formula derived in Section 3.2:

$$
MTTD = \left(k - \dfrac{1}{2}\right) T_h = \left(4 - 0.5\right) \cdot 0.5 = 3.5 \cdot 0.5 = \boxed{1.75 \text{ seconds}}
$$

**(iii) Bandwidth Consumption:**

Heartbeats per node per second: $f_h = 1 / T_h = 1 / 0.5 = 2$ messages/sec.

Bytes per node per second: $M_{\text{node}} = 2 \cdot 200 = 400$ bytes/sec.

Total cluster bandwidth:

$$
B_{\text{cluster}} = 100 \cdot 400 = 40{,}000 \text{ bytes/sec}
$$

Convert to kbps:

$$
B_{\text{cluster}} = \dfrac{40{,}000 \cdot 8}{1000} = \boxed{320 \text{ kbps}}
$$

*Marking scheme for Q12: 2 marks for working principle + 2 marks for derivation + 1 mark for final formula; 1 mark per numerical answer with units = 3 marks for part (b).*

---

> **Question B — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, PECPT752, Q11(a,b)] (Internal Choice Alternative)**

### (a) [7 Marks] Compare and contrast **Heartbeat**, **Monitoring**, and **Logging** as fault detection techniques. Use a tabular comparison along at least 5 dimensions and justify which technique is most suitable for detecting (i) crash faults, (ii) performance faults, and (iii) Byzantine faults.

### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the **ϕ-Accrual Failure Detector** algorithm. Show how the suspicion level ϕ is computed from heartbeat inter-arrival times, and discuss its advantage over traditional binary detectors in a 200-node Cassandra-like cluster.

---

**Model Solution for Q11(a):**

**Comparison Table (any 5 dimensions earn full marks):**

| Dimension | Heartbeat | Monitoring | Logging |
|---|---|---|---|
| Type | Active probe | Passive telemetry | Reactive record |
| Latency | Seconds | 15–60 s | Post-event |
| Overhead | High (network) | Medium (CPU/sampling) | Medium (I/O, storage) |
| Detects Crash | Yes | Yes (CPU=0) | Yes (silent log) |
| Detects Performance | No | Yes (latency, throughput) | Indirectly |
| Detects Byzantine | Limited | Partial (anomalies) | Yes (forensic) |
| Storage | None | Time-series DB | Log archive |

**Justifications:**
- **(i) Crash faults → Heartbeat is best.** It gives the lowest detection latency (seconds) and directly signals the absence of liveness.
- **(ii) Performance faults → Monitoring is best.** Heartbeats only say "alive"; logs only say "what happened". Monitoring captures *quantitative* symptoms (latency p99, queue depth, memory pressure).
- **(iii) Byzantine faults → Logging is best.** A Byzantine node sends wrong/selective heartbeats and may forge metrics, but it cannot retroactively erase its logged actions without collusion with all replicas.

*Marking scheme: 3 marks for the table + 4 marks for the three justifications (≈1.3 marks each).*

---

**Model Solution for Q11(b):**

The **ϕ-Accrual Failure Detector**, introduced by Hayashibara et al. (2004), is used in **Cassandra** and **Akka** clusters. Unlike a binary detector that outputs ALIVE/DEAD, it outputs a **continuous suspicion level ϕ** that grows over time as the absence of a heartbeat persists.
