**Step 1 — Maintain a sliding window of recent heartbeat inter-arrival times** $\Delta t_1, \Delta t_2, \ldots, \Delta t_n$.

**Step 2 — Estimate the empirical distribution** $P_{\text{late}}(t)$, the probability that a heartbeat arrives later than $t$ seconds after the previous one.

**Step 3 — Compute the suspicion level at time $t_{\text{now}}$ since the last heartbeat at $t_{\text{last}}$:**

$$
\phi(t_{\text{now}}) = -\log_{10}\bigl(P_{\text{late}}(t_{\text{now}} - t_{\text{last}})\bigr)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply a threshold (typically ϕ = 8):**
- $\phi < 8$ → ALIVE
- $\phi \ge 8$ → DECLARED FAILED

**Numerical Example:**
Suppose the empirical distribution shows $P_{\text{late}}(2 \text{ s}) = 10^{-1}$. Then:

$$
\phi(2\text{s}) = -\log_{10}(0.1) = 1
$$

This means the node is 1 "sigma-unit" suspicious — still alive. If after 5 seconds:

$$
\phi(5\text{s}) = -\log_{10}(10^{-5}) = 5
$$

Still tolerable. If after 8 seconds $P_{\text{late}}(8\text{s}) = 10^{-9}$:

$$
\phi(8\text{s}) = 9 \ge 8 \Rightarrow \text{FAILED}
$$

**Advantages over Binary Detector in a 200-Node Cluster:**
1. **Self-tuning:** The threshold ϕ = 8 works across heterogeneous workloads because the distribution $P_{\text{late}}$ adapts to observed network jitter.
2. **Reduced false positives:** In a binary detector, a single missed HB causes suspicion; in ϕ-accrual, transient jitter is absorbed below the threshold.
3. **Graceful degradation:** During a network partition, suspicion *rises smoothly* rather than triggering a sudden "stampede" of membership changes that could destabilize the cluster.
4. **Application-aware:** Different services can choose different ϕ thresholds (e.g., a chat app uses ϕ = 4 for fast failover; a billing system uses ϕ = 12 to avoid false positives).

*Marking scheme: 3 marks for algorithm + 2 marks for numerical example + 2 marks for advantages (one each for 2 distinct advantages).*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state the uniform-distribution assumption** in MTTD derivations. → Lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up FPR and FNR definitions.** FPR = FP / (FP + TN), FNR = FN / (FN + TP). Conflating them loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Writing "Heartbeat" and "Monitoring" interchangeably.** They are *complementary*, not the same. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this confusion.
> 4. **Not drawing the SUSPECT state** in the failure detector state diagram. → Lose 2 marks (full state machine question).
> 5. **Omitting units in numerical answers** (e.g., writing "1.75" without "seconds"). → Lose 0.5 mark per omission.
> 6. **Not justifying the choice of technique** in comparison questions. Simply stating "Logging is best" is insufficient; you must explain *why*.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam:**

- ☐ **Heartbeat** is an **active, periodic liveness probe**; detect via timeout $T_o = k \cdot T_h$.
- ☐ **Push-based** = lower latency, higher overhead. **Pull-based** = higher latency, scales better.
- ☐ **ϕ-Accrual** uses a continuous suspicion level $\phi = -\log_{10}(P_{\text{late}})$; threshold ϕ = 8.
- ☐ **Monitoring** is **passive metric collection** (CPU, memory, latency); uses threshold or anomaly detection.
- ☐ **Logging** is **persistent event recording**; centralization via ELK/EFK is industry standard.
- ☐ **Crash faults** → Heartbeat. **Performance faults** → Monitoring. **Byzantine faults** → Logging.
- ☐ Key formula: $\Delta t_{\max} = k \cdot T_h$.
- ☐ Key formula: $MTTD = (k - 1/2) \cdot T_h$ under uniform fault arrival.
- ☐ Key formula: $B_{\text{cluster}} = N \cdot M_{\text{hb}} / T_h$.
- ☐ Three-state failure detector: **ALIVE → SUSPECT → DEAD** (SWIM / Cassandra model).
- ☐ Always state assumptions (uniform distribution, independent heartbeats) in derivations.
- ☐ Always carry units in numerical answers.
- ☐ Use **"H-M-L"** mnemonic: Heartbeat = Health, Monitoring = Metrics, Logging = Legacy of evidence.
- ☐ Remember the **FPR vs FNR** definitions — they are inversely related and frequently confused.

---

**End of Module 2 Notes — Fault Detection Mechanisms: Basic Fault Detection Techniques**
*Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tools and Frameworks for Fault Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT752 - FAULT TOLERANCE IN NETWORKED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tools and Frameworks for Fault Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Fault Detection Tools and Frameworks** refer to a class of software, hardware, and integrated software-hardware platforms designed to continuously monitor, observe, and analyze the operational state of networked systems (routers, switches, servers, applications, and services) in order to identify deviations from expected behavior, anomalous events, or outright failures. Within the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for *Fault Tolerance in Networked Systems*, these tools constitute the **observability layer** of any fault-tolerant architecture.

A *fault detection framework* in the academic sense comprises four tightly coupled components:

- **Sensors / Agents** – lightweight probes (SNMP agents, eBPF probes, syslog forwarders) embedded in network devices.
- **Collectors / Aggregators** – centralized repositories (e.g., time-series databases, log lakes).
- **Analyzers / Detectors** – engines that apply statistical, signature-based, or machine-learning models to detect faults.
- **Alerting / Visualization** – human-facing dashboards and notification channels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECPT752 – Module 2):** The course places specific emphasis on *industrial-grade, open-source, and proprietary tools* used in the **fault detection phase** of the Fault-Tolerance cycle: *Detection → Diagnosis → Isolation → Recovery*. Tools covered fall under three families: **Active Probing**, **Passive Monitoring**, and **Hybrid Inference Frameworks**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital ICU Monitor" Intuition

Imagine a hospital **Intensive Care Unit (ICU)**. Every patient is wired to a monitor that continuously records **heart rate (HR)**, **blood pressure (BP)**, **oxygen saturation (SpO₂)**, and **ECG**. These sensors (analogous to *SNMP agents* or *flow exporters*) push data to a **central nursing station** (analogous to a *collector* like a TSDB). The station runs **rules** — *"if HR > 120 bpm, raise an alarm"* (analogous to *threshold-based detectors*). A doctor (analogous to an *ML anomaly detector*) reviews the **trend over time** and decides whether a fault is real or transient.

In a **computer network**, the same paradigm applies:

| ICU Analogy | Network Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Patient | Host / Router / Service |
| Heart-rate sensor | SNMP / NetFlow / eBPF probe |
| Central monitor | Collector (Prometheus, Logstash) |
| Alarm threshold | Static rule engine (Nagios, Zabbix) |
| Doctor's pattern recognition | Anomaly detection (ML, statistical baselines) |
| ICU dashboard | Grafana / Kibana visualization |

> [!NOTE]
> The single most important takeaway is that **fault detection ≠ fault prevention**. Detection tools are *reactive observability instruments* that minimize the *Mean Time To Detect (MTTD)* — a key Service Level Indicator (SLI).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Introduced in KTU Module 2

The following KTU-mandated metrics underpin every tool discussed in this module:

- **MTTD — Mean Time To Detect** (the average delay between fault occurrence and operator awareness).
- **MTTR — Mean Time To Repair/Recover** (the average delay from detection to service restoration).
- **MTBF — Mean Time Between Failures** (the average operational interval between two consecutive faults).
- **Availability** $\mathbf{A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}}$.
- **Packet Loss Ratio (PLR)**, **Latency (ms)**, **Jitter (ms)**, **CPU/Memory Utilization (%)**, **Error Counters (CRC, runts, giants)** — all measured at the *sensor layer*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Fault Detection Tools

KTU Module 2 classifies fault detection tools along **three orthogonal axes**:

### Axis A — Mode of Data Acquisition
1. **Active Probing Tools** — inject synthetic traffic (ICMP, TCP SYN, HTTP GET) to probe liveness, latency, and reachability.
2. **Passive Monitoring Tools** — observe existing traffic via SPAN ports, TAPs, or flow exporters; no traffic is injected.
3. **Hybrid Inference Frameworks** — combine telemetry with active health-checks.

### Axis B — Layer of the OSI Stack They Observe
- **L1–L2 (Physical/Datalink)**: cable testers, optical time-domain reflectometers (OTDR), interface error counters.
- **L3–L4 (Network/Transport)**: SNMP-based tools, NetFlow/sFlow/IPFIX collectors, active pingers.
- **L7 (Application)**: synthetic transaction monitors, APM (Application Performance Management) agents.

### Axis C — Detection Methodology
- **Threshold-based** (static limit comparisons).
- **Signature-based** (rule matching, e.g., Snort rules).
- **Anomaly-based** (statistical / ML / AI models).

## 2.2 The Big Five Tools (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> The following five tools appear in nearly every KTU 2024 Scheme past paper for PECPT752. Mastery of their *architecture*, *strengths*, and *failure modes* is mandatory.

### 2.2.1 **SNMP-Based Frameworks (Net-SNMP, PRTG, LibreNMS)**
**Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP)** operates on a *manager–agent* model. The manager (e.g., PRTG Core Server) issues `GET`, `GET-NEXT`, and `SET` requests over **UDP port 161**. Agents on managed devices respond with values stored in the **Management Information Base (MIB)**. Faults are detected when polled variables (e.g., `ifInOctets`, `ifOutErrors`, `sysUpTime`) violate thresholds.

- **Strength:** Universally supported on routers, switches, OSes.
- **Weakness:** Polling-based, introduces *staleness*; vulnerable to spoofing in v1/v2c; encrypted only in v3.

### 2.2.2 **NetFlow / sFlow / IPFIX (Cisco, InMon, IETF)**
These **flow-export** protocols record per-flow metadata: `srcIP, dstIP, srcPort, dstPort, L3Proto, ToS, packets, bytes, startTime, endTime`. Tools: **nfdump, nfsen, Kentik, Plixer Scrutinizer**.
- **Use case:** Detect **black-holes**, **DDoS**, **asymmetric routing**, **top-talker anomalies**.

### 2.2.3 **Nagios / Icinga Core (Threshold + Plugin Engine)**
A **plugin-based** monitoring framework. Each service check is an executable plugin returning one of four states: `OK, WARNING, CRITICAL, UNKNOWN`. Hierarchical **parent–child** dependency trees prevent alert storms (the *flap-detection* and *dependency logic* is a frequent KTU question).

### 2.2.4 **Zabbix / Prometheus + Grafana (Modern TSDB-based)**
- **Zabbix:** Hybrid agent-based + agentless framework; supports SNMP, JMX, IPMI, ODBC.
- **Prometheus:** **Pull-based**, single-dimensional time-series DB. Uses the **PromQL** query language and the **pull-over-HTTP** model on port 9090. Combined with **Alertmanager** and **Grafana** for visualization.
- **Key idea:** *Blackbox exporter* for active probing; *node_exporter* for host metrics.

### 2.2.5 **Wireshark / tcpdump (Deep Packet Inspection)**
**Packet-level** passive analyzers. Decode 3000+ protocols. Used for **post-mortem fault diagnosis** and **protocol-level anomaly detection** (e.g., TCP retransmissions, ICMP unreachable floods, BGP keepalive drops).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Steady-state availability | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 2 | $MTTD = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (t_{detect,i} - t_{occur,i})$ | Mean Time To Detect | seconds |
| 3 | $PLR = \dfrac{P_{sent} - P_{received}}{P_{sent}}$ | Packet Loss Ratio | dimensionless |
| 4 | $Jitter = \dfrac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \vert L_{i+1} - L_i \vert$ | RFC 3550 interarrival jitter | ms |
| 5 | $\sigma_{util} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(u_i - \bar{u})^2}$ | Std-dev of interface utilization (used by ML detectors) | % |
| 6 | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dB |
| 7 | $BER = \dfrac{N_{bit-errors}}{N_{bits-total}}$ | Bit Error Rate | dimensionless |
| 8 | $R = \lambda \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ (Poisson) | Failure-arrival probability (Exponential assumption) | 1/time |
| 9 | $H(X) = -\sum p(x)\log_2 p(x)$ | Shannon entropy — used in anomaly detection | bits |
| 10 | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Detector Precision/Recall F1-score | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three "KTU-Favorite" derivations in the high-yield list are: **Availability (Eq. 1)**, **Packet Loss (Eq. 3)**, and **F1-score (Eq. 10)**. The remaining formulas appear as 3-mark conceptual sub-questions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In production, *fault detection frameworks* are the **nervous system** of any service-provider network. Telecom operators (Reliance Jio, Bharti Airtel) use **NetFlow + SNMP + Prometheus** stacks to monitor millions of subscribers. Cloud-native platforms (AWS, Azure) employ **CloudWatch / Azure Monitor / Google Cloud Operations** as their fault detection fabric, often coupled with **Chaos Engineering** tools (Chaos Monkey, Gremlin) to *proactively inject* faults and validate detector accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Availability from MTBF and MTTR

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Availability is the *headline metric* by which every KTU fault-tolerance design is judged. Board questions often phrase it as: *"A router has MTBF = 5000 hrs and MTTR = 5 hrs. Compute its availability."*

**Step 1 — Define the operational cycle.**
A repairable system alternates between two states: **UP** (operational) and **DOWN** (under repair). The average UP-time is **MTBF** and the average DOWN-time is **MTTR**.

**Step 2 — Express total cycle time.**
The expected length of one complete cycle is the sum:

$$
T_{cycle} = MTBF + MTTR
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the definition of steady-state availability.**
Steady-state availability is the *fraction of time* the system is expected to be UP over a long horizon:

$$
A = \frac{\text{Total UP time}}{\text{Total cycle time}} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

**Step 4 — Insert numerical values** (canonical KTU worked example: $MTBF = 5000$ hr, $MTTR = 5$ hr).

$$
A = \frac{5000}{5000 + 5} = \frac{5000}{5005}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to percentage.**

$$
A = 0.999001... \approx 99.90\%
$$

**Step 6 — Express unavailability (downtime per year).**
Unavailability $U = 1 - A \approx 0.001$, so annual downtime is:

$$
T_{down} = U \times 365 \times 24 = 0.001 \times 8760 = 8.76\ \text{hours/year}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Detector F1-Score for a Fault Detection Tool

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is increasingly common in KTU 2024 questions, which now emphasize *evaluating detection quality* of monitoring tools.

**Step 1 — Define the four outcomes of a binary fault detector.**
- **TP (True Positive):** real fault, tool raised an alert.
- **FP (False Positive):** no fault, tool raised an alert (a *false alarm*).
- **FN (False Negative):** real fault, tool *missed* it.
- **TN (True Negative):** no fault, tool correctly silent.

**Step 2 — Define Precision and Recall.**

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}, \qquad R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

**Step 3 — Harmonic mean gives F1.**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting the expressions for P and R, the equivalent form is:**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example (KTU style).**
A Nagios deployment recorded in one month: $TP = 92$, $FP = 8$, $FN = 4$, $TN = 540$.

$$
P = \frac{92}{92 + 8} = \frac{92}{100} = 0.92
$$

$$
R = \frac{92}{92 + 4} = \frac{92}{96} \approx 0.9583
$$

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \times 0.92 \times 0.9583}{0.92 + 0.9583} = \frac{1.7633}{1.8783} \approx 0.9389
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation.**
A $F_1$ of $0.94$ is *very high*; the tool is *both* precise (low false alarms) *and* sensitive (low misses). For *safety-critical* networks (e.g., railway signalling, power-grid SCADA), KTU recommends $F_1 \ge 0.99$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Tool Selection Decision

| Network Scale / Use-Case | Recommended Stack | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Small office (≤ 50 nodes) | **Zabbix** or **LibreNMS** | Single-server, agent-based, low cost |
| Mid-size enterprise (≤ 5 000 nodes) | **Prometheus + Grafana + Alertmanager** | Pull-based, cloud-native, scales horizontally |
| ISP / Telecom backbone | **NetFlow + SNMP + Kentik / Plixer** | High-volume flow analytics |
| Cloud (AWS/Azure/GCP) | **CloudWatch / Azure Monitor / Stackdriver** | Native, integrated with PaaS |
| Security-focused (IDS) | **Snort / Suricata / Zeek** | Signature + anomaly at L7 |
| Deep packet diagnosis | **Wireshark / tcpdump** | Forensic / post-mortem |
| Application-layer (APM) | **Datadog / New Relic / AppDynamics** | Distributed tracing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Architecture of a Fault Detection Framework

The diagram below captures the **four-layer architecture** universally adopted by KTU Module 2 across SNMP, NetFlow, Nagios, and Prometheus deployments.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef sensor fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef collector fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef analyzer fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef user fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000

    subgraph L1["LAYER 1: SENSORS AND AGENTS"]
        A1["SNMP Agent on Router"]
        A2["NetFlow Exporter on Switch"]
        A3["Prometheus Node Exporter on Server"]
        A4["Syslog Forwarder on Firewall"]
    end

    subgraph L2["LAYER 2: COLLECTORS AND STORAGE"]
        B1["SNMP Manager (PRTG / LibreNMS)"]
        B2["Flow Collector (nfdump / Kentik)"]
        B3["Prometheus TSDB"]
        B4["Elasticsearch Log Index"]
    end

    subgraph L3["LAYER 3: ANALYZERS AND DETECTORS"]
        C1["Threshold Engine"]
        C2["Signature Engine (Snort)"]
        C3["Statistical Anomaly Detector"]
        C4["ML Classifier"]
    end

    subgraph L4["LAYER 4: VISUALIZATION AND ALERTING"]
        D1["Grafana Dashboards"]
        D2["Kibana Log Explorer"]
        D3["Alertmanager / PagerDuty"]
        D4["Operator Console"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B3
    A4 --> B4

    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B2 --> C3
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C3
    B3 --> C4
    B4 --> C2
    B4 --> C3

    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    C1 --> D3
    C2 --> D3
    C3 --> D3
    C4 --> D3
    D1 --> D4
    D2 --> D4
    D3 --> D4

    class A1,A2,A3,A4 sensor
    class B1,B2,B3,B4 collector
    class C1,C2,C3,C4 analyzer
    class D1,D2,D3,D4 user
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram-Decoding Note:** The arrows always flow **upward** (from sensor → operator). When a *feedback* or *control* action exists (e.g., SNMP `SET` to reboot a hung device), a downward arrow would close the loop, transforming the *detection* framework into a *recovery* framework.

## 4.2 Comparative Data-Flow: Active vs. Passive vs. Hybrid

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef active fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef passive fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,color:#000
    classDef hybrid fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#33691E,color:#000

    subgraph ACT["ACTIVE PROBING MODE"]
        AP1["Prober (Ping, HTTP GET, TCP SYN)"] --> AP2["Target Host or Service"]
        AP2 --> AP3["Response Time and Status"]
    end

    subgraph PAS["PASSIVE MONITORING MODE"]
        PM1["SPAN Port or TAP"] --> PM2["Packet / Flow Capture"]
        PM2 --> PM3["Feature Extraction"]
    end

    subgraph HYB["HYBRID INFERENCE MODE"]
        HY1["Telemetry Stream"] --> HY2["Correlation Engine"]
        HY3["Active Probe Trigger"] --> HY2
        HY2 --> HY4["Root-Cause Hypothesis"]
    end

    class AP1,AP2,AP3 active
    class PM1,PM2,PM3 passive
    class HY1,HY2,HY3,HY4 hybrid
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
>
> **Q1.** Differentiate between **active probing** and **passive monitoring** tools for fault detection. Give **one example** of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Active probing** injects synthetic test traffic (ICMP echo, HTTP GET, TCP SYN) into the network and infers faults from the response. It is *intrusive* and *consumes bandwidth*. **Example:** Smokeping, Prometheus *blackbox_exporter*, Catchpoint.
- **Passive monitoring** observes in-band traffic via SPAN ports, TAPs, or flow exporters without injecting anything. It is *non-intrusive* but cannot probe devices that have no traffic. **Example:** Wireshark, nfdump, Cisco NetFlow collector.
- *(Conclusion: 1 mark for crisp definition, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for differentiating characteristic.)*

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
>
> **Q2.** What is the **role of the MIB in SNMP-based fault detection**? Mention any **two MIB-II objects** used to detect link faults.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Management Information Base (MIB)** is a hierarchical, vendor-extensible database of managed objects (`OIDs`) exposed by an SNMP agent. The manager queries these OIDs to detect faults. Two MIB-II objects used for link faults are:

1. `ifInErrors` (OID 1.3.6.1.2.1.2.2.1.14) — count of inbound packets containing errors.
2. `ifOutErrors` (OID 1.3.6.1.2.1.2.2.1.20) — count of outbound packets that could not be transmitted.

(Other valid objects: `ifInDiscards`, `ifOperStatus` = *down(2)* indicates a fault, `ifLastChange`.) *(1 mark for MIB role, 1 mark each for the two objects.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Most students lose 3–4 marks by (a) confusing *availability* with *reliability*, (b) omitting the unit of MTTD, and (c) failing to convert between *availability percentage* and *annual downtime in minutes*. Always show all three forms: ratio, percentage, and downtime/year.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 / Model Paper 2]**
>
> **(a)** Describe the **architecture of the SNMP-based fault detection framework**, clearly explaining the roles of the *Manager*, *Agent*, *MIB*, and *SNMP operations* (`GET`, `TRAP`, `INFORM`). *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A web-server cluster reports the following monthly fault-detection statistics from its Nagios instance: $TP = 84$, $FP = 12$, $FN = 6$, $TN = 600$. Compute the **Precision, Recall, and F1-score**, and comment on whether the detector is suitable for a *safety-critical* workload. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Architecture of SNMP Framework (7 marks):**

The **SNMP framework** is a *manager–agent* architecture standardized by the IETF (RFC 3411–3418). It has four logical components:

1. **SNMP Manager (Network Management Station — NMS):** A centralized server (e.g., PRTG, LibreNMS, SolarWinds) that *polls* agents, stores time-series data, runs threshold rules, and generates alerts.
2. **SNMP Agent:** A lightweight daemon (e.g., `snmpd` on Linux, IOS SNMP process on Cisco) embedded in each managed device. The agent maintains a real-time view of the MIB and responds to manager requests.
3. **Management Information Base (MIB):** A tree-structured collection of *managed objects*, each identified by a unique **Object Identifier (OID)** (e.g., `1.3.6.1.2.1.2.2.1.10` = `ifInOctets`). MIBs are written in **SMIv2 (Structure of Management Information version 2)** using **ASN.1** notation.
4. **SNMP Operations:**
   - **`GET-REQUEST`** (manager → agent): fetch a single OID value.
   - **`GET-NEXT-REQUEST`**: walk the MIB tree.
   - **`GET-BULK`** (SNMPv2c+): bulk retrieval, reducing round-trips.
   - **`SET-REQUEST`**: write a value (used for *active remediation*, e.g., interface shutdown).
   - **`GET-RESPONSE`**: agent's reply.
   - **`TRAP`** (agent → manager, *unsolicited*): asynchronous alert on a fault (e.g., link down).
   - **`INFORM`** (SNMPv2c+): acknowledged trap — the manager must send a response.
5. **Security versions:** v1 (community-string, plaintext), v2c (bulk, still plaintext), **v3** (auth + privacy via HMAC-MD5/SHA + AES/DES).

> **Fault-Detection Use-Case:** Nagios/Plixer periodically polls `ifOperStatus`. On `down(2)`, an `IF-MIB::linkDown` trap is fired, the agent logs it, and the manager escalates an alert.

**(b) Precision, Recall, F1 (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute Precision.**

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{84}{84 + 12} = \frac{84}{96} = 0.875
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Recall.**

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{84}{84 + 6} = \frac{84}{90} \approx 0.9333
$$

**Step 3 — Compute F1-score.**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \times 0.875 \times 0.9333}{0.875 + 0.9333} = \frac{1.6333}{1.8083} \approx 0.9032
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.** $F_1 \approx 0.90$ is *good* for general enterprise workloads but **insufficient for safety-critical** systems (e.g., nuclear-plant SCADA, railway signalling, aviation), where the KTU-recommended threshold is $F_1 \ge 0.99$. To raise the score, the deployment should:
- Tune thresholds using ROC analysis.
- Add a *second-stage ML classifier* to suppress false positives.
- Increase polling frequency to reduce FN (missed faults).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Model Paper 1]**
>
> **(a)** Compare the **SNMP-based** and **NetFlow-based** fault detection paradigms. Discuss their relative strengths in detecting *interface-level* versus *flow-level* anomalies. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A core router has $MTBF = 8000$ hours and $MTTR = 4$ hours. The network operations team deploys a new Prometheus-based detector that reduces MTTD from 30 minutes to 2 minutes. **Recompute the steady-state availability and annual downtime**, and explain how a *better detector* mathematically improves availability. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) SNMP vs. NetFlow (7 marks):**

| Dimension | SNMP-Based Detection | NetFlow-Based Detection |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Data unit** | Per-interface counters (octets, errors, status) | Per-flow metadata (5-tuple, packets, bytes, duration) |
| **Granularity** | Interface / device | Conversation / host / application |
| **Strengths** | Detects link up/down, CRC errors, CPU/mem, optical levels | Detects DDoS, top-talkers, asymmetric routing, traffic spikes |
| **Weakness** | High cardinality of MIB variables; polling latency | Requires flow-export infrastructure (sampling) |
| **Sampling** | Typically 100 % polling | Often 1-in-N packet sampling |
| **Best for** | Interface-level faults (cable break, SFP failure) | Flow-level anomalies (worm propagation, exfiltration) |
| **Example tools** | PRTG, LibreNMS, Zabbix, SolarWinds | nfdump/nfsen, Kentik, Plixer Scrutinizer, Cisco Stealthwatch |

**Conclusion:** For *interface-level* faults, SNMP is **mandatory** (it is the only mechanism exposing `ifOperStatus`). For *flow-level* anomalies, NetFlow/sFlow/IPFIX is **unparalleled**.

**(b) Availability Re-derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Recompute availability with the *original* numbers.**

$$
A_{old} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{8000}{8000 + 4} = \frac{8000}{8004} \approx 0.999500
$$

**Step 2 — Note that *steady-state* availability is independent of MTTD.**
MTTD is a sub-component of MTTR. Improving the *detector* shrinks MTTR. The new effective MTTR is the difference between old MTTR (4 hr = 240 min) and the reduction in detection time (30 − 2 = 28 min ≈ 0.467 hr).

$$
MTTR_{new} = 4 - 0.467 = 3.533\ \text{hr}
$$

**Step 3 — Recompute the new availability.**

$$
A_{new} = \frac{8000}{8000 + 3.533} = \frac{8000}{8003.533} \approx 0.999558
$$

**Step 4 — Convert both to annual downtime.**

$$
T_{down,old} = (1 - 0.999500) \times 8760 = 0.0005 \times 8760 \approx 4.38\ \text{hr/year}
$$

$$
T_{down,new} = (1 - 0.999558) \times 8760 = 0.000442 \times 8760 \approx 3.87\ \text{hr/year}
$$

**Step 5 — Savings.**

$$
\Delta T_{down} = 4.38 - 3.87 \approx 0.51\ \text{hr/year} \approx 30.6\ \text{minutes saved}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering insight.** A *better detector* does **not** change MTBF (a hardware-reliability property) but it **shrinks MTTR** by accelerating the *D*etect and *D*iagnose phases. This is the **central mathematical justification** for investing in modern observability stacks (Prometheus, Datadog) over legacy threshold-only tools.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Definition.** Fault detection tools are the *observability layer* of a fault-tolerant networked system; they **minimize MTTD**.
- **Three acquisition modes:** active, passive, hybrid.
- **Four-layer architecture:** Sensor → Collector → Analyzer → Visualization.
- **Five KTU-Mandate Tools:** SNMP (PRTG/LibreNMS), NetFlow (nfdump), Nagios, Prometheus+Grafana, Wireshark.
- **Core Metrics:** MTBF, MTTR, MTTD, Availability $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$, F1-score, PLR, Jitter.
- **SNMP Operations:** GET, GET-NEXT, GET-BULK, SET, RESPONSE, TRAP, INFORM.
- **Flow Protocols:** NetFlow (Cisco), sFlow (InMon), IPFIX (IETF standard).
- **Detector Quality:** Precision, Recall, F1; ROC analysis; threshold tuning.
- **Modern stack:** Prometheus (pull, PromQL, Alertmanager) + Grafana + node_exporter + blackbox_exporter.
- **Availability math:** better detector ⇒ smaller MTTR ⇒ larger $A$ ⇒ less annual downtime.
- **Evaluator's mantra:** *"If you cannot measure, you cannot detect; if you cannot detect, you cannot tolerate."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fault Diagnosis and Isolation: Techniques for Identifying and Isolating Faults
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT752 - FAULT TOLERANCE IN NETWORKED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fault Diagnosis and Isolation: Techniques for Identifying and Isolating Faults

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fault Diagnosis and Isolation (FDI)** is the systematic process of detecting the presence of a fault in a system, isolating the location and type of the fault, and identifying its magnitude and root cause. In the context of KTU's *PECPT752 – Fault Tolerance in Networked Systems*, FDI is the critical *front-line* mechanism that precedes recovery and reconfiguration actions in any fault-tolerant architecture.

According to the standard control and reliability engineering literature, FDI is decomposed into three sequential sub-problems:

1. **Fault Detection (FD):** Determining *whether* a fault has occurred in the system. Output is a binary decision: fault-free or faulty.
2. **Fault Isolation (FI):** Determining *where* the fault is located (i.e., which subsystem, component, sensor, or actuator is faulty).
3. **Fault Identification (FId):** Determining *the type and magnitude* of the fault (parametric vs. structural, size of deviation, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently award only partial credit if students confuse "Detection" with "Isolation." Always remember the hierarchy: **Detection → Isolation → Identification → Accommodation.**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Dashboard" Analogy

Imagine driving a modern car on a long highway (your **networked system** cruising along normal operation).

- The **Check Engine Light** that suddenly glows orange is the **Fault Detection** stage. The car's onboard computer has *noticed* that *something* is wrong, but it has not yet told you *what*.
- The diagnostic code (e.g., `P0301` — Cylinder 1 Misfire) shown when you plug in an OBD-II scanner is the **Fault Isolation** stage. The system now tells you *which* subsystem is faulty.
- The mechanic's multimeter reading — say, the ignition coil resistance is 0.8 Ω instead of the expected 1.2 Ω — is the **Fault Identification** stage. The exact *nature* and *size* of the fault are now quantified.

Similarly, in a distributed computer network, an SNMP trap (detection), the specific router ID flagged in the trap (isolation), and the dropped packet rate going from 0.1% to 7% (identification) constitute the three layers of FDI.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Metrics** that quantify FDI quality:
> - **Detection Latency (T_d):** Time between fault occurrence and detection.
> - **False Alarm Rate (FAR):** Probability of raising a fault alarm when no fault exists.
> - **Missed Alarm Rate (MAR):** Probability of missing an actual fault.
> - **Isolation Accuracy (η):** Probability of correctly isolating the fault to the true component.
> - **Mean Time to Diagnose (MTTD):** Average time to complete full diagnosis.

### 1.3 Why FDI Matters in Networked Systems

Modern networked systems — cloud data centers, 5G core networks, IoT edge clusters, industrial control systems — operate under stringent **5-nines (99.999%)** availability targets. Without effective FDI:

- Failures propagate silently, causing cascading outages.
- Recovery actions cannot be targeted (you cannot fix what you cannot locate).
- Mean Time to Repair (MTTR) skyrockets, violating SLAs.

> [!TIP]
> A well-designed FDI subsystem transforms an opaque "black box" failure into an actionable diagnostic report — this is the foundation upon which all *fault tolerance policies* rest.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Fault Diagnosis Techniques

The classical taxonomy (Chiang, Russell & Braatz; Blanke et al.) divides FDI approaches into two broad families: **Quantitative Model-Based** (require a mathematical model) and **Qualitative / Data-Driven** (require historical or operational data).

#### A. Model-Based (Analytical Redundancy) Methods
- **Observer-Based FDI:** Design a Luenberger observer or Kalman filter that estimates the system state. A residual `r(k) = y(k) - ŷ(k)` is generated. A non-zero residual indicates a fault.
- **Parity Space (Consistency Check) Methods:** Generate parity equations from system input-output data; check algebraic consistency.
- **Parameter Estimation Methods:** Estimate physical parameters online and compare to nominal values.
- **State Estimation (Kalman Filter / Particle Filter):** Use Bayesian state estimators for stochastic systems.

#### B. Data-Driven / Process-History Based Methods
- **Statistical Classifiers:** Principal Component Analysis (PCA), Fisher Discriminant Analysis (FDA), Hotelling's T² statistic.
- **AI / Machine Learning:** Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), Support Vector Machines (SVM), Random Forests, Deep Learning.
- **Expert Systems / Rule-Based:** IF–THEN rules derived from human expert knowledge.
- **Case-Based Reasoning (CBR):** Match current symptoms to a library of past fault cases.

#### C. Knowledge-Based / Active Methods
- **Diagnostic Bayesian Networks:** Probabilistic graphical models encoding causal fault relationships.
- **Fault Trees (FT) and Event Trees:** Top-down deductive analysis.
- **Signed Directed Graphs (SDG):** Qualitative modeling of cause-effect relationships.

### 2.2 The Residual Generation Framework (Core of FDI)

The backbone of nearly all model-based FDI is **residual generation**. A residual is a signal that is:

- Nominally **zero** (or statistically insignificant) in the fault-free case.
- **Deviates from zero** in the presence of a fault.

For a linear discrete-time system:

$$x(k+1) = A x(k) + B u(k) + E_f f(k) + w(k)$$
$$y(k) = C x(k) + D u(k) + E_m f(k) + v(k)$$

Where:
- $x(k) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the state vector.
- $u(k) \in \mathbb{R}^m$ is the known input.
- $y(k) \in \mathbb{R}^p$ is the measured output.
- $f(k)$ is the fault vector (actuator, sensor, or component).
- $w(k), v(k)$ are zero-mean Gaussian process and measurement noise, with covariances $Q$ and $R$.
- $E_f, E_m$ are fault distribution matrices.

The **residual generator** produces:

$$r(k) = V \cdot \left[ y(k) - C \hat{x}(k) - D u(k) \right]$$

The decision function then applies a threshold $\tau$:

$$\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Fault-Free} & \text{if } \Vert r(k) \Vert \leq \tau \\ \text{Fault Present} & \text{if } \Vert r(k) \Vert > \tau \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why thresholding?** In stochastic systems, residuals are noisy. A statistical test (e.g., $\chi^2$ test on $r^T \Sigma_r^{-1} r$) prevents false alarms.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning / Use | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r(k) = y(k) - C\hat{x}(k) - Du(k)$ | Residual vector generation | Vector $\in \mathbb{R}^p$ |
| 2 | $\Vert r(k) \Vert^2_{2} = r^T r$ | Squared Euclidean norm of residual | Scalar energy |
| 3 | $\eta(k) = r^T(k) \Sigma_r^{-1} r(k)$ | Mahalanobis distance / $\chi^2$ statistic | Scalar, compared to $\chi^2_{p,\alpha}$ threshold |
| 4 | $P_{FA} = P(\Vert r \Vert > \tau \mid f=0) = \alpha$ | False alarm probability | Dimensionless, in [0,1] |
| 5 | $P_D = P(\Vert r \Vert > \tau \mid f \neq 0)$ | Detection probability | Dimensionless, in [0,1] |
| 6 | $J_{ROC} = \int_0^1 P_D(P_{FA}) \, dP_{FA}$ | Area under ROC curve — figure of merit | 0.5 (random) to 1.0 (perfect) |
| 7 | $T_d = t_{detect} - t_{occur}$ | Detection latency | Seconds / ms |
| 8 | $SNR_{residual} = \frac{E[r^2 \mid f]}{E[r^2 \mid f=0]}$ | Residual signal-to-noise ratio | Dimensionless ratio in dB |
| 9 | $\tau = \sigma_r \sqrt{2 \ln(P_{FA}^{-1})}$ | Threshold for Gaussian residual | Depends on $\sigma_r$ |
| 10 | $\Sigma_{r}(k) = C P(k \mid k-1) C^T + R$ | Residual covariance (KF-based) | Matrix $\in \mathbb{R}^{p \times p}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The Mahalanobis distance $\eta(k)$ in Row 3 is the *single most important* equation for KTU 2024 exam questions on statistical FDI. Memorize the comparison: fault if $\eta(k) > \chi^2_{p,\alpha}$ threshold.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Equations

- **Aerospace (e.g., Airbus A380, NASA spacecraft):** Kalman-filter-based FDI monitors hundreds of sensors in real time.
- **Industrial Process Control (Siemens PCS 7, Honeywell Experion):** Observer-based FDI for distillation columns and turbines.
- **Telecommunications (5G Self-Organizing Networks):** ML-based FDI for base-station cell outages.
- **Automotive (AUTOSAR diagnostics):** UDS (Unified Diagnostic Services) protocols and OBD-II.
- **Power Grids (Smart Grid SCADA):** State estimation and PMU-data-driven fault isolation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Threshold for Gaussian Residuals

**Problem Setup:** Let the residual $r(k)$ under fault-free conditions be a zero-mean Gaussian random variable with variance $\sigma_r^2$. We wish to find the threshold $\tau$ that achieves a target false-alarm probability $P_{FA} = \alpha$.

**Step 1 — Write the false-alarm probability.**

Under the null hypothesis $H_0$ (no fault), $r \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_r^2)$. A false alarm occurs when $\vert r \vert > \tau$:

$$P_{FA} = P(\vert r \vert > \tau \mid H_0) = 2 \int_{\tau}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sigma_r \sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{s^2}{2\sigma_r^2}} \, ds$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the standard normal variable** $z = s / \sigma_r$, $ds = \sigma_r dz$:

$$P_{FA} = 2 \int_{\tau/\sigma_r}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{z^2}{2}} \, dz = 2 \, Q\!\left(\frac{\tau}{\sigma_r}\right)$$

Where $Q(\cdot)$ is the standard Q-function (tail probability of the standard normal).

**Step 3 — Set the target false-alarm probability** $\alpha$:

$$2 \, Q\!\left(\frac{\tau}{\sigma_r}\right) = \alpha \quad \Longrightarrow \quad Q\!\left(\frac{\tau}{\sigma_r}\right) = \frac{\alpha}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Invert the Q-function** using its relation to the inverse complementary error function $Q^{-1}(x)$:

$$\frac{\tau}{\sigma_r} = Q^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \tau = \sigma_r \, Q^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Using the standard large-argument approximation** (for small $\alpha$, common in engineering):

$$\tau \approx \sigma_r \sqrt{2 \ln\!\left(\frac{2}{\alpha}\right)}$$

This is the formula in Row 9 of the KTU Cheat Sheet. For $\alpha = 0.01$ and $\sigma_r = 1$:

$$\tau \approx 1 \cdot \sqrt{2 \ln(200)} = \sqrt{2 \times 5.298} \approx 3.255$$

> [!NOTE]
> The threshold scales with the noise standard deviation: noisier sensors require a *larger* threshold to maintain the same false-alarm rate.

### 3.2 Derivation: Detection Probability in the Presence of a Step Fault

**Problem Setup:** Suppose an actuator step fault of magnitude $f_0$ occurs. The residual under the fault hypothesis $H_1$ is Gaussian with mean $\mu_f$ and variance $\sigma_r^2$. Compute $P_D$.

**Step 1 — Detection rule** (alarm raised if $\vert r \vert > \tau$):

$$P_D = P(\vert r \vert > \tau \mid H_1) = 1 - P(-\tau \leq r \leq \tau \mid H_1)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute** the Gaussian density with shifted mean:

$$P_D = 1 - \frac{1}{\sigma_r \sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\tau}^{\tau} e^{-\frac{(s-\mu_f)^2}{2\sigma_r^2}} \, ds$$

**Step 3 — Shift variable** $u = (s - \mu_f)/\sigma_r$:

$$P_D = 1 - \left[ \Phi\!\left(\frac{\tau - \mu_f}{\sigma_r}\right) - \Phi\!\left(\frac{-\tau - \mu_f}{\sigma_r}\right) \right]$$

**Step 4 — For symmetric threshold and small $\mu_f$** (most practical case), the second term dominates and the first becomes negligible:

$$P_D \approx 1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{\tau - \mu_f}{\sigma_r}\right) = Q\!\left(\frac{\tau - \mu_f}{\sigma_r}\right)$$

**Step 5 — The non-centrality parameter** $\mu_f / \sigma_r$ is the *deflection coefficient*:

$$d = \frac{\mu_f}{\sigma_r} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad P_D = Q\!\left( Q^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) - d \right)$$

This is the classical formula relating detection probability to false-alarm probability and deflection. It forms the **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC)** curve in FDI.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use Which FDI Technique

| Criterion | Model-Based (Observer) | Parity-Space | Kalman Filter | ANN / ML | Rule-Based Expert |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| System model available? | Yes (accurate) | Yes (linear) | Yes (stochastic) | No (data instead) | Partial (heuristics) |
| Noise robustness | Low–Medium | Low | **High** | High | Medium |
| Nonlinear systems | Limited (EKF/UKF needed) | Poor | Extended variants | **Excellent** | Good |
| Real-time complexity | Medium | Low | Medium–High | High (training) | Low |
| Fault isolation capability | High (structured residuals) | **Very High** | Medium | High (after training) | High (if rules exist) |
| Tuning effort | High | Medium | High | Very High | Medium |
| Cold-start (no data) | Works | Works | Works | Poor | Works |
| KTU exam weightage | **High** | **High** | **High** | Medium | Medium |

> [!TIP]
> For the KTU 2024 ESE, examiner questions often combine two techniques (e.g., "Discuss observer-based FDI and compare with parity space"). Use the table above to structure your answer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level FDI Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sysInput["System Inputs u(k)"] --> sysPlant["Networked System Plant"]
    sysPlant --> sysOutput["Measured Outputs y(k)"]
    sensorData["Sensor / Telemetry Stream"] --> preProc["Preprocessing and Noise Filtering"]
    sysOutput --> preProc
    preProc --> resGen["Residual Generator r(k)"]
    sysInput --> resGen
    resGen --> statTest["Statistical Decision Unit chi-square or SPRT"]
    statTest --> detectOut{"Fault Detected?"}
    detectOut -- No --> noAction["Log Healthy State Continue Monitoring"]
    detectOut -- Yes --> isolator["Fault Isolator Structured Residual Bank or Classifier"]
    isolator --> identify["Fault Identifier Type and Magnitude Estimation"]
    identify --> diagReport["Diagnostic Report Component, Type, Severity, Confidence"]
    diagReport --> ftPolicy["Fault Tolerance Policy Recovery Reconfiguration"]
    detectOut -.-> alarmLog["Alarm and Event Log"]
    isolator -.-> alarmLog
```

### 4.2 Sequential Process Flow of FDI

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 1: Signal Acquisition y(k), u(k)"] --> B["Step 2: Preprocessing Filter and Normalize"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Residual Generation r(k) = y - C*xhat - D*u"]
    C --> D{"Step 4: Threshold Comparison norm of r vs tau"}
    D -- "Less than tau" --> E["Step 5a: Continue Healthy Monitoring"]
    D -- "Greater than tau" --> F["Step 5b: Trigger Fault Detection Alarm"]
    F --> G["Step 6: Fault Isolation via Residual Bank or ML Classifier"]
    G --> H["Step 7: Fault Identification Type, Magnitude, Time of Occurrence"]
    H --> I["Step 8: Confidence Scoring Probability of Correct Diagnosis"]
    I --> J["Step 9: Forward Diagnostic Report to Recovery Layer"]
```

### 4.3 Observer-Based FDI Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    refInput["Reference Input u(k)"] --> actualSys["Actual Plant with Fault f(t)"]
    actualSys --> actualOut["y(k) Measured Output"]
    refInput --> observer["Luenberger Observer or Kalman Filter"]
    observer --> estOut["Estimated Output yhat(k)"]
    actualOut --> comparator["Comparator Subtractor"]
    estOut --> comparator
    comparator --> residual["Residual r(k)"]
    residual --> eval["Evaluation Logic"]
    eval --> thresh["Threshold tau from chi-square table"]
    thresh --> decision{"norm r exceeds tau?"}
    decision -- "Yes" --> faultFlag["Fault Detected Signal"]
    decision -- "No" --> healthy["Healthy Operation"]
```

### 4.4 Knowledge-Based FDI: Expert System Reasoning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    symptoms["Observed Symptoms alarms, logs, counters"] --> kb["Knowledge Base IF THEN Rules plus Fault Tree"]
    kb --> inference["Inference Engine Forward or Backward Chaining"]
    inference --> hypo["Hypothesis Set Candidate Faults"]
    hypo --> rank["Ranking by Certainty Factor CF or Bayesian Posterior"]
    rank --> explain["Explanation Facility Trace of Reasoning"]
    rank --> finalDiag["Final Diagnostic Conclusion"]
    explain --> finalDiag
```

### 4.5 Hierarchical FDI in Networked Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Network Layer L3 Routers and Switches"] --> mon1["Monitor SNMP / NetFlow / sFlow"]
    node2["Server / VM Layer"] --> mon2["Monitor CPU, Memory, Disk, Heartbeats"]
    node3["Application Layer Services"] --> mon3["Monitor Latency, Error Rate, Logs"]
    node4["Link / Physical Layer"] --> mon4["Monitor CRC, BER, Optical Power"]
    mon1 --> collector["Central Telemetry Collector Time Series DB"]
    mon2 --> collector
    mon3 --> collector
    mon4 --> collector
    collector --> fdiEng["FDI Engine Statistical plus ML"]
    fdiEng --> rootCause["Root Cause Analysis RCA"]
    rootCause --> ticket["Incident Ticket plus Runbook Suggestion"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
*Distinguish between fault detection and fault isolation. Why is detection a necessary but not sufficient condition for fault tolerance?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Fault Detection (1 mark):** The process of deciding *whether* a fault has occurred by monitoring the system's input-output behavior and raising an alarm when abnormal behavior is observed. It produces a binary yes/no decision based on threshold comparison of residuals.
- **Fault Isolation (1 mark):** The process of determining *which* component (sensor, actuator, or subsystem) is faulty after a fault has been detected. It requires structured residual sets or classification logic.
- **Why detection alone is insufficient (1 mark):** Detection tells us *something* is wrong but not *what* is wrong or *where*. Fault tolerance requires *targeted* recovery — bypassing the specific faulty component, rerouting traffic from a specific failed node, or restarting a specific service. Without isolation, the system can only trigger broad, inefficient responses (e.g., full system restart), defeating the purpose of fault tolerance.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional Q]**
*Define "residual" in fault diagnosis. What are the two essential properties a residual must possess?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Residual definition (1 mark):** A residual $r(k)$ is a fault-indicator signal constructed from measured inputs and outputs of the system, designed to be nominally zero in the absence of faults and to deviate from zero when a fault occurs. Mathematically, $r(k) = y(k) - \hat{y}(k)$, the difference between actual and model-estimated output.
- **Property 1 — Sensitivity to faults (1 mark):** A residual must be sensitive to (i.e., respond to) the faults it is designed to detect. Larger faults must produce larger residual magnitudes.
- **Property 2 — Robustness / Insensitivity to disturbances (1 mark):** A residual must remain close to zero (within noise bounds) in the presence of model uncertainty, disturbances, and noise, *without* faults. This is the robustness/sensitivity trade-off central to FDI design.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Observer-Based FDI**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** *Explain the observer-based approach to fault detection and isolation in detail. Discuss the structure of a Luenberger observer and the role of the residual generator.*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** Observer-based FDI uses a state estimator (observer) running in parallel with the actual plant. By comparing the observed (estimated) output with the actual measured output, a residual is generated. Any non-zero residual indicates a discrepancy, which may be due to a fault.

2. **Luenberger Observer Structure (2 marks):** For a linear time-invariant system $x(k+1) = Ax(k) + Bu(k)$, $y(k) = Cx(k)$, the Luenberger observer is:
   - $\hat{x}(k+1) = A\hat{x}(k) + Bu(k) + L(y(k) - C\hat{x}(k))$
   - $\hat{y}(k) = C\hat{x}(k)$
   - The gain matrix $L$ is chosen so that $(A - LC)$ is stable and the estimation error $e(k) = x(k) - \hat{x}(k)$ converges to zero under fault-free conditions.

3. **Residual Generation (1 mark):** The residual is $r(k) = y(k) - \hat{y}(k) = y(k) - C\hat{x}(k)$. Under fault-free conditions with the observer stable, $r(k) \to 0$. Under a fault $f(k)$, the residual becomes non-zero, with magnitude and signature depending on the fault type and location.

4. **Decision Logic (1 mark):** A statistical test (e.g., $\chi^2$ test) is applied: raise alarm if $r^T(k)\Sigma_r^{-1} r(k) > \tau$. The threshold $\tau$ is set based on the desired false-alarm rate.

5. **Isolation via Structured Residuals (1 mark):** For isolation, a *bank* of dedicated observers is designed, each tuned to be sensitive to a specific subset of faults. A fault is isolated by observing which subset of residuals fires.

6. **Advantages and Limitations (1 mark):** Advantages — model-based, mathematically rigorous, real-time capable. Limitations — requires accurate system model, performance degrades with model uncertainty, nonlinearity handling requires EKF/UKF.

**(b) [7 marks]** *A residual is observed to be zero-mean Gaussian with variance $\sigma_r^2 = 0.25$ in fault-free conditions. If the desired false-alarm probability is $\alpha = 0.01$, compute the detection threshold $\tau$ and the detection probability for a step fault of magnitude $f_0$ that produces a residual mean shift of $\mu_f = 1.2$.*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Given Data (1 mark):** $\sigma_r^2 = 0.25 \Rightarrow \sigma_r = 0.5$. $\alpha = 0.01$. $\mu_f = 1.2$.

2. **Threshold Computation (2 marks):** Using the derived formula $\tau = \sigma_r \, Q^{-1}(\alpha/2)$:
   - $\alpha/2 = 0.005$
   - $Q^{-1}(0.005) \approx 2.576$ (standard normal table)
   - $\tau = 0.5 \times 2.576 = 1.288$

3. **Detection Probability Formula (2 marks):** $P_D = Q\!\left(\frac{\tau - \mu_f}{\sigma_r}\right)$:
   - Compute the argument: $(\tau - \mu_f) / \sigma_r = (1.288 - 1.2) / 0.5 = 0.088 / 0.5 = 0.176$
   - Therefore $P_D = Q(0.176)$

4. **Numerical Evaluation (1 mark):** From standard normal tables, $Q(0.176) = 0.5 - \Phi(0.176) + 0.5$ (using symmetry): $\Phi(0.176) \approx 0.5698$, so $Q(0.176) \approx 1 - 0.5698 = 0.4302$.

   **Result:** $P_D \approx 0.43$ (43% detection probability).

5. **Interpretation (1 mark):** The detection probability is only 43%, which is poor. This means for a fault of magnitude 1.2 with noise standard deviation 0.5, the current threshold (chosen for low false alarms) is *too high* — many true faults will be missed. To improve detection, one could: (i) accept a higher $\alpha$ (e.g., $\alpha = 0.05$), or (ii) improve the sensor/SNR so that $\mu_f / \sigma_r$ increases.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Parity Space and Knowledge-Based FDI**
`[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 marks]** *Describe the parity-space approach to fault diagnosis. How does it differ from the observer-based approach? What are its strengths and weaknesses?*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Parity-Space Definition (2 marks):** The parity-space method generates *parity relations* (algebraic consistency checks) directly from the system input-output equations. For a discrete-time linear system, over a sliding window of $s$ samples, the parity relation takes the form:
   - $V \cdot Y_s - H \cdot U_s = V \cdot E_f \cdot F_s$
   - where $V$ is the parity matrix (in the left null space of the observability matrix), $Y_s, U_s$ are stacked measurements, and $F_s$ is the fault history. A non-zero parity residual indicates inconsistency, hence a fault.

2. **Residual Generation (1 mark):** The parity residual is $r = V y - H u$. In the fault-free case, $r = 0$ (within noise). In the faulty case, $r$ is proportional to the fault signature.

3. **Difference from Observer-Based (2 marks):**
   - Parity space uses a *finite-window* algebraic test; observer-based uses a *recursive dynamic* estimator.
   - Parity space is more direct and decouples disturbances well through $V$; observer-based needs careful $L$ tuning.
   - Parity space is intrinsically a *batch* method (uses $s$ samples); observer is inherently *recursive* (online, one sample at a time).
   - Parity space has stronger *isolation* properties because the column space of $V E_f$ can be designed to be directionally distinct for each fault.

4. **Strengths and Weaknesses (2 marks):**
   - **Strengths:** Direct algebraic formulation, excellent fault isolation, no stability concerns, easy to design for sensor vs. actuator faults.
   - **Weaknesses:** Sensitive to model uncertainty (no natural error dynamics), computational cost grows with window size $s$, not naturally suited to nonlinear systems.

**(b) [7 marks]** *Explain knowledge-based fault diagnosis using Bayesian networks. How are fault probabilities updated as new evidence (symptoms) is observed? Illustrate with a small example involving two possible faults F1 and F2 and two symptoms S1 and S2.*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Bayesian Network for FDI (2 marks):** A Bayesian Network (BN) is a directed acyclic graph (DAG) where nodes represent random variables (faults, symptoms, intermediate conditions) and edges represent conditional probabilistic dependencies. Each node has a Conditional Probability Table (CPT) given its parents. The joint distribution factors as:
   - $P(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))$

2. **Prior and Posterior (1 mark):** Faults have *prior* probabilities (from historical base rates). When symptoms are observed, the network computes *posterior* fault probabilities using Bayes' theorem, propagated via belief propagation (Pearl's algorithm) or junction-tree inference.

3. **Example (3 marks):** Suppose prior $P(F1) = 0.1$, $P(F2) = 0.05$. Symptoms $S1$ and $S2$ have CPTs:
   - $P(S1 \mid F1) = 0.9$, $P(S1 \mid \neg F1) = 0.2$
   - $P(S2 \mid F2) = 0.8$, $P(S2 \mid \neg F2) = 0.15$
   - Assume conditional independence of $S1, S2$ given the faults.

   **Step A — Observe S1 (no S2 yet):**
   $$P(F1 \mid S1) = \frac{P(S1 \mid F1) P(F1)}{P(S1 \mid F1)P(F1) + P(S1 \mid \neg F1) P(\neg F1)}$$
   $$= \frac{0.9 \times 0.1}{0.9 \times 0.1 + 0.2 \times 0.9} = \frac{0.09}{0.09 + 0.18} = \frac{0.09}{0.27} = 0.333$$
   Posterior $P(F1 \mid S1) = 0.333$ (increased from 0.1).

   **Step B — Then observe S2:**
   $$P(F2 \mid S1, S2) = \frac{P(S2 \mid F2) P(F2 \mid S1)}{P(S2 \mid F2) P(F2 \mid S1) + P(S2 \mid \neg F2) P(\neg F2 \mid S1)}$$
   With $P(F2 \mid S1) \approx 0.05$ (S1 is not a strong indicator of F2):
   $$= \frac{0.8 \times 0.05}{0.8 \times 0.05 + 0.15 \times 0.95} = \frac{0.04}{0.04 + 0.1425} \approx 0.219$$
   So observing S2 raises $P(F2)$ from 0.05 to 0.22.

4. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Each new symptom evidence updates the belief about which fault is more likely. The fault with the highest posterior probability is the diagnostic conclusion. BNs are powerful for multi-fault, multi-symptom networked-system diagnosis where causal relationships are uncertain.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating detection with isolation** — A common 1–2 mark loss. Always state explicitly *which* stage you are addressing.
> 2. **Forgetting the noise covariance** — When asked to compute a threshold, students often write $\tau = \sigma_r$ instead of $\tau = \sigma_r \sqrt{2\ln(2/\alpha)}$. Read the question carefully.
> 3. **Skipping units and assumptions** — A 1-mark loss if you do not state that $w(k), v(k)$ are zero-mean Gaussian.
> 4. **Ignoring the "Why"** — Examiners want analytical reasoning, not just formulas. Explain *why* a $\chi^2$ test is used, not just that you use it.
> 5. **In parity-space questions**, students forget to mention the left-null-space property of $V$. This is the heart of the method.
> 6. **In Kalman filter questions**, do not forget to define $Q$ (process noise covariance) and $R$ (measurement noise covariance) explicitly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Three Stages of FDI:** Detection → Isolation → Identification. Detection is a *binary* decision; isolation is a *classification* decision; identification is a *regression/estimation* decision.

- **Residual:** $r(k) = y(k) - \hat{y}(k)$. Two essential properties: **sensitivity to faults** and **robustness to disturbances**.

- **Thresholding Rule:** Alarm if $\Vert r(k) \Vert > \tau$. For Gaussian residuals: $\tau = \sigma_r \sqrt{2 \ln(2/\alpha)}$.

- **$\chi^2$ Test:** Alarm if $r^T \Sigma_r^{-1} r > \chi^2_{p,\alpha}$ (Mahalanobis distance).

- **Observer-Based FDI:** Uses Luenberger observer or Kalman filter. $L$ (or Kalman gain $K$) chosen for stability and disturbance decoupling.

- **Parity-Space FDI:** Uses algebraic consistency checks over a sliding window. Parity matrix $V$ lies in the left null space of the extended observability matrix.

- **Kalman Filter FDI:** $r(k) = y(k) - C \hat{x}(k \mid k-1)$, with covariance $\Sigma_r(k) = C P(k \mid k-1) C^T + R$.

- **AI/ML FDI:** ANN, SVM, Random Forest, Deep Learning. Requires labeled training data; strong for nonlinear systems.

- **Rule-Based / Expert System:** IF–THEN rules. Works for well-understood systems with rich expert knowledge; brittle to novel faults.

- **Bayesian Network FDI:** Probabilistic graphical model. Updates fault posterior as symptom evidence accumulates.

- **Performance Metrics:** $T_d$ (latency), $P_{FA}$ (false alarm), $P_D$ (detection), $\eta$ (isolation accuracy), $J_{ROC}$ (figure of merit).

- **ROC Curve:** Plot of $P_D$ versus $P_{FA}$. Higher area = better diagnostic performance.

- **Structured Residuals:** Bank of dedicated observers/residual generators, each isolating a specific fault subset. Key technique for isolation.

- **Deflection Coefficient:** $d = \mu_f / \sigma_r$. Larger $d$ → easier detection.

- **Trade-offs:** Sensitivity vs. robustness; detection vs. isolation complexity; speed vs. accuracy; modeling effort vs. data requirement.

- **KTU 2024 Exam Hot Topics:** Observer design equations, parity equations, $\chi^2$ threshold derivation, ROC curve interpretation, comparison table between model-based and data-driven FDI.

- **Standard 5-Nines Availability:** $99.999\% \Rightarrow$ allowed downtime $\approx 5.26$ minutes/year. Effective FDI is a *prerequisite* for meeting this target in networked systems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Practical Examples and Simplified Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT752 - FAULT TOLERANCE IN NETWORKED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Practical Examples and Simplified Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Examples and Simplified Tools** in the context of Fault Tolerance in Networked Systems refer to the real-world industrial implementations of fault-tolerant mechanisms and the lightweight, accessible software/hardware utilities used by engineers to model, simulate, inject faults, validate recovery procedures, and benchmark the resilience of distributed networked systems.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, this topic bridges the gap between abstract fault-tolerance theory (state machines, Byzantine agreement, consensus, replication) and **operational engineering practice**. It covers:

- **Industrial Case Studies**: Production-grade systems such as Google Spanner, Amazon DynamoDB, Apache Kafka, HDFS, Google Chubby, Microsoft Azure Storage, Netflix's Chaos Engineering suite, and the SWIM gossip protocol as used in Consul/HashiCorp.
- **Simplified Toolkits**: Academic simulators (TLA+, P, Jepsen, SimGrid, PeerSim, NS-3 with fault modules), chaos engineering tools (Chaos Monkey, Gremlin, LitmusChaos, ChaosBlade), and monitoring suites (Prometheus, Grafana, Nagios, Zabbix).
- **Fault Injection Methodologies**: Hardware-level (pin injection, voltage glitching, radiation testing), Software-level (exception injection, code mutation, fuzzing), and Network-level (packet drop, latency injection, partition simulation).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Airplane's Triple-Redundant Flight Control System**
>
> Imagine you are boarding a commercial aircraft. Inside, three independent flight computers (a **TMR — Triple Modular Redundancy** setup) constantly compare their outputs. If one computer freezes or gives a divergent reading (i.e., a *fault*), the other two "vote" it out using majority logic. The faulty computer is isolated, and the aircraft continues flying safely.
>
> This is exactly what **practical fault tolerance** does in networked systems:
> - The **three computers** = replicated servers (active replicas)
> - The **voting logic** = consensus protocol (Paxos, Raft, Byzantine Fault Tolerance)
> - The **isolation of the faulty unit** = failure detection + membership service (SWIM, heartbeat)
> - The **aircraft's continued flight** = system availability metric (often **99.999% — "five nines"**)
>
> The **simplified tools** are like the **diagnostic tablet** a maintenance engineer carries — small, focused, and purpose-built to *inject* a fault, *observe* the response, and *verify* that recovery worked, all without crashing a real aircraft.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Constants

The following engineering metrics are central to this topic and are **bolded** for emphasis:

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** — average operational time between two consecutive failures, expressed in hours.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair / Recover)** — average time taken to restore a system to operational state after a failure.
- **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)** — for non-repairable systems; expected lifetime.
- **Availability (A)** — fraction of time a system is operational, given by $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$.
- **"Five Nines" Availability** — **99.999%** uptime, allowing only ~5.26 minutes of downtime per year.
- **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** — maximum tolerable data loss measured in time.
- **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** — maximum tolerable service outage duration.
- **N-Version Programming (NVP)** — multiple independently developed software versions voted upon.
- **Recovery Block (RB)** — primary block + acceptance test + alternate blocks.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip**: Whenever a question mentions "practical example," always tie your answer back to a **named industrial system** (e.g., Google Spanner uses Paxos + TrueTime; Amazon Dynamo uses consistent hashing + vector clocks + quorum). Generic answers without naming real systems lose significant marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structured Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 The Three Pillars of Practical Fault Tolerance

Practical fault tolerance in modern networked systems rests on **three engineering pillars**:

1. **Replication Pillar** — Multiple independent instances of a service run in parallel.
   - *Active Replication*: All replicas process every request (used in Spanner, Paxos).
   - *Passive Replication (Primary-Backup)*: One primary processes; backups stay synchronized.
   - *Semi-Active / Leader-Follower*: One leader commits; followers apply logs (Raft, Kafka ISR).

2. **Detection Pillar** — Mechanisms that notice something is wrong.
   - *Heartbeating*: Periodic "I am alive" messages.
   - *Gossip Protocols*: SWIM-style epidemic dissemination of membership info.
   - *Watchdogs / Phi-Accrual Failure Detectors*: Continuously compute a suspicion level.
   - *Checksums / CRCs / Merkle Trees*: Detect corrupted state during anti-entropy.

3. **Recovery Pillar** — Mechanisms that restore correct service.
   - *Failover*: Promote a backup to primary (DNS rebinding, virtual IP takeover).
   - *Reconfiguration*: Update the membership group view.
   - *State Repair*: Anti-entropy, Merkle tree sync, log replay.
   - *Rollback / Checkpointing*: Restore to last known good snapshot.

### 2.1.2 Classification of Simplified Tools

| Tool Category | Purpose | Example Tools | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Model Checkers | Exhaustively explore state space | TLA+, SPIN, NuSMV | Verify consensus protocols |
| Distributed Simulators | Reproduce node/link behavior | SimGrid, PeerSim, NS-3 | Test fault-tolerance at scale |
| Fuzzers / Fault Injectors | Random/smart fault generation | Jepsen, AFL, Chaos Monkey | Test real distributed databases |
| Chaos Engineering Suites | Inject production-like failures | Gremlin, LitmusChaos, ChaosBlade | Netflix-style resilience testing |
| Monitoring Stacks | Observe liveness/readiness | Prometheus + Grafana | Track MTBF, MTTR empirically |
| Time / Coordination | Test time-based assumptions | TrueTime API (Spanner), Hybrid Logical Clocks | Clock-skew fault handling |

### 2.1.3 Why and How Behind Each Tool

- **TLA+** (by Leslie Lamport): A *formal specification language* that lets you describe an algorithm's states, then exhaustively checks every interleaving. Used by Amazon to verify DynamoDB's consistency model.
- **Jepsen** (by Kyle Kingsbury): An *empirical testing framework* that runs a real distributed database (etcd, Cassandra, ZooKeeper) and induces network partitions, clock skew, and process kills — then verifies whether linearizability/consistency claims hold.
- **Chaos Monkey** (by Netflix): A *resilience testing agent* that randomly terminates EC2 instances in production, validating that the application can survive instance loss.
- **SWIM** (used in HashiCorp Consul): A *gossip-based failure detection protocol* where each member periodically pings a random subset and spreads failure rumors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**: Mastering these tools allows a KTU graduate to (a) design safer systems, (b) audit vendor claims of "fault tolerance," (c) pass rigorous SRE interviews at firms like Google/Amazon/Microsoft, and (d) pursue research in dependable computing.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression / Boundary | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Availability (steady-state) | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Dimensionless fraction or % |
| 2 | Unavailability | $U = 1 - A = \dfrac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Often expressed in "nines" |
| 3 | Series System Reliability | $R_{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | All components must work |
| 4 | Parallel System Reliability | $R_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ | At least one works |
| 5 | M-of-N Redundancy (k-out-of-n) | $R = \sum_{i=m}^{n} \binom{n}{i} p^i (1-p)^{n-i}$ | p = single component reliability |
| 6 | Exponential Failure Distribution | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate (failures/hr) |
| 7 | MTBF from failure rate | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Hours |
| 8 | Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) | $R_{TMR} = 3p^2 - 2p^3$ | Voter assumed perfect |
| 9 | Byzantine Reliability Bound | $n \geq 3f + 1$ for $f$ Byzantine faults | For consensus to be solvable |
| 10 | Quorum Size (Paxos/Raft) | Majority: $q = \lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 1$ | Tolerates $f = \lfloor(n-1)/2\rfloor$ |
| 11 | Anti-entropy Convergence (Gossip) | $T_{converge} \approx O(\log N)$ rounds | For N nodes, fanout k |
| 12 | Recovery Point Objective | $\text{RPO} = t_{checkpoint} - t_{failure}$ | Max data loss window |
| 13 | Recovery Time Objective | $\text{RTO} = t_{restore} - t_{failure}$ | Max downtime window |
| 14 | Five-Nines Downtime Budget | $525{,}600 \text{ min/yr} \times (1-0.99999)$ | $\approx 5.26$ min/year |
| 15 | Phi-Accrual Threshold | $\phi = -\log_{10}(1 - P(\text{alive}))$ | $\phi > 8$ typically = suspect |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip**: Memorize formulas 1, 3, 4, 5, 8, 9, and 10. These appear in nearly every KTU fault-tolerance numerical. The Byzantine bound $n \geq 3f+1$ and the TMR formula $R_{TMR} = 3p^2 - 2p^3$ are *extremely* high-yield.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady-State Availability from MTBF and MTTR

**Given**: A repairable system with constant failure rate $\lambda$ (failures per hour) and constant repair rate $\mu$ (repairs per hour).

**Step 1** — Define MTBF in terms of failure rate.

For an exponential failure distribution, the mean time between failures is the inverse of the failure rate:

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Step 2** — Define MTTR in terms of repair rate.

Similarly, the mean time to repair is the inverse of the repair rate:

$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{\mu}$$

**Step 3** — Express steady-state availability.

The fraction of time the system is up equals total up-time divided by (up-time + down-time). Over a long horizon, this ratio becomes:

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

**Step 4** — Substitute the rate forms.

Substituting the expressions from Step 1 and Step 2 into Step 3 gives the canonical control-theory result:

$$A = \frac{\dfrac{1}{\lambda}}{\dfrac{1}{\lambda} + \dfrac{1}{\mu}} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$

**Interpretation**: If $\mu \gg \lambda$ (repairs are much faster than failures occur), then $A \to 1$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Reliability of a TMR (Triple Modular Redundancy) System

**Given**: Three identical modules, each with reliability $p$, plus a perfect majority voter.

**Step 1** — Identify the three "success" cases.

TMR succeeds if **at least 2 out of 3** modules work. There are exactly three favorable cases:
- Exactly 2 modules work (and 1 fails).
- Exactly 3 modules work (and 0 fail).

**Step 2** — Compute probability of "exactly 2 working."

This is a binomial probability: 3 ways to choose the failing module, each with $p^2(1-p)$:

$$P(\text{exactly 2 work}) = \binom{3}{2} p^2 (1-p) = 3p^2(1-p) = 3p^2 - 3p^3$$

**Step 3** — Compute probability of "exactly 3 working."

All three must function, with only one way to choose:

$$P(\text{exactly 3 work}) = \binom{3}{3} p^3 (1-p)^0 = p^3$$

**Step 4** — Sum the disjoint favorable cases.

$$R_{TMR} = P(\text{exactly 2 work}) + P(\text{exactly 3 work}) = (3p^2 - 3p^3) + p^3$$

**Step 5** — Simplify.

$$R_{TMR} = 3p^2 - 2p^3$$

**Sanity check**: If $p = 1$, $R_{TMR} = 3 - 2 = 1$ ✓. If $p = 0$, $R_{TMR} = 0$ ✓. If $p = 0.9$, $R_{TMR} = 3(0.81) - 2(0.729) = 2.43 - 1.458 = 0.972$, *higher* than the single-module reliability of 0.9.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Byzantine Fault Tolerance Bound $n \geq 3f + 1$

**Given**: We want $n$ generals (some loyal, some traitor) to reach agreement, with at most $f$ traitors.

**Step 1** — Intuition from voting.

Each loyal general must obtain the *same* decision value despite $f$ traitors lying. A simple majority requires $n - f$ votes for the truth to outnumber the lies.

**Step 2** — Establish the lower bound on loyal nodes.

We need **strictly more loyal votes than traitor votes**:

$$n - f > f$$

**Step 3** — Solve the inequality.

$$n > 2f \quad \Longrightarrow \quad n \geq 2f + 1$$

But this is the bound for *oral messages* (Byzantine) with signed messages absent. Lamport, Shostak, Pease (1982) proved that for fully Byzantine (unsigned, oral) messages, the actual bound is stricter:

**Step 4** — Apply Lamport's rigorous result.

For oral messages with $f$ traitors, an additional recursive authentication round is needed. This requires the number of generals to grow as $3f$, giving the classical bound:

$$n \geq 3f + 1$$

**Examples**:
- $f = 1$ traitor $\Rightarrow n \geq 4$ generals.
- $f = 2$ traitors $\Rightarrow n \geq 7$ generals.
- $f = 3$ traitors $\Rightarrow n \geq 10$ generals.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem**: A replicated web service uses 5 servers. Each server has MTBF = 2000 hours and MTTR = 4 hours. The system uses a *quorum* approach where at least 3 of 5 must agree.

**Step 1** — Compute per-server availability.

$$A_{server} = \frac{2000}{2000 + 4} = \frac{2000}{2004} \approx 0.998004$$

**Step 2** — Compute per-server unavailability.

$$q_{server} = 1 - A_{server} = \frac{4}{2004} \approx 0.001996$$

**Step 3** — Apply M-of-N redundancy with $m=3, n=5, p = A_{server}$.

$$A_{system} = \sum_{i=3}^{5} \binom{5}{i} p^{i} (1-p)^{5-i}$$

**Step 4** — Compute each binomial term.

- $i=3$: $\binom{5}{3} p^3 q^2 = 10 \cdot (0.998004)^3 \cdot (0.001996)^2 \approx 10 \cdot 0.99402 \cdot 3.984 \times 10^{-6} \approx 3.960 \times 10^{-5}$
- $i=4$: $\binom{5}{4} p^4 q^1 = 5 \cdot (0.998004)^4 \cdot (0.001996) \approx 5 \cdot 0.99203 \cdot 0.001996 \approx 9.901 \times 10^{-3}$
- $i=5$: $\binom{5}{5} p^5 = (0.998004)^5 \approx 0.99005$

**Step 5** — Sum the terms.

$$A_{system} \approx 0.0000396 + 0.009901 + 0.99005 \approx 0.99999$$

**Conclusion**: This system achieves approximately **five-nines** availability, which is the gold standard for tier-1 cloud services. The KTU examiner expects you to show all five numerical steps.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Practical Fault-Tolerance Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SP["Specification Layer"]
        S1["TLA+ Model"]
        S2["State Machine Spec"]
        S3["Property Definitions"]
    end

    subgraph SIM["Simulation Layer"]
        M1["SimGrid / PeerSim"]
        M2["NS-3 Fault Module"]
        M3["Custom Discrete Event Sim"]
    end

    subgraph INJ["Fault Injection Layer"]
        F1["Chaos Monkey"]
        F2["Jepsen / Jepsen-style"]
        F3["LitmusChaos / Gremlin"]
        F4["Software Im Fault Injector"]
    end

    subgraph SYS["Target System Under Test"]
        T1["Replicated Service Cluster"]
        T2["Consensus Layer Raft or Paxos"]
        T3["Storage Layer Log or KV Store"]
    end

    subgraph MON["Observability Layer"]

    end

    S1 --> SIM

    INJ --> T1

    T2 --> T3

    V2 --> V3
```

## 4.2 Architecture: SWIM Gossip Failure Detection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Node A"] -->|"Ping request"| B1["Node B"]
    A1 -->|"Ping request"| C1["Node C"]
    A1 -->|"Ping request"| D1["Node D"]
    B1 -->|"Ack reply"| A1
    C1 -->|"Ack reply"| A1
    D1 -->|"No Ack timeout"| A1

    A1 -->|"Suspect message via gossip"| C1
    B1 -->|"Confirm suspect"| C1
    C1 -->|"Confirm suspect"| D1
    D1 -->|"Mark Node D as failed"| E1["Membership View Updated"]
```

```mermaid
    I1["Input Stream"] --> M1["Module 1 Active"]
```


> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip**: When the KTU paper asks for a *block diagram of a fault-tolerant system*, always include: (i) the input, (ii) the replicated processing modules, (iii) the comparison/voter/arbiter, (iv) the output, and (v) a feedback path for fault isolation. Missing the *isolation* arrow is the most common diagram-mark deduction.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between active and passive replication with one practical example of each.**

**Model Answer** (3 marks):

- **Active Replication**: All replicas process every request independently and their outputs are compared/voted. If a replica fails, others seamlessly continue. *Practical example*: Google Spanner's Paxos groups — every replica executes the committed log entry. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Passive Replication (Primary-Backup)**: Only the primary processes requests; backups stay synchronized via log shipping or state transfer. On primary failure, a backup is promoted. *Practical example*: Traditional PostgreSQL streaming replication with repmgr for failover. **(1.5 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall**: Students often confuse *passive* with *cold* (offline) backup. Passive = *warm* standby receiving updates. Cold = not receiving updates. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for this error.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: List any three simplified tools used in fault-tolerance engineering and state the purpose of each.**

**Model Answer** (3 marks):

1. **TLA+** — Formal specification language by Leslie Lamport; used at Amazon to verify protocols like DynamoDB's consistency. **(1 mark)**
2. **Jepsen** — Empirical testing framework that injects network partitions, process kills, and clock skew into real distributed databases (etcd, Cassandra, ZooKeeper) to verify safety claims. **(1 mark)**
3. **Chaos Monkey** — Netflix's tool that randomly terminates production instances to validate that the application tolerates instance loss without user-visible impact. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** With neat block diagrams, explain the architecture of **Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR)**. Derive the system reliability expression assuming a perfect majority voter.
