with n' greater than n Accepted --> [*]: Crash Promised --> [*]: Crash ``` ## 4.4 Multi-Paxos — Optimization Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef stepBox fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cmpBox fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 S1[Step 1: Phase 1 once]:::stepBox S2[Step 2: Elect stable Leader]:::stepBox S3[Step 3: Leader skips Prepare for slot i]:::stepBox S4[Step 4: Only Accept phase per slot]:::stepBox S5[Step 5: Replicate log entry to followers]:::stepBox C1[Throughput up 10x]:::cmpBox C2[Latency 1 RTT per command]:::cmpBox S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 S5 --> C1 S5 --> C2 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define Paxos. List the three roles in Paxos and state any two safety properties it guarantees.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Paxos is a distributed consensus algorithm proposed by Leslie Lamport that allows a group of unreliable processors to agree on a single value despite network failures and process crashes. - **Three Roles (1 mark):** (a) Proposer, (b) Acceptor, (c) Learner. - **Two Safety Properties (1 mark):** 1. **Validity:** Only a value that was actually proposed may be chosen. 2. **Agreement:** No two distinct values are ever chosen. --- ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is a quorum in Paxos? Why must it be a strict majority?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Quorum (1.5 marks):** A quorum is a subset of acceptors that the algorithm uses as a coordination point. In Paxos, any group of $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$ acceptors constitutes a quorum. - **Why Strict Majority (1.5 marks):** Two majorities of an odd-sized set of $N$ acceptors must share at least one acceptor (the *Quorum Intersection Property*: $|Q_1 \cap Q_2| \geq 1$). This overlap guarantees that once a value is chosen, any subsequent proposal can *see* that value, preserving the **Agreement** safety property. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Describe the two phases of the Paxos algorithm with a neat message-flow diagram. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Paxos cluster has **7 acceptors**. How many crash faults can it tolerate? Justify using the formula. What is the quorum size? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — The Two Phases of Paxos (7 marks):** **Phase 1 — Prepare / Promise (3.5 marks):** - The **Proposer** assigns a globally unique, monotonically increasing **ballot number** $n$ to its proposal. - It broadcasts a `Prepare(n)` message to a *majority* of acceptors. - An **Acceptor** that has not yet promised a higher ballot responds with `Promise(n)`. It also includes the value (if any) of the highest-numbered proposal it has *already accepted* — this is the critical mechanism for preserving Invariant I2. - An acceptor that has already promised a higher ballot sends a *Nack*, and the proposer must retry with a higher ballot. **Phase 2 — Accept / Accepted (3.5 marks):** - If the Proposer receives promises from a **majority quorum**, it must: - **Adopt** the value reported with the *highest* ballot (if any acceptor reported one), or - **Use its own** proposed value if no acceptor reported a previously accepted value. - It sends `Accept(n, value)` to the same majority of acceptors. - An acceptor accepts **iff** it has not promised a higher ballot. - When a **majority of acceptors accept**, the value is **CHOSEN**, and the acceptors notify all Learners. - Learners then apply the value to the replicated state machine. **Diagram (already shown in Section 4.2 — Sequence Diagram of Paxos):** Refer to the Prepare → Promise → Accept → Accepted sequence. **Part (b) — Fault Tolerance Calculation (7 marks):** Given $N = 7$ acceptors. **Step 1 — Apply the fault-tolerance formula:** $$F_{\max} = \left\lfloor \frac{N - 1}{2} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{7 - 1}{2} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 3 \right\rfloor = 3$$ **Step 2 — Quorum size:** $$Q = \left\lfloor \frac{N}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \frac{7}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4$$ **Step 3 — Verification (2 marks):** - After $F = 3$ crashes, alive acceptors $= 7 - 3 = 4$. - For progress, we need at least $Q = 4$ acceptors → *just enough* to commit. - This is the *minimum* viable configuration; the system degrades to the bare quorum and remains operational. **Final Answer:** A 7-node Paxos cluster tolerates **3 crash faults** with a **quorum of 4** acceptors. **(Full 7 marks)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the Quorum Intersection Property of Paxos. Prove that any two quorums in a 5-node cluster must share at least one node. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Differentiate between **Basic Paxos**, **Multi-Paxos**, and **Cheap Paxos**. Mention one real-world system that uses each. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Quorum Intersection Proof for N = 5 (7 marks):** **Statement (1 mark):** The Quorum Intersection Property states that for any two quorums $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ in a Paxos cluster of $N$ acceptors, $|Q_1 \cap Q_2| \geq 1$. In other words, any two majorities share at least one acceptor. **Proof (6 marks):** **Step 1:** Quorum size for $N = 5$: $$Q = \left\lfloor \frac{5}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$$ **Step 2:** Worst-case scenario — $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ are *disjoint* (share nothing). The total acceptors needed would be: $$|Q_1| + |Q_2| = 3 + 3 = 6$$ **Step 3:** But the cluster has only $N = 5$ acceptors. Since $6 > 5$, two disjoint majorities **cannot exist**. **Step 4:** By the **Pigeonhole Principle**, two subsets of size 3 drawn from 5 elements must overlap in at least: $$3 + 3 - 5 = 1 \text{ element}$$ **Step 5:** Therefore: $$|Q_1 \cap Q_2| \geq 2Q - N = 2(3) - 5 = 1 \text{ acceptor}$$ **Conclusion:** Any two quorums in a 5-node Paxos cluster share at least one acceptor. This is the mathematical foundation of Paxos's **Safety**: once a value is chosen by one quorum, the next proposer's quorum will see the *same* acceptor (and therefore the same value). **(7 marks complete)** **Part (b) — Comparison Table (7 marks):** | Feature | Basic Paxos | Multi-Paxos | Cheap Paxos | |---------|-------------|-------------|-------------| | **Consensus Scope** | Single value per round | Stream of values (a log) | Single value, lower cost | | **Phases per Value** | 2 RTT (Prepare + Accept) | 1 RTT after leader election (Accept only) | 2 RTT | | **Acceptor Count** | $2F + 1$ | $2F + 1$ | $F + 1$ acceptors + $F$ reconfig servers | | **Throughput** | Low (one value per round) | High (leader reuse) | Low–Medium | | **Real-World System** | Leslie Lamport's original proof-of-concept | **Google Chubby**, Spanner, Megastore | Cost-sensitive academic / research deployments | | **Complexity** | Medium | High (requires leader election) | High (reconfiguration logic) | **Key Differentiator (1 mark):** Multi-Paxos is the *production* version; it elects a *stable leader* after one Prepare phase and reuses it for all subsequent slots, slashing latency from 2 RTT to 1 RTT. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cause Mark Deductions in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Forgetting the "Highest-Ballot Adoption" rule in Phase 1b** (3 marks lost in part-a answers). If an acceptor already accepted a value, the proposer MUST adopt that value, not its own. This is the *most-skipped* step in student answers. > 2. **Confusing Quorum with Majority**: A quorum is *any* set of size $\geq \lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$, not exactly $Q$. Some acceptors can be *extra*. > 3. **Writing Paxos is "Byzantine Fault Tolerant"**: It is **NOT**. Basic Paxos handles only **Crash Faults**. Mentioning "Byzantine Paxos" without the modifier will lose 1 mark. > 4. **Ignoring the FLP Impossibility context**: Examiners often award 1 mark for linking Paxos liveness to the FLP result and the need for a *leader*. > 5. **Forgetting to draw the Promise/Accept arrows in the diagram**: KTU examiners demand a *neat labelled diagram* with at least Prepare, Promise, Accept, and Accepted arrows. A textual description without a diagram loses 2 marks in part-(a). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Paxos = Distributed Consensus Algorithm** by **Leslie Lamport** (1998). > - **Three roles:** Proposer, Acceptor, Learner (one node can play multiple). > - **Two phases:** Phase 1 = Prepare/Promise; Phase 2 = Accept/Accepted. > - **Critical rule:** If any acceptor reports a previously accepted value, the proposer MUST adopt the one with the **highest ballot number**. > - **Quorum:** $Q = \lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$ (strict majority). > - **Fault tolerance:** $N = 2F + 1$ acceptors tolerate $F$ crashes. > - **Quorum Intersection:** Any two majorities overlap by at least 1 acceptor. > - **Safety is unconditional; Liveness requires an eventually-stable leader.** > - **Variants to remember:** Basic, Multi, Cheap, Fast, Byzantine, EPaxos. > - **Real-world:** Chubby (Multi-Paxos), ZooKeeper (Zab), etcd (Raft derivative). > - **FLP Impossibility (1985):** Deterministic consensus is *impossible* in a purely asynchronous system with even *one* crash fault → Paxos assumes eventual leader for liveness. > - **Commit time:** 2 RTT for Basic Paxos; 1 RTT for Multi-Paxos after leader election. > - **Safety Invariants:** I1 (accept first proposal), I2 (value-stability across ballots), I3 (majority commits). > - **Exam mantra:** "**N = 2F + 1, Q = F + 1**" — write this on your answer sheet as soon as the question involves Paxos sizing.

topics.title: Raft
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT752 - FAULT TOLERANCE IN NETWORKED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Raft

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Raft** is a **distributed consensus algorithm** designed by Diego Ongaro and John Ousterhout (2014) to manage a replicated log across a cluster of machines. It produces a result equivalent to (multi-)Paxos but is structured around **decomposed sub-problems** (Leader Election, Log Replication, Safety) and a **strong leadership** model, making it substantially more understandable and practically deployable for replicated state machines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Raft is a consensus algorithm that ensures a replicated log is identical across all non-faulty nodes in a distributed system, even in the presence of arbitrary (Byzantine-resistant variants exist) failures, by electing a single leader per term who is solely responsible for log replication.

### Formal Properties
- **Safety:** Never returns an incorrect result under non-Byzantine conditions ($\le \lfloor (N-1)/2 \rfloor$ failed nodes).
- **Availability:** Remains available as long as a **majority** of servers are up and reachable.
- **Liveness:** A leader will eventually be elected (using randomized election timeouts).
- **Time-bound consistency:** Uses logical **terms** (monotonically increasing integers) as logical clocks.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Parliament of Servers"

Imagine a **parliament of 5 ministers** drafting a national policy document (the log). They cannot all write at once — that causes chaos.

1. **Election:** The ministers hold a quick vote. The one with the most votes becomes the **Prime Minister** (the *Leader*). The others become **backbenchers** (the *Followers*).
2. **Legislative Drafting (Log Replication):** Only the PM may introduce new bills. The PM sends a draft (an *AppendEntries RPC*) to all backbenchers.
3. **Veto (Commit):** A bill becomes law only when a **majority (3 of 5)** of ministers have a copy of it on their desk.
4. **Sudden Resignation (Leader Crash):** If the PM collapses, the backbenchers start a *countdown timer*. Whoever's timer expires first becomes a *Candidate* and demands votes. The new PM is whoever wins the next majority.
5. **New Term:** Every new election starts a fresh **Term number** (like Session 1, Session 2...) so old terms' decisions cannot be confused with new ones.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Intuition:** Raft = *One elected boss (Leader) per term, who tells everyone else what to write, and a majority must agree before it's official.*

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Plain Meaning | KTU Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **Term** | A session of parliament (monotonic integer) | $T$ |
| **Leader** | The PM — the only node accepting client writes | $L$ |
| **Follower** | A passive minister waiting for instructions | $F$ |
| **Candidate** | A minister campaigning to become PM | $C$ |
| **Election Timeout** | Countdown before declaring the leader dead | $t_e$ |
| **Heartbeat** | The PM's periodic "I'm alive" message | $h$ |
| **Log Entry** | A single command appended to the log | $e$ |
| **Commit Index** | Highest log entry known to be persisted on a majority | $ci$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** The **election timeout** is typically randomized in the range **150 ms – 300 ms**, and **heartbeat intervals** are roughly **1/10th** of the timeout (e.g., 15–30 ms). Always bolded in KTU answers.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Raft

Raft decomposes the consensus problem into three **independent, modular sub-problems** — this is the single most important architectural fact for KTU answers.

### Pillar 1 — Leader Election
- Every node starts as a **Follower**.
- Followers passively listen; they expect a heartbeat every $h$ ms.
- If $h$ is not received within the **randomized election timeout** $t_e \sim U[150, 300]$ ms, the Follower increments its current term $T \leftarrow T+1$ and transitions to **Candidate**.
- The Candidate votes for itself, issues `RequestVote` RPCs in parallel to all other nodes, and waits for a **majority response**.
- Three possible outcomes for a Candidate:
  1. **Wins** (votes $\ge \lceil N/2 \rceil$ + 1) → becomes **Leader**, immediately sends heartbeats.
  2. **Discovers a valid Leader** (receives valid `AppendEntries`) → reverts to **Follower**.
  3. **Split Vote / Timeout** → increments term, restarts election with a *new random timeout*.

### Pillar 2 — Log Replication
- The **Leader** is the *sole acceptor* of client requests.
- For each request, the Leader appends a new entry to its log, then issues `AppendEntries` RPCs to all Followers in parallel.
- When an entry is replicated to a **majority** of nodes, it is marked **committed** ($ci$ advances) and applied to the state machine.
- The Leader then notifies all Followers of the new $ci$ in subsequent heartbeats.
- **Log Matching Property:** *If two logs contain an entry with the same index and term, then the logs are identical in all entries up through that index.*

### Pillar 3 — Safety
Guarantees that, once an entry is committed, it will appear in the logs of *all* future Leaders. This is enforced by:
- **Election Restriction (the most subtle rule):** A Candidate must have a log that is "at least as up-to-date" as the voter's log to receive a vote. "Up-to-date" is defined by:
  1. Higher last-term wins; if same last-term, **longer log wins**.
- **Leader Completeness Property:** *If a log entry is committed in term $T$, it will appear in the logs of every leader of all terms $> T$.*

---

## 2.2 State Machine — The Heart of Raft

Every node persists three pieces of state and two transient pieces of state.

| State Type | Field | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Persistent (on disk) | `currentTerm` | Latest term seen by this server |
| Persistent | `votedFor` | Candidate ID this server voted for in current term |
| Persistent | `log[]` | Array of log entries `{term, command}` |
| Volatile (RAM) | `commitIndex` | Highest index known to be committed |
| Volatile | `lastApplied` | Highest index applied to state machine |
| Leader-only Volatile | `nextIndex[]` | For each peer: next log index to send |
| Leader-only Volatile | `matchIndex[]` | For each peer: highest index known replicated |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **high-yield formula card** — memorize it. Every line below is a guaranteed "definition/short answer" mark.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Majority Quorum | $Q = \lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$ | Required for commit & for election win |
| Max Faulty Tolerated | $f = \lfloor (N-1)/2 \rfloor$ | Crash-fault model (non-Byzantine) |
| Election Timeout Range | $t_e \in [150, 300]$ ms | Random per node, per round |
| Heartbeat Interval | $h \approx t_e / 10$ | Usually 15–50 ms |
| Up-to-date comparator | $\vert T_a^{last} \vert \;?\; \vert L_a \vert \;?\; L_b$ | Compare last-term, then log length |
| Term monotonicity | $T_{new} > T_{old}$ (strict) | Reject RPCs with stale terms |
| Commit rule | $\exists$ majority with $e_i$ at $i$, AND $T(e_i) = T_{current}$ | Prevents old-term committal in one term |
| Availability bound | $T_{broadcast} \ll t_e \ll MTBF_{server}$ | Time hierarchy for liveness |

> **Avoid the pipe bug:** In the table above, $\vert T_a^{last} \vert$ uses `\vert` instead of `|` so the markdown table does not break.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Used in production by:** etcd (Kubernetes' backbone), Consul (HashiCorp), CockroachDB, TiKV, WeChat's infrastructure.
- **Why engineers choose Raft over Paxos:** Independently-verifiable logs, predictable leadership, fast linearizable reads from the leader.
- **Failure model addressed:** Crash-stop, network partitions (CP in CAP), packet loss, delays.
- **What Raft does NOT cover:** Byzantine (malicious) failures — for that, PBFT or HotStuff are used. *(Examiner's favorite trick question!)*

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quorum Overlap & Safety

**Claim:** Two majorities in the same term cannot both commit *different* entries at the same index.

**Proof by Quorum Intersection:**

Let the cluster size be $N$, an odd positive integer. A majority requires $Q = \lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$ votes.

$$
Q + Q = 2\left(\left\lfloor \frac{N}{2} \right\rfloor + 1\right)
$$

For odd $N = 2k+1$:

$$
2\left(\left\lfloor \frac{2k+1}{2} \right\rfloor + 1\right) = 2(k + 1) = 2k + 2 = N + 1
$$

Since $Q + Q = N + 1 > N$, by the **Pigeonhole Principle**, two majorities must share at least:

$$
(Q + Q) - N = (N + 1) - N = 1 \text{ node}
$$

This single shared node is the **witness of consistency** — it cannot have voted for two different entries at the same index in the same term. ∎

> **Why it matters:** This is the *mathematical reason* Raft can never lose committed data during a partition, even if the old leader keeps trying to append entries in an isolated minority.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Leader Completeness via the Up-to-Date Check

**Claim:** A newly elected Leader $L_{new}$ at term $T_{new}$ must contain *all* entries committed in any term $T < T_{new}$.

**Step-by-step logical chain:**

Let $e_c$ be any entry committed in term $T_c < T_{new}$. By definition of commit, $e_c$ exists on a **majority set** $M_c$ in term $T_c$.

During the election of $L_{new}$:

1. $L_{new}$ requested votes from a majority set $V_{new}$.
2. By quorum intersection (from 3.1), $M_c \cap V_{new} \ne \emptyset$. Call this intersection node $x$.
3. Node $x$ has $e_c$ in its log at the time it votes for $L_{new}$.
4. For $x$ to vote for $L_{new}$, $L_{new}$'s log must be **at least as up-to-date** as $x$'s log.
5. The up-to-date rule (compare last-term, then length) means: any log entry present in $x$'s log must also be present (or superseded) in $L_{new}$'s log.
6. Therefore $e_c \in L_{new}.\text{log}$. ∎

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's pivot point:** A common KTU trap is asking "Why can't a stale leader overwrite a committed entry?" The answer hinges entirely on this derivation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — State Transition Table (Analytical Matrix)

Every state transition is **deterministic** given an event. Below is the full Raft state-machine matrix, which KTU regularly asks students to draw.

| Current State | Event / Trigger | New State | Side Effects |
|---|---|---|---|
| Follower | Timeout with no heartbeat | Candidate | $T \mathrel{+}= 1$, votes for self, resets $t_e$ |
| Follower | `AppendEntries` from valid Leader | Follower | Resets $t_e$, returns success |
| Follower | `AppendEntries` with stale $T$ | Follower | Replies `false`, updates $T$ to higher |
| Follower | `RequestVote` with up-to-date candidate | Follower | Records vote, resets $t_e$ |
| Candidate | Receives **majority** votes | Leader | Sends initial heartbeats |
| Candidate | Discovers valid Leader (heartbeat) | Follower | Resets $t_e$ |
| Candidate | Election timeout expires | Candidate | $T \mathrel{+}= 1$, new election round |
| Leader | Client request | Leader | Appends entry, replicates via `AppendEntries` |
| Leader | Entry stored on majority | Leader | Advances $ci$, notifies followers |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Election Outcome Probabilities (Split Vote)

Split votes are the *only* mechanism that *can* delay progress in Raft (it is otherwise lock-free). The probability of a split vote in a single round with $N$ nodes and random timeouts in $[T_{min}, T_{max}]$:

$$
P_{split} = \frac{\text{favorable intervals}}{\text{all intervals}} \approx \left(\frac{1}{N-1}\right)^{N-1}
$$

For a 5-node cluster:

$$
P_{split} = \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{4} = \frac{1}{256} \approx 0.39\%
$$

Expected rounds to elect a leader:

$$
E[\text{rounds}] = \frac{1}{1 - P_{split}} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.0039} \approx 1.004
$$

> **Real-world consequence:** This is why Raft almost never *actually* splits votes despite the possibility — randomization makes collisions rare.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A 5-node Raft cluster has one Leader and one Follower that has been partitioned off. What is the smallest cluster that can still commit a new entry?

**Solution:**

$$
N_{reachable} = 5 - 1 = 4 \quad \text{(partitioned Follower excluded)}
$$

$$
Q = \left\lfloor \frac{4}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = 3
$$

The Leader needs **3 affirmative acknowledgements** (including itself) to mark an entry committed. With 4 reachable nodes, that is achievable. So the cluster *can* commit — but if 2 nodes were partitioned off:

$$
N_{reachable} = 3, \quad Q = 2
$$

It still works (3 ≥ 2). Only when $N_{reachable} = 2$ does the system halt, because $Q = 2$ is satisfied but the partitioned Leader is also stale. This illustrates the **CP nature of Raft**: it chooses Consistency over Availability under partition.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid State Machine of a Raft Server

The state diagram below captures every legal transition. (Per KTU safeguards, all node IDs are alphanumeric; no reserved keywords are used; labels are plain text.)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> F1 : start as Follower
    F1 : Follower
    C1 : Candidate
    L1 : Leader

    F1 --> C1 : election timeout expires
    F1 --> F1 : valid AppendEntries received
    F1 --> F1 : grant vote to candidate

    C1 --> L1 : received majority of votes
    C1 --> F1 : discovers higher term or valid leader
    C1 --> C1 : election timeout expires, new term

    L1 --> F1 : discovers higher term
    L1 --> L1 : client request appended
    L1 --> L1 : AppendEntries replicated to majority

    note right of L1
      Leader sends periodic
      heartbeats to all Followers
      to prevent new elections
    end note

    note right of C1
      Candidate votes for self
      and sends RequestVote RPCs
      in parallel
    end note
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: A Replicated Log via Raft

When a topic (log replication) requires a physical-data-flow drawing, we map the **functional flow** instead of a physical sketch.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CLIENT] -->|clientRequest| B[LEADER NODE]
    B -->|AppendEntries RPC| C[FOLLOWER 1]
    B -->|AppendEntries RPC| D[FOLLOWER 2]
    B -->|AppendEntries RPC| E[FOLLOWER 3]
    B -->|AppendEntries RPC| F[FOLLOWER 4]

    C -->|ACK success| B
    D -->|ACK success| B
    E -->|ACK success| B
    F -->|ACK success| B

    B -->|commit on majority| G[STATE MACHINE]
    G -->|response| A

    H[ELECTION LAYER] -.->|RequestVote| B
    H -.->|RequestVote| C
    H -.->|RequestVote| D
    H -.->|RequestVote| E
    H -.->|RequestVote| F

    B -.->|heartbeat every h ms| C
    B -.->|heartbeat every h ms| D
    B -.->|heartbeat every h ms| E
    B -.->|heartbeat every h ms| F

    style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style B fill:#7FCBFF,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style C fill:#D5D5D5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style D fill:#D5D5D5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style E fill:#D5D5D5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style F fill:#D5D5D5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style G fill:#B6F0B6,stroke:#0F4F0F,color:#000
    style H fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#660000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **solid arrows** are the hot path of *log replication* (client → leader → followers → state machine → reply). The **dotted arrows** are the *control plane*: elections and heartbeats. This separation is the architectural insight that makes Raft a **CP system**.

---

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram: A Successful Leader Election Round

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant F1 as Follower 1
    participant F2 as Follower 2
    participant F3 as Follower 3
    participant F4 as Follower 4
    participant F5 as Follower 5 (becomes Candidate)

    F5 ->> F5 : election timeout fires
    F5 ->> F5 : term T plus 1, voteFor = self
    F5 ->> F1 : RequestVote term T
    F5 ->> F2 : RequestVote term T
    F5 ->> F3 : RequestVote term T
    F5 ->> F4 : RequestVote term T

    F1 -->> F5 : vote granted
    F2 -->> F5 : vote granted
    F3 -->> F5 : vote granted
    F4 -->> F5 : vote denied (longer log)

    Note over F5 : 3 of 4 votes plus self equals majority of 5
    F5 ->> F5 : transition to Leader
    F5 ->> F1 : AppendEntries heartbeat term T
    F5 ->> F2 : AppendEntries heartbeat term T
    F5 ->> F3 : AppendEntries heartbeat term T
    F5 ->> F4 : AppendEntries heartbeat term T
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Raft and Paxos consensus algorithms. List the three sub-problems that Raft decomposes consensus into.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Raft is a consensus algorithm designed to be more understandable than Paxos while providing equivalent fault tolerance. The key differences are:

| Aspect | Raft | Paxos |
|---|---|---|
| Leadership | Strong leader per term | Weak, any proposer can lead |
| Decomposition | 3 explicit sub-problems | Monolithic |
| Log structure | Sequential, easy to reason about | Multi-ballot, complex |
| Understandability | High (designed for pedagogy) | Low (famously subtle) |

The three sub-problems of Raft are:
1. **Leader Election** — choosing one coordinator per term.
2. **Log Replication** — propagating client commands from the leader to a majority of followers.
3. **Safety** — ensuring committed entries survive leader changes (Leader Completeness + State Machine Safety).

*(1 mark for tabular difference, 1.5 marks for sub-problems, 0.5 mark for clarity.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]
**Explain the concept of "Term" in Raft. How does a node behave upon receiving an RPC with a stale term?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **term** is a monotonically increasing integer that acts as Raft's logical clock. Each term begins with an election, and at most one leader is elected per term. Terms are exchanged in every RPC (`RequestVote`, `AppendEntries`).

Behaviour upon receiving an RPC with a **stale term** $T_{rpc} < T_{local}$:
1. The receiver **rejects the RPC** (returns `false`).
2. The receiver **does not** modify its own state machine based on the rejected request.
3. The leader, upon learning of a higher term, **immediately steps down** to Follower and updates its `currentTerm`.

This mechanism is what makes Raft **safe across partitions** — old leaders cannot confuse new majorities. *(2 marks for term definition, 1 mark for stale-term behaviour.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> *As per KTU 2024 scheme, every Part B question carries an internal choice. Two fully independent alternatives are provided.*

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**Q. (a)** With a neat state diagram, explain the **three server states and their transitions** in the Raft consensus algorithm. Discuss the role of randomized election timeouts in preventing split-vote scenarios. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**The Three States (5 marks):**

1. **Follower** *(passive state)*: Issues no RPCs; responds to RPCs from candidates and the leader. Resets its election timer whenever it receives a valid `AppendEntries` heartbeat from the leader.

2. **Candidate** *(transient state)*: Used during an election. On election timeout, the follower increments its `currentTerm`, votes for itself, and issues parallel `RequestVote` RPCs to all other peers. Stays a candidate until one of three outcomes: (i) wins the election, (ii) discovers a valid leader, (iii) another timeout elapses.

3. **Leader** *(active state)*: Sends periodic heartbeats to all followers, accepts client requests, replicates log entries, and advances the `commitIndex` once an entry is replicated to a majority.

**Transitions:**
- Follower → Candidate (on election timeout with no heartbeat)
- Candidate → Leader (on receiving a majority of votes)
- Candidate → Follower (on discovering a higher term or a valid leader)
- Leader → Follower (on discovering a higher term — e.g., from a partitioned peer that came back)

**Role of Randomized Election Timeouts (2 marks):**

In a split vote, no candidate receives a majority. If all nodes used *identical* timeouts, every new round would also split → infinite livelock. By **randomizing** the timeout in the range **150–300 ms** (uniform distribution), statistically at least one node's timer will fire well before the others, allowing it to claim votes before competition arrives. The probability of repeated splits is:

$$
P_{split}^{k} = \left(\frac{1}{N-1}\right)^{k(N-1)} \to 0 \text{ as } k \to \infty
$$

Therefore liveness is guaranteed in expected constant time.

---

**Q. (b)** Describe the **Log Replication** mechanism in Raft. Explain the **Log Matching Property** and how the leader uses `nextIndex` and `matchIndex` to maintain consistency across followers. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Log Replication Process (3 marks):**

1. The client sends a request to the **Leader** (redirected by followers).
2. The Leader appends a new entry to its log: `{term: T, command: cmd}`.
3. The Leader issues `AppendEntries` RPCs in parallel to all followers.
4. Each follower appends the entry (after consistency checks) and replies `success`.
5. When the Leader receives `success` from a **majority**, it marks the entry **committed**, applies it to its state machine, and notifies followers of the new `commitIndex` in subsequent heartbeats.

**Log Matching Property (2 marks):**

If two logs contain an entry with the same `(index, term)`, then:
- They contain the **same command** at that index.
- They are **identical in all preceding entries**.

This is preserved by a consistency check in `AppendEntries`: the leader sends `prevLogIndex` and `prevLogTerm`. The follower rejects if its entry at `prevLogIndex` does not match `prevLogTerm`.

**`nextIndex` and `matchIndex` (2 marks):**

The Leader maintains two arrays per follower:
- `nextIndex[i]` — the next log index the Leader will send to follower $i$. Initialized to `(leaderLastLogIndex + 1)`.
- `matchIndex[i]` — the highest index known to be replicated on follower $i$.

If an `AppendEntries` is rejected (e.g., because of a log inconsistency after a leader change), the Leader **decrements `nextIndex[i]`** and retries. This is a *linear* backoff (one step at a time) which works because the leader is correct and the follower's log must eventually match somewhere — the worst case is bounded by the length of the leader's log.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget that `matchIndex` is *updated only after a successful* `AppendEntries` reply, not optimistically. Marking a follower as "matched" before the RPC returns is a guaranteed $-2$ mark deduction.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternative]*

**Q. (a)** Discuss the **safety properties** of Raft in detail. Explain the **Election Restriction** rule and prove why it preserves the **Leader Completeness Property**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Five Raft Safety Properties (3 marks):**

1. **Election Safety:** At most one leader per term.
2. **Leader Append-Only:** A leader never overwrites or deletes entries in its own log; only appends.
3. **Log Matching:** As defined earlier.
4. **Leader Completeness:** If an entry is committed in term $T$, it appears in every leader's log for all terms $> T$.
5. **State Machine Safety:** If a server has applied a log entry at a given index to its state machine, no other server will ever apply a *different* entry at the same index.

**Election Restriction Rule (2 marks):**

A server votes for a candidate **only if** the candidate's log is at least as up-to-date as the voter's. "Up-to-date" is defined by the tuple `(lastTerm, logLength)`, compared lexicographically:
- A higher `lastTerm` wins.
- If `lastTerm` is equal, a longer log wins.

**Proof Sketch of Leader Completeness (2 marks):**

Suppose entry $e_c$ is committed in term $T_c$. Then it exists on a majority $M_c$. When a new leader $L_{new}$ is elected in term $T_{new} > T_c$, $L_{new}$ must have received votes from a majority $V_{new}$. By quorum intersection, $M_c \cap V_{new} \ne \emptyset$. Pick any node $x$ in the intersection. Since $x$ voted for $L_{new}$, $L_{new}$'s log is at least as up-to-date as $x$'s, which contains $e_c$. Therefore $L_{new}$ also contains $e_c$. ∎

---

**Q. (b)** A **7-node Raft cluster** suffers a network partition isolating **3 nodes** (including the current leader) from the remaining **4 nodes**. Describe step-by-step what happens. Will the system make progress? Justify with the majority quorum formula. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Initial state:** $N = 7$, Leader $L$ in the minority partition of size 3. Majority requirement: $Q = \lfloor 7/2 \rfloor + 1 = 4$.

**Step-by-step behaviour (5 marks):**

1. **Majority partition (4 nodes)** — minority still sees an old leader.
   - The 4 reachable followers stop receiving heartbeats from $L$ because $L$ is isolated.
   - Election timeouts in the 4-node partition expire (in 150–300 ms, randomized).
   - One of them becomes a **Candidate**, increments term to $T+1$, and issues `RequestVote`.
   - Of the 4 nodes, the candidate needs $Q = 4$ votes (including self). It can collect votes from the other 3 reachable followers, achieving a majority of 4 out of 7.
   - A **new leader $L'$** is elected at term $T+1$.

2. **Minority partition (3 nodes including old $L$)**:
   - The old leader $L$ continues to think it is the leader and may attempt to replicate entries, but its `AppendEntries` RPCs reach nobody.
   - $L$ cannot achieve a majority of 4 → cannot commit new entries.
   - When the partition heals, the old $L$ will receive an `AppendEntries` from $L'$ with a higher term $T+1$ and **immediately step down** to Follower.

**Will the system make progress? (2 marks):**

**Yes, the 4-node side makes progress.** The 3-node side does not, but Raft is a CP system — it prioritizes **Consistency over Availability**. No two leaders can coexist in the same term, and no entry can be committed without a majority, so **no data inconsistency** can occur.

$$
\text{Progress quorum} = Q = 4 \le 4 \quad \text{(achievable on majority side)} \quad \blacksquare
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfall:** Many students write "the system halts" because they forget that the *reachable* majority is what matters — not the *original* cluster size. Always count reachable nodes first, then compute $Q$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (10 must-know points for the 3-hour exam):**

- ☐ Raft = **decomposed consensus**: Leader Election + Log Replication + Safety.
- ☐ Three server states: **Follower, Candidate, Leader** — know every legal transition.
- ☐ **Term** is a monotonic logical clock; old leaders step down on higher term.
- ☐ **Quorum** $Q = \lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$ — required for both commit and election win.
- ☐ Heartbeat interval $\approx$ **1/10 of election timeout** ($t_e \in [150, 300]$ ms).
- ☐ **Randomized timeouts** guarantee liveness by preventing split-vote livelock.
- ☐ **Election Restriction** = `(lastTerm, logLength)` lexicographic comparison — keys Leader Completeness.
- ☐ **Log Matching Property** is preserved via `prevLogIndex` + `prevLogTerm` checks in `AppendEntries`.
- ☐ Raft is **CP (Consistency + Partition tolerance)**; under minority partition, the minority side becomes unavailable but never inconsistent.
- ☐ Raft handles **crash faults only**, not Byzantine faults — for malicious nodes, use PBFT / HotStuff.

> **Final Examiner's Mantra:** *If you forget everything else, remember: **One leader per term, majority wins, randomized timeouts save the day.***
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Concepts of Coordination Services: ZooKeeper and Consul
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT752 - FAULT TOLERANCE IN NETWORKED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic Concepts of Coordination Services: ZooKeeper and Consul

# Basic Concepts of Coordination Services: ZooKeeper and Consul

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Coordination Service?

A **Coordination Service** is a distributed system primitive that provides reliable, consistent, and fault-tolerant shared services (such as configuration management, leader election, distributed locks, membership, and synchronization) to a cluster of loosely-coupled application processes. It abstracts the complexity of distributed consensus into a simple, high-level API so that application developers need not re-implement the Paxos/Raft family of protocols from scratch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECPT752 Module 3):** A coordination service exposes a small, well-defined set of primitives (znodes, watches, sessions in ZooKeeper; KV store, service catalog, health checks in Consul) on top of a fault-tolerant replicated state machine.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of a **Coordination Service** as the **"Air Traffic Control Tower"** of a large airport.

- Multiple airplanes (distributed application processes) are constantly taking off, landing, and taxiing.
- Without the tower, planes would collide or arrive in a chaotic mess.
- The tower (ZooKeeper / Consul) does not *fly* the planes; it only **shares a consistent view of state** (who has permission to land, runway assignments, weather updates).
- If the tower's primary radar fails, a backup tower takes over seamlessly — analogous to **leader election and failover**.

Two major implementations studied under the KTU 2024 Scheme are:

1. **Apache ZooKeeper** — Originally developed at Yahoo (now an Apache top-level project), written in **Java**, widely used in the Hadoop ecosystem (HBase, Kafka, Yarn, Solr).
2. **HashiCorp Consul** — Developed by HashiCorp, written in **Go**, focused on service discovery and multi-datacenter service mesh capabilities.

> [!NOTE]
> Both systems are **CP systems** under the **CAP theorem** (Consistency + Partition tolerance) when configured with quorum-based consensus. They prioritize strong consistency over availability during network partitions.

### 1.3 Why Are Coordination Services Needed?

| Distributed Problem                | Why It Is Hard                                  | How Coordinator Solves It                  |
|------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------|
| Leader Election                    | Multiple nodes may declare themselves leader     | Sequential ephemeral znodes / Raft ballots |
| Distributed Locking                | Network delays cause race conditions             | Quorum-based lock acquisition              |
| Configuration Management           | Configs must be consistent across many nodes      | Centralized, versioned KV store            |
| Group Membership                   | Detecting node failures (or false positives)     | Heartbeats + session timeouts              |
| Barrier Synchronization            | Waiting for N tasks to complete                  | Atomic counter operations                  |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Apache ZooKeeper — Deep Dive

#### 2.1.1 Architecture

ZooKeeper runs as an **ensemble** of servers (typically **3, 5, or 7** nodes). The ensemble uses a **Leader / Follower** topology, but clients connect to *any* server (a node) in the ensemble. The leader is elected via the **ZAB (ZooKeeper Atomic Broadcast)** protocol.

```
Clients ──TCP──> ZK Ensemble (Leader + Followers) ──ZAB protocol──> Replicated Znode Tree
```

#### 2.1.2 The Znode Data Model

ZooKeeper exposes its state through a **hierarchical namespace** similar to a Unix filesystem. Each node in the tree is called a **znode** and is identified by a path (e.g., `/services/orders/leader`).

**Znode Types (High-Yield for KTU):**

| Type                | Lifetime                                  | Typical Use                                    |
|---------------------|--------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------|
| **Persistent**      | Survives client disconnection              | Storing permanent configuration                 |
| **Ephemeral**       | Deleted automatically when session ends    | Group membership, leader election indicators    |
| **Persistent Sequential** | Persistent + monotonically increasing seq# | Distributed queues, barrier synchronization      |
| **Ephemeral Sequential** | Ephemeral + monotonically increasing seq# | Leader election (e.g., Kafka brokers)           |

Each znode stores up to **1 MB** of data and maintains a **stat structure** containing `version`, `cversion`, `aclversion`, `ctime`, `mtime`, and `dataLength`.

#### 2.1.3 Watches and Sessions

- A **session** is established when a client connects; sessions have a configurable **timeout** (e.g., 2 × tickTime to 20 × tickTime).
- A **watch** is a one-time trigger fired by the server when a znode's data or children change. Clients must re-register watches after a fire.
- Watches are **ordered** with respect to other events from the same session.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A common KTU pitfall: **Watches are one-shot, not persistent.** Students frequently lose marks for assuming a watch continues to fire on every change.

#### 2.1.4 The ZAB Protocol

**ZAB = ZooKeeper Atomic Broadcast.** It guarantees:
- **Total order** of all transactions
- **Causal order** preserved
- **Crash recovery** semantics

ZAB has two modes:

1. **Recovery (Leader Election) Phase** — When the ensemble starts or leader fails, a new leader is elected (only a server with the most recent transaction log can become leader — this is the "truncation" property).
2. **Broadcast (Active Messaging) Phase** — Leader proposes transactions, follows send ACKs, leader commits once a **quorum** acknowledges.

#### 2.1.5 ZooKeeper Guarantees (the famous four)

1. **Sequential Consistency** — Updates from a client are applied in the order sent.
2. **Atomicity** — Either all servers apply an update or none.
3. **Single System Image** — A client sees the same view regardless of which server it connects to.
4. **Reliability (Timeliness)** — Once an update is applied, it persists until the client overwrites it.

### 2.2 HashiCorp Consul — Deep Dive

#### 2.2.1 Architecture

A Consul deployment uses two main agent types:

- **Server agents** — Participate in the Raft consensus quorum; store the cluster state.
- **Client agents** — Lightweight processes that run on every node, forward RPCs to servers, and run the gossip protocol locally.

A typical production deployment has **3 or 5 servers per datacenter** and one client agent per service host.

```
                ┌──────────────────────────┐
                │      Consul Servers      │  ← Raft quorum
                │  (KV, Catalog, ACLs)     │
                └──────────┬───────────────┘
                           │  RPC (8300)
   ┌────────────┐   ┌──────┴──────┐   ┌────────────┐
   │Consul Client│   │Consul Client│   │Consul Client│  ← Serf gossip (8301)
   │ on Node A   │   │ on Node B   │   │ on Node C   │
   └────────────┘   └────────────┘   └────────────┘
```

#### 2.2.2 Core Feature Set

| Feature                    | Description                                                                 |
|----------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Service Discovery**      | Services register via HTTP API or DNS interface (port 8600).                |
| **Health Checking**        | Multiple check types: script, HTTP, TCP, TTL, Docker, etc.                  |
| **KV Store**               | Strongly consistent, versioned, transactional key-value store.              |
| **Multi-Datacenter**       | WAN gossip (Serf) across datacenters via port 8302.                         |
| **Secure Service Mesh**    | Built-in mTLS between services using intentions and Connect.                |
| **Sessions & Locking**     | Built on the KV store; supports both Lock and Session semantics.            |

#### 2.2.3 The Raft Consensus Algorithm (Server Side)

Consul servers use **Raft** (a more understandable alternative to Paxos) to maintain a strongly consistent replicated log of state changes.

**Raft States (one per server, one at a time):**
- **Follower** — Passive, responds to RPCs from leader/candidates.
- **Candidate** — Used during elections.
- **Leader** — Handles all client interactions and log replication.

**Election timing constraint:**
$$ T_{election} \gt T_{broadcast} $$

That is, election timeouts must be larger than typical broadcast RPC round-trips to prevent split-vote instability.

#### 2.2.4 The Gossip Protocol (LAN/WAN)

Consul uses **Serf**, a SWIM-based gossip protocol, for:
- **Failure detection** — Probabilistic ping/ack/PingReq cycles.
- **Membership & message dissemination** — Scales to thousands of nodes.
- **WAN federation** — Linking datacenters.

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU exam point: **Consul uses TWO different protocols — Raft for the control plane (servers) and Serf gossip for the data plane (clients, WAN).** Many students confuse which protocol is used where.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept                          | Formula / Rule                                       | Engineering Utility                                    |
|----------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------|
| **ZooKeeper Quorum Size**        | $Q = \lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 1$                        | Tolerates $f = \lfloor (n-1)/2 \rfloor$ failures       |
| **Recommended ensemble size**    | $n = 2f + 1$ for odd numbers (3, 5, 7)              | Best cost vs fault-tolerance trade-off                 |
| **Consul Server Quorum**         | $Q_{raft} = \lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$                 | Same as Raft general rule                              |
| **Election Timeout Constraint**  | $T_{election} \gt T_{broadcast}$ (typically 50–500 ms vs 10–50 ms) | Prevents unnecessary leader churn                      |
| **Gossip Fan-out**               | $k = \log_2 N$ peers probed per round                | Provides failure detection in $O(\log N)$ time         |
| **Session Timeout Range (ZK)**   | $2 \cdot t \le T_{session} \le 20 \cdot t$ where $t = \text{tickTime}$ | Bounds heart-beat detection latency                    |
| **Znode Max Data Size**          | $1 \; \text{MB}$                                      | Hard limit imposed by ZooKeeper                        |
| **Watch Semantics (ZK)**         | One-shot, ordered, $\textbf{latched}$                | Forces client re-registration                          |

> [!TIP]
> Examiners often test the **quorum formula** — memorize $Q = \lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 1$ and be ready to apply it to both ZooKeeper and Consul scenarios.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: ZooKeeper vs Consul

| Dimension                       | Apache ZooKeeper                              | HashiCorp Consul                                      |
|----------------------------------|------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------|
| **Primary Focus**                | General-purpose coordination primitive        | Service discovery + service mesh                       |
| **Language**                     | Java                                          | Go                                                     |
| **Consensus Protocol**           | ZAB (ZooKeeper Atomic Broadcast)              | Raft                                                   |
| **Membership/Discovery Protocol** | Custom (sessions + heartbeats)                 | Gossip (Serf / SWIM)                                   |
| **Data Model**                   | Hierarchical znode tree                        | Flat KV store + Service Catalog                       |
| **Watch/Notification Model**     | One-shot watches                              | Long-running blocking queries                          |
| **Service Discovery**            | Not native (must build on top of znodes)      | First-class via DNS (port 8600) and HTTP API (8500)   |
| **Health Checking**              | Not built-in (ephemeral znode = implicit)      | First-class, multiple check types                      |
| **Multi-Datacenter**             | Solved at application level (e.g., Exhibitor) | Native (WAN gossip on port 8302)                       |
| **Use Cases**                    | Kafka, HBase, Hadoop, Solr                    | Microservices, dynamic routing, Vault, Nomad          |
| **Strong Consistency?**          | Yes (linearizable writes)                      | Yes (Raft on KV and Catalog)                           |
| **API Style**                    | Java/C client, ZK recipes                     | RESTful HTTP, DNS, gRPC                                |

### 3.2 Derivation of Quorum Tolerance

We derive the maximum number of failures $f$ a cluster of $n$ servers can tolerate while still being able to form a majority.

**Step 1:** State the definition of a quorum.

A quorum is a set of servers whose size is strictly greater than half of the total:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &\gt \dfrac{n}{2} \\
Q &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{n}{2} \right\rfloor + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

The first equation states the *strict majority* requirement. The second line gives the **smallest integer** satisfying that inequality.

**Step 2:** To survive $f$ simultaneous failures, the remaining $n - f$ servers must still satisfy the quorum requirement.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n - f &\ge Q \\
n - f &\ge \left\lfloor \dfrac{n}{2} \right\rfloor + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $Q$ and solve for $f$ in the worst case (i.e., when $n$ is odd, which is the standard recommendation).

$$
\begin{aligned}
n - f &\ge \dfrac{n+1}{2} \\
2n - 2f &\ge n + 1 \\
2f &\le n - 1 \\
f &\le \dfrac{n-1}{2} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{n-1}{2} \right\rfloor
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the formula to common KTU numericals.

| Ensemble Size $n$ | Quorum $Q$ | Tolerable Failures $f$ | Comment                                  |
|--------------------|------------|------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| 3                  | 2          | 1                      | Minimum production deployment            |
| 5                  | 3          | 2                      | Recommended for production               |
| 7                  | 4          | 3                      | Large-scale or geo-distributed           |
| 4                  | 3          | 1                      | **Disallowed** — even sizes waste a node |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why is even-numbered ensemble size not recommended in ZooKeeper?"* The answer lies in the derivation above — an even $n$ does not increase $f$, but it increases the quorum size and therefore the write latency.

### 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough — Leader Election via Ephemeral Sequential Znode

This is the **classic Kafka/HBase leader election recipe**, frequently asked in KTU Part B questions.

**Step 1:** A group of $N$ candidates wants to elect one leader.

Each candidate $C_i$ creates an **ephemeral sequential znode** under a well-known parent path, e.g., `/election/candidate-`.

The znode name becomes `candidate-<seq>`, where `<seq>` is a monotonically increasing integer assigned by ZooKeeper.

**Step 2:** The candidate reads the entire sibling set and sorts by sequence number.

**Step 3:** The candidate with the **smallest sequence number** is the leader.

All other candidates watch the znode whose sequence is **immediately smaller** than their own (a *predecessor watch*).

**Step 4:** If the leader's session expires, its ephemeral znode is auto-deleted, the predecessor's watch fires, and the next-in-line candidate becomes leader with no manual intervention.

**Mathematical guarantee:**

If $S = \{s_1 \lt s_2 \lt \dots \lt s_N\}$ are the sequences, the leader is uniquely determined as the candidate holding $s_1$, because:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Leader}(t) &= \arg\min_i s_i(t) \quad \text{among live candidates} \\
\text{Failure}(s_1) \Rightarrow \text{Leader}(t+\Delta) &= \arg\min_i s_i(t+\Delta) = s_2
\end{aligned}
$$

The handover time $\Delta$ is bounded by:

$$
\Delta = T_{watch-fire} + T_{create-znode} \le 2 \cdot T_{session-timeout}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — ZooKeeper Ensemble Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    c1[Client A] -->|TCP 2181| n1[ZK Server 1]
    c2[Client B] -->|TCP 2181| n2[ZK Server 2]
    c3[Client C] -->|TCP 2181| n3[ZK Server 3]
    n1 <-->|ZAB Proposal Ack| n2
    n2 <-->|ZAB Proposal Ack| n3
    n3 <-->|ZAB Proposal Ack| n1
    n1 -.Follower.-> leaderServer((Leader n2))
    n2 -.Leader.-> leaderServer
    n3 -.Follower.-> leaderServer
    zkTree[(Znode Tree /services/orders/leader)]:::storage
    n1 --- zkTree
    n2 --- zkTree
    n3 --- zkTree
    classDef storage fill:#fff5d4,stroke:#aa8800,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Znode Tree for Service Coordination

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root[(/)]
    root --> services[/services/]
    root --> config[/config/]
    root --> locks[/locks/]
    services --> orders[/orders/]
    orders --> oP1[order-locks]
    orders --> oE1[worker-0000000001]
    orders --> oE2[worker-0000000002]
    orders --> oE3[worker-0000000003]
    config --> c1[db.connection.string]
    config --> c2[feature.flags]
    locks --> l1[resource-A]
    locks --> l2[resource-B]
    oE1:::ephemeral
    oE2:::ephemeral
    oE3:::ephemeral
    classDef ephemeral fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#aa3333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Legend:** Red nodes represent *ephemeral sequential* znodes (typical for leader election).

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Consul Multi-Datacenter Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DC1[Datacenter 1]
        s1A[Consul Server A] --- s1B[Consul Server B] --- s1C[Consul Server C]
        s1A --- s1B
        s1B --- s1C
        s1C --- s1A
        cli1A[Client Agent on App Node 1] -.gossip 8301.-> s1A
        cli1B[Client Agent on App Node 2] -.gossip 8301.-> s1B
        cli1C[Client Agent on App Node 3] -.gossip 8301.-> s1C
        cli1A -.LAN gossip.-> cli1B
        cli1B -.LAN gossip.-> cli1C
    end
    subgraph DC2[Datacenter 2]
        s2A[Consul Server D] --- s2B[Consul Server E] --- s2C[Consul Server F]
        cli2A[Client Agent on App Node 4] -.gossip 8301.-> s2A
        cli2B[Client Agent on App Node 5] -.gossip 8301.-> s2B
        cli2A -.LAN gossip.-> cli2B
    end
    s1A <-.WAN gossip 8302.-> s2A
    s1B <-.WAN gossip 8302.-> s2B
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Coordination Service Request Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant App as Application Client
    participant Coord as Coordinator Server
    participant Quorum as Quorum Replicas
    App->>Coord: write(znode/key, value)
    Coord->>Coord: assign zxid / log index
    Coord->>Quorum: AppendEntries / ZAB Proposal
    Quorum-->>Coord: Majority ACK
    Coord->>Quorum: COMMIT
    Coord-->>App: ACK + new version
    Note over App,Coord: Watch fires on read for stale clients
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any **three** types of znodes in Apache ZooKeeper and give one example use-case for each.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Persistent znode** — Remains in the tree until explicitly deleted. *Use-case:* storing a database connection string under `/config/db.url` that all services must read.
2. **Ephemeral znode** — Auto-deleted when the creating client's session ends. *Use-case:* indicating that a worker process is alive; its disappearance triggers re-assignment.
3. **Ephemeral Sequential znode** — Ephemeral + auto-incremented sequence number. *Use-case:* leader election recipes (smallest sequence becomes leader).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse *sequential* with *persistent*. Marks are deducted for writing "sequential persistent znode" as a separate type — sequential is a *modifier*, not a base type.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Why is it recommended to use an **odd** number of servers in a ZooKeeper ensemble? Mention the formula for the quorum size.*

**Model Answer:**

A ZooKeeper ensemble of $n$ servers requires a strict majority to commit a write, given by:

$$
Q = \left\lfloor \dfrac{n}{2} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

If $n$ is even, increasing the cluster size by one node does *not* increase the tolerable failures $f = \lfloor (n-1)/2 \rfloor$, but it *does* increase the quorum size and therefore the write latency. Hence odd sizes ($n = 3, 5, 7$) are optimal for cost-vs-resilience.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Marks are lost for failing to explicitly write the **quorum formula** $Q = \lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 1$. Always quote the formula in numeric-answer questions.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of Apache ZooKeeper**, the role of the leader, and the guarantees provided by the ZAB protocol.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Describe the **leader election recipe using ephemeral sequential znodes**. Show how the algorithm tolerates the failure of the current leader.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

- ZooKeeper runs as an **ensemble** of $n$ (odd, 3/5/7) servers, all replicating the in-memory znode tree and on-disk transaction log.
- One server is elected **Leader**; the rest are **Followers**. Clients connect to *any* server.
- The **ZAB protocol** has two phases:
  1. **Recovery / Leader Election** — A candidate with the most recent log is elected (truncation property).
  2. **Broadcast** — Leader assigns a globally unique `zxid` (ZooKeeper Transaction ID, 64 bits: epoch + counter), proposes the transaction, and commits it once a **quorum** ($\lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 1$) of followers acknowledge.
- **Guarantees provided:**
  1. Sequential consistency of writes.
  2. Atomicity (all-or-nothing).
  3. Single System Image.
  4. Reliability / Timeliness of updates.

> *(Include the Mermaid diagram 4.1 from SECTION_4 for full marks.)*

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

- **Step 1:** Each candidate $C_i$ creates an ephemeral sequential znode under `/election/candidate-`.
- **Step 2:** Each candidate reads all siblings, sorts them by sequence, and identifies the one with the **smallest sequence** as the leader.
- **Step 3:** All non-leader candidates set a **watch on their predecessor's znode**.
- **Step 4:** When the leader's session expires (e.g., crash), ZooKeeper deletes its ephemeral znode. The predecessor's watch fires, and that node becomes the new leader.
- **Failure Tolerance:** If the leader crashes, the next-smallest candidate automatically becomes leader in a time bounded by $2 \cdot T_{session-timeout}$, with no human intervention. Multiple simultaneous failures are tolerated as long as at least one candidate remains alive.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often **forget to mention the predecessor-watch** and simply state "the smallest znode is leader" without describing what happens on leader failure. This costs up to 3 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **architecture of HashiCorp Consul**, distinguishing between server agents and client agents. What roles do the **Raft consensus protocol** and the **Serf gossip protocol** play?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **Apache ZooKeeper and HashiCorp Consul** along ten functional dimensions. Under what circumstances would you prefer one over the other in a fault-tolerant deployment?

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

- **Server agents** form a Raft quorum (typically 3 or 5 per datacenter). They store the **KV store**, **Service Catalog**, **ACLs**, and **intentions**. A leader is elected via Raft; writes are linearizable.
- **Client agents** run on every application host. They register services, run health checks, and forward RPCs to servers. They participate in **LAN gossip (Serf)** on port 8301, which provides membership and failure detection.
- **Raft** is used for the **control plane** (consistency of state on servers) — leader election, log replication, KV store transactions.
- **Serf gossip** is used for the **data plane** (membership, WAN federation on port 8302) — failure detection, multi-DC message propagation, and eventual-consistency of service health.

> *(Include the Mermaid diagram 4.3 from SECTION_4 for full marks.)*

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

A 10-row comparison table covering: language, consensus protocol, gossip usage, data model (znode tree vs flat KV), service discovery capability, health checking, multi-DC support, watch model, primary ecosystem, and typical use cases. Refer to the **Analytical Matrix in Section 3.1** of these notes for the complete grid.

**Selection Heuristic:**

- Choose **ZooKeeper** when the workload is **coordination-heavy** (Hadoop, Kafka, HBase) and the data model is naturally hierarchical.
- Choose **Consul** when the workload is **service-mesh / microservice-discovery-heavy**, multi-datacenter, and you need first-class health checks, mTLS, and DNS-based discovery.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Marks are deducted for stating *"Consul is better than ZooKeeper"* or vice-versa without context. Comparison questions in KTU **demand situational reasoning** — always justify your preference.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **coordination service** is a fault-tolerant replicated state machine that provides primitives like leader election, locking, configuration, and membership to distributed applications.
- **ZooKeeper** uses the **ZAB (ZooKeeper Atomic Broadcast)** protocol over a **hierarchical znode tree**; **Consul** uses **Raft** for state replication and **Serf gossip** for membership/discovery.
- The four znode types are **Persistent, Ephemeral, Persistent Sequential, Ephemeral Sequential** — sequential is a *modifier* of the base type, not a separate type.
- **Watches in ZooKeeper are one-shot** — clients must re-register them after firing. **Consul uses long-running blocking queries** instead.
- **Quorum formula:** $Q = \lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 1$ ; maximum tolerable failures: $f = \lfloor (n-1)/2 \rfloor$.
- **Odd ensemble sizes (3, 5, 7) are recommended** because even sizes waste a node (same $f$, larger $Q$).
- **Leader election via ephemeral sequential znode** = smallest sequence becomes leader, predecessor-watch triggers automatic failover.
- **Consul ports to remember:** 8300 (server RPC), 8301 (LAN gossip), 8302 (WAN gossip), 8500 (HTTP API), 8600 (DNS).
- Both systems are **CP systems** under CAP, prioritizing consistency during network partitions.
- Always state the **quorum formula explicitly** in numericals; always draw the **architecture diagram** for full marks in 7-mark sub-questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Balancing and Failover: Principles of Load Balancing in Fault-Tolerant Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT752 - FAULT TOLERANCE IN NETWORKED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Load Balancing and Failover: Principles of Load Balancing in Fault-Tolerant Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Load Balancing (LB)** is a fault-tolerance and performance-engineering mechanism that distributes workloads uniformly across multiple computing resources (servers, links, processors, or storage nodes) connected as a pool, cluster, or farm, such that no single resource becomes a single point of failure (SPOF) and the aggregate system capacity is fully utilized. Within the context of **Fault-Tolerant Networked Systems**, load balancing is a *proactive* fault-avoidance strategy — it not only optimizes throughput and response time, but also enables the system to *gracefully absorb* the loss of one or more nodes by redistributing their traffic to surviving nodes.

**Failover** is the *reactive* counterpart: it is the automatic, seamless transfer of workload from a *failed primary* node to a *redundant standby* node, with the goal of preserving service availability and continuity. Together, Load Balancing and Failover constitute the two pillars of **continuous service delivery** in distributed systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECPT752 — Module 3):**
> Load balancing and failover are treated as **complementary** techniques. Load balancing = *steady-state distribution*; Failover = *exceptional-state transfer*. Examiners frequently ask students to *contrast* the two.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a **busy toll plaza with multiple booths**:

- **Load Balancing** is like the *automatic traffic signal* at the plaza entrance that opens the green arrow for the booth with the **shortest queue** at that moment. As one queue grows longer, cars are dynamically redirected to a less-congested booth. The plaza keeps moving smoothly even though cars arrive in random bursts.

- **Failover** is like what happens when **one toll booth suddenly shuts down** (operator sick, machine broken). The cars already lined up there are *not* sent home — the system instantly re-routes them to the *next available booth*, and the closed booth's workload is *absorbed* by the others. The plaza never stops; the only visible effect is a slightly longer wait at the surviving booths.

In this analogy:
- The **cars** = client requests / packets / jobs
- The **toll booths** = server pool members (back-end nodes)
- The **traffic signal controller** = the **Load Balancer** (a software/hardware entity like HAProxy, NGINX, F5 BIG-IP, AWS ELB)
- The **closed booth** = a **failed node**
- **Re-routing upon failure** = the **Failover mechanism**

## 1.3 The Five Guiding Principles of Load Balancing in Fault-Tolerant Systems

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus lists **five canonical principles** that every load-balancing scheme must satisfy to qualify as *fault-tolerant*:

1. **Transparency** — End-users/clients must be unaware of which back-end node actually serviced their request. To the client, the service appears as a single, logical entity.
2. **Scalability** — The scheme must allow new nodes to be added (or removed) without re-architecting the system. Capacity should grow *linearly* (or near-linearly) with the number of nodes.
3. **Fairness / Uniformity** — Workload (CPU%, request count, bandwidth) should be distributed as evenly as practical across all healthy nodes.
4. **Fault Isolation & Redistribution** — When a node fails, its share must be detected (via *health-checks*) and re-dispatched to surviving nodes within an acceptable time bound (typically seconds).
5. **Locality Awareness (optional but common)** — The dispatcher may prefer a node that is *topologically close* to the client to minimize latency.

## 1.4 Core Metrics & Standard Parameters

| Metric | Standard Symbol | Typical Unit | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Availability** | $A$ | % (uptime) | $A = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}$ |
| **Mean Time To Failure** | **MTTF** | hours | Reliability of a node |
| **Mean Time To Repair** | **MTTR** | hours | Failover speed |
| **Throughput** | $\lambda$ | requests/sec | Service capacity |
| **Response Time** | $T_{resp}$ | ms | QoS metric |
| **Utilization** | $\rho$ | fraction (0–1) | $\rho = \lambda / \mu$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always quote **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures) = MTTF + MTTR for *repairable* systems. The KTU board examiner often tests whether students confuse **MTTF** with **MTBF**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Load-Balancing Strategies

Load-balancing algorithms are broadly classified along two axes:

**(a) Decision Time** — *Static* vs *Dynamic*
**(b) Decision Scope** — *Local* (within one cluster) vs *Global* (across data-centers / WAN)

### 2.1.1 Static Load Balancing

Decisions are made **at design time** using a pre-determined rule. The dispatcher does *not* inspect the live state of back-end nodes.

| Algorithm | Rule | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Round Robin (RR)** | $i \leftarrow (i + 1) \bmod N$ | Homogeneous nodes, equal-cost requests |
| **Weighted Round Robin (WRR)** | Cycles weighted by node capacity $w_i$ | Heterogeneous nodes (different CPU/RAM) |
| **Hash-Based (IP/URL Hash)** | $i \leftarrow H(\text{key}) \bmod N$ | Session affinity (sticky sessions) |
| **Random** | Uniform random pick | Stateless services, very high request rate |
| **Deterministic / Optimal** | Solves an ILP offline for known workload | Batch jobs, predictable traffic |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why static?** Simpler, stateless, no probing overhead. **Limitation:** Cannot react to node failure or load spikes — a node receiving traffic while *actually down* causes request loss. Hence, static LB alone is *insufficient* for fault-tolerant systems; it must be paired with **health-checks**.

### 2.1.2 Dynamic Load Balancing

Decisions are made **at runtime** based on real-time feedback.

| Algorithm | Decision Criterion |
|---|---|
| **Least-Connection (LC)** | Send to node with fewest active TCP connections |
| **Weighted Least-Connection (WLC)** | LC scaled by node weight $w_i$ |
| **Least-Response-Time (LRT)** | Send to node with lowest moving-average latency |
| **Resource-Based (Adaptive)** | Send to node with most spare CPU/RAM/bandwidth |
| **Feedback-Based / Predictive** | Uses ARIMA, ML, or queue-length forecasts |
| **Centralized vs Distributed** | Single global scheduler vs peer-to-peer gossip |

### 2.1.3 Local vs Global Load Balancing

- **Local LB** operates *inside* a data-center, dispatching across servers in the same rack/cluster (e.g., LVS, HAProxy).
- **Global Server Load Balancing (GSLB)** operates at the *DNS* or *Anycast* level, steering clients to the *geographically closest* data-center (e.g., AWS Route 53, Akamai).

## 2.2 Layer-4 vs Layer-7 Load Balancing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: **"Differentiate L4 and L7 load balancing. Which is more 'intelligent'?"**

| Aspect | **L4 (Transport)** | **L7 (Application)** |
|---|---|---|
| OSI Layer | TCP/UDP | HTTP/HTTPS/gRPC |
| Decision input | IP, port | URL path, headers, cookies, payload |
| Speed | Very fast (hardware offload possible) | Slower (parses packet) |
| SSL termination | Usually pass-through | Can terminate & re-encrypt |
| Content-based routing | ❌ | ✅ (`/api → API server`, `/img → CDN`) |
| Example | F5 LTM, AWS NLB | NGINX, AWS ALB, Envoy |

## 2.3 Failover Mechanisms

A failover scheme is defined by the **standby mode** of the redundant node:

### 2.3.1 Cold Standby (Active-Passive, Off)
- Backup is **powered off** until primary fails.
- Failover time = boot time + service-start time (minutes). 
- **Cheapest**, but unacceptable for high-availability systems.

### 2.3.2 Warm Standby
- Backup is **booted** and **idle**, running a lightweight heartbeat with the primary.
- Failover time = seconds.
- Used in moderate-availability systems.

### 2.3.3 Hot Standby (Active-Passive, On)
- Backup is **fully running**, receiving **mirrored state updates** from primary.
- Failover time ≈ sub-second. 
- **Standard for telecom & banking** (e.g., VRRP, HSRP, Pacemaker/Corosync).

### 2.3.4 Active-Active
- **All nodes** are simultaneously serving traffic.
- Each node is the *primary* for some subset of the workload.
- Maximizes utilization; requires shared state (e.g., distributed session store, DB replication).
- Examples: DNS round-robin, anycast ECMP, clustered databases.

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Board Pattern:** Availability is the *single most tested* formula in Module 3. Memorize all variants below.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Basic Availability** | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | $0 \le A \le 1$ |
| 2 | **Unavailability** | $U = 1 - A$ | Often called *downtime fraction* |
| 3 | **Annual Downtime (hours)** | $D = (1 - A) \times 8760$ | 8760 = hours/year |
| 4 | **Series (no redundancy)** | $A_{series} = \displaystyle\prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | All must work |
| 5 | **Parallel (1-out-of-N)** | $A_{parallel} = 1 - \displaystyle\prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - A_i)$ | Any one works |
| 6 | **N+1 Redundancy** | $A_{N+1} = A_N \cdot \left[1 - (1-A_{1})^{1}\right]$ | $N$ primary + 1 spare |
| 7 | **MTBF (repairable)** | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | For repairable systems |
| 8 | **M/M/1 Queue Response Time** | $T_{resp} = \dfrac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$ | $\rho = \lambda / \mu < 1$ |
| 9 | **M/M/1 Queue Wait Time** | $T_{wait} = \dfrac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda}$ | Little's Law derivation |
| 10 | **Aggregate Throughput (N nodes)** | $\Lambda = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} \lambda_i$ | For uniform load $\Lambda = N\lambda$ |
| 11 | **Capacity Gain Factor** | $G = \dfrac{\Lambda_{balanced}}{\Lambda_{unbalanced}}$ | Ideal = $N$ |
| 12 | **Imbalance Factor** | $\sigma_{im} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum (L_i - \bar{L})^2}}{\bar{L}}$ | Lower is better |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Datacenters & Cloud** — AWS ELB, Azure Load Balancer, GCP Cloud Load Balancing all implement these principles.
- **CDN & Edge** — Global Server LB directs users to nearest edge node (Akamai, Cloudflare).
- **Telecom (5G core)** — Network Function Virtualization (NFV) uses load balancers to distribute UPF/AMF traffic.
- **High-Performance Computing** — MPI's `MPI_Comm_split` and schedulers (SLURM) perform job-level load balancing.
- **Databases** — ProxySQL, HAProxy in front of MySQL/PostgreSQL clusters.
- **Microservices** — Kubernetes `Service` (kube-proxy) and **Ingress** controllers are the de-facto L7 load balancers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Availability of an N+1 Redundant Cluster

> **Problem:** A service is hosted on $N$ active servers, each with availability $A_1$, backed by $1$ hot-standby spare with the same $A_1$. During normal operation, only the $N$ active servers carry traffic. If any one of the $N$ fails, the spare takes over. Assuming failure independence and instantaneous detection, derive $A_{N+1}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define events.**
Let $E_1$ = "at least one of the $N$ active servers is up" and $E_2$ = "the spare is up". The cluster is available iff **both** events occur (in series, since the spare can only help if at least one active survives — for the *basic* case, even if all $N$ are down, the spare alone cannot carry the full $N$-fold load; we adopt the *parallel* interpretation: the system survives if either the $N$-active group is fully working *or* the spare carries on after one active dies).

**Step 2 — Probability that the active group is fully working.**
Assuming independence, all $N$ active servers must be up:
$$P(\text{all N active up}) = A_1^N$$

**Step 3 — Probability of graceful degradation (exactly one active fails, spare takes over).**
Exactly one of the $N$ active fails, the remaining $N-1$ are up, and the spare is up:
$$P(\text{1 fail, spare up}) = \binom{N}{1} (1 - A_1)\,A_1^{N-1}\,A_1 = N(1 - A_1)\,A_1^{N}$$

**Step 4 — Total cluster availability (sum of all surviving scenarios).**
For $N+1$ with the spare being a *hot* substitute (can cover loss of $\le 1$ active), the cluster survives if (a) all $N$ active are up, OR (b) exactly 1 active is up AND the spare is up. The $(N-1)$ failed-active case is not survivable since the spare can only replace *one* server. Hence:
$$A_{N+1} = A_1^N + N(1 - A_1)A_1^N$$

**Step 5 — Factorize.**
$$A_{N+1} = A_1^N \big[\,1 + N(1 - A_1)\,\big]$$

> **Limiting check:** If $A_1 = 1$, $A_{N+1} = 1$ ✓. If $N = 0$ (only spare), $A_{N+1} = A_1$ ✓.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Annual Downtime from Availability

> **Problem:** A system promises $99.99\%$ ("four nines") availability. How many minutes of downtime per year is allowed?

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Unavailability fraction.**
$$U = 1 - A = 1 - 0.9999 = 0.0001$$

**Step 2 — Convert to hours per year.**
$$D_{\text{hours}} = U \times 8760 = 0.0001 \times 8760 = 0.876 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to minutes per year.**
$$D_{\text{minutes}} = 0.876 \times 60 = 52.56 \text{ minutes/year}$$

> **KTU quick-look table (memorize):**

| "Nines" | Availability | Annual Downtime |
|---|---|---|
| Two 9s | 99 % | 87.6 hours |
| Three 9s | 99.9 % | 8.76 hours |
| **Four 9s** | **99.99 %** | **52.56 minutes** |
| Five 9s | 99.999 % | 5.26 minutes |
| Six 9s | 99.9999 % | 31.5 seconds |

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — M/M/1 Response Time for a Load-Balanced Queue
