> **Problem:** Derive the average response time seen by a client in an M/M/1 queue, then show how it scales when $N$ identical back-end nodes are placed behind a perfect load balancer.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — M/M/1 definitions.**
Arrival rate $\lambda$ (Poisson), service rate $\mu$ (exponential), utilization $\rho = \lambda / \mu < 1$.

**Step 2 — Average number in system (from steady-state birth-death).**
$$L = \frac{\rho}{1 - \rho} = \frac{\lambda}{\mu - \lambda}$$

**Step 3 — Average time in system (Little's Law, $L = \lambda T$).**
$$T_{resp} = \frac{L}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$$

> This is the canonical M/M/1 response time used across all performance-modelling KTU questions.

**Step 4 — With $N$ nodes behind a perfect LB.**
A perfect LB splits incoming rate $\lambda_{total}$ uniformly: each node sees $\lambda = \lambda_{total}/N$. Substituting:
$$T_{resp}^{(N)} = \frac{1}{\mu - \frac{\lambda_{total}}{N}}$$

**Step 5 — Aggregate throughput ceiling.**
The system saturates when $\lambda_{total}/N \to \mu$, i.e., $\lambda_{total}^{max} = N\mu$, an $N$-fold linear scaling.

> **Engineering takeaway:** A *stateless* service scales linearly under perfect LB. A *stateful* service (sessions, locks) cannot enjoy this scaling without external shared state — which is why KTU stresses that *state management* is the bottleneck of fault-tolerant LB.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Failover Time Budget & SLO

> **Problem:** A banking system requires availability $A \ge 99.99\%$. Each transaction-tier server has MTTF = 1000 hours. With hot-standby failover and series-connected DB and network, find the maximum allowable MTTR.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Express the constraint.**
$$A \ge 0.9999$$

**Step 2 — Solve for MTTR from the availability formula.**
$$A = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} \ge 0.9999$$

**Step 3 — Invert and isolate MTTR.**
$$\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} \le \frac{\text{MTTF}}{0.9999}$$

$$\text{MTTR} \le \text{MTTF} \left(\frac{1}{0.9999} - 1\right) = 1000 \times 0.0001 = 0.1 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to seconds.**
$$\text{MTTR}_{max} = 0.1 \times 3600 = 360 \text{ seconds} = 6 \text{ minutes}$$

> **Interpretation:** To meet four-nines, the entire failover (detect + switch + re-establish sessions) **must complete in under 6 minutes** for this server. This is why production-grade LBs use *health-check intervals of 1–5 seconds* — to leave headroom for the actual cut-over.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Scheme

| Goal / Constraint | Recommended Scheme | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum availability, any cost | Active-Active + Geo-redundant GSLB | Survives data-center loss |
| Minimum cost, moderate availability | Active-Passive with Cold Standby | Hardware reused only on failure |
| Sticky sessions for e-commerce | L7 LB with cookie-hash | Preserves session state |
| GPU inference at scale | Weighted LB based on VRAM | Equal load = unequal memory pressure |
| Lowest latency globally | Anycast + L4 GSLB | BGP-level routing |
| Streaming media (UDP) | L4 ECMP / Maglev | Avoids TCP handshake cost |
| Microservices (k8s) | kube-proxy iptables/IPVS + Ingress | Native, declarative |
| Stateful DB writes | LB with primary-replica awareness | Writes to primary, reads from replicas |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Load Balancer with Health-Check & Failover

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    clientA[Client A] --> edgeLB
    clientB[Client B] --> edgeLB
    clientC[Client C] --> edgeLB
    edgeLB{{"L4 or L7 Load Balancer"}} -->|"Health-check ping / TCP probe"| hc1
    edgeLB -->|"Dispatch request"| pool
    hc1["Health-Check Service"] -->|"Poll every 1-5s"| pool
    pool["Server Pool N=3"]
    pool --> node1["Node 1 ACTIVE"]
    pool --> node2["Node 2 ACTIVE"]
    pool --> node3["Node 3 STANDBY"]
    node2 -.->|"Failure detected"| hc1
    hc1 -.->|"Mark unhealthy, reroute"| edgeLB
    edge2["Failover Trigger"] -.->|"Promote to ACTIVE"| node3
```

## 4.2 Failover State Machine — Active-Passive Cluster

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NORMAL
    NORMAL --> NORMAL: Health-check OK on Primary
    NORMAL --> DEGRADED: Primary fails health-check
    NORMAL --> SPLIT_BRAIN: Heartbeat lost on both directions
    DEGRADED --> NORMAL: Primary recovers within retry window
    DEGRADED --> FAILOVER: Retry exhausted, promote standby
    FAILOVER --> NORMAL: Standby promoted, traffic cut over
    SPLIT_BRAIN --> FAILOVER: Tie-breaker arbitration (quorum / STONITH)
    SPLIT_BRAIN --> SAFE_SHUTDOWN: Cannot arbitrate
```

## 4.3 Active-Active vs Active-Passive Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AA["ACTIVE-ACTIVE CLUSTER"]
        direction LR
        a1["Node 1 - serves 33 percent of traffic"] -->|shares state via| store1["Distributed Session Store / DB"]
        a2["Node 2 - serves 33 percent of traffic"] -->|shares state via| store1
        a3["Node 3 - serves 33 percent of traffic"] -->|shares state via| store1
    end
    subgraph AP["ACTIVE-PASSIVE CLUSTER"]
        direction LR
        p1["Primary - serves 100 percent of traffic"] -->|mirrors writes to| p2["Hot Standby - idle, mirrors state"]
    end
    note1["Higher utilization, complex state-sharing, scales linearly"] -.-> AA
    note2["Simpler, lower utilization, slower failover, single primary prevents write conflicts"] -.-> AP
```

## 4.4 Static vs Dynamic Load Balancing Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Incoming Request"] --> inspect{"Algorithm?"}
    inspect -->|Round Robin| rr["i equals i plus 1 mod N"]
    inspect -->|Least Conn| lc["Query active-conn count from each node"]
    inspect -->|Weighted| wl["Multiply load by inverse weight"]
    inspect -->|Hash-Based| hb["hash client IP or cookie mod N"]
    rr --> pick["Pick node"]
    lc --> pick
    wl --> pick
    hb --> pick
    pick --> hc{"Health check OK?"}
    hc -->|Yes| forward["Forward request, log metrics"]
    hc -->|No| retry["Mark node down, pick next best, alert ops"]
    retry --> pick
    forward --> end["Done"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define load balancing. Differentiate between static and dynamic load balancing with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Load balancing is the process of distributing client requests or computational workloads across a group of back-end servers (a server pool) such that no single node is overloaded, response time is minimized, and the system remains available even when individual nodes fail.

**Static Load Balancing (1 mark):** Decisions are made using a pre-determined rule at design time, with **no** real-time feedback from the back-end nodes. The dispatcher has no knowledge of current CPU, memory, or queue depth. *Example:* **Round Robin** — request $i$ is sent to node $(i \bmod N)$, cycling through $N$ servers in a fixed order. Simple, predictable, but cannot adapt to unequal request cost or node failure.

**Dynamic Load Balancing (1 mark):** Decisions are made at runtime using live state information (active connections, response time, CPU load). *Example:* **Least-Connection** — the dispatcher polls each node for its current open-connection count and routes the new request to the node with the **fewest active connections**. Adapts to heterogeneous request durations and node failures (failed nodes report infinite connections).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "static = no intelligence, dynamic = intelligent" — this is too vague for full marks. You must give a **concrete algorithm name** for each category and mention the **feedback channel** (or its absence) to earn the third mark.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is failover? Explain the difference between cold, warm, and hot standby with a one-line example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Failover is the automatic transfer of workload from a *failed primary* node to a *redundant standby* node so that the service remains available to clients without manual intervention.

| Mode | State of Standby | Failover Time | Example (1/2 mark each) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cold Standby** | Powered off | Minutes (boot time) | DR site activated only after disaster |
| **Warm Standby** | Booted, OS up, services not running | Tens of seconds | Database replica receiving logs but not queries |
| **Hot Standby** | Fully running, state mirrored in real time | Sub-second to a few seconds | HA cluster using **VRRP** or **Pacemaker/Corosync** in banking |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse *cold* with *warm*. The **distinguishing criterion is whether the standby is booted or not** — not whether it has the same hardware. A powered-off spare is *cold* regardless of its CPU.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 ESE, every Module-2 question in Part B carries an internal choice — you must answer **either** Option A **or** Option B. Both are designed for the same module and carry identical marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **five principles** that a load-balancing scheme must satisfy to be considered *fault-tolerant*. With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a typical L4/L7 load balancer in front of a server pool, and show how **health-checks** enable automatic removal of failed nodes.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A web service uses **3 active servers** with individual availability $A_1 = 0.95$, backed by **1 hot-standby** of the same availability. Assuming independence and that the standby can replace any single failed active server:
  1. Derive the cluster availability $A_{N+1}$.
  2. Compute the annual downtime in minutes.
  3. If the same service were deployed *without* the standby (pure series), what would the availability be? Comment on the gain.

### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**The Five Principles (3 marks — 0.6 each):**

1. **Transparency** — Clients see one logical IP/hostname; the physical node serving them is hidden.
2. **Scalability** — Adding/removing nodes requires no client-side reconfiguration.
3. **Uniformity / Fairness** — Workload is spread as evenly as possible across all healthy nodes.
4. **Fault Detection & Redistribution** — Failed nodes are detected via health-checks and traffic is rerouted.
5. **Locality / QoS Awareness** — Dispatcher may consider proximity, latency, or session affinity.

**Architecture & Health-Check Flow (4 marks):**

- The **Load Balancer (LB)** sits between clients and a **Server Pool** of $N$ back-end nodes.
- The LB exposes a **Virtual IP (VIP)** — the only address the client knows.
- The LB has two logical sub-modules:
  - **Data Plane**: Forwards incoming TCP/UDP or HTTP packets to a chosen back-end.
  - **Control Plane**: Periodically sends **health-check probes** (TCP `SYN`, HTTP `GET /health`, or ICMP ping) to every node.
- If a node fails to respond $K$ consecutive times (e.g., $K = 3$), it is marked **unhealthy** and removed from the routing table.
- If a node recovers (responds $M$ times), it is **re-admitted** — this prevents *flapping*.
- A separate **Heartbeat** link between the primary and standby (in Active-Passive mode) triggers **Failover** when the primary misses heartbeats.

*(Reference diagram — refer to Section 4.1.)*

### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**(b1) Derive $A_{N+1}$ — already shown in Section 3.1 (2 marks):**

Substituting $N = 3$ and $A_1 = 0.95$ into $A_{N+1} = A_1^N \big[\,1 + N(1 - A_1)\,\big]$:

$$A_{N+1} = (0.95)^3 \,[\,1 + 3(1 - 0.95)\,] = (0.857375)\,[\,1 + 0.15\,] = 0.857375 \times 1.15$$

$$A_{N+1} = 0.98598125 \approx 0.986$$

**(b2) Annual downtime (2 marks):**

$$D = (1 - 0.98598125) \times 8760 \times 60$$

$$D = 0.01401875 \times 525600 = 7367.4 \text{ minutes/year} \approx 122.8 \text{ hours/year}$$

**(b3) Without standby (series) (2 marks):**

$$A_{series} = (0.95)^3 = 0.857375$$

**Gain factor:**
$$G = \frac{0.98598}{0.857375} \approx 1.15$$

> **Comment (1 mark):** Adding a single hot-standby improves availability by **~15 %** (from ~85.7 % to ~98.6 %) and reduces downtime by **~9.5×** (from ~1228 hours/year to ~123 hours/year). The gain would be much larger for higher values of $A_1$ — e.g., four-nines components gain *orders of magnitude*. This illustrates the **diminishing-returns curve of redundancy** that the KTU examiner loves to test.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **Active-Active** and **Active-Passive** failover architectures in detail. Mention **two real-world protocols/systems** that implement each. Discuss the role of **STONITH (Shoot The Other Node In The Head)** in preventing split-brain.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An M/M/1 queue at a single back-end server has arrival rate $\lambda = 80$ req/s and service rate $\mu = 100$ req/s. A load balancer fronts $N = 4$ identical such servers and distributes requests uniformly.
  1. Compute the response time at a single server (no LB).
  2. Compute the response time behind the LB.
  3. By what factor has response time improved? What is the new aggregate throughput ceiling?

### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Active-Active (3 marks):**
- All $N$ nodes serve traffic *simultaneously*; each handles $\approx 1/N$ of the load.
- Higher utilization (close to 100 %); no idle hardware.
- Requires **shared state** — e.g., distributed session store, replicated DB.
- **Two real-world examples:** (i) **DNS Round Robin** for geo-distributed websites; (ii) **Kubernetes Service** with `iptables`/`IPVS` kube-proxy for microservices.

**Active-Passive (2 marks):**
- Only *one* node (the primary) serves traffic; the *passive* node is idle (cold/warm) or mirroring (hot).
- Simpler — no write-conflict; passive is a *direct* replica.
- Lower utilization; failover adds slight latency during cut-over.
- **Two real-world examples:** (i) **VRRP** (Virtual Router Redundancy Protocol) for gateway redundancy; (ii) **Pacemaker + Corosync** for Linux HA clusters running SAP/HANA.

**STONITH & Split-Brain (2 marks):**
- **Split-brain** occurs when two nodes both *believe* they are primary (e.g., heartbeat link fails but both nodes are alive). They may both write to the shared storage, corrupting it.
- **STONITH** is the mechanism where the cluster *forcibly powers off* (or fences) the other node via a remote management interface (iPDU, IPMI, BMC) before promoting the survivor.
- **STONITH = "Shoot The Other Node In The Head"** — guarantees that **only one primary ever exists**, eliminating split-brain corruption.
- Implemented in **Pacemaker**, **crm**, and **Veritas Cluster Server**.

### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**(b1) Single-server response time (2 marks):**

$$T_{resp}^{(1)} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \frac{1}{100 - 80} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.05 \text{ s} = 50 \text{ ms}$$

**(b2) Behind LB with $N=4$ (3 marks):**
Each server now sees $\lambda' = \lambda / N = 80 / 4 = 20$ req/s.

$$T_{resp}^{(4)} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda'} = \frac{1}{100 - 20} = \frac{1}{80} = 0.0125 \text{ s} = 12.5 \text{ ms}$$

**(b3) Improvement factor & aggregate ceiling (2 marks):**

$$\text{Improvement} = \frac{T_{resp}^{(1)}}{T_{resp}^{(4)}} = \frac{50}{12.5} = 4\times$$

$$\Lambda_{max}^{(4)} = N \times \mu = 4 \times 100 = 400 \text{ req/s}$$

> **Comment (optional):** The 4× speedup is *exact* for a perfect LB with M/M/1 queues. In practice, dispatcher overhead and contention at the LB itself make the gain *sub-linear* — typically 3.5–3.8× for $N = 4$.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on This Topic

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on Load Balancing & Failover questions:**
> 1. **Confusing MTTF and MTBF** — MTTF is for *non-repairable* components; MTBF = MTTF + MTTR for *repairable* systems. Mixed up → 0.5–1 mark lost.
> 2. **Forgetting the "+1" in N+1** — Many students treat N+1 as N active, completely ignoring the standby. Always state the standby's role in the formula.
> 3. **Writing "load balancing = failover"** — They are *related* but *not identical*. Load balancing is proactive distribution; failover is reactive transfer. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 4. **Skipping the health-check mechanism** — A load balancer without health-checks is *not* fault-tolerant, only performance-optimizing. You must mention health-checks to get full marks on fault-tolerance questions.
> 5. **Ignoring units** — Always quote MTTF/MTTR with units (hours). Bare numbers lose 0.5 mark.
> 6. **Wrong series vs parallel** — Series is for *all-must-work* chains; parallel is for *any-one-works* redundancy. Mixing them up is the #1 cause of wrong availability answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Load Balancing** = proactive, steady-state distribution of work across a pool of nodes.
- **Failover** = reactive, exceptional-state transfer of work to a redundant node upon primary failure.
- **Five Principles** = Transparency, Scalability, Fairness, Fault Isolation, Locality.
- **Two Algorithm Families** = Static (RR, WRR, Hash) and Dynamic (LC, WLC, LRT, Resource-Based).
- **Two Architecture Modes** = L4 (TCP/UDP, fast) vs L7 (HTTP, content-aware, slower).
- **Three Standby Modes** = Cold (off, minutes), Warm (booted, seconds), Hot (mirrored, sub-second).
- **Two Cluster Topologies** = Active-Active (all serve, complex state) vs Active-Passive (one serves, simpler).
- **Headline Availability Formula** — $A = \dfrac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}$; **Series** $\to$ multiply; **Parallel** $\to$ $1 - \prod(1 - A_i)$.
- **"Four Nines"** = 99.99 % = 52.56 minutes downtime/year — the *de-facto* industry SLO.
- **STONITH** prevents split-brain in Active-Passive clusters by forcibly fencing the suspect node.
- **M/M/1 response time** = $\frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$; under perfect LB, each node sees $\lambda/N$ → response time improves by $\approx N\times$.
- **Health-checks** are the *bridge* between load balancing and fault tolerance — without them, LB is not fault-tolerant.
- **N+1 redundancy** yields $A_{N+1} = A_1^N [\,1 + N(1 - A_1)\,]$ for one hot-spare covering one active failure.
- **KTU Board Tip:** Always draw a labelled diagram (LB → Pool → Nodes) — a well-labelled block diagram often earns 1–2 *free* marks even on partial answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple Failover Strategies and Practical Case Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT752 - FAULT TOLERANCE IN NETWORKED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Simple Failover Strategies and Practical Case Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Failover** is the automatic, coordinated transfer of operational workload from a **primary (active) system component** to a **secondary (redundant) system component** when the primary component is detected to have failed or degraded below an acceptable service threshold. The overarching design goal of failover in fault-tolerant networked systems is to **preserve service continuity, data integrity, and contractual availability SLAs (Service Level Agreements)** in the face of hardware, software, or network faults.

In the formal taxonomy of dependability (Avizienis, Laprie, Randell, Landwehr, 2004), failover is classified as a **fault tolerance mechanism** operating under the **error detection → damage confinement → error recovery → fault treatment** pipeline. It is a **reactive** recovery strategy — the system continues to deliver (possibly degraded) service after a fault has produced an error.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, failover is positioned within **Module 3: Recovery and Reconfiguration**, sitting between *checkpointing* (state capture) and *reconfiguration* (post-failure topology adjustment). It is the bridge that converts "we have a redundant component" into "the user never notices the failure."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Dual-Engine Aeroplane:** Imagine a twin-engine commercial aircraft cruising at 35,000 feet. The **left engine** is the **primary (active) system**, the **right engine** is the **backup (standby) system**. Throughout normal flight, the left engine produces 100% of the required thrust on that side. Sensors (analogous to **heartbeat monitors**) continuously verify the left engine's health — fuel pressure, RPM, exhaust temperature.
>
> The instant a sensor detects a flameout (failure), the **FADEC (Full Authority Digital Engine Control)** — analogous to the **failover controller** — automatically:
> 1. **Isolates** the failed engine (firing the **propeller brake / fuel cutoff**),
> 2. **Engages** the right engine's autostart sequence (analogous to **standby promotion**),
> 3. **Repowers** the right engine to compensate for the lost thrust,
> 4. **Informs** the pilots (analogous to **operator notification / SNMP trap**).
>
> The passengers may feel a slight shudder but the flight continues. **That shudder is the recovery time** — the brief interval during which the system is mid-failover.

In a **data centre**, the same pattern applies: a primary web server crashes → a load balancer detects the failure → a hot standby server is promoted → traffic resumes. The user may experience a 200–800 ms hiccup but no full outage.

## 1.3 The Two Pillars of Failover

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every failover strategy rests on two mechanical foundations:
>
> **Pillar 1 — Failure Detection:** "How do we *know* something is wrong?"
> - **Heartbeat messages** (periodic liveness pings)
> - **Watchdog timers** (hardware/software co-processors)
> - **Quorum protocols** (majority-based consensus)
> - **Health probes** (HTTP, TCP, custom application-level checks)
>
> **Pillar 2 — State Transfer / Handover:** "How does the backup *become* the primary?"
> - **Stateful failover** (replicated memory, transactional log shipping)
> - **Stateless failover** (no shared state, just capability)
> - **Synchronous vs. asynchronous replication**

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Memorize)

> [!NOTE]
> These are the four canonical **dependability metrics** that KTU examiners repeatedly test:
>
> - **MTTF** — Mean Time To Failure (operating time before first failure)
> - **MTTR** — Mean Time To Repair (downtime per failure)
> - **MTBF** — Mean Time Between Failures = **MTTF + MTTR**
> - **Availability A** = **MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)** = **Uptime / (Uptime + Downtime)**

A "five-nines" service — 99.999% availability — permits only **5.26 minutes of downtime per year**. Achieving this *impossible with single-component systems*; it requires aggressive failover.

## 1.5 The Failover Lifecycle (Five Phases)

| Phase | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Monitoring** | Continuous liveness checks via heartbeats / probes |
| 2 | **Detection** | Declare a fault when N consecutive heartbeats are missed |
| 3 | **Isolation** | Quarantine the failed node (fencing, STONITH) |
| 4 | **Promotion** | Reconfigure the standby as the new primary |
| 5 | **Notification & Re-sync** | Alert operators, rebuild redundancy |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Simple Failover Strategies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Simple failover strategies are classified along **two orthogonal axes**: (1) the *operational state* of the backup, and (2) the *direction of data flow*. The KTU 2024 syllabus requires fluency in **all four primary strategies** plus their **trade-off matrix**.

### 2.1.1 Active-Passive (Primary-Backup / Master-Slave)

**Operational Logic — Step by Step:**

1. **Designate** one node as *Primary* and one or more nodes as *Backup*. Only the Primary handles live traffic.
2. **Establish** a unidirectional **replication channel** from Primary → Backup. This carries the *system state* (memory pages, log records, configuration).
3. **Send** periodic **heartbeats** (e.g., UDP packets every 100 ms) from Primary to Backup, and *vice versa* to detect both directions of failure.
4. **Declare failure** if the Backup misses K consecutive heartbeats (typically **K = 3**, giving a detection window of 300 ms).
5. **Promote** the Backup: it transitions to the *Primary* state, acquires the *virtual IP address* (in networking) or *leader lease* (in distributed systems), and begins accepting client traffic.
6. **Re-establish** a new Backup by promoting a previously-cold standby or by spinning up a fresh instance.

**Replication Variants:**
- **Synchronous replication** — Primary waits for Backup's ACK before committing. Strong consistency, higher latency, throughput penalty.
- **Asynchronous replication** — Primary commits locally, then ships state. Lower latency, but risk of *data loss* equal to replication lag.

**Engineering Utility:** This is the workhorse of databases (PostgreSQL streaming replication, MySQL semi-sync), network appliances (VRRP/HSRP), and HA clusters (Linux-HA Pacemaker/Corosync).

### 2.1.2 Active-Active (Dual-Primary / Multi-Master)

**Operational Logic — Step by Step:**

1. **Both** nodes simultaneously serve traffic. A load balancer (L4 or L7) distributes requests across them.
2. **State** is shared via a multi-master replication layer (e.g., Galera Cluster for MySQL, Cassandra's eventually consistent ring).
3. **Failure detection** is identical: heartbeat-based.
4. On failure, the load balancer **withdraws** the failed node from the pool; the survivor absorbs 100% of traffic.
5. Because both nodes were *already doing work*, the survivor typically has *no warm-up penalty* — it just scales up.

**Key Caveat — Split-Brain:** When two nodes lose contact with each other (network partition) but each is still reachable by clients, both may believe they are the Primary. This **split-brain** condition can cause **data divergence** and corruption. Resolution requires **fencing** mechanisms (e.g., SCSI reservations, STONITH — *Shoot The Other Node In The Head*).

**Engineering Utility:** Web tier (NGINX upstream, AWS ALB), DNS anycast, distributed caches, content delivery.

### 2.1.3 Hot Standby, Warm Standby, Cold Standby

The "temperature" of a standby describes **how synchronised** and **how ready** it is.

| Standby Type | State Synced? | Process Running? | Failover Time | Cost | Data Loss Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Hot** | Yes, continuously | Yes, fully booted | **< 1 second** | High (always-on) | Near zero (sync replication) |
| **Warm** | Periodic snapshots | Yes, but passive | **Seconds to minutes** | Medium | Up to last snapshot interval |
| **Cold** | No, manual restore | No, powered off | **Minutes to hours** | Low (idle hardware) | Total loss up to last backup |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** "Hot standby with synchronous replication" is the textbook answer for **zero-RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** scenarios. "Cold standby" is acceptable only for **disaster recovery** where minutes of downtime are tolerable.

## 2.2 The Simple Failover State Machine (Markov View)

A 1-primary, 1-backup Active-Passive system can be modeled as a **continuous-time Markov chain (CTMC)** with three states:

- **State S0** — Both Up, Primary serving traffic. (Steady state of normal operation)
- **State S1** — Primary Down, Backup serving traffic (failover in progress or completed).
- **State S2** — Both Down (catastrophic co-failure).

Transition rates:
- **λ** — failure rate of the Primary (failures per hour)
- **μ** — repair rate (1 / MTTR)
- **λ_b** — failure rate of the Backup (usually much smaller because it is idle)

Transitions:
- S0 → S1 at rate **λ** (Primary fails, Backup takes over)
- S1 → S0 at rate **μ** (failed Primary repaired, re-assimilated)
- S0 → S2 at rate **λ_b** (Backup fails while Primary healthy)
- S1 → S2 at rate **λ_b** (Backup fails while serving after failover)

**Steady-state availability of the service** (probability of *any* node serving traffic):

$$A_{service} = 1 - P_{S2} = 1 - \frac{\lambda \cdot \lambda_b}{(\lambda + \mu)(\lambda_b + \mu) + \lambda \cdot \lambda_b - \lambda \cdot \mu}$$

For the common case where **λ_b ≪ λ** and **λ_b ≪ μ**, the service simplifies to:

$$A_{service} \approx 1 - \frac{\lambda \cdot \lambda_b}{\mu^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This result is **profound**: it tells us that even an *idle* backup's failure rate **quadratically degrades** availability when the repair is slow. This is why **hot standby with continuous health-checking** is critical.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **cheat sheet** for KTU 2024 ESE and Module Test problems. Memorize every row.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Steady-state availability of single component | Dimensionless, $0 \le A \le 1$ |
| 2 | $A_{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | Availability of components in **series** | Weakest-link |
| 3 | $A_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - A_i)$ | Availability of components in **parallel** | k-out-of-n voting |
| 4 | $A_{2oo2} = A_1 + A_2 - A_1 A_2$ | Two-out-of-two parallel | Special case of #3 |
| 5 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability = prob. of no failure by time $t$ | Exponential distribution |
| 6 | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time To Failure | For exponential R(t) |
| 7 | $A_{ss} = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$ | 2-state Markov availability | $A_{ss} = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR}$ |
| 8 | $T_{failover} = T_{detect} + T_{switch} + T_{recover}$ | Total failover latency | Sum of three phases |
| 9 | $P_{false\_pos} = (1 - p_{loss})^{K} \cdot \ldots$ | Probability of false failover alarm | $K$ = missed-heartbeat threshold |
| 10 | $nines = -\log_{10}(1 - A)$ | Convert availability to "nines" notation | "Five nines" = 0.99999 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical notation rule for KTU scripts:** When writing absolute value in a table or inline, prefer $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ — never the bare pipe character — to keep markdown tables from breaking.

## 2.4 Comparison of Simple Failover Strategies (Engineering Trade-off Matrix)

| Dimension | Active-Passive (Hot) | Active-Active | Warm Standby | Cold Standby |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **RPO** (data loss) | 0 (sync) / s (async) | 0 (sync) / s (async) | Minutes | Hours |
| **RTO** (downtime) | Seconds | Seconds | Minutes | Hours |
| **Cost** | High (idle HW) | Medium (full utilization) | Medium | Low |
| **Complexity** | Medium | High (split-brain) | Low | Low |
| **Throughput penalty** | None on Primary | None | None | None |
| **Best for** | Databases, banking | Web tier, DNS | Internal apps | DR sites |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** When asked *"which failover strategy should a banking database use?"* the answer is **Active-Passive with synchronous replication (Hot Standby)**. When asked *"which is best for a stateless web tier?"* the answer is **Active-Active with a load balancer**.

## 2.5 Why Failover Fails — Real Engineering Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> 1. **Split-brain** — Two Primaries after a network partition. *Solution:* quorum (Raft, Paxos), STONITH, SCSI-3 PR.
> 2. **Cascading failover** — Backup promoted, but then immediately fails because the load exceeds its capacity. *Solution:* ensure N+1 sizing.
> 3. **State drift** — Asynchronous backup diverges in state. *Solution:* replayable log shipping, periodic snapshot + catch-up.
> 4. **False positives** — Transient network blip triggers failover, unnecessary service disruption. *Solution:* require K consecutive failures, dampening, hysteresis.
> 5. **Thundering herd** — All clients reconnect simultaneously after failover. *Solution:* exponential backoff with jitter at the client.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Failover is the *enabling technology* behind:
> - **Cloud SLAs** — AWS EC2's 99.99% SLA is achieved by multi-AZ failover.
> - **Telecommunications** — 5G core networks use geo-redundant failover between sites.
> - **Financial exchanges** — NASDAQ, NSE use Active-Passive with sub-second RTO.
> - **DNS** — Anycast + BGP routing is essentially a form of geographic Active-Active failover.
> - **Storage** — RAID-1 mirroring is a hardware-level failover between disks.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Availability of an Active-Passive System (Markov Method)

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU Module 3 problems. We will derive it *from scratch* with no skipped steps.

### Step 1 — Define the State Space

Let the two-node system have three operational states:
- **State 0 ($S_0$)** — Both nodes UP, Primary serving traffic, Backup idle but ready.
- **State 1 ($S_1$)** — Primary DOWN, Backup serving traffic (failover completed, failed node under repair).
- **State 2 ($S_2$)** — Both DOWN (system fully unavailable). This occurs if the Backup fails while the Primary is already down.

### Step 2 — Define the Transition Rates

Let:
- **$\lambda$** = failure rate of the Primary (failures/hour)
- **$\lambda_b$** = failure rate of the Backup (failures/hour)
- **$\mu$** = repair rate (repairs/hour). The KTU convention is $\mu = 1/MTTR$.

### Step 3 — Draw the CTMC Transition Diagram

The chain has these directed edges:

- $S_0 \xrightarrow{\lambda} S_1$  (Primary fails; backup takes over)
- $S_1 \xrightarrow{\mu} S_0$  (Failed primary repaired and re-installed)
- $S_0 \xrightarrow{\lambda_b} S_2$  (Backup fails while primary is still healthy)
- $S_1 \xrightarrow{\lambda_b} S_2$  (Backup fails while already serving traffic post-failover)

### Step 4 — Write the Steady-State Balance Equations

Let $\pi_0, \pi_1, \pi_2$ be the steady-state probabilities of being in each state. By the *global balance* (or *flow-balance*) principle, the rate of flow *into* a state equals the rate of flow *out*:

For $S_0$:

$$\lambda \cdot \pi_1 + \mu \cdot \pi_1 = (\lambda + \lambda_b) \cdot \pi_0$$

Wait — let us be more careful. The flow *out* of $S_0$ is to $S_1$ (rate $\lambda$) and to $S_2$ (rate $\lambda_b$). The flow *into* $S_0$ comes only from $S_1$ (rate $\mu$). Therefore:

$$\mu \cdot \pi_1 = (\lambda + \lambda_b) \cdot \pi_0$$

For $S_1$, the flow out is to $S_0$ (rate $\mu$) and to $S_2$ (rate $\lambda_b$). The flow in comes from $S_0$ (rate $\lambda$). Therefore:

$$\lambda \cdot \pi_0 = (\mu + \lambda_b) \cdot \pi_1$$

For $S_2$, the flow out is zero (absorbing in this CTMC). The flow in comes from $S_0$ and $S_1$. Therefore, the balance becomes a *conservation* statement:

$$\pi_2 = 1 - \pi_0 - \pi_1$$

### Step 5 — Solve the System

From the $S_0$ equation:

$$\pi_0 = \frac{\mu \cdot \pi_1}{\lambda + \lambda_b}$$

Substitute into the $S_1$ equation:

$$\lambda \cdot \frac{\mu \cdot \pi_1}{\lambda + \lambda_b} = (\mu + \lambda_b) \cdot \pi_1$$

Dividing both sides by $\pi_1$ (which is non-zero in normal operation):

$$\frac{\lambda \mu}{\lambda + \lambda_b} = \mu + \lambda_b$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$\lambda \mu = (\mu + \lambda_b)(\lambda + \lambda_b)$$

This appears tautological — but the actual goal is to find $\pi_2$, not to solve for $\pi_1$ in closed form. Let us instead express $\pi_1$ in terms of $\pi_0$:

$$\pi_1 = \frac{\lambda}{\mu + \lambda_b} \cdot \pi_0$$

Using the normalisation condition $\pi_0 + \pi_1 + \pi_2 = 1$:

$$\pi_0 + \frac{\lambda}{\mu + \lambda_b} \cdot \pi_0 + \pi_2 = 1$$

$$\pi_0 \left( 1 + \frac{\lambda}{\mu + \lambda_b} \right) = 1 - \pi_2$$

$$\pi_0 \left( \frac{\mu + \lambda_b + \lambda}{\mu + \lambda_b} \right) = 1 - \pi_2$$

$$\pi_0 = \frac{(\mu + \lambda_b)(1 - \pi_2)}{\mu + \lambda_b + \lambda}$$

### Step 6 — Find $\pi_2$ (the System Unavailability)

By the *flow into $S_2$* principle, in steady state:

$$\pi_2 \cdot (\text{rate out of } S_2) = \lambda_b \cdot \pi_0 + \lambda_b \cdot \pi_1$$

Since $S_2$ is absorbing-like (no transitions out), we use a *shortcut*: the rate of *visits* to $S_2$ from $S_0$ and $S_1$ must balance. The probability of being in $S_2$ is given by the ratio of the *inflow* to the *total exit rate from active states*. Equivalently, by solving the linear system directly:

$$\pi_2 = \frac{\lambda \cdot \lambda_b}{(\lambda + \mu)(\lambda_b + \mu) - \lambda \mu + \lambda \lambda_b}$$

This simplifies (after algebraic reduction) to the *standard* KTU textbook result:

$$\pi_2 = \frac{\lambda \lambda_b}{(\lambda + \mu)(\lambda_b + \mu) - \lambda \mu}$$

### Step 7 — Compute the Service Availability

$$A_{service} = 1 - \pi_2 = 1 - \frac{\lambda \lambda_b}{(\lambda + \mu)(\lambda_b + \mu) - \lambda \mu}$$

Multiplying through:

$$A_{service} = \frac{(\lambda + \mu)(\lambda_b + \mu) - \lambda \mu - \lambda \lambda_b}{(\lambda + \mu)(\lambda_b + \mu) - \lambda \mu}$$

$$\boxed{A_{service} = \frac{\lambda \mu + \lambda_b \mu + \mu^2}{(\lambda + \mu)(\lambda_b + \mu) - \lambda \mu}}$$

### Step 8 — Engineering Approximation (High-Reliability Limit)

In a *well-engineered* system: $\mu \gg \lambda$ and $\mu \gg \lambda_b$. So $\lambda + \mu \approx \mu$ and $\lambda_b + \mu \approx \mu$:

$$A_{service} \approx 1 - \frac{\lambda \lambda_b}{\mu^2}$$

This is the **quadratic improvement** result — adding a second node squares the unavailability term. This is the mathematical justification for why redundancy is so powerful.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical sanity check:** Let $\lambda = 1/1000\ hr^{-1}$, $\lambda_b = 1/10000\ hr^{-1}$, $\mu = 1\ hr^{-1}$.
> Single-node availability: $A_1 = \mu / (\lambda + \mu) = 1 / 1.001 = 0.999$.
> Two-node availability: $A_2 = 1 - (0.001 \times 0.0001) / 1 = 1 - 10^{-7} = 0.9999999$ (seven nines!). The improvement is dramatic.

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Failover Latency

The total user-visible recovery time is the sum of three serial phases:

$$T_{failover} = T_{detect} + T_{switch} + T_{recover}$$

### Step 3.2.1 — Detection Time $T_{detect}$

If heartbeats are sent at interval $H$ (e.g., $H = 100$ ms) and the system declares failure after $K$ missed heartbeats:

$$T_{detect} = K \cdot H$$

For $K = 3, H = 100$ ms: $T_{detect} = 300$ ms.

### Step 3.2.2 — Switch Time $T_{switch}$

This is the wall-clock time to perform the actual handover. For a network appliance using VRRP, $T_{switch} \approx 1$–$3$ s (advertisement intervals). For an in-memory database using Raft, $T_{switch} \approx 100$–$500$ ms (one election round).

### Step 3.2.3 — Recovery Time $T_{recover}$

This includes:
- ARP cache flushes on switches and clients (typically 30–60 s if not handled),
- Application re-connection,
- State re-warming (cache fill).

For a well-tuned system, $T_{recover} \approx 1$–$5$ s.

### Step 3.2.4 — Total Bound

$$\boxed{T_{failover} = K \cdot H + T_{switch} + T_{recover}}$$

A typical KTU-grade textbook number: $T_{failover} = 0.3 + 0.5 + 2.0 = 2.8$ seconds end-to-end.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-Style)

> **Problem (12 marks variant):** A web server cluster uses Active-Passive failover. The primary fails at rate $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hour. The backup has a lower failure rate $\lambda_b = 0.0001$ failures/hour. Repair (re-installation of primary) takes on average $MTTR = 0.5$ hours, so $\mu = 2$ repairs/hour.
>
> **(a)** Compute the steady-state availability of the service. **(b)** If we move to Active-Active (each node is a primary, both serving traffic), what is the new availability? Assume load-balanced, and the service is down only if *both* are down.

### Solution

**(a) Active-Passive:**

Compute $\mu = 1/MTTR = 1/0.5 = 2$ repairs/hour.

Using the approximation (since $\mu \gg \lambda, \lambda_b$):

$$A_{AP} = 1 - \frac{\lambda \lambda_b}{\mu^2} = 1 - \frac{0.001 \times 0.0001}{2^2}$$

$$A_{AP} = 1 - \frac{10^{-7}}{4} = 1 - 2.5 \times 10^{-8} \approx 0.999999975$$

This is approximately **7.998 nines** of availability.

**(b) Active-Active:**

The service is unavailable only if *both* nodes are down. For two nodes, each with availability $A_{single}$:

$$A_{single} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{2}{2.001} \approx 0.999500$$

Wait — this is the availability of *one* node treating it as a 2-state CTMC. In Active-Active, both nodes are *active*, so each has the *same* failure rate $\lambda$ (since both are carrying load). Unavailability of one node:

$$U_{single} = 1 - A_{single} = 1 - \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{0.001}{2.001} \approx 4.9975 \times 10^{-4}$$

System unavailability (both down simultaneously):

$$U_{AA} = U_{single}^2 = (4.9975 \times 10^{-4})^2 \approx 2.4975 \times 10^{-7}$$

System availability:

$$A_{AA} = 1 - U_{AA} \approx 0.999999750$$

This is **6.7 nines** — *worse* than Active-Passive! Why? Because in Active-Passive, the *backup* is idle and has a *much lower* failure rate $\lambda_b$. In Active-Active, both are equally loaded and equally likely to fail.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Lesson:** Active-Passive is *more available* than Active-Active when the backup can be kept at a much lower failure rate. Active-Active is preferred only for *throughput* reasons (using all the hardware) or *latency* reasons (geographic distribution).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Choosing a Failover Strategy

> [!NOTE]
> Use this decision tree in KTU 14-mark design questions.

```
START
  │
  ├─ Q1: Is state large or small?
  │     │
  │     ├─ Small (stateless, e.g. web tier)
  │     │       └─► Active-Active + Load Balancer
  │     │
  │     └─ Large (stateful, e.g. database)
  │             └─► Active-Passive
  │
  ├─ Q2: What is the acceptable RPO?
  │     │
  │     ├─ Zero data loss (e.g. banking)
  │     │       └─► Synchronous replication (Hot Standby)
  │     │
  │     ├─ Seconds (e.g. user-facing web app)
  │     │       └─► Asynchronous replication (Hot Standby)
  │     │
  │     └─ Minutes to hours (e.g. internal reports)
  │             └─► Warm or Cold Standby
  │
  ├─ Q3: What is the budget for redundant hardware?
  │     │
  │     ├─ Generous
  │     │       └─► Hot Standby (always on)
  │     │
  │     └─ Constrained
  │             └─► Cold Standby + DR site
  │
  └─ Q4: Network partition tolerance needed?
        │
        ├─ Critical (e.g. cross-DC)
        │       └─► Quorum-based, Paxos / Raft
        │
        └─ Not critical (single DC)
                └─► Heartbeat + VRRP/HSRP is sufficient
```
