<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Network Partitioning. State the CAP Theorem and explain its relevance to fault-tolerant systems.

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Network Partitioning (1 mark):** A network partition is the failure mode in which the nodes of a distributed system are split into two or more disconnected sub-groups; messages cannot be exchanged across sub-groups, but intra-group communication continues.

2. **CAP Theorem (1 mark):** As conjectured by Eric Brewer in 2000 and proved by Gilbert and Lynch in 2002, a distributed data store can simultaneously provide at most two of: **Consistency (C)**, **Availability (A)**, **Partition Tolerance (P)**.

3. **Relevance to fault tolerance (1 mark):** Because partitions are inevitable in real deployments, $P$ is non-negotiable; designers must choose between CP (sacrificing availability, e.g., HBase) and AP (sacrificing strong consistency, e.g., Cassandra). This choice directly determines which failure modes the system tolerates gracefully.

### Question 2: What is Hinted Handoff? How does it improve write availability during a network partition?

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Hinted handoff is a sloppy-quorum technique wherein, when a write cannot reach its target replica due to a partition or node failure, the data and the intended replica ID are stored as a "hint" at another reachable node in the cluster.

2. **Availability improvement (1 mark):** The write is acknowledged as soon as $W$ hints/actual writes succeed on the first available $W$ healthy nodes, decoupling the write latency from the liveness of the canonical replicas. Write availability becomes a function of the $k^{th}$ nearest healthy node, not the canonical quorum.

3. **Replay (1 mark):** When the original replica recovers (link restored, node restarted), the holding node replays the hint to the original replica and deletes the hint. This preserves durability without blocking the write path.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option A

### Question A: Discuss in detail the CAP theorem and quorum-based consistency mechanisms for handling network partitions. Derive the quorum success probability for a 5-node cluster.

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

#### Part (a): CAP Theorem and Quorum Consistency (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Brewer's Conjecture (1 mark):**
In a distributed data store shared by clients connected over an asynchronous network that may suffer message loss:
- **C** = linearizable consistency (every read returns the most recent write),
- **A** = every request receives a response (no error),
- **P** = operations continue under arbitrary message loss.

Brewer conjectured that at most two can be guaranteed simultaneously.

**Step 2 — Gilbert–Lynch Proof Sketch (2 marks):**
Assume a system with two nodes $N_1$ and $N_2$ and a single shared register $x$. Suppose $C$, $A$, and $P$ all hold. After a write $w$ to $N_1$, the network partitions. $N_2$ must still respond to reads (A), but cannot learn of $w$ (P). It returns the old value, violating C. Therefore at most two of $\{C, A, P\}$ can hold — a contradiction if all three are required.

**Step 3 — Practical Implications (1 mark):** Real systems pick CP (HBase, MongoDB-strong, etcd) or AP (Cassandra, DynamoDB, Riak). PACELC adds Else-branch: in absence of partition, choose Latency vs Consistency.

**Step 4 — Quorum System (2 marks):**
For $N$ replicas with read quorum $R$ and write quorum $W$, the condition $R + W > N$ ensures every read set intersects every write set, guaranteeing a read sees the latest write. A stronger condition $R > N/2$ and $W > N/2$ provides strict consistency and prevents split-brain.

**Step 5 — Strict vs Sloppy (1 mark):**
- *Strict quorum:* all writes/reads touch canonical replicas; higher latency under partition.
- *Sloppy quorum:* writes go to the first $W$ healthy nodes from a preferred list; latency is bounded by the $k^{th}$ nearest replica. Hinted handoff reconciles the canonical state on recovery.

#### Part (b): Quorum Success Probability Derivation (7 marks)

**Given:** $N = 5$, $W = 3$, $R = 3$, per-node failure probability $p = 0.1$.

**Step 1 — Probability Model (1 mark):**
Each node is independently alive with probability $(1-p) = 0.9$. The number of alive nodes is $X \sim \text{Binomial}(N, 1-p)$.

**Step 2 — Write Success (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X \geq W) &= \sum_{i=3}^{5} \binom{5}{i} (0.9)^i (0.1)^{5-i} \\
&= \binom{5}{3}(0.9)^3(0.1)^2 + \binom{5}{4}(0.9)^4(0.1)^1 + \binom{5}{5}(0.9)^5 \\
&= 10 \cdot 0.729 \cdot 0.01 + 5 \cdot 0.6561 \cdot 0.1 + 1 \cdot 0.59049 \\
&= 0.0729 + 0.32805 + 0.59049 \\
&= 0.99144
\end{aligned}
$$

So $P(\text{write succeeds}) \approx 99.14\%$.

**Step 3 — Read Success (2 marks):**
By symmetry with $R = 3$, $P(\text{read succeeds}) = 0.99144$.

**Step 4 — Partition Tolerance Discussion (1 mark):**
Under a network partition that isolates 2 of 5 nodes (worst case $N-2 = 3$ alive), the minority partition can still satisfy $W = 3$ exactly at the boundary. The system remains just barely write-available. With sloppy quorum and $k = 3$, writes can be redirected to any 3 reachable nodes, improving real availability to nearly $1 - 0.1^3 = 0.999$.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option B

### Question B: Explain vector clocks, CRDTs, and anti-entropy mechanisms for handling latency and eventual consistency in partitioned distributed systems. Include a derivation of Merkle tree comparison cost.

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Choice B]**

#### Part (a): Vector Clocks and CRDTs (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Why Logical Clocks? (1 mark):**
Physical clocks in distributed systems cannot be perfectly synchronised (limited by NTP skew, clock drift, and network delay). Lamport's logical clocks provide a partial order, but cannot distinguish concurrent events from causally related ones. Vector clocks solve this by maintaining one counter per node.

**Step 2 — Vector Clock Rules (2 marks):**
- *Local event at node $i$:* $VC_i[i] \mathrel{+}= 1$.
- *Send message:* sender increments its own counter, attaches $VC$ to message.
- *Receive message at node $j$:* $VC_j[k] = \max(VC_j[k],\, VC_{msg}[k])$ for all $k$; then $VC_j[j] \mathrel{+}= 1$.

**Step 3 — Causality Check (1 mark):**
$VC_a \leq VC_b$ iff $\forall i,\; VC_a[i] \leq VC_b[i]$ and $VC_a \neq VC_b$. If neither dominates, the events are **concurrent** ($VC_a \parallel VC_b$).

**Step 4 — Conflict Resolution (1 mark):**
Concurrent writes produce *siblings*. The system applies a resolver: Last-Write-Wins (using attached wall-clock timestamp), application-specific merge (e.g., shopping cart union), or a CRDT that ensures automatic convergence.

**Step 5 — CRDTs (2 marks):**
- **G-Counter** (Grow-only counter): each node $i$ has a local counter $c_i$; total = $\sum c_i$. Merge takes per-component max.
- **PN-Counter** (Positive-Negative): pair of G-Counters, supports increments and decrements.
- **G-Set** (Grow-only Set): union on merge.
- **2P-Set** (Two-Phase Set): pair of G-Sets (added, tombstoned); supports add and remove once.
- **OR-Set** (Observed-Remove Set): tags each addition with a unique id, removes tag-specific elements.

**Example — G-Counter merge:**

$$
VC_a = \langle 3, 1, 2 \rangle, \quad VC_b = \langle 2, 4, 2 \rangle
$$

$$
\text{merge}(VC_a, VC_b) = \langle \max(3,2),\; \max(1,4),\; \max(2,2) \rangle = \langle 3, 4, 2 \rangle
$$

#### Part (b): Anti-Entropy, Gossip, and Merkle Tree Cost (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Anti-Entropy Concept (1 mark):**
Anti-entropy is a background process that reconciles replica divergence without requiring client-driven coordination. It can be push, pull, or push-pull, and runs at a configurable interval $T_{AE}$.

**Step 2 — Merkle Tree Construction (2 marks):**
- Hash the keys in each leaf bucket to obtain $H_{leaf}$.
- Pair adjacent leaves and hash to get $H_{parent}$.
- Recurse up to the root $H_{root}$.
- A tree of $L = 2^h$ leaves has height $h$, total internal + leaf nodes $= 2L - 1$.

**Step 3 — Merkle Comparison Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Compare $H_{root}$ between two replicas. If equal, trees are identical.
2. If different, recursively compare children whose hashes differ.
3. Leaves whose hashes differ indicate divergent keys; only those are exchanged.

**Step 4 — Cost Derivation (2 marks):**
With $L$ buckets and height $h = \log_2 L$:
- Worst-case comparisons per round: $h = \log_2 L$.
- Bandwidth: $O(N_{divergent} \cdot \log L)$ versus $O(N_{keys})$ without Merkle trees.
- **Numerical example:** $L = 1024$, $h = 10$. So a divergence localised to one bucket requires at most **10 hash exchanges** and **1 leaf data transfer**, regardless of cluster size.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning and Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (2024 Scheme Pattern):**
> 1. **CAP confusion:** Writing "CAP says choose any two" without justifying that $P$ is *non-negotiable* costs **1 mark** in 7-mark questions.
> 2. **Quorum condition written as $R + W \geq N$:** This is *weak*; the correct strong condition is $R + W > N$. Examiners deduct **½ mark** for the off-by-one.
> 3. **Mixing up strict and sloppy quorum:** Do not state that sloppy quorum satisfies $R + W > N$ — it relaxes membership to a *preferred* list. The intersection property is preserved with respect to the first $W$ healthy nodes, not the canonical set.
> 4. **Vector clock vs Lamport clock:** Do not use Lamport scalar timestamps for conflict detection — they cannot detect concurrency. Always state that vector clocks are required for causal order.
> 5. **CRDT commutativity:** A common error is to claim "CRDTs use a coordinator" — they do not. The defining property is that operations are commutative, associative, and idempotent, eliminating the need for coordination.
> 6. **Merkle tree cost:** Stating $O(N)$ cost is wrong. Always derive $O(\log N)$ in the derivation steps.
> 7. **Latency formula:** Writing $L = d/s$ alone ignores transmission, processing, and queuing delays. Full KTU credit requires the four-component decomposition.
> 8. **Hinted handoff timing:** Students often forget to specify that hints have a **Time-To-Live** and are dropped if not replayed within $T_{hint}$.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorise for KTU 2024 ESE**

- [x] **CAP Theorem:** $C$, $A$, $P$; can guarantee at most two; $P$ is non-negotiable in real systems.
- [x] **CAP trade-off classes:** CP (HBase, etcd, Spanner) vs AP (Cassandra, DynamoDB, Riak).
- [x] **PACELC:** Else-branch trade-off between Latency and Consistency.
- [x] **Quorum condition:** $R + W > N$; strict variant $R, W > N/2$.
- [x] **Strict vs Sloppy quorum:** strict touches canonical replicas; sloppy uses first $W$ healthy.
- [x] **Hinted Handoff:** store hint at healthy node; replay on recovery; has TTL.
- [x] **Anti-Entropy:** background reconciliation; Merkle-tree based for efficiency.
- [x] **Merkle Tree cost:** $O(\log L)$ comparisons for $L$ leaf buckets.
- [x] **Gossip protocols:** $O(\log N)$ convergence rounds with fanout $f$.
- [x] **Vector clocks:** $n$-tuple; causal order; concurrency detection by partial-order test.
- [x] **CRDTs:** state-based (CvRDT) and op-based (CmRDT); commutative, associative, idempotent merge.
- [x] **Latency decomposition:** $L = T_{prop} + T_{trans} + T_{proc} + T_{queue}$.
- [x] **RTT:** $L_{RTT} = 2(T_{prop} + T_{trans} + T_{proc} + T_{queue})$.
- [x] **Read repair:** asynchronous fix on stale read.
- [x] **Lease-based consistency:** $T_{lease} < RTT_{max}$ for safe cache/lock validity.
- [x] **Speculative execution & prefetching:** mask WAN latency for user-perceived performance.
- [x] **Binomial availability model:** $P(\text{quorum reachable}) = \sum_{i=W}^{N} \binom{N}{i}(1-p)^i p^{N-i}$.
- [x] **Multi-master replication:** active-active across datacenters; uses CRDTs for convergence.
- [x] **Sloppy quorum parameter $k$:** $W$ writes accepted from first $k$ healthy nodes of the preference list.
- [x] **Failure detectors:** φ-accrual, SWIM, gossip-style heartbeat for partition detection.
- [x] **Hinted handoff store:** in-memory + on-disk, ordered by replica ID and timestamp.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Strategy Tip:** For 14-mark questions, structure your answer as: (1) Definition (2 marks), (2) Mechanism (3 marks), (3) Mathematical analysis (2 marks), and (4) Engineering example (2 marks). The remaining 5 marks go to sub-part coverage. Always include a labelled diagram wherever possible — KTU examiners award up to **1 extra mark** for a correct, well-labelled schematic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fault Tolerance in Modern Systems: Cloud Computing and Microservices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT752 - FAULT TOLERANCE IN NETWORKED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fault Tolerance in Modern Systems: Cloud Computing and Microservices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *PECPT752 – Fault Tolerance in Networked Systems*, **Fault Tolerance in Modern Distributed Systems** refers to the engineered ability of a cloud-native or microservice-based application to **continue delivering its specified functional and non-functional services** to end-users despite the occurrence of hardware faults, software bugs, network partitions, or partial system failures. The cloud paradigm shifts fault tolerance from a *single-host redundancy* problem to a **distributed resilience engineering** problem across geo-distributed data centers, virtual machines, containers, and loosely-coupled service instances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECPT752 – Module 4):**
> The 2024 scheme explicitly expects students to differentiate between **traditional fault tolerance** (reliable hardware, ECC memory, dual-redundant servers) and **modern cloud fault tolerance** (software-defined resilience, stateless service replication, chaos engineering, eventual consistency, and SLO-driven recovery).

A modern cloud system tolerates faults across **five orthogonal layers**:
1. **Infrastructure Layer** – VM/host failures, hypervisor crashes, data center outages.
2. **Platform Layer** – Container runtime failures (Docker, containerd), orchestration node failures (Kubernetes nodes).
3. **Service Layer** – Microservice crashes, API timeouts, dependency unavailability.
4. **Data Layer** – Replica divergence, partition tolerance failures, consistency conflicts.
5. **Network Layer** – Inter-zone link failures, DNS resolution failures, BGP route flaps.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Airline Federation"

Imagine a single-aircraft airline. A fault (engine failure) is handled by *redundancy inside the same plane* — duplicate hydraulic lines, backup instruments, and a co-pilot. This is **traditional fault tolerance**.

Now imagine a **global airline federation** consisting of 500 aircraft operating across 80 airports. A fault (one aircraft grounded, one airport closed by weather, an ATC system crash) is handled not by duplicating parts of *one* plane, but by:
- **Re-routing passengers** to other operational aircraft (load balancing).
- **Holding flights at alternate airports** (graceful degradation).
- **Switching to a backup reservation system** running in another data center (active-active clusters).
- **Allowing check-in to proceed even if the loyalty-points service is down** (degraded mode operation).

This is **cloud-native fault tolerance** — the resilience is *distributed*, *software-defined*, and *policy-driven* rather than residing inside a single physical box.

---

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Mandatory Bold)

The following industry-standard **Service Level Objectives (SLOs)** and reliability metrics govern every KTU examination answer on this topic:

- **MTBF** – Mean Time Between Failures (hours)
- **MTTR** – Mean Time To Repair / Recovery (hours)
- **MTTF** – Mean Time To Failure (hours, for non-repairable components)
- **Availability (A)** – Uptime fraction, dimensionless ratio
- **RPO** – Recovery Point Objective (acceptable data loss in seconds)
- **RTO** – Recovery Time Objective (acceptable downtime in seconds)
- **SLA** – Service Level Agreement (contractual uptime, e.g., 99.99%)

> [!NOTE]
> **Availability Tiers in Cloud (Industry Standard):**
> $$\text{Availability} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$
> - **Two 9s** → 99.0% → 87.6 hours downtime/year
> - **Three 9s** → 99.9% → 8.76 hours downtime/year
> - **Four 9s** → 99.99% → 52.6 minutes downtime/year
> - **Five 9s** → 99.999% → 5.26 minutes downtime/year (Google-grade)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Pillars of Cloud-Native Fault Tolerance

The KTU 2024 scheme groups modern fault-tolerance techniques into six canonical patterns (derived from Chris Richardson's *Microservices Patterns* and the CNCF resilience taxonomy). Every KTU 14-mark answer should reference these explicitly.

**Pillar 1 — Redundancy (Active-Active / Active-Passive)**
- *Why:* A single instance will fail; multiple instances provide failover capacity.
- *How:* Cloud providers offer Multi-AZ (Availability Zone) deployment groups. AWS EC2 Auto Scaling Groups, Azure VM Scale Sets, and GCP Managed Instance Groups all implement health-check-driven replacement.

**Pillar 2 — Replication (Synchronous vs Asynchronous)**
- *Why:* State must survive instance loss.
- *How:*
  - **Synchronous replication:** Write acknowledged only after *all* replicas persist. Guarantees RPO = 0 but increases write latency.
  - **Asynchronous replication:** Write acknowledged after *local* persist. Lower latency but RPO > 0.

**Pillar 3 — Load Balancing & Health Probes**
- *Why:* Distribute traffic away from unhealthy instances.
- *How:* L4 (transport) and L7 (application) balancers (NGINX, HAProxy, Envoy, AWS ALB) probe `/health` endpoints and remove failed instances from the rotation pool.

**Pillar 4 — Circuit Breaker Pattern**
- *Why:* Prevent cascading failure when a downstream service is slow or down.
- *How:* The breaker has three states — **CLOSED** (normal), **OPEN** (failing fast, no calls to downstream), **HALF-OPEN** (probing recovery with limited traffic).

**Pillar 5 — Bulkhead Isolation**
- *Why:* Contain blast radius — failure of one service thread pool must not exhaust resources for unrelated services.
- *How:* Separate thread pools, separate connection pools, separate process namespaces (Kubernetes pods). Named after the watertight compartments in ship hulls.

**Pillar 6 — Graceful Degradation & Fallback**
- *Why:* Maintain partial functionality instead of total outage.
- *How:* Return cached data, simplified responses, or default values when a dependency fails. Example: showing static product images if the dynamic pricing service is down.

---

### 2.2 CAP Theorem — The Cloud Fault Tolerance Trade-off

In any distributed cloud system, the **CAP Theorem** (Brewer, 2000) states that during a network partition (P), a system can guarantee at most two of the three properties:

- **C** – Consistency (every read sees the latest write)
- **A** – Availability (every request receives a response)
- **P** – Partition tolerance (system operates despite network failures)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since network partitions are *inevitable* in cloud WANs, real systems choose between **CP** (e.g., HBase, MongoDB with majority write concern) and **AP** (e.g., Cassandra, DynamoDB, Riak). KTU expects you to cite concrete examples.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table is the **exam-day cheat sheet** for any quantitative question on availability, reliability, and queue-based fault modeling.

| Formula | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Domain of Validity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | MTBF, MTTR in hours | Repairable systems |
| Unavailability | $U = 1 - A = \dfrac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | All systems |
| Downtime per year | $D_{yr} = (1-A) \times 8760 \text{ hours}$ | Hours/year | All systems |
| Series reliability | $R_{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | $R_i \in [0,1]$ | Independent components |
| Parallel reliability | $R_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1-R_i)$ | $R_i \in [0,1]$ | Independent components |
| k-out-of-n reliability | $R_{k/n} = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}$ | $R \in [0,1]$ | Identical components |
| Exponential failure | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate, $t$ = time | Memoryless systems |
| MTTF exponential | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Hours | Constant failure rate |
| Retry success prob. | $P_{k} = 1 - (1-p)^{k}$ | $p$ = single success prob., $k$ = retries | Independent attempts |
| Little's Law | $L = \lambda \cdot W$ | $L$ = items in system, $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $W$ = wait time | Steady-state queues |
| M/M/1 utilization | $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | $\lambda$ = arrival, $\mu$ = service | $\rho < 1$ for stability |
| M/M/1 response time | $W = \dfrac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$ | Seconds | Single-server queue |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The series vs parallel reliability distinction is the mathematical backbone of every *redundancy design* — placing two power supplies in parallel (parallel reliability) inside a server (series with CPU, RAM) is precisely what gives modern cloud nodes their "five 9s" of component-level availability before software resilience even begins.

---

### 2.4 The Fault-Tolerance Decision Matrix

Choosing a fault-tolerance strategy depends on workload criticality. The following matrix maps SLO requirements to design choices:

| Criticality Tier | Target Availability | Recommended Strategy | Example Workload |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tier 0 – Mission Critical | 99.999% | Active-Active Multi-Region + Sync Replication | Payment processing, Air traffic control |
| Tier 1 – Business Critical | 99.99% | Active-Passive Multi-AZ + Async Replication | E-commerce checkout, Banking |
| Tier 2 – Operational | 99.9% | Single Region Multi-AZ + Auto Scaling | Internal dashboards, Reporting |
| Tier 3 – Best Effort | 99.0% | Single AZ + Stateless pods | Batch analytics, Dev/Test |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Parallel System Availability

A cloud microservice is deployed in **parallel** across $n$ independent instances. Each instance has availability $a$ and failure rate $\lambda$. We derive the overall system availability.

**Step 1 — Define single-instance unavailability.**
The probability that a *single* instance is *down* is:
$$q = 1 - a$$

**Step 2 — Compute the probability that *all* instances are simultaneously down.**
Because failures are independent, the joint probability of *all* $n$ instances failing is the product of their individual failure probabilities:
$$P(\text{all down}) = q^n = (1-a)^n$$

**Step 3 — Compute system availability (the complement).**
The system is *available* if at least one instance is up:
$$A_{parallel} = 1 - (1-a)^n$$

**Step 4 — Verify with $n=1$ (single instance, no redundancy).**
$$A_{parallel} = 1 - (1-a)^1 = a \;\; \checkmark$$
This correctly reduces to single-instance availability, as expected.

**Step 5 — Verify with $n=2$ (basic active-passive failover pair).**
$$A_{parallel} = 1 - (1-a)^2 = 2a - a^2$$
If $a = 0.99$, then $A_{parallel} = 1.98 - 0.9801 = 0.9999$ — the classic "two 9s becomes four 9s" result.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Series System Availability

When several *mandatory* components must *all* function for the system to be available (e.g., a microservice call chain of **Gateway → Auth → Payment → DB**), they are in series.

**Step 1 — Express each component's availability.**
Component $i$ has availability $a_i$.

**Step 2 — Multiply (series reliability rule).**
$$A_{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} a_i$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example with $n=4$ call hops.**
Assume each hop has $a_i = 0.99$ (a reasonable per-call SLA for a healthy microservice):
$$A_{series} = (0.99)^4 = 0.96059601 \approx 0.9606$$
This represents only **96.06% availability** — a 14× degradation from a single 99% component. This is the *microservices tax* and the reason **bulkheads and circuit breakers are mandatory** in long call chains.

**Step 4 — Mitigation by parallelizing critical hops.**
If the Payment service is replicated 3-way (parallel) with each replica at $a = 0.99$:
$$A_{payment,3} = 1 - (0.01)^3 = 0.999999$$
The new chain availability becomes:
$$A_{chain} = 0.99 \times 0.99 \times 0.999999 \times 0.99 \approx 0.9802$$
Parallelizing the critical hop recovers the chain to **98.02%** — a 2× improvement, demonstrating the practical power of redundancy at bottleneck services.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mean Time Between Failures for Active-Passive Cluster

An active-passive cluster has a single active node with MTTF $\mu_1$ and a hot-standby passive node with MTTF $\mu_2$. The cluster fails only when *both* nodes have failed.

**Step 1 — Identify the cluster failure condition.**
Cluster fails ⇒ active fails **AND** standby also fails (or is unavailable) before active is repaired.

**Step 2 — Use the exponential failure model.**
For exponentially distributed failures with rate $\lambda_i = 1/\mu_i$, the probability density of time-to-failure is:
$$f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the unconditional MTTF of the cluster.**
For a two-node redundant system with perfect failure detection and switchover, the cluster MTTF is approximately:
$$\text{MTTF}_{cluster} = \mu_1 + \frac{\mu_1^2}{2\mu_2}$$
This is the standard result from reliability block diagram analysis with hot-standby redundancy.

**Step 4 — Numerical example.**
If $\mu_1 = 1000$ hours and $\mu_2 = 800$ hours:
$$\text{MTTF}_{cluster} = 1000 + \frac{(1000)^2}{2 \times 800} = 1000 + 625 = 1625 \text{ hours}$$
Compared to the single-node MTTF of 1000 hours, the cluster extends the MTTF by **62.5%** at the cost of one extra node.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Circuit Breaker Threshold Analysis

The circuit breaker pattern is *probabilistic*. Suppose a downstream service has a per-call failure probability $p$ and we set the breaker's **failure threshold** to open after $k$ consecutive failures.

**Step 1 — Probability the breaker opens on the $k$-th call (given it was closed).**
The breaker opens only after $k$ *consecutive* failures. The probability of this exact pattern (fail, fail, ..., fail) of length $k$ is:
$$P_{open} = p^k$$

**Step 2 — Probability the breaker *remains closed* after $n$ calls.**
The breaker stays closed unless $k$ consecutive failures occur. For $n < k$, $P_{stay\,closed} = 1$. For general analysis, the expected number of calls before opening is:
$$E[N] = \sum_{n=k}^{\infty} n \cdot P(\text{first opens at }n)$$

**Step 3 — Engineering interpretation.**
A high $p$ (e.g., 0.5) with $k=5$ gives $P_{open} = 0.03125$ per trial of 5 — fast protection. A low $p$ (e.g., 0.01) with $k=10$ gives $P_{open} = 10^{-20}$ — slow protection, which is desirable for healthy services. This governs the **adaptive threshold tuning** in libraries like Netflix Hystrix / Resilience4j.

---

### 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Property | Traditional Fault Tolerance | Cloud-Native Fault Tolerance |
|---|---|---|
| Redundancy unit | Entire physical server / disk array | Container / pod / microservice instance |
| Failure detection | Hardware watchdog, IPMI, BMC | Liveness/readiness probes, heartbeats, sidecars |
| Recovery time | Hours (manual hardware swap) | Seconds (orchestrator re-scheduling) |
| State management | Shared disk, RAID, SAN | Distributed databases, CRDTs, eventual consistency |
| Scalability | Vertical (bigger box) | Horizontal (more instances) |
| Cost model | CapEx (buy hardware) | OpEx (pay per use) |
| Failure assumption | Rare, catastrophic | Common, partial, transient |
| Primary tool | ECC, dual-PSU, hot spare | Circuit breaker, bulkhead, chaos engineering |
| Testing method | Burn-in, MTBF rating | Chaos Monkey, Litmus, fault injection |
| SLA enforcement | Contractual uptime warranty | SLO burn-rate alerts, error budgets |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The shift from "prevent faults" to "embrace faults" is the philosophical cornerstone of cloud-native resilience. In your 14-mark answers, contrast the two paradigms explicitly — it earns the **cognitive-level "Analyze"** marks per Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cloud-Native Fault Tolerance — Layered Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the five fault-tolerance layers of a modern cloud application, the failure modes handled at each layer, and the engineering controls that deliver resilience.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userA[End User Request] --> cdnA[CDN Edge Layer]
    cdnA --> lbA[L7 Load Balancer with Health Probes]
    lbA --> gwA[API Gateway with Circuit Breaker and Rate Limit]
    gwA --> msA1[Auth Microservice Pod 1]
    gwA --> msA2[Auth Microservice Pod 2]
    gwA --> msA3[Auth Microservice Pod 3]
    msA1 --> busA[Event Bus Kafka]
    msA2 --> busA
    msA3 --> busA
    busA --> orcA[Order Service Stateless Pods]
    orcA --> payA[Payment Service with Bulkhead Pool]
    payA --> dbA1[(Primary Database Sync Replica)]
    dbA1 --> dbA2[(Secondary DB Sync Replica AZ2)]
    dbA1 --> dbA3[(DR Async Replica Cross Region)]
    payA --> cacheA[(Redis Cache Fallback Layer)]
    orcA --> monA[Observability Stack Prometheus Grafana Jaeger]
    monA --> chaosA[Chaos Engineering Controller Litmus]
    chaosA -. fault injection .-> msA1
    chaosA -. fault injection .-> payA
    monA --> alertA[SRE Alert Manager SLO Burn Rate]
    alertA --> oncallA[On Call Engineer Pager]

    classDef infra fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#0d47a1
    classDef svc fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00,color:#e65100
    classDef data fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#4a148c
    classDef obs fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#1b5e20

    class cdnA,lbA infra
    class gwA,msA1,msA2,msA3,busA,orcA,payA,cacheA svc
    class dbA1,dbA2,dbA3 data
    class monA,chaosA,alertA,oncallA obs
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the schematic:** The diagram is read top-to-bottom as the *request path* and the bottom row as the *cross-cutting concern* of observability and chaos. Solid arrows denote request/data flow; dashed arrows denote *fault injection* simulating real failures. The color coding separates the *infrastructure* (blue), *service* (orange), *data* (purple), and *observability* (green) planes — a taxonomy directly aligned with the **KTU 2024 Module 4 learning outcomes**.

---

### 4.2 Circuit Breaker State Machine

The following Mermaid state machine captures the three operational states of a circuit breaker and the events that trigger transitions.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateClosed
    stateClosed: STATE_CLOSED\nNormal operation\nCalls pass through to downstream
    stateOpen: STATE_OPEN\nFailing fast\nCalls rejected immediately
    stateHalf: STATE_HALF_OPEN\nProbing recovery\nLimited test traffic allowed

    stateClosed --> stateOpen: failure count exceeds threshold
    stateOpen --> stateHalf: cooldown timer expires
    stateHalf --> stateClosed: probe calls succeed
    stateHalf --> stateOpen: probe calls fail

    stateClosed: STATE_CLOSED
    stateOpen: STATE_OPEN
    stateHalf: STATE_HALF_OPEN
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering mapping:** The *failure count threshold* is the parameter $k$ from derivation 4.3, and the *cooldown timer* is a tunable period (typically 30s to 60s) that prevents the breaker from re-closing prematurely while the downstream is still recovering.

---

### 4.3 Microservice Failure Recovery Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    reqA[Incoming Request] --> probeA{Is instance healthy?}
    probeA -- yes --> routeA[Route to healthy instance]
    probeA -- no --> remA[Remove from load balancer pool]
    remA --> scaleA[Orchestrator schedules replacement pod]
    scaleA --> warmA[New pod initializes and warms caches]
    warmA --> readyA[Readiness probe returns 200 OK]
    readyA --> routeA
    routeA --> respA[Response returned to client]
    remA -. alert .-> obsA[Observability fires SLO burn alert]
    obsA --> runA[Runbook: rollback or scale out]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU mapping:** This diagram directly supports Module 4 CO4 — *Apply fault tolerance mechanisms in modern cloud and microservice environments.* In a 14-mark answer, you can reproduce this schematic and annotate each arrow with the corresponding KTU syllabus keyword (e.g., *health probe*, *readiness gate*, *runbook automation*).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
*Define fault tolerance in the context of cloud-native microservices. Differentiate it from traditional hardware-level fault tolerance in two points.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Fault tolerance in cloud-native microservices is the ability of a distributed, loosely-coupled application composed of independently deployable services to **continue delivering specified functionality** despite partial failures of individual services, containers, or zones.
- **(1 mark)** *Difference 1:* Traditional fault tolerance uses *hardware redundancy* (dual PSUs, ECC RAM, RAID) within a single physical host; cloud-native uses *software-defined redundancy* (multiple container instances, multi-AZ deployment).
- **(1 mark)** *Difference 2:* Traditional recovery is *manual and slow* (hardware replacement taking hours); cloud-native recovery is *automated* via orchestrators (Kubernetes re-schedules a failed pod in seconds).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
*State the CAP theorem. Explain why a cloud database like Amazon DynamoDB is classified as an AP system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** CAP theorem: In any distributed data store, during a network **P**artition, one can guarantee at most two of **C**onsistency, **A**vailability, and **P**artition tolerance simultaneously.
- **(1 mark)** DynamoDB prioritizes **Availability** and **Partition tolerance** — it continues to accept reads and writes from any region even when inter-region links fail, using consistent hashing and vector clocks for conflict resolution.
- **(1 mark)** It does **not** guarantee strong consistency in partitioned mode — replicas may diverge temporarily and are reconciled via *last-writer-wins* or application-level conflict resolution, hence it is an **AP** system.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
*(a)* Compare and contrast the **active-active** and **active-passive** redundancy strategies for cloud microservices. With a suitable block diagram, explain how each handles a node failure scenario. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A payment microservice is deployed with **3 parallel instances**, each having an availability of **0.995**. Compute:
   (i) The probability that *all* instances fail simultaneously.
   (ii) The overall system availability.
   (iii) The expected annual downtime in minutes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**
- **(2 marks)** *Active-Active:* All instances serve traffic concurrently. On failure, the load balancer health probe fails, traffic is redistributed among the surviving instances. Zero downtime if at least one instance is healthy. Higher cost (all instances bill for compute).
- **(2 marks)** *Active-Passive:* Only the active instance serves traffic; passive instance stays warm/sync-replicated. On failure, the failover controller promotes the passive instance. Brief downtime during promotion (seconds to minutes). Lower compute cost.
- **(3 marks)** *Block diagram (textual):* Draw a **Load Balancer → [Active Node + Passive Node]** topology. For active-active, both nodes connect to LB. For active-passive, the LB connects only to the active node, with a *heartbeat* arrow to the passive node and a *promotion* arrow from the controller.

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

Given: $n = 3$, $a = 0.995$, so $q = 1 - a = 0.005$.

**(i) Probability all three fail simultaneously [2 marks]:**
$$P(\text{all down}) = q^3 = (0.005)^3 = 125 \times 10^{-9} = 1.25 \times 10^{-7}$$

**(ii) Overall system availability [3 marks]:**
$$A_{parallel} = 1 - q^3 = 1 - 1.25 \times 10^{-7} \approx 0.999999875$$
This corresponds to **"five 9s plus"** availability — exceptional resilience.

**(iii) Expected annual downtime [2 marks]:**
$$U = 1 - A = 1.25 \times 10^{-7}$$
$$D_{yr} = U \times 8760 \times 60 \text{ minutes} = 1.25 \times 10^{-7} \times 525600 = 0.0657 \text{ minutes} \approx 3.94 \text{ seconds per year}$$

**Comparison with single instance:**
A single instance at $a = 0.995$ would experience $0.005 \times 525600 = 2628$ minutes/year of downtime (≈ 43.8 hours). The 3-way parallel deployment reduces downtime by a factor of **40,000×**.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
*(a)* With a neat state-transition diagram, explain the **Circuit Breaker pattern**. List the three states and describe the events that trigger transitions. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A distributed system consists of **four mandatory services** in a call chain: Gateway (availability 0.999), Authentication (0.99), Order (0.995), and Database (0.999). Compute:
   (i) Overall chain availability assuming all services in series.
   (ii) If the Order service is now replicated 3-way (each replica 0.995), recompute the chain availability.
   (iii) Comment on the improvement. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**
- **(2 marks)** *Definition:* The circuit breaker is a resilience pattern that wraps a remote call to a downstream service. It monitors failure rates and **short-circuits** calls to a failing dependency, preventing cascading failures and resource exhaustion.
- **(3 marks)** *Three states with transitions:*
  - **CLOSED** → calls pass through. Failure counter increments on each failed call. When count exceeds threshold → transitions to **OPEN**.
  - **OPEN** → all calls fail fast (return cached or default response). After cooldown timer expires → transitions to **HALF-OPEN**.
  - **HALF-OPEN** → a limited probe of calls is sent. If they succeed → transitions to **CLOSED**. If they fail → returns to **OPEN**.
- **(2 marks)** *Real-world example:* Netflix Hystrix, Resilience4j, Polly (.NET) all implement this pattern. The breaker typically includes *sliding-window failure rate*, *minimum call threshold*, and *slow-call duration threshold*.

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**(i) Series availability [2 marks]:**
$$A_{series} = 0.999 \times 0.99 \times 0.995 \times 0.999$$
Computing step-by-step:
$$A_{series} = (0.999 \times 0.999) \times (0.99 \times 0.995)$$
$$= 0.998001 \times 0.98505$$
$$= 0.98308 \approx 0.9831$$
This is only **"two 9s"** availability — unacceptable for a production payment chain.

**(ii) Chain availability with 3-way Order service [3 marks]:**
First, parallel availability of Order:
$$A_{order,3} = 1 - (1-0.995)^3 = 1 - (0.005)^3 = 1 - 1.25 \times 10^{-7} \approx 0.9999999$$
New chain:
$$A_{chain} = 0.999 \times 0.99 \times 0.9999999 \times 0.999$$
$$= 0.998001 \times 0.99 \times 0.9999999$$
$$= 0.988021 \times 0.9999999$$
$$\approx 0.98802$$
This represents **98.8% availability** — almost **"three 9s"**.

**(iii) Comment [2 marks]:**
- The parallel replication of the Order service **alone** improved chain availability from **98.31%** to **98.80%** — a relative improvement of **0.5 percentage points** or roughly **1.5× lower downtime**.
- The dominant remaining bottleneck is the **Authentication** service at 0.99. Applying the same 3-way parallel treatment to Auth would yield $A_{auth,3} = 1 - 10^{-6} = 0.999999$, pushing the chain to **99.9%+**.
- This illustrates the **Pareto principle** in reliability engineering: a small number of bottleneck services dominate the system MTTF.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Annual downtime must always be in *minutes* if the formula uses $525600$ minutes/year, or *hours* if using $8760$ hours/year. Mixing units loses **1 mark**.
> 2. **Independence assumption:** Series and parallel reliability formulas assume *statistically independent* failures. In practice, correlated failures (common-cause: power outage, AZ-wide disruption) violate this. Always state the assumption explicitly to earn full marks.
> 3. **CAP confusion:** Students often write "CAP means pick any two" without noting that *partition tolerance is mandatory in cloud*. The correct phrasing is "CP or AP" — losing this distinction costs **1 mark**.
> 4. **Forgetting MTTR:** Availability = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR). Some students omit MTTR, treating availability as equivalent to reliability. Reliability ≠ Availability — keep them separate.
> 5. **State machine direction:** Circuit breaker transitions are *directional*; drawing arrows in the wrong direction (e.g., OPEN → CLOSED directly without HALF-OPEN) is a **2-mark deduction** per KTU 2024 valuation key.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Definition:** Cloud fault tolerance is *software-defined, distributed resilience* across services, zones, and regions — not a single-box hardware redundancy problem.
- [ ] **Key metrics:** MTBF, MTTR, MTTF, Availability, RPO, RTO, SLA — know the units and inter-relations cold.
- [ ] **The six pillars:** Redundancy, Replication, Load Balancing, Circuit Breaker, Bulkhead, Graceful Degradation.
- [ ] **CAP Theorem:** Real cloud systems choose CP (HBase) or AP (Cassandra) — never "all three" since P is mandatory.
- [ ] **Parallel availability formula:** $A_{parallel} = 1 - (1-a)^n$ — the "five 9s" result of even 2-way replication.
- [ ] **Series availability formula:** $A_{series} = \prod a_i$ — long call chains degrade quickly, motivating circuit breakers.
- [ ] **MTTF for hot-standby cluster:** $\mu_1 + \mu_1^2 / (2\mu_2)$ — useful for design problems.
- [ ] **Circuit Breaker states:** CLOSED → OPEN → HALF-OPEN → CLOSED. Know the trigger conditions for each transition.
- [ ] **Bulkhead pattern:** Isolates thread pools / connection pools to contain blast radius — named after ship compartments.
- [ ] **Chaos engineering:** Proactively inject faults (using tools like Chaos Monkey, Litmus, Gremlin) to validate resilience in production.
- [ ] **Chaos engineering principle (Newman):** The most effective way to prevent failure is to *cause failure* in a controlled, observable way during business hours rather than discovering it at 3 AM.
- [ ] **Kubernetes fault tolerance primitives:** Liveness probes (restart), readiness probes (route traffic), ReplicaSets, PodDisruptionBudgets, anti-affinity rules across AZs.
- [ ] **Statelessness:** A foundational property — services that hold no local state can be killed and replaced instantly without data loss.
- [ ] **Idempotency:** Critical for safe retries — a retried operation should produce the same outcome as a single execution.
- [ ] **Data replication trade-off:** Synchronous = RPO 0, higher latency. Asynchronous = RPO > 0, lower latency. Choose based on RPO requirement.
- [ ] **Observability pillars:** Metrics (Prometheus), Logs (Loki/ELK), Traces (Jaeger). Without observability, fault tolerance is blind.
- [ ] **SRE error budgets:** $1 - \text{SLO} = \text{error budget}$ — when consumed, freeze deployments and focus on reliability work.
- [ ] **KTU cognitive focus:** Module 4 tests the *Apply* and *Analyze* levels — expect derivations, comparisons, and pattern identification, not just definitions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real-World Applications and Case Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT752 - FAULT TOLERANCE IN NETWORKED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Real-World Applications and Case Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Applications and Case Studies** in fault-tolerant networked systems refer to the systematic analysis, design evaluation, and architectural review of large-scale distributed systems that employ **redundancy, replication, error detection, recovery, and consensus protocols** to sustain continuous operation despite hardware faults, software bugs, network partitions, and operator errors.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, this topic synthesizes the fault-tolerance principles studied across Modules 1–3 (e.g., replication, Byzantine agreement, checkpointing, self-stabilization) and examines their **industrial deployment** in safety-critical, mission-critical, and business-critical infrastructures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Examiners frequently map this topic to *Course Outcome CO4* — "Apply fault-tolerance techniques to real-world distributed and networked systems" and test it using **case-study-based analytical questions (14-mark type)** rather than pure definition questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of fault tolerance in a real network the way you think of **seatbelts, airbags, and ABS brakes in a modern car**:

- **Primary path (Driver + Steering):** the main system that keeps the car moving.
- **Airbags / ABS (Backup components):** dormant helpers that activate only when the primary path fails.
- **Crumple zones (Fault containment):** absorb the impact so the *whole* car does not fall apart.
- **Black box (Logging/Diagnostics):** records what went wrong so engineers can fix the *root cause* for the next generation.

In networked systems, the "steering" is a database transaction, the "airbag" is a replica server, the "crumple zone" is a process-isolation sandbox, and the "black box" is an event log streamed to a monitoring service. The case studies in this module show **how engineers actually bolted these safety features onto production systems at Google, Amazon, NASA, Boeing, and global telecom providers.**

## 1.3 Classification of Real-World Fault-Tolerant Systems

| Class | Tolerance Level | Downtime/Yr (Target) | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Safety-Critical** | Must **never** fail catastrophically | 0 s (loss of life risk) | Aircraft FBW, Nuclear reactors, Medical ventilators |
| **Mission-Critical** | Very high | < 5 min | Spacecraft, Defense C2, ATM switching |
| **Business-Critical** | High ("five 9s") | ~ 5.26 min | Banking, E-commerce, Cloud DBs |
| **High-Availability** | High | ~ 52.6 min | Email, Web portals, Streaming |
| **Best-Effort** | Low | Hours OK | Batch analytics, dev environments |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard industry availability metric is the **"Nines" rating** — number of consecutive 9s in the fraction of uptime. **5 nines = 99.999%**, **6 nines = 99.9999%**. These figures are **bolded standards** you must memorize for KTU derivations.

## 1.4 Why Case Studies Matter in KTU Examinations

- They convert *abstract theorems* (Byzantine Generals, FLP impossibility) into *measurable engineering trade-offs* (latency vs. consistency, CAP triangle).
- They reveal that **theoretical impossibility results are routinely bypassed in industry** through:
  1. **Timing assumptions** (Google Spanner uses GPS + atomic clocks to bound clock skew).
  2. **Trusted components** (AWS uses hardware security modules to remove Byzantine behavior).
  3. **Operational discipline** (Netflix's Chaos Monkey accepts failures as the steady state).
- They expose the *human factor*: most real outages are caused by **operator/configuration errors**, not hardware faults.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Fault-Tolerance Patterns Observed in Industry

Across virtually every large-scale real-world deployment, four architectural patterns recur. KTU questions on this topic almost always want you to **identify** and **justify** these patterns in a chosen case study.

### Pattern A — Active Replication with Quorum Reads/Writes
- Every request is sent to **N replicas**; a **read quorum $R$** and a **write quorum $W$** must overlap: $R + W > N$.
- *Used in:* **Amazon DynamoDB**, **Apache Cassandra**, **Riak**.
- *Trade-off:* tunable consistency vs. availability vs. latency.

### Pattern B — Primary-Backup with Synchronous Replication
- One primary node serializes writes; backups apply them in lock-step before ACK.
- *Used in:* **Google Spanner** (with Paxos groups), **MySQL Group Replication**, **Oracle RAC**.
- *Trade-off:* strong consistency but a single-leader bottleneck.

### Pattern C — N-Modular Redundancy (NMR) with Majority Voting
- $N$ (odd, typically 3 or 5) independent channels run in parallel; a **voter** selects the majority output.
- *Used in:* **Boeing 777 Flight Controls** (triplex + dissimilarity), **Space Shuttle GPCs** (4 computers, 3 in sync + 1 standby), **NASA rovers**.
- *Trade-off:* high hardware cost, but tolerates **Byzantine** faults.

### Pattern D — Crash-Only / Restart-from-Checkpoint Services
- Every server is designed to crash and restart cleanly; state recovered from a persistent log or checkpoint.
- *Used in:* **Google File System (GFS) Master**, **MapReduce JobTracker**, **Kubernetes pods**, **Erlang/OTP telecom nodes**.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Pattern

| Pattern | Why it was chosen | How it tolerates faults |
|---|---|---|
| Quorum (Dynamo) | Massive write throughput, partition tolerance mandatory | Survives loss of up to $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor$ replicas (sloppy quorum) |
| Primary-Backup (Spanner) | Global external consistency for financial-grade transactions | Survives primary crash via Paxos leader election |
| NMR (Boeing 777) | Loss-of-life unacceptable; sensors/software can be Byzantine | Masks faulty output through majority vote |
| Crash-Only (Erlang) | Telecom nodes expected to run for years; hot-swap upgrades | Process supervision trees restart failed nodes in milliseconds |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates **every quantitative formula** you may need for numerical questions on this topic. All constants and units are explicitly shown.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR}$ | Dimensionless fraction; $MTTF$ = Mean Time To Failure (h), $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair (h) |
| 2 | **Unavailability (U)** | $U = 1 - A$ | Fraction of downtime |
| 3 | **Downtime per year** | $DT_{yr} = U \times 8760 \text{ h}$ | Hours/yr of expected outage |
| 4 | **"Nines"** | $N_9 = -\log_{10}(1-A)$ | E.g., $A=0.99999 \Rightarrow N_9=5$ |
| 5 | **Series reliability** | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | All components must work |
| 6 | **Parallel reliability** | $R_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1-R_i)$ | At least one must work |
| 7 | **k-out-of-n (cold-standby)** | $R = \sum_{i=k}^{n}\binom{n}{i}R^{i}(1-R)^{n-i}$ | Probability at least $k$ of $n$ work |
| 8 | **Exponential failure law** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, $\lambda = 1/MTTF$ | Constant hazard rate; time $t$ in hours |
| 9 | **System MTTF (parallel, identical)** | $MTTF_{p} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{2} + \dots + \dfrac{1}{n}\right)$ | For $n$ identical components with rate $\lambda$ |
| 10 | **M/M/1 queue availability** | $A = 1 - P_0 \cdot \rho^{N+1}$ | Approx. for repairable single-server queue |
| 11 | **Byzantine threshold** | $n \geq 3f + 1$ | Minimum replicas to tolerate $f$ Byzantine faults |
| 12 | **Paxos quorum** | $Q > n/2$ | Majority quorum |
| 13 | **Dynamo R + W > N** | $R + W > N$ | Overlap guarantee for strong read-after-write |
| 14 | **Markov availability (2-state)** | $A = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$ | $\mu = 1/MTTR$ (repair rate) |
| 15 | **Network partition tolerance** | Pick **2 of 3** in CAP | You cannot have C + A + P simultaneously during a partition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units and constants you must memorize:**
> - Hours per year = **8760 h**
> - Minutes per year = **525,600 min**
> - $1 - 0.99999 = 10^{-5} \Rightarrow$ **5.26 min/yr downtime** (the famous "five 9s" budget)
> - Hazard rate $\lambda$ has units of **failures per hour (fph)**.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Formulas

- **Cloud SLAs:** AWS, Azure, and GCP publish **uptime SLAs** as percentages that map directly to the "Nines" table. A $99.99\%$ SLA means they will refund you for any year in which downtime exceeds $52.6$ minutes.
- **Aerospace certification:** DO-178C and DO-254 require quantitative reliability targets (typically $10^{-9}$ probability of catastrophic failure per flight hour). Designers must demonstrate the chosen NMR + voter architecture achieves this.
- **Telecom 5G core:** The 3GPP spec mandates **"five 9s"** availability for the AMF/SMF control plane — a direct application of Pattern A or Pattern B.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section demonstrates **exhaustive step-by-step derivations** of the analytical results most often tested in KTU 14-mark questions on this topic. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: "Five 9s" Downtime Budget

> **Problem (KTU 2024 Scheme style):** *A cloud database service claims an availability of $99.999\%$. Compute (i) the maximum permitted downtime per year in minutes, and (ii) the unavailability $U$.*

### Step 1 — Identify the given data
- Availability $A = 99.999\% = 0.99999$.
- Hours per year $= 8760$ h.

### Step 2 — Compute unavailability
By definition, $U = 1 - A$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= 1 - 0.99999 \\
  &= 0.00001 \\
  &= 1 \times 10^{-5}.
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to hours per year
The annual downtime in hours is $DT_{yr} = U \times 8760$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
DT_{yr} &= 1 \times 10^{-5} \times 8760 \\
        &= 0.0876 \text{ hours/year}.
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to minutes per year
Since $1$ hour $= 60$ minutes, $DT_{yr}$ in minutes is $DT_{yr} \times 60$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
DT_{min} &= 0.0876 \times 60 \\
         &= 5.256 \text{ minutes/year}.
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Answer
- $U = 1 \times 10^{-5}$
- Maximum permitted downtime $\approx$ **5.26 minutes per year**.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical "five 9s" benchmark** used in almost every KTU/industry specification.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Series–Parallel Availability of a Three-Tier Web Application

> **Problem:** *An e-commerce site uses (1) a load balancer with $A_1 = 0.999$, (2) **two** web servers each with $A_w = 0.99$ running in active-active parallel, and (3) a primary database with $A_p = 0.9999$ backed by a hot-standby replica with $A_s = 0.9999$. Compute the overall system availability assuming the three tiers are in series.*

### Step 1 — Availability of the parallel web tier
Two web servers in active-active parallel succeed if **at least one** works.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{web} &= 1 - (1 - A_w)(1 - A_w) \\
        &= 1 - (1 - 0.99)^2 \\
        &= 1 - (0.01)^2 \\
        &= 1 - 0.0001 \\
        &= 0.9999.
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Availability of the database tier (hot-standby pair)
A hot-standby pair survives the failure of *one* node. Since the standby is hot, the system fails only if **both** are down simultaneously.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{db} &= 1 - (1 - A_p)(1 - A_s) \\
       &= 1 - (1 - 0.9999)(1 - 0.9999) \\
       &= 1 - (0.0001)^2 \\
       &= 1 - 1 \times 10^{-8} \\
       &= 0.99999999.
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Combine all three tiers in series
For components in series, all must work. $A_{sys} = A_1 \times A_{web} \times A_{db}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{sys} &= 0.999 \times 0.9999 \times 0.99999999 \\
        &= 0.9989 \times 0.99999999 \\
        &\approx 0.9989000 \; (\text{to 7 s.f.}) \\
        &= 99.89000\%.
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute annual downtime budget
$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{sys} &= 1 - 0.9989 = 0.0011, \\
DT_{yr} &= 0.0011 \times 8760 \text{ h} \\
        &= 9.636 \text{ h/year}.
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Answer
- $A_{sys} \approx 0.9989$ ($\approx$ **"three nines"**).
- $\approx 9.64$ h/yr of downtime.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner insight:** The *load balancer* ($A_1 = 0.999$) is the **single point of failure** and dominates the budget. Doubling the web servers helped, but the LB still drags the system from "five 9s" down to "three 9s". This is why enterprise architectures use **redundant load balancers in pairs**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: NMR (Triple-Modular Redundant) Voter — Reliability Improvement

> **Problem:** *A flight-control computer uses three identical processors with per-processor reliability $R = 0.95$ over a $10$-hour flight, plus a perfect majority voter. Compute (i) the reliability of the TMR system, (ii) the improvement factor, and (iii) show that adding a **dissimilar** 4th channel improves Byzantine tolerance.*

### Step 1 — Probabilities of each mode of operation
We have $n = 3$ channels, each with $R = 0.95$ and $Q = 1 - R = 0.05$.

The system succeeds if **at least 2 of 3** channels agree. Use the binomial distribution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{2 OK}) &= \binom{3}{2} R^{2} Q^{1} = 3 \times (0.95)^2 \times 0.05 = 3 \times 0.9025 \times 0.05 = 0.135375, \\
P(\text{3 OK}) &= \binom{3}{3} R^{3} Q^{0} = (0.95)^3 = 0.857375.
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — TMR system reliability
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{TMR} &= P(\text{2 OK}) + P(\text{3 OK}) \\
        &= 0.135375 + 0.857375 \\
        &= 0.99275.
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Improvement factor over a single processor
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Improvement} &= \dfrac{R_{TMR}}{R} = \dfrac{0.99275}{0.95} \approx 1.045.
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Show Byzantine tolerance with dissimilar redundancy
Replace one of the three identical channels with a **dissimilar** processor (different ISA, different OS, different compiler) so it cannot share a common software bug. The voter uses **mid-value selection** (e.g., median of three sensor readings) to mask a Byzantine output.

For $n = 3$ channels to tolerate $f = 1$ Byzantine fault, the classic bound $n \geq 3f + 1$ gives $n \geq 4$. Hence the **Boeing 777 actually deploys 4 channels (3 primary + 1 backup)** rather than 3, even though a strict TMR suffices for *crash* faults. The 4th dissimilar channel breaks the symmetry that a single malware payload could otherwise exploit.

### Final Answer
- $R_{TMR} = 0.99275$ (over a 10-h mission).
- Improvement over a single channel $\approx 4.5\%$.
- 4-channel dissimilar NMR is the industry standard for **Byzantine-resilient** safety-critical control.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Markov Two-State Availability for a Repairable Server

> **Problem:** *A single server has failure rate $\lambda = 10^{-4}$ failures/hour and repair rate $\mu = 1$ repair/hour. Compute the steady-state availability.*

### Step 1 — Set up the two-state Markov chain
States: **0 (Up)**, **1 (Down)**.
Transition $0 \xrightarrow{\lambda} 1$, $1 \xrightarrow{\mu} 0$.

### Step 2 — Balance equation
At steady state, the flow into state 1 equals the flow out:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi_0 \cdot \lambda &= \pi_1 \cdot \mu.
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Normalization
$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi_0 + \pi_1 &= 1, \\
\pi_1 &= \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}, \\
\pi_0 &= \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}.
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute numerical values
$$
\begin{aligned}
A = \pi_0 &= \dfrac{1}{1 \times 10^{-4} + 1} = \dfrac{1}{1.0001} \approx 0.999900.
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Cross-check via MTTF/MTTR
$MTTF = 1/\lambda = 10{,}000$ h, $MTTR = 1/\mu = 1$ h.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} = \dfrac{10000}{10000 + 1} = \dfrac{10000}{10001} \approx 0.9999.
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Answer
$A \approx 0.9999$ — approximately **"four nines"** availability.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Theorems to Case Studies

| Theorem / Principle | Theorem Statement | Real-World Mapping | Trade-Off Exposed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CAP Theorem** | In a partition, choose C or A | Dynamo chooses A + P (sloppy quorum); Spanner chooses C + P (TrueTime) | Latency vs. consistency |
| **FLP Impossibility** | No deterministic consensus in async systems with even 1 crash | Paxos/Chubby uses *timing assumptions* (bounded delays) + randomization | Liveness requires non-determinism |
| **Byzantine Generals** | Need $3f+1$ nodes for $f$ Byzantine | Used in blockchain (PBFT in Hyperledger, 4-channel Boeing) | High cost |
| **Two-Army Problem** | Cannot guarantee agreement over unreliable channel | TCP handshakes, three-phase commit timeouts | Extra latency for reliability |
| **Conway's Law** | System mirrors org structure | GFS, BigTable designed by small Google teams | Coupling of social + technical structure |
| **Cut-and-Paste Replication** | $R + W > N$ for strong consistency | DynamoDB, Cassandra | Tunable per request |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** The most common 14-mark question is *"Discuss how Dynamo / Spanner / GFS addresses fault tolerance in distributed storage. Comment on the CAP trade-offs."* Memorize the **Dynamo R+W>N** and **Spanner TrueTime** concepts in one line each.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid diagrams capture the **architectural essence** of the most important case-study systems. They are deliberately abstracted to remain legible while showing the key fault-tolerance elements (replicas, voters, quorums, leaders).

## 4.1 Amazon Dynamo — Sloppy Quorum & Hinted Handoff

```mermaid
graph LR
    client1["Client A"]
    client2["Client B"]

    coord1["Coordinator Node 1"]
    coord2["Coordinator Node 2"]

    ring1["Replica Ring Node X"]
    ring2["Replica Ring Node Y"]
    ring3["Replica Ring Node Z"]

    handoff["Hinted Handoff Store"]

    client1 -- "Put(key)" --> coord1
    client2 -- "Get(key)" --> coord2

    coord1 -- "W=2 of N=3" --> ring1
    coord1 -- "W=2 of N=3" --> ring2
    coord1 -- "W=2 of N=3" --> ring3

    coord2 -- "R=2 of N=3" --> ring1
    coord2 -- "R=2 of N=3" --> ring2

    ring1 -. "temporarily down" .-> handoff
    coord1 -. "redirect to neighbour" .-> handoff
```

> **Reading guide:** Each Put/Get is forwarded to a coordinator, which fans out writes to the first $N$ healthy nodes in a consistent-hashing ring. If a node is down, the write is **stored at the next healthy node** in the ring with a "hint" so it can be replayed later — this is the famous **hinted handoff** mechanism.

## 4.2 Google Spanner — Paxos Groups Across Datacenters

```mermaid
graph TB
    app["Application Client"]

    proxy["Spanner Proxy"]

    pg1["Paxos Group 1 Leader"]
    pg1f1["Paxos Group 1 Follower A"]
    pg1f2["Paxos Group 1 Follower B"]

    pg2["Paxos Group 2 Leader"]
    pg2f1["Paxos Group 2 Follower A"]
    pg2f2["Paxos Group 2 Follower B"]

    ts1["TrueTime API GPS + Atomic Clock"]
    ts2["TrueTime API GPS + Atomic Clock"]

    app -- "SQL Transaction" --> proxy
    proxy -- "2PC Prepare" --> pg1
    proxy -- "2PC Prepare" --> pg2

    pg1 -- "Paxos Log" --> pg1f1
    pg1 -- "Paxos Log" --> pg1f2

    pg2 -- "Paxos Log" --> pg2f1
    pg2 -- "Paxos Log" --> pg2f2

    pg1 -. "external consistency" .-> ts1
    pg2 -. "external consistency" .-> ts2
```

> **Reading guide:** Every tablet (shard) is a **Paxos group**; a 2-phase commit spans groups. The **TrueTime API** exposes bounded clock uncertainty so Spanner can order transactions globally — Google's answer to the CAP theorem's C-vs-P choice.

## 4.3 Boeing 777 Primary Flight Computer — Dissimilar NMR

```mermaid
graph LR
    pilot["Pilot Inputs"]

    s1["Channel LEFT Processor A"]
    s2["Channel CENTER Processor B"]
    s3["Channel RIGHT Processor C"]
    s4["Channel STANDBY Processor D"]

    v1["Voter 1"]
    v2["Voter 2"]
    v3["Voter 3"]
    v4["Voter 4"]

    act["Actuators Aileron Elevator Rudder"]

    pilot --> s1
    pilot --> s2
    pilot --> s3
    pilot --> s4

    s1 --> v1
    s2 --> v2
    s3 --> v3
    s4 --> v4

    v1 --> act
    v2 --> act
    v3 --> act
    v4 --> act

    v1 -. "disagreement" .-> v2
    v2 -. "disagreement" .-> v3
    v3 -. "disagreement" .-> v4
```

> **Reading guide:** Three *primary* channels run in lock-step with a majority voter. A **4th dissimilar standby** is brought online if the voters detect disagreement. Each channel uses **different hardware and software** (Ada on one, C on another) so a single bug cannot affect more than one.

## 4.4 Three-Tier Banking System with Redundancy

```mermaid
graph TB
    user["Customer Browser / Mobile App"]

    lb1["Load Balancer 1"]
    lb2["Load Balancer 2"]

    web1["Web Server 1"]
    web2["Web Server 2"]
    web3["Web Server N"]

    app1["App Server 1"]
    app2["App Server 2"]

    dbp["DB Primary"]
    dbs["DB Synchronous Replica"]
    dbb["DB Asynchronous DR Replica"]

    user --> lb1
    user --> lb2

    lb1 --> web1
    lb1 --> web2
    lb1 --> web3
    lb2 --> web1
    lb2 --> web2
    lb2 --> web3

    web1 --> app1
    web2 --> app2

    app1 --> dbp
    app2 --> dbp

    dbp -- "sync replication" --> dbs
    dbp -- "async replication" --> dbb
```

> **Reading guide:** Each tier is a fault-containment boundary. The **synchronous replica** protects against primary node loss; the **asynchronous DR replica** protects against datacenter loss. This is the canonical 5-min RPO / 1-h RTO design used by retail banks.

## 4.5 Smart-Grid SCADA — Hierarchical Fault Tolerance

```mermaid
graph TB
    cc["Control Center SCADA Master"]

    sub1["Substation RTU 1"]
    sub2["Substation RTU 2"]
    sub3["Substation RTU 3"]

    ied1["IED Feeder 1"]
    ied2["IED Feeder 2"]
    ied3["IED Feeder 3"]

    cc -- "DNP3 / IEC 61850" --> sub1
    cc -- "DNP3 / IEC 61850" --> sub2
    cc -- "DNP3 / IEC 61850" --> sub3

    sub1 --> ied1
    sub1 --> ied2
    sub2 --> ied3
```

> **Reading guide:** A **hot-standby SCADA master** (not shown) and dual-redundant communication links ensure grid control survives substation or link failures. The hierarchical structure provides **fault containment** so a feeder fault does not cascade to the entire substation.
