# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** *Differentiate between fault tolerance, fault resilience, and high availability in the context of a cloud data center.*  **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
- **Fault tolerance:** The system continues to provide *correct* service even when one or more components fail, typically through replication and voting. Example: Boeing 777 triplex flight computer.
- **Fault resilience:** The system *degrades gracefully* — for example, dropping non-critical features — rather than failing outright. Example: Netflix lowering video bitrate when CDN nodes fail.
- **High availability:** The system minimizes *downtime* (e.g., 99.99%) by fast restart and redundancy, but a brief service interruption is acceptable. Example: Gmail's mailbox server.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners expect these three terms in a single sentence each, with a named real-world example. The 3-mark split is usually **1 + 1 + 1**.

---

### **Q2.** *State the CAP theorem and explain how Amazon Dynamo and Google Spanner resolve it differently.*  **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
- **CAP theorem:** A distributed data store facing a network partition can offer at most two of the three properties: **C**onsistency, **A**vailability, **P**artition tolerance.
- **Dynamo** (and DynamoDB): chooses **A + P** using sloppy quorums and last-writer-wins vector clocks. Writes never block, but consistency is eventual.
- **Spanner**: chooses **C + P** using Paxos-replicated groups and the **TrueTime** API, which bounds clock uncertainty via GPS + atomic clocks. Writes can be slow but are externally consistent.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Q.** *(a)* With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of **Amazon's Dynamo** key-value store. Explain how it achieves fault tolerance and supports the CAP trade-off.   **[7 Marks]**
>
> *(b)* A web service has three tiers in series: load balancer (LB) with $A_{LB} = 0.999$, two parallel application servers each with $A_a = 0.98$, and a primary database with a synchronous backup, both with $A_{db} = 0.999$. Compute the overall system availability and the expected annual downtime in minutes.   **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Solution to A(a):** Dynamo Architecture

**Step 1 — Identify the core techniques used by Dynamo.**
Dynamo uses (i) consistent hashing for partitioning, (ii) replication with sloppy quorums ($R + W \leq N$, but typically $R + W > N$ for strong read-after-write), (iii) vector clocks for versioning, (iv) hinted handoff for transient failures, and (v) Merkle-tree anti-entropy for permanent divergence repair.

**Step 2 — Describe partitioning & replication.**
Each key is hashed to a position on a 128-bit ring. The key is stored on the **first $N$ healthy nodes** clockwise from its position (default $N = 3$). This gives **N-fold replication** with no single master.

**Step 3 — Describe quorum read/write.**
- $W$ = number of nodes that must acknowledge a write.
- $R$ = number of nodes that must respond to a read.
- Setting $R + W > N$ guarantees overlap and strong read-after-write; setting $R + W \leq N$ gives faster but eventually-consistent access.

**Step 4 — Describe failure handling.**
- *Hinted handoff:* if a replica is unreachable, the write goes to the **next healthy node** in the ring with a hint; the data is forwarded back when the original node recovers.
- *Anti-entropy (Merkle trees):* background process hashes each partition tree and reconciles divergent replicas without coordinator involvement.

**Step 5 — CAP trade-off.**
Dynamo sacrifices **strong consistency** (C) for **availability and partition tolerance (A + P)** — the AP corner of the CAP triangle. Reads are **always** served, even during partitions, but conflicts are detected via vector clocks and must be resolved by the application (last-writer-wins or merge function).

**Block diagram** — see **Section 4.1** above.

---

#### **Solution to A(b):** Three-Tier Availability Calculation

**Step 1 — Parallel application tier**
Two servers in parallel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{app} &= 1 - (1 - A_a)^2 = 1 - (0.02)^2 = 1 - 0.0004 = 0.9996.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Database tier (primary + sync backup)**
A pair survives the loss of one node:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{db} &= 1 - (1 - A_{db})(1 - A_{db}) = 1 - (0.001)^2 = 1 - 1\times 10^{-6} = 0.999999.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Series combination with the load balancer**
$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{sys} &= A_{LB} \times A_{app} \times A_{db} \\
        &= 0.999 \times 0.9996 \times 0.999999.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply in stages**
$$
\begin{aligned}
0.999 \times 0.9996 &= 0.9986004, \\
0.9986004 \times 0.999999 &= 0.9985994 \;(\text{to 7 s.f.}).
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Annual downtime**
$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{sys} &= 1 - 0.9985994 = 0.0014006, \\
DT_{min} &= 0.0014006 \times 8760 \times 60 \\
         &= 0.0014006 \times 525{,}600 \\
         &= 736.04 \text{ minutes/year}.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $A_{sys} \approx 0.9986$ (≈ "three nines"), and **~736 minutes (~12.3 hours) of downtime per year**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Q.** *(a)* With a block diagram, explain the architecture of **Google's GFS / Spanner** distributed storage and discuss the role of the **TrueTime API** in tolerating clock skew across datacenters.   **[7 Marks]**
>
> *(b)* A spacecraft uses **triple modular redundancy (TMR)** with three identical processors. Each processor has a constant failure rate $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ failures/hour. A perfect majority voter selects the correct output. For a mission duration of $t = 1000$ hours, compute: (i) the reliability of a single processor, (ii) the reliability of the TMR system, and (iii) the **unreliability improvement factor** of TMR over a single processor.   **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Solution to B(a):** GFS / Spanner Architecture & TrueTime

**Step 1 — Describe the Spanner hierarchy.**
Spanner is Google's **globally-distributed, multi-version, externally-consistent** database. Data is sharded into **tablets**; each tablet is managed by a **Paxos group** of replicas spread across zones. A **2-phase commit** coordinates a transaction across multiple Paxos groups.

**Step 2 — Role of the TrueTime API.**
TrueTime is a Google API that returns an **interval** $[t_{earliest}, t_{latest}]$ guaranteed (with very high probability) to contain the true wall-clock time. The interval is computed from **GPS receivers** and **atomic clocks** in every datacenter, with the wider of the two taken as the bound on uncertainty $\varepsilon$.

**Step 3 — Why TrueTime matters.**
To order transactions globally, Spanner assigns a **timestamp** that is the *commit wait time* — the node waits until the TrueTime interval is **definitely in the past** before declaring a transaction visible. This converts a *probabilistic* asynchronous failure into a *deterministic* bounded delay, allowing Spanner to provide **external consistency** (linearizability) despite being globally distributed.

**Step 4 — Fault tolerance contribution.**
- Paxos tolerates replica crashes and partitions.
- TrueTime + commit wait tolerates clock skew that would otherwise break consistency.
- 2PC + Paxos combines **intra-group consensus** with **cross-group atomicity**.

**Block diagram** — see **Section 4.2** above.

---

#### **Solution to B(b):** TMR Reliability for a Spacecraft

**Step 1 — Single-processor reliability**
With constant hazard $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ per hour, the reliability over $t = 1000$ h is

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(5 \times 10^{-5})(1000)} = e^{-0.05}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the series expansion $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x + x^2/2 - x^3/6$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{-0.05} &\approx 1 - 0.05 + \frac{(0.05)^2}{2} - \frac{(0.05)^3}{6} \\
          &= 1 - 0.05 + 0.00125 - 0.0000208 \\
          &\approx 0.951229.
\end{aligned}
$$

So $R \approx 0.9512$.

**Step 2 — TMR system reliability**
TMR succeeds when **at least 2 of 3** processors work. The complementary event is **0 or 1 working**.

Probability that **all three** fail (reliability complement of $R$ is $Q = 1 - R$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{0 OK}) &= Q^3 = (1 - 0.951229)^3 = (0.048771)^3.
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing $Q^3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.048771)^2 &= 0.00237858, \\
(0.048771)^3 &= 0.00237858 \times 0.048771 = 1.1603 \times 10^{-4}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Probability that **exactly 1** of 3 works:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{1 OK}) &= \binom{3}{1} R \cdot Q^2 \\
              &= 3 \times 0.951229 \times 0.00237858 \\
              &= 3 \times 0.00226236 \\
              &= 0.00678708.
\end{aligned}
$$

Probability of **system failure** (≤ 1 working):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{fail}) &= P(\text{0 OK}) + P(\text{1 OK}) \\
              &= 0.00011603 + 0.00678708 \\
              &= 0.00690311.
\end{aligned}
$$

Probability of **system success** (TMR reliability):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{TMR} &= 1 - P(\text{fail}) = 1 - 0.00690311 = 0.99309689.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Improvement factor**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IF} &= \dfrac{R_{TMR}}{R} = \dfrac{0.99309689}{0.951229} \approx 1.0440.
\end{aligned}
$$

In terms of *unreliability*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F = 1 - R &= 0.048771, \\
F_{TMR} = 1 - R_{TMR} &= 0.006903, \\
\text{Reduction} &= \dfrac{F_{TMR}}{F} = \dfrac{0.006903}{0.048771} \approx 0.1416.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- Single-processor reliability $R \approx 0.9512$.
- TMR reliability $R_{TMR} \approx 0.9931$.
- Unreliability is reduced by a factor of $\approx 7\times$ (or $\approx 14\%$ of the original).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss areas in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing $A$, $R$, and uptime** — *availability* is the asymptotic fraction of time the system is *up*; *reliability* is the probability the system survives a *finite interval*. Many students swap them in derivations. **Tip:** always state which one you are computing.
> 2. **Forgetting the "5.26 min/year"** — when the question says "five nines", examiners expect you to derive the downtime *step by step* (8760 h × $10^{-5}$ × 60 min). Showing the intermediate calculation is worth 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing CAP corners** — Dynamo is **AP**, Spanner is **CP**, BigTable+HBase is **CP**, Cassandra defaults to **AP**. Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the block diagram** — for 7-mark case-study sub-parts, the diagram is *worth at least 2 marks*. A hand-drawn (or Mermaid) labeled block diagram with arrows is mandatory.
> 5. **MTTF vs MTBF** — *MTTF* applies to *non-repairable* items; *MTBF* applies to *repairable* ones. Some questions will explicitly say "the server is repairable" — that is your cue to use $\text{MTBF} = MTTF + MTTR$.
> 6. **Byzantine bound** — students often write $n > 3f$ instead of $n \geq 3f + 1$. The "+1" is mandatory in the Lamport–Shostak–Pease theorem statement.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Five 9s = 99.999%** availability ⇒ maximum **5.26 min/yr** downtime. Six nines ⇒ **31.5 s/yr**.
- [ ] **Hours per year = 8760 h**, **minutes per year = 525,600 min**. Always cite these when computing downtime.
- [ ] **Series reliability** $R_s = \prod R_i$; **parallel reliability** $R_p = 1 - \prod (1 - R_i)$.
- [ ] **TMR** tolerates one crash; **NMR with $n = 3f+1$** tolerates $f$ Byzantine faults.
- [ ] **Dynamo** → AP, eventual consistency, sloppy quorum, hinted handoff, vector clocks, $R+W > N$ for strong.
- [ ] **Spanner** → CP, externally consistent, **Paxos** per tablet, **2PC** across tablets, **TrueTime** (GPS + atomic clocks) to bound skew.
- [ ] **GFS** → master + chunk servers, 64 MB chunks, 3× replication, master failover via Chubby lock.
- [ ] **MapReduce** → master reassigns failed tasks; speculative execution for stragglers.
- [ ] **Chubby / ZooKeeper** → distributed coordination via Paxos/Zab, used for leader election and distributed locks.
- [ ] **Primary-backup** with **synchronous replication** = strong consistency, single-leader bottleneck.
- [ ] **Active-active** parallel = higher availability but needs conflict resolution (e.g., Dynamo-style).
- [ ] **Crash-only software** (Erlang OTP, Kubernetes pods) = restart-friendly, supervisable, no partial states.
- [ ] **NASA / Boeing / Airbus** use **dissimilar NMR** (different hardware + software stacks) to defeat common-mode failures.
- [ ] **Operator error** causes the majority of real-world outages — log everything, automate restarts, use chaos engineering (Netflix Chaos Monkey).
- [ ] **CAP is not a static choice** — systems can switch corners (e.g., Spanner waits out partitions to keep C; Dynamo serves stale to keep A).
- [ ] **FLP impossibility** ⇒ real systems use timing assumptions (timeouts, $\Delta$-bounded delays) and randomization.
- [ ] **TrueTime** is the *engineering trick* that lets Spanner circumvent the asynchronous-model FLP barrier for practical deployments.
- [ ] In KTU answers, **always** state (i) the CAP corner, (ii) the failure model assumed (crash vs Byzantine), and (iii) the **availability target** with explicit nines.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Considerations: Impact of Security Breaches on Fault Tolerance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT752 - FAULT TOLERANCE IN NETWORKED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Security Considerations: Impact of Security Breaches on Fault Tolerance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 | SECURITY CONSIDERATIONS: IMPACT OF SECURITY BREACHES ON FAULT TOLERANCE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Syllabus Aligned)**
> *Security Considerations* in the context of fault tolerance refer to the systematic evaluation of how adversarial actions — unauthorized intrusions, malicious faults, denial-of-service events, and information leakage — degrade the **availability**, **reliability**, and **integrity** guarantees of a fault-tolerant networked system. A *security breach* is any successful compromise of a system's security policy, and its *impact on fault tolerance* is measured by the system's residual capacity to deliver correct service despite the breach.

In plain words: **a fault-tolerant system is only useful if it keeps working when something goes wrong — and "something going wrong" includes being attacked**. A security breach is, from the fault-tolerance perspective, just another (intentional) fault. The question is: does the system still limp home safely, or does it collapse?

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The Bank Vault with Three Locks

Imagine a bank vault with **three independent locks** on the door. Even if one lock jams (a hardware fault) or a key breaks (a software bug), the vault stays shut because the other two locks still work. Now imagine a **thief** who:
- Steals one of the keys (a *confidentiality breach*),
- Tampers with a lock so it always opens (an *integrity breach*),
- Piles snow against the door so staff cannot reach it (an *availability breach* / DoS).

The vault's fault tolerance has not technically changed — three locks are still three locks — but its **effective fault tolerance has plummeted**, because the attacker turned two of the three locks into liabilities. Security considerations, in KTU parlance, treat the *attacker* as the worst-case fault source and ask how the system must be designed so that even after the attacker succeeds, the service survives.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core CIA Triad — Recurring Theme in KTU Module 4**
>
> - **C — Confidentiality**: Only authorized entities read data.
> - **I — Integrity**: Data is not altered undetectably.
> - **A — Availability**: Service is reachable when needed.
>
> A fault-tolerant system that fails any of these three pillars is considered to have suffered a **security-induced fault**.

### 1.2 Why This Topic Matters in PECPT752

Fault tolerance historically dealt with *random* and *Byzantine* faults. Module 4 broadens the scope to *malicious* (intelligent, adversarial) faults. KTU expects students to understand that:

1. Security breaches are **intentional faults**, not random ones — they are coordinated, goal-oriented, and adaptive.
2. A perfectly fault-tolerant system (e.g., a Byzantine-tolerant replicated database) becomes worthless if its replicas can be silently corrupted by an attacker.
3. **Intrusion tolerance** and **fault tolerance** converge in modern networked systems; the line between them is almost erased.

### 1.3 Key Metrics (KTU Favourite)

| Metric | Symbol | Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mean Time Between Failures | **MTBF** | Expected time between consecutive system failures | hours |
| Mean Time To Repair | **MTTR** | Expected time to restore service after failure | hours |
| Availability | **A** | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) |
| Confidentiality Loss | **C_loss** | Fraction of sensitive data exposed per breach | % |
| Integrity Violation Rate | **IVR** | Probability an unauthorized write succeeds per request | — |
| MTTF (Fail) | **MTTF** | Mean Time To Failure (non-repairable) | hours |

> [!TIP]
> KTU often asks students to **re-derive availability** under compromised conditions. The trick is that a security breach *increases* the effective failure rate, which **decreases MTBF**, which **decreases availability**. Master the formula $A=\dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR}}$ and the rest follows.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Threat Model — What Can an Attacker Do?

A fault-tolerant system is only as good as its **threat model**, which is a structured list of attacker capabilities. KTU Module 4 typically classifies attackers along three axes:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Threat-Model Triangle**
>
> 1. **Capability** — What the attacker can do (read, write, deny service).
> 2. **Knowledge** — What the attacker knows about the system (full / partial / black-box).
> 3. **Resources** — How much compute, time, money the attacker has.

### 2.2 The Three Classes of Security-Induced Fault

Step-by-step classification (this is KTU high-yield):

**Step 1 — Identify the fault type.**
Every security breach produces a *fault* of one of three kinds:

- **Confidentiality breach** → fault type: *unauthorized disclosure*.
- **Integrity breach** → fault type: *unauthorized modification / masquerade*.
- **Availability breach** → fault type: *denial of service (DoS)*.

**Step 2 — Identify the fault source.**
- *Malicious external attacker* (e.g., a hacker).
- *Malicious insider* (a legitimate user who abuses privileges).
- *Compromised component* (a node taken over by malware and now acting against the system).

**Step 3 — Identify the system response.**
- **Detection** — does the system notice the breach?
- **Containment** — can the breach be isolated?
- **Recovery** — can the system roll back to a known-good state?
- **Continued operation** — does the system keep serving during the breach?

If any of these four steps fails, the system has **lost fault tolerance to a security breach**.

### 2.3 The Security–Fault-Tolerance Intersection

The KTU syllabus expects students to argue that **fault tolerance *without* security is incomplete**, and *vice versa*. The two disciplines intersect as follows:

| Dimension | Pure Fault Tolerance | Pure Security | Integrated (KTU Module 4 View) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Threat | Random hardware / software faults | Intelligent adversaries | Both, simultaneously |
| Goal | Continue correct service | Enforce CIA triad | Continue *correct and trustworthy* service |
| Mechanism | Redundancy, voting, checkpoints | Encryption, authentication, ACLs | Redundancy + cryptographic verification |
| Metric | MTBF, availability | Time-to-compromise, exposure | **Composite resilience metric** |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use \vert and \mid in tables — never the bare pipe character.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use in KTU |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR}}$ | Steady-state availability | Base availability calculation |
| 2 | $A_{\text{comp}} = A \cdot \big(1-P_{\text{breach}}\big)$ | Availability under breach probability $P_{\text{breach}}$ | Showing how a breach degrades $A$ |
| 3 | $\text{MTBF}_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda + \lambda_s}$ | Effective MTBF when security-induced failure rate $\lambda_s$ is added | Combining random + malicious faults |
| 4 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability (no failure by time $t$) | Survival probability of a replica |
| 5 | $P_{\text{breach}} = 1-(1-p)^{N}$ | Probability at least one of $N$ replicas is breached (each with probability $p$) | Replicated system analysis |
| 6 | $A_{\text{vote}} = 1-\sum_{k=f+1}^{N}\binom{N}{k}p^{k}(1-p)^{N-k}$ | Availability of an $N$-modular redundant system with $f$ tolerable faults under per-node breach probability $p$ | TMR / NMR fault analysis |
| 7 | $T_{\text{recovery}} = T_{\text{detect}}+T_{\text{isolate}}+T_{\text{rebuild}}$ | Total recovery time after breach | MTTR-like metric for attacks |
| 8 | $\text{MTTDL} = \dfrac{\text{MTTF}_{\text{array}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N_{\text{disk}}} \binom{N_{\text{disk}}}{j} \text{silent\_corruption}^j}$ | Mean Time To Data Loss (with silent corruption from attackers) | RAID-like analysis |

### 2.5 Why a Security Breach *Worsens* Availability — Intuition

Real-world utility: every telecom operator, every cloud provider, every banking network computes these numbers to **price insurance, justify redundancy investments, and comply with SLAs**. The same formulas let an engineer answer: *"If we add encryption everywhere, do we lose availability?"* — yes, by a tiny amount, but we gain enough fault tolerance to malicious faults that the trade is favourable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Availability Under Security Breach

We derive how a security breach quantitatively reduces the system's availability.

**Given:**
- Baseline availability (no attack): $A_0$.
- Probability that the system is breached at any moment: $P_b$.
- Conditional availability given breach: $A_b$ (typically $A_b \ll A_0$).

**Find:** Effective availability $A_{\text{eff}}$ of the system under ongoing breach pressure.

### Step 1 — Decompose system time
The fraction of time the system is *not* breached is $(1-P_b)$. The fraction of time it *is* breached is $P_b$.

$$A_{\text{eff}} = (1-P_b)\cdot A_0 + P_b \cdot A_b$$

### Step 2 — Substitute typical values
For an unprotected system during a sustained attack, $P_b \to 1$ and $A_b \to 0$ (service is down).

$$\lim_{P_b \to 1} A_{\text{eff}} = A_b$$

### Step 3 — Show that even a *partial* breach reduces availability
If $A_0 = 0.9999$ (four nines) and $P_b = 0.05$ with $A_b = 0.5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{eff}} &= (1-0.05)(0.9999) + (0.05)(0.5) \\
&= 0.95 \times 0.9999 + 0.025 \\
&= 0.949905 + 0.025 \\
&= 0.974905
\end{aligned}
$$

The availability drops from **0.9999 → 0.9749**, i.e. the system goes from "four nines" (≈52 min downtime/year) to "two nines" (≈21 days downtime/year). That is the **numerical impact of a security breach on fault tolerance**.

### Step 4 — Express in terms of MTBF/MTTR
Since $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR}}$, we solve for the new MTBF under attack. Let $A_{\text{comp}}=A \cdot (1-P_b)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{comp}} &= \frac{\text{MTBF}_{\text{comp}}}{\text{MTBF}_{\text{comp}}+\text{MTTR}} \\
\Rightarrow \text{MTBF}_{\text{comp}} &= \frac{A_{\text{comp}} \cdot \text{MTTR}}{1-A_{\text{comp}}} \\
&= \frac{A(1-P_b)\cdot\text{MTTR}}{1-A(1-P_b)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **KTU key result**: under breach, MTBF shrinks roughly by a factor of $(1-P_b)$.

### Step 5 — Combine random and malicious failure rates
If the random hardware failure rate is $\lambda$ and the security-induced "failure" rate is $\lambda_s$:

$$\text{MTBF}_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\lambda + \lambda_s}$$

**Real-world example.** A web server has $\lambda = 1/8760\ \text{h}^{-1}$ (one failure per year). A DDoS attacker creates an effective failure rate of $\lambda_s = 1/24\ \text{h}^{-1}$ (one outage per day):

$$\text{MTBF}_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{8760}+\frac{1}{24}} \approx 23.94\ \text{hours}$$

The system's MTBF dropped from one year to **less than one day**. This is exactly why KTU insists that *fault tolerance without intrusion tolerance is brittle*.

## 3.2 Derivation: Probability a Redundant Cluster is Breached

We have $N$ replicas, each independently breached with probability $p$. The probability that **at least one** replica is compromised is:

$$P_{\text{any}} = 1-(1-p)^{N}$$

**Step 1** — Apply the binomial rule: probability *no* replica is breached is $(1-p)^N$.

**Step 2** — Complement: $P_{\text{any}} = 1-(1-p)^N$.

**Step 3** — Example with $N=3$, $p=0.01$:

$$P_{\text{any}} = 1-(0.99)^{3} = 1-0.970299 = 0.0297$$

A **3× increase in vulnerability** versus a single node. This is the *redundancy paradox*: more replicas ⇒ larger attack surface ⇒ higher probability of at least one breach.

**Step 4** — Mitigation: diversity. If replicas use **heterogeneous OS / software**, the per-replica breach probabilities become *non-identical*, and the formula becomes a union bound:

$$P_{\text{any}} \le \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \quad \text{(Boole's inequality)}$$

Diversity therefore *bounds* but does not eliminate the problem.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Breach Type vs. Fault-Tolerance Property Lost

This matrix is a **KTU examiner favourite** and appears almost verbatim in past papers:

| Breach Type | Confidentiality Loss | Integrity Loss | Availability Loss | Typical Mechanism | Fault-Tolerance Mechanism That Fails |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Eavesdropping / Sniffing** | High | None | None | Wire-tap, packet capture | Encryption, key management |
| **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** | Medium | High | Low | ARP spoofing, BGP hijack | Mutual authentication, certificates |
| **Replay Attack** | Low | High | None | Re-sending old valid messages | Nonces, sequence numbers |
| **Masquerade / Spoofing** | Medium | High | Low | IP/MAC spoofing | Strong authentication, signed tokens |
| **DoS / DDoS** | None | None | **Catastrophic** | Flooding, resource exhaustion | Rate-limiting, redundancy, scrubbing |
| **Malware / Logic Bomb** | High | High | High | Trojan, rootkit | Sandboxing, integrity checksums |
| **Insider Threat** | High | High | Medium | Privileged misuse | Least privilege, audit trails |
| **Zero-Day Exploit** | Variable | High | High | Unknown CVE | Defense-in-depth, micro-segmentation |
| **Ransomware** | High | High | High | File encryption, extortion | Immutable backups, offline copies |
| **Side-Channel Attack** | High | Low | Low | Timing, power analysis | Constant-time algorithms, shielding |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often list only DoS as an "availability breach". They forget that **integrity breaches** (e.g., corrupted replicas in a Byzantine-voting system) can *also* destroy availability, because the system may halt to avoid voting in corrupted data. Mark-loss is real.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Format)

**Problem.** A replicated database has $N=5$ replicas. Each replica has random hardware failure rate $\lambda = 10^{-4}\ \text{h}^{-1}$. An attacker can breach each replica with rate $\lambda_s = 10^{-3}\ \text{h}^{-1}$. Compute:
(a) Effective MTBF of a single replica.
(b) Probability that at least one replica is breached in a 24-hour window.
(c) Effective cluster availability assuming MTTR = 4 hours.

**Solution.**

(a) Effective failure rate of one replica:

$$\lambda_{\text{eff}} = \lambda + \lambda_s = 10^{-4}+10^{-3} = 1.1\times10^{-3}\ \text{h}^{-1}$$

$$\text{MTBF}_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{1.1\times10^{-3}} \approx 909\ \text{hours} \approx 37.9\ \text{days}$$

(b) Probability of breach in $t=24$ h, assuming Poisson process:

$$P_b = 1-e^{-\lambda_s t} = 1-e^{-10^{-3}\times 24} = 1-e^{-0.024} \approx 0.0237$$

For at least one of five replicas (assuming independence):

$$P_{\text{any}} = 1-(1-0.0237)^5 \approx 1-(0.9763)^5 \approx 1-0.8867 = 0.1133$$

So about **11.33%** chance that within 24 hours, the attacker compromises at least one replica.

(c) Cluster availability with $N=5$, can tolerate $f=2$ breaches (quorum of 3) and MTTR = 4 h:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{cluster}} &\approx \frac{\text{MTBF}_{\text{cluster}}}{\text{MTBF}_{\text{cluster}}+\text{MTTR}} \\
\text{MTBF}_{\text{cluster}} &\approx \frac{\text{MTBF}_{\text{eff}}}{f+1} = \frac{909}{3} \approx 303\ \text{h} \\
A_{\text{cluster}} &\approx \frac{303}{303+4} \approx 0.9870
\end{aligned}
$$

Without the attack ($\lambda_s = 0$): $\text{MTBF}_{\text{eff}}=10^{4}$ h, cluster MTBF $\approx 3333$ h, $A \approx 0.9988$.

**Conclusion:** the security breach reduced cluster availability from 0.9988 ("three nines") to 0.9870 ("two nines") — a **~50× increase in expected downtime**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level View: Where Fault Tolerance Meets Security

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef boundary fill:#1f2937,stroke:#9ca3af,color:#f9fafb,stroke-width:1px
    classDef component fill:#0f766e,stroke:#5eead4,color:#ecfeff,stroke-width:1px
    classDef threat fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#fecaca,color:#fff1f2,stroke-width:1px
    classDef outcome fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#bfdbfe,color:#eff6ff,stroke-width:1px

    user[END USER]:::boundary
    firewall[FIREWALL AND IDS]:::component
    auth[AUTHENTICATION LAYER]:::component
    redundancy[REDUNDANT REPLICAS N=5]:::component
    monitor[SECURITY MONITOR SIEM]:::component
    recovery[AUTOMATED RECOVERY]:::component

    attacker[ADVERSARY]:::threat
    dos[DOS ATTACK]:::threat
    mitm[MAN IN THE MIDDLE]:::threat
    malware[MALWARE INSIDER]:::threat

    cia1[CONFIDENTIALITY LOSS]:::outcome
    cia2[INTEGRITY LOSS]:::outcome
    cia3[AVAILABILITY LOSS]:::outcome

    attacker --> dos
    attacker --> mitm
    attacker --> malware

    dos --> firewall
    mitm --> auth
    malware --> redundancy

    firewall --> monitor
    auth --> monitor
    redundancy --> monitor

    monitor --> cia1
    monitor --> cia2
    monitor --> cia3

    cia3 --> recovery
    cia2 --> recovery
    cia1 --> recovery

    firewall --> user
    auth --> user
    redundancy --> user
    recovery --> user
```

**Reading the diagram.**
- The **left side** enumerates the three principal threat actors relevant to fault-tolerant systems (DoS, MITM, malware).
- The **centre column** lists the four pillars that a fault-tolerant networked system must defend: firewall/IDS, authentication, redundancy, monitoring.
- The **right side** shows the three CIA outcomes that constitute the *faults* introduced by the attacker.
- The **bottom loop** shows the recovery path that restores availability, integrity, and confidentiality once a breach is detected.

## 4.2 State Machine — System State Under Attack

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    classDef ok fill:#064e3b,stroke:#34d399,color:#ecfdf5
    classDef warn fill:#78350f,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fffbeb
    classDef bad fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#fca5a5,color:#fff1f2

    [*] --> NORMAL

    NORMAL: NORMAL OPERATION
    NORMAL --> DEGRADED : DETECT ANOMALY
    NORMAL --> BREACHED : CONFIRMED INTRUSION

    DEGRADED: DEGRADED MODE
    DEGRADED --> NORMAL : FALSE POSITIVE
    DEGRADED --> BREACHED : ANOMALY ESCALATES

    BREACHED: BREACHED UNDER MITIGATION
    BREACHED --> QUARANTINED : ISOLATE COMPONENT
    BREACHED --> RECOVERY : TRIGGER RECOVERY

    QUARANTINED: QUARANTINED
    QUARANTINED --> RECOVERY : CLEAN NODE

    RECOVERY: RECOVERY IN PROGRESS
    RECOVERY --> NORMAL : SERVICE RESTORED
    RECOVERY --> BREACHED : RECOVERY FAILS

    BREACHED --> FAILED : TOLERANCE EXCEEDED

    FAILED: SYSTEM FAILED
    FAILED --> [*]
```

**Reading the diagram.**
- The system begins in **NORMAL** and may transition to **DEGRADED** on anomaly detection (an IDS alert) or directly to **BREACHED** on confirmed intrusion.
- **QUARANTINED** represents the "intrusion-containment" state — the classic fault-tolerance technique of isolating a faulty replica now extended to a *maliciously* faulty replica.
- **FAILED** is reached only when the malicious faults exceed the system's tolerance budget (e.g., $>f$ replicas breached in an $N$-modular redundant system).

## 4.3 Defence-in-Depth Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef L1 fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#93c5fd,color:#eff6ff
    classDef L2 fill:#0f766e,stroke:#5eead4,color:#ecfeff
    classDef L3 fill:#854d0e,stroke:#fcd34d,color:#fffbeb
    classDef L4 fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#fecaca,color:#fff1f2
    classDef L5 fill:#312e81,stroke:#a5b4fc,color:#eef2ff
    classDef core fill:#111827,stroke:#9ca3af,color:#f9fafb

    A1[PERIMETER FIREWALL]:::L1 --> A2[NETWORK IDS]:::L1 --> A3[VPN TUNNEL]:::L1
    A3 --> B1[MUTUAL TLS]:::L2 --> B2[OAUTH 2 TOKENS]:::L2 --> B3[ROLE BASED ACCESS]:::L2
    B3 --> C1[TRIP WIRES]:::L3 --> C2[HOST IDS]:::L3 --> C3[FILE INTEGRITY MONITOR]:::L3
    C3 --> D1[ENCRYPTED STORAGE]:::L4 --> D2[SEALED LOGS]:::L4 --> D3[HARDWARE TAMPER DETECT]:::L4
    D3 --> E1[SERVICE MESH MTLS]:::L5 --> E2[ZERO TRUST POLICY ENGINE]:::L5 --> E3[ATTESTATION]:::L5
    E3 --> CORE[FAULT TOLERANT SERVICE CORE]:::core
```

The five concentric rings correspond to the layered defence recommended by the KTU syllabus. Even if attackers penetrate **L1** (perimeter) and **L2** (authentication), the inner rings (L3, L4, L5) still preserve confidentiality, integrity, and availability of the **fault-tolerant service core**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Students frequently lose 2–4 marks by (a) treating *security* and *fault tolerance* as separate disciplines instead of intersecting ones, (b) listing attack types without linking each to the specific fault-tolerance property it compromises, and (c) writing the availability formula without deriving the *security-induced* failure rate. Always link breach → fault type → fault-tolerance mechanism lost → quantitative impact.

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define a security breach in the context of fault tolerance and explain how it differs from a benign fault.**

**Model Answer (≈3 marks):**
A *security breach* is any successful violation of a system's security policy that compromises confidentiality, integrity, or availability (CIA). It is caused by an **intelligent, adaptive adversary** with a goal (e.g., data theft, service disruption). It differs from a benign fault in three ways:

1. **Intentionality** — breaches are deliberate; benign faults are random.
2. **Coordination** — an attacker may target multiple components simultaneously (coordinated attack), whereas random faults are statistically independent.
3. **Adaptivity** — the attacker learns from failed attempts and changes strategy, while benign faults are stationary.

A fault-tolerant system that ignores these differences is brittle; modern systems therefore extend fault-tolerance mechanisms (redundancy, voting, checkpointing) to the *malicious* case under the banner of **intrusion tolerance**.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**List and briefly explain any three security breaches that directly impact the *availability* property of a fault-tolerant system.**

**Model Answer (≈3 marks):**

1. **Denial-of-Service (DoS) / Distributed DoS (DDoS)** — floods the system with traffic, exhausting bandwidth, CPU, or memory, so legitimate users cannot obtain service. Impact: $\text{MTBF} \downarrow \Rightarrow A \downarrow$.
2. **Ransomware** — encrypts files and demands payment, making the system unavailable until recovery (often from offline backups).
3. **Routing attack (e.g., BGP hijack)** — re-routes traffic to a black hole, isolating the system from its users.
4. **Resource-exhaustion bug exploitation** — attacker triggers a memory leak to crash a process.

Each reduces availability; the metric $A=\dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR}}$ quantifies the degradation.

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain, with a neat diagram, how a security breach impacts the **fault-tolerance cycle** (fault → error → failure → detection → containment → recovery → resumed service). Use a specific example, such as a DDoS attack on a replicated web service.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cloud database uses **Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR)** with $N=3$ replicas. Each replica has random hardware failure rate $\lambda = 5\times 10^{-5}\ \text{h}^{-1}$ and is breached by an attacker with rate $\lambda_s = 2\times 10^{-4}\ \text{h}^{-1}$. MTTR per replica is 3 hours.
(i) Compute the **effective MTBF** of one replica.
(ii) Compute the **cluster availability**.
(iii) Comment on the trade-off between redundancy and security.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Cycle of fault tolerance under security breach**

The classical fault-tolerance cycle has six stages. Under a security breach, each stage takes on a *malicious* interpretation:

| Stage | Benign Case | Security-Breach Case |
|---|---|---|
| Fault | Hardware bit-flip | Attacker exploits CVE / injects packet |
| Error | Incorrect memory value | Replica returns tampered data |
| Failure | Service crashes | Quorum is broken → writes rejected |
| Detection | Watchdog timer | IDS / SIEM alert, signature match |
| Containment | Kill faulty replica | Quarantine compromised node |
| Recovery | Restart replica | Re-image from trusted golden copy |

(Insert the state-machine diagram from Section 4.2 here for full marks.)

*Specific example — DDoS on a TMR web service:* Attacker floods the load balancer → one replica is starved of CPU → its responses time out → the load balancer routes around it → the system continues with two replicas (still above the quorum of 2). If the attacker breaches a *second* replica by exploiting an unpatched web vulnerability, the system falls below the quorum and **fails closed**. Detection comes from anomaly-based IDS, containment from automatic IP blacklisting, recovery from spinning up a fresh replica from a verified image.

**(b) Quantitative analysis**

**(i) Effective MTBF of one replica:**

$$\lambda_{\text{eff}} = \lambda + \lambda_s = 5\times 10^{-5} + 2\times 10^{-4} = 2.5\times 10^{-4}\ \text{h}^{-1}$$

$$\text{MTBF}_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{2.5\times 10^{-4}} = 4000\ \text{hours} \approx 166.7\ \text{days}$$

**(ii) Cluster availability:**
For TMR, the cluster fails if **≥2 replicas** are simultaneously down. Approximating each replica's availability independently:

$$A_{\text{replica}} = \frac{\text{MTBF}_{\text{eff}}}{\text{MTBF}_{\text{eff}}+\text{MTTR}} = \frac{4000}{4000+3} \approx 0.99925$$

Probability a single replica is down at a random instant: $U_{\text{replica}} = 1-A_{\text{replica}} \approx 7.5\times 10^{-4}$.

Probability **at least two** replicas are down (binomial):

$$P_{f} = \binom{3}{2}U^{2}A + \binom{3}{3}U^{3}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_f &= 3 \times (7.5\times 10^{-4})^{2} \times 0.99925 + (7.5\times 10^{-4})^{3} \\
&\approx 3 \times 5.625\times 10^{-7} + 4.22\times 10^{-10} \\
&\approx 1.687\times 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$A_{\text{cluster}} = 1 - P_f \approx 1 - 1.687\times 10^{-6} = 0.999998313$$

**(iii) Trade-off comment (≈2 marks):**
Redundancy improves fault tolerance against *random* faults but expands the *attack surface*. The attacker's per-replica breach probability is non-zero, so the probability that at least one replica is breached scales as $1-(1-p)^N$. Designers must therefore combine **redundancy with diversity** (different OS, different implementations) and **defence-in-depth** (perimeter, authentication, monitoring) to keep $\lambda_s$ small.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **CIA triad** as it applies to fault tolerance. For each pillar, give one attack example and one corresponding fault-tolerance countermeasure.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A replicated storage system has $N=4$ replicas. The per-replica probability of being silently corrupted by an attacker in any given hour is $p=0.001$.
(i) What is the probability that **no** replica is corrupted in 24 hours?
(ii) What is the probability that **at least one** is corrupted in 24 hours?
(iii) If the system uses a **majority quorum** ($f=1$), compute the probability that **at least two** replicas are corrupted in 24 hours, and discuss what fault-tolerance action the system must take.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CIA triad and fault tolerance** (≈7 marks — write as a structured essay; key bullets):

- **Confidentiality:** Attack example — eavesdropping on unencrypted inter-replica traffic. Countermeasure — TLS / mutual authentication between replicas; the fault-tolerance mechanism is "trusted communication channel".
- **Integrity:** Attack example — replay attack where an attacker resends a stale-but-valid write. Countermeasure — nonces and sequence numbers; the fault-tolerance mechanism is "self-checking state machine".
- **Availability:** Attack example — DDoS flooding. Countermeasure — redundancy plus traffic scrubbing; the fault-tolerance mechanism is "graceful degradation".

Conclude by stating that *all three pillars* must hold for the system to be considered fault-tolerant in the modern sense; otherwise we have **false fault tolerance** — the system appears to tolerate faults but is silently leaking data, accepting tampered writes, or going offline under load.

**(b) Quantitative analysis:**

**(i)** Probability a single replica is *not* corrupted in 1 hour: $1-p = 0.999$.
In 24 hours (assuming independence): $(1-p)^{24} = (0.999)^{24}$.

$$(0.999)^{24} = e^{24 \ln 0.999} \approx e^{-0.02401} \approx 0.97627$$

So probability *no* replica is corrupted in 24 h: **$P_0 \approx 0.97627$**.

**(ii)** Probability *at least one* replica is corrupted in 24 h:

$$P_{\geq 1} = 1 - (1-p)^{24} = 1 - 0.97627 = 0.02373$$

Across **all four replicas** (each with the same hourly corruption probability), the per-replica 24-hour corruption probability is $1-(0.999)^{24} \approx 0.02373$. Probability *at least one of four* is corrupted:

$$P_{\text{any}} = 1-(1-0.02373)^{4} = 1-(0.97627)^{4}$$

$$
(0.97627)^4 \approx 0.90703 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{\text{any}} \approx 0.09297
$$

So ≈ **9.3%** chance that within 24 hours, an attacker silently corrupts at least one replica.

**(iii)** Probability at least two of four replicas corrupted in 24 h, given each independent with probability $q = 0.02373$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\geq 2} &= 1 - P_0 - P_1 \\
&= 1 - (1-q)^{4} - 4q(1-q)^{3} \\
&= 1 - 0.90703 - 4(0.02373)(0.97627)^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
(0.97627)^3 \approx 0.92906 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 4(0.02373)(0.92906) \approx 0.08819
$$

$$P_{\geq 2} \approx 1 - 0.90703 - 0.08819 = 0.00478$$

So ≈ **0.48%** daily, or roughly **once every 209 days**, the majority quorum is broken. The system must then:

1. **Detect** the corruption (via signed checksums, Merkle-tree comparisons, or Byzantine-fault detectors).
2. **Quarantine** the corrupted replicas.
3. **Recover** them from a known-good, cryptographically verified snapshot.
4. **Re-establish quorum** with the remaining good replicas, possibly by spinning up new ones from a trusted image.

This is the operational meaning of **intrusion tolerance**: the system continues to serve correctly even while under attack, *provided* the attacker has not yet exceeded the redundancy budget.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4**

- [x] A **security breach** is an *intentional, adversarial fault*; it is not a random fault and must be modelled separately.
- [x] The **CIA triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) is the standard way to classify the impact of a breach.
- [x] **Availability formula:** $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR}}$. A breach *increases* the failure rate, *decreases* MTBF, *decreases* $A$.
- [x] **Combined failure rate:** $\lambda_{\text{eff}} = \lambda + \lambda_s$; effective MTBF is $\text{MTBF}_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda+\lambda_s}$.
- [x] **Redundancy paradox:** more replicas ⇒ larger attack surface ⇒ $P_{\text{any}} = 1-(1-p)^N$ grows with $N$.
- [x] **Diversity** (heterogeneous replicas) bounds but does not eliminate the risk; use the union bound $P_{\text{any}} \le \sum p_i$.
- [x] **TMR** tolerates $f=1$ replica failure; an $N$-modular system tolerates $f=\lfloor(N-1)/2\rfloor$ Byzantine failures *if* messages are signed.
- [x] **Intrusion tolerance = fault tolerance + security**; the modern interpretation of fault tolerance explicitly includes malicious faults.
- [x] **Five layers of defence:** perimeter, authentication, host integrity, encrypted storage, zero-trust service mesh.
- [x] **Quantitative exam tip:** always show the formula, the substitution, and the final numerical answer with units.
- [x] **Common exam trap:** students list attack types without linking them to the *specific* CIA pillar and *specific* fault-tolerance mechanism that fails. Avoid this by using the matrix in §3.3.
- [x] **Recovery time:** $T_{\text{recovery}} = T_{\text{detect}}+T_{\text{isolate}}+T_{\text{rebuild}}$; reducing any term directly raises availability.
- [x] **Silent corruption / ransomware / DoS** are the three breach types most frequently appearing in KTU questions; prepare numericals for each.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Designing Fault-Tolerant Systems with Basic Security Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT752 - FAULT TOLERANCE IN NETWORKED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Designing Fault-Tolerant Systems with Basic Security Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fault-tolerant system design** is the disciplined engineering process of architecting computational and networked systems such that they continue to deliver specified services at an acceptable level of performance despite the presence of hardware faults, software defects, operator errors, or malicious attacks. When **security considerations** are folded into this design, the system must simultaneously defend against *adversarial* faults (intentional, strategic, intelligent) in addition to the classical *non-adversarial* faults (random, independent, Byzantine in nature).

A **secure fault-tolerant system** is formally defined as a system $S$ that satisfies both properties:

$$
\forall f \in \mathcal{F} : \text{Service}(S, f) \geq S_{\min} \quad \wedge \quad \forall a \in \mathcal{A} : \text{Confidentiality}(S,a) = \text{preserved}
$$

where $\mathcal{F}$ is the anticipated fault set, $\mathcal{A}$ is the anticipated adversary set, and $S_{\min}$ is the minimum acceptable service level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic unifies two traditionally separate domains — **dependability** (reliability, availability, maintainability, safety, integrity) and **security** (confidentiality, authenticity, non-repudiation). A modern KTU examiner expects the answer to demonstrate awareness that *a security breach IS a fault*, and *a fault CAN BE induced by an attack*.

### 1.2 The Four Pillars of Dependability + Security

| Pillar | Definition | Security Counterpart |
|---|---|---|
| **Reliability** | Continuity of correct service | Attack-resilience |
| **Availability** | Readiness for correct service | DoS resistance |
| **Integrity** | Absence of improper state alterations | Authenticity + non-repudiation |
| **Confidentiality** | Absence of unauthorized disclosure | Encryption, access control |
| **Maintainability** | Ability to undergo modifications | Patchability, secure updates |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Fortified Bank Vault

Imagine designing a bank's vault. A **fault-tolerant** vault must remain functional even if:
- A lock jams (hardware fault)
- An employee makes a calculation error (software fault)
- A power outage occurs (operational fault)
- An earthquake hits (environmental fault)

A **secure fault-tolerant** vault must additionally remain functional when:
- A thief *deliberately* jams the lock (induced fault)
- A hacker tampers with the access logs (integrity attack)
- A burglar cuts the power (DoS attack on availability)
- An insider colludes (Byzantine fault)

The bank engineer must therefore design a system where **redundancy** (multiple locks, multiple guards) coexists with **security controls** (biometric checks, encrypted logs, intrusion alarms). Neither aspect can be retrofitted later without massive cost — this is the central design philosophy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Security and fault tolerance are *synergistic*, not competing. A system with weak authentication cannot detect when a "fault" is really an attacker in disguise. Without fault tolerance, security controls (which are themselves software) become single points of failure.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Design

- **MTTF** (Mean Time To Failure) — **MTTR** (Mean Time To Repair) — **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures)
- **Availability** $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$
- **Reliability** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ for exponential distribution
- **MTTFdl** (Mean Time To Data Loss) for storage systems
- **Intrusion Recovery Time (IRT)** — security-specific
- **Mean Time to Security Compromise (MTSC)** — security-specific
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Integrated Design Process

A fault-tolerant secure system is designed in **six iterative phases**:

1. **Threat & Fault Modelling** — enumerate what can go wrong (random faults, malicious attacks, environmental hazards, operator errors)
2. **System Requirements Specification** — define acceptable service degradation under each threat
3. **Architecture Selection** — choose redundancy topology (passive/active/hybrid) AND security topology (defense-in-depth)
4. **Mechanism Integration** — implement fault-tolerance primitives (replication, voting, checkpointing) *coupled with* security primitives (authentication, encryption, audit)
5. **Validation & Testing** — fault injection + penetration testing
6. **Deployment & Maintenance** — patches, updates, monitoring

### 2.2 Core Design Principles

| # | Principle | Fault-Tolerance Aspect | Security Aspect |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Redundancy** | Multiple components perform same function | Diverse replicas prevent common-mode attack |
| 2 | **Diversity** | Different implementations prevent common-mode bugs | Different OS/software prevents single exploit |
| 3 | **Modularity** | Isolated failures | Compartmentalized security breaches |
| 4 | **Layering (Defense in Depth)** | Multiple fault barriers | Multiple security controls |
| 5 | **Least Privilege** | Limited blast radius of faults | Limited damage from compromised components |
| 6 | **Fail-Safe Defaults** | Safe state on failure | Deny-by-default access control |
| 7 | **Complete Mediation** | Every request checked | Every access authenticated + authorized |
| 8 | **Open Design** | Algorithms public for review | No security through obscurity |
| 9 | **Psychological Acceptability** | Easy correct use | Users don't bypass security for convenience |
| 10 | **Economy of Mechanism** | Simple = fewer bugs | Simple = fewer vulnerabilities |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Availability of a system | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | MTBF, MTTR | Dimensionless ratio (0 to 1) or percentage |
| Steady-state availability | $A_{\infty} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate, $\mu$ = repair rate | $1/\lambda$ = MTTF, $1/\mu$ = MTTR |
| Series system reliability | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | $R_i$ = reliability of component $i$ | All components must work |
| Parallel (k-of-n) reliability | $R_p = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}$ | $R$, $n$ replicas, $k$ needed | Used for active replication |
| Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) | $R_{\text{TMR}} = R^3 + 3R^2(1-R)$ | $R$ = single module reliability | Majority voting |
| Byzantine quorum size | $n \geq 3f+1$ | $f$ = number of Byzantine faults tolerated | Replicas needed |
| MTTF of redundant pair (active) | $\text{MTTF}_{\text{active}} = \frac{3}{2\lambda}$ | $\lambda$ per component | For dual modular redundancy with comparator |
| Cryptographic hash strength | $H_{\text{strength}} = \frac{k}{2}$ bits | $k$ = hash output bits | Against preimage attack |
| Symmetric key size vs RSA | $k_{\text{sym}} \approx 14 \times k_{\text{RSA}} / 10$ | — | NIST equivalence (bits) |
| Mean Time to Security Failure | $\text{MTSF} = \int_0^{\infty} R_{\text{sec}}(t)\, dt$ | $R_{\text{sec}}(t)$ = probability of no compromise | Security analogue of MTTF |
| Attack success probability | $P_{\text{attack}} = 1 - (1-p_a)^{n}$ | $p_a$ = single-replica attack prob., $n$ = replicas | Without diversity |
| Attack success w/ diversity | $P_{\text{attack,diverse}} = p_a^n$ | $n$ diverse replicas | Independence assumed |
| Replication factor for FT-security | $n \geq 2f_{\text{fault}} + f_{\text{sec}} + 1$ | $f_{\text{fault}}$ + $f_{\text{sec}}$ = faults + compromised | Integrated requirement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The most common KTU question on this topic asks for the **minimum number of replicas** needed. For classical Byzantine fault tolerance: $n \geq 3f+1$. For *secure* Byzantine fault tolerance (where the adversary can also compromise up to $f$ replicas via cryptographic break): still $n \geq 3f+1$, *provided* the cryptography holds. If the adversary can break the crypto, the bound changes to $n \geq 3f+1$ where $f$ counts *both* faulty AND compromised replicas.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These design principles are applied in:
- **Aerospace avionics** (fly-by-wire systems need both fault tolerance against sensor failure AND security against cyber-attack on navigation)
- **Financial transaction networks** (SWIFT, blockchain consensus)
- **Smart grids** (SCADA systems need fault tolerance for component aging AND security against nation-state attacks)
- **Cloud data centers** (replicated storage across geographically distributed sites with encrypted communications)
- **Medical device networks** (pacemakers, infusion pumps — safety-critical + life-safety)

### 2.5 The Security-Fault Tolerance Trade-off Matrix

| Approach | Fault Tolerance | Security | Cost | Latency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Passive replication + plaintext | High | Low | Low | Low |
| Active replication + encryption | High | High | Medium | Medium |
| N-version programming + signed I/O | High | High | High | Medium |
| Hardware TMR + TPM | Very High | High | Very High | Low |
| Software-implemented FT + crypto | Medium | Medium | Low | Medium |
| Blockchain consensus | High | Very High | Very High | High |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Replica Count for Combined Fault + Security Tolerance

**Problem Setup.** We have a replicated service. Up to $f$ replicas can fail in a Byzantine manner (arbitrary behaviour), and *separately*, up to $c$ replicas can be compromised by an adversary who controls their cryptographic keys. The adversary is computationally bounded so cannot break signatures during one round. We need to find the minimum $n$ such that correct clients can still receive correct responses.

**Step 1 — Classical Byzantine Bound.**

The classical PBFT result states that with $f$ Byzantine faults, we need:
$$
n \geq 3f + 1
$$

The reasoning: the client must receive $2f+1$ matching replies out of $n$ total, and at most $f$ of those replies can be wrong.

**Step 2 — Adding Compromise.**

If the adversary also compromises $c$ replicas, these compromised replicas behave *as if* they are Byzantine (they follow the adversary's protocol). Therefore the effective Byzantine count becomes:
$$
f_{\text{effective}} = f + c
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Bound.**

$$
n \geq 3(f + c) + 1 = 3f + 3c + 1
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the Voting Quorum.**

We need the client to receive $2f_{\text{effective}} + 1 = 2(f+c) + 1 = 2f + 2c + 1$ matching replies. Out of $n = 3f + 3c + 1$ total replicas, the maximum faulty is $f+c$, so:
$$
\text{correct replies} = n - (f+c) = 3f + 3c + 1 - f - c = 2f + 2c + 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

This matches the quorum exactly, so the derivation is consistent.

**Step 5 — Special Case.**

If $f = 0$ (no natural faults, only security threats) and $c = 1$ (one compromised replica tolerated):
$$
n \geq 3(0) + 3(1) + 1 = 4
$$

This means even a *purely security* threat requires a 4-replica system for BFT-style consensus.

### 3.2 Derivation: Availability of a Dual-Node Hot Standby with Secure Heartbeat

**System Model.** Primary node $P$ serves requests. Backup $B$ takes over if $P$ fails. Heartbeats are exchanged over an *authenticated* channel to prevent an attacker from falsely signalling $P$ as "down" (which would cause a needless failover — a security-induced fault).

**Step 1 — Define Component Parameters.**

Let:
- $\lambda$ = failure rate of each node
- $\mu$ = repair rate
- $p_{\text{spoof}}$ = probability an attacker successfully spoofs a heartbeat per check

**Step 2 — Availability of a Single Node.**

A single node has steady-state availability:
$$
A_1 = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}
$$

**Step 3 — Series System: Node + Secure Heartbeat Channel.**

For the heartbeat channel to be useful, it must be authenticated. Let $A_h$ be the availability of the secure channel. The combined "node + heartbeat" subsystem has availability:
$$
A_{\text{subsys}} = A_1 \cdot A_h
$$

**Step 4 — Hot Standby Parallel System.**

The two-node hot-standby has availability (assuming independent failures):
$$
A_{\text{HS}} = 1 - (1 - A_{\text{subsys,P}})(1 - A_{\text{subsys,B}})
$$

**Step 5 — Expand and Simplify.**

$$
A_{\text{HS}} = 1 - (1 - A_1 A_h)^2
$$

$$
A_{\text{HS}} = 1 - \left(1 - 2 A_1 A_h + A_1^2 A_h^2\right)
$$

$$
A_{\text{HS}} = 2 A_1 A_h - A_1^2 A_h^2
$$

**Step 6 — Account for Spoofing-Induced False Failover.**

The attacker can force a false failover with probability $p_{\text{spoof}}$ per heartbeat interval. If the failover itself is correct (backup takes over), availability is not lost, but *integrity* is briefly violated. The unavailability due to spoofing is approximately:
$$
U_{\text{spoof}} \approx p_{\text{spoof}} \cdot T_{\text{failover}}
$$

where $T_{\text{failover}}$ is the time the system is in an inconsistent state during the transition.

**Step 7 — Net Availability.**

$$
A_{\text{net}} \approx A_{\text{HS}} - U_{\text{spoof}}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Reliability of TMR with One Diverse Channel

**Step 1 — Single Module Reliability.**

Let $R$ be the reliability of one module over time interval $t$.

**Step 2 — TMR with Voter.**

The TMR system is correct if:
- All 3 modules work: probability $R^3$
- Exactly 2 modules work (voter picks the majority): probability $3 R^2 (1-R)$

$$
R_{\text{TMR}} = R^3 + 3R^2(1-R)
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$
R_{\text{TMR}} = R^3 + 3R^2 - 3R^3 = 3R^2 - 2R^3
$$

**Step 4 — Add Diversity for Security.**

If we make the 3 replicas *diverse* (different OS, different compiler), the probability of a common-mode attack is reduced. The probability of a successful attack on all 3 (assuming independent attack vectors) is:
$$
P_{\text{attack,all}} = p_a^3
$$

**Step 5 — Combined Reliability + Security.**

$$
R_{\text{TMR,secure}} = (1 - p_a)^3 \cdot R^3 + 3(1-p_a)^2 p_a \cdot R^2 (1-R) + 3(1-p_a) p_a^2 \cdot R(1-R)^2 + p_a^3 \cdot (1-R)^3 + \text{voting terms}
$$

For the *secure* case where diversity eliminates common-mode attacks, only independent faults remain, and the system is correct if the majority is both *operational* and *uncompromised*. The simplified form is:
$$
R_{\text{TMR,secure}} = (R(1-p_a))^3 + 3(R(1-p_a))^2 (1 - R(1-p_a))
$$

Let $R' = R(1-p_a)$ be the *effective* reliability. Then:
$$
R_{\text{TMR,secure}} = 3(R')^2 - 2(R')^3
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Diversity *multiplicatively* reduces the effective failure rate. If $R = 0.99$ and $p_a = 0.10$, then $R' = 0.891$, and $R_{\text{TMR,secure}} = 3(0.794) - 2(0.708) = 2.382 - 1.416 = 0.966$. The loss of reliability from security exposure is significant — the designer must weigh this against the cost of diversity.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Design | Reliability (t=1y) | Availability | Security Level | Cost Factor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Single node | $e^{-\lambda}$ | $\frac{\mu}{\lambda+\mu}$ | None | 1× |
| Dual modular (passive) | $2e^{-\lambda} - e^{-2\lambda}$ | High | Low | 2.2× |
| TMR (identical) | $3e^{-2\lambda} - 2e^{-3\lambda}$ | Very high | Low | 3.5× |
| TMR (diverse) | $3e^{-2\lambda'}-2e^{-3\lambda'}$ | Very high | Medium-high | 6× |
| TMR (diverse + crypto) | $3e^{-2\lambda'}-2e^{-3\lambda'}$ | Very high | High | 7× |
| PBFT (4 nodes) | By BFT formula | Very high | High | 8× |
| Blockchain (1000 nodes) | Effectively 1.0 | 1.0 | Very high | 1000× |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Integrated Fault-Tolerant + Secure System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Client1[Client Application]
    Client2[Client Application]
    Client3[Client Application]

    subgraph SecEdge["SECURE EDGE LAYER"]
        FW1[Firewall]
        IDS1[Intrusion Detection System]
        Auth1[Authentication Gateway]
    end

    subgraph FTLayer1["FAULT-TOLERANT REPLICATION LAYER"]

        RepB[Replica B - Diverse OS]

        Voter[Majority Voter with Audit Log]
    end

    subgraph SecCore["SECURE INTERNAL NETWORK"]
        VPN1[Mutual TLS Channel]
        KMG1[Key Management Service HSM]
        SEC1[Secure Enclave TPM]
    end

    subgraph Storage1["REPLICATED SECURE STORAGE"]
        Store1[Encrypted Store 1]
        Store2[Encrypted Store 2]
        Store3[Encrypted Store 3]
    end

    subgraph MonLayer1["MONITORING and RECOVERY"]
        Mon1[Fault + Intrusion Monitor]
        Rec1[Automated Recovery Module]
    end

    Client1 --> FW1
    Client2 --> FW1
    Client3 --> FW1
    FW1 --> IDS1
    IDS1 --> Auth1
    Auth1 --> RepA
    Auth1 --> RepB
    Auth1 --> RepC

    RepA <--> VPN1
    RepB <--> VPN1
    RepC <--> VPN1

    Voter --> Store2
    Voter --> Store3

    SEC1 -.protects.-> Voter
    Mon1 -.observes.-> RepA
    Mon1 -.observes.-> RepB
    Mon1 -.observes.-> RepC

    Rec1 -.triggers.-> RepB
    Rec1 -.triggers.-> RepC
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow for Selecting Fault + Security Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([System Requirements]) --> Q1{Critical Safety?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Fault Type?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Data Sensitivity?}

    Q2 -->|Byzantine| Path1[Use BFT Replication]
    Q2 -->|Crash only| Path2[Use Active or Passive Replication]
    Q2 -->|Omission| Path3[Use Quorum Replication]

    Q3 -->|High| Q4{Adversary Capability?}
    Q3 -->|Low| Path4[Simple Redundancy Sufficient]

    Q4 -->|Strong Crypto Possible| Path5[PBFT with Diverse HW]
    Q4 -->|Crypto Limited| Path6[Air-Gapped Redundancy]

    Path1 --> End([Integrated Design Output])

    Path3 --> End
    Path4 --> End
    Path5 --> End

    Path7 --> End
```

### 4.3 Threat × Defense Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Threats["THREAT SOURCES"]
        THW[Hardware Fault]
        TSW[Software Bug]

    end

        DHW[Hardware Redundancy TMR]

### 4.4 Lifecycle of a Secure Fault-Tolerant Request

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Client Signs Request with Private Key] --> S2[TLS Handshake with Mutual Auth]
    S2 --> S3[Load Balancer Routes to Healthy Replica]
    S3 --> S4[Replica Verifies Signature]
    S4 --> S5{Valid?}
    S5 -->|No| S6[Reject and Log to SIEM]
    S5 -->|Yes| S7[Execute Transaction Locally]
    S7 --> S8[Sign Response with Replica Key]
    S8 --> S9[Broadcast to Other Replicas for Voting]
    S9 --> S10{Voting Agreement}
    S10 -->|Majority Agree| S11[Commit to All Replicated Stores]
    S10 -->|No Agreement| S12[Trigger Recovery Protocol]
    S11 --> S13[Return Signed Response to Client]
    S12 --> S14[Isolate Suspected Faulty Replica]
    S14 --> S15[Replace with Verified Good Replica]
