# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - PECPT752 Model Question]
**Q: Define the term "Fault-Tolerant System Project Presentation" as evaluated under the KTU 2024 B.Tech scheme. List any FOUR mandatory components that such a presentation must contain.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *Fault-Tolerant System Project Presentation* is a structured 15–20 minute oral demonstration by a B.Tech student team that exhibits the design, implementation, and evaluation of a networked system engineered to maintain operation despite component failures.

The four mandatory components are:
1. **Fault Model Definition** (what can break) — 1 mark
2. **Redundancy/Detection/Recovery Architecture** (how we tolerate) — 1 mark
3. **Experimental Evaluation** (metrics, graphs, demo) — 0.5 marks
4. **Q&A Defense with Justified Design Trade-offs** — 0.5 marks

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - PECPT752 Model Question]
**Q: Differentiate between "Internal Review" and "External Review" in the KTU 2024 project presentation lifecycle. Mention at least THREE points of difference.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Internal Review | External Review |
|---|---|---|
| **Evaluator** | Project guide + 1–2 faculty members | Panel of 3–5 external/internal experts |
| **Purpose** | Formative feedback, gate to proceed | Summative grading, marks finalization |
| **Frequency** | 2–3 times during the semester | Once at the end of semester |
| **Marks Weightage** | Typically 30–40% of internal | Typically 60–70% of total |

(Each row: 0.5 marks; maximum 3 rows counted: 3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Module carries Part-B questions with internal choice. Two alternatives are provided below.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - PECPT752 Model Paper Style]

**Q: Your team has designed a fault-tolerant distributed key-value store using the Raft consensus algorithm. Prepare and justify the following elements of your project presentation:**
**(a)** Define the fault model, architectural components, and the specific Raft mechanisms that ensure fault tolerance. **[7 marks]**
**(b)** Derive the MTTR of your system given the following experimental data: 5 failures occurred, with recovery times of 2.3 s, 1.8 s, 2.1 s, 1.9 s, and 2.4 s respectively. Also calculate the availability of your system if the MTBF is 720 hours. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **Fault Model (1.5 marks):** The system tolerates **crash faults** (also called fail-stop faults) — the most common Raft assumption. It does not tolerate **Byzantine faults**. Node crashes are detected via **heartbeat timeouts** (typically 150–300 ms).

2. **Architectural Components (2.5 marks):**
   - **Leader** node handles all client write requests.
   - **Followers** replicate the leader's log entries.
   - **Candidate** state during election.
   - **Persistent log** stored on stable storage for crash recovery.
   - **Term counter** ensures globally consistent election ordering.

3. **Raft Mechanisms Ensuring Fault Tolerance (3 marks):**
   - **Leader Election:** Randomized timeout (150–300 ms) prevents split votes.
   - **Log Replication:** Leader appends entries, replicates to majority (quorum of $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 1$).
   - **Safety:** Only logs replicated to a majority are committed; committed entries are never lost.
   - **Liveness guarantee** if majority of nodes are up and timings are bounded.
   - Tolerates up to $\lfloor (n-1)/2 \rfloor$ crash failures (e.g., in a 5-node cluster, 2 simultaneous failures).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute MTTR using the arithmetic mean formula (2.5 marks):**

$$ MTTR = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{repair,i}}{n} $$

$$ MTTR = \dfrac{2.3 + 1.8 + 2.1 + 1.9 + 2.4}{5} \text{ seconds} $$

Summing the numerator:

$$ 2.3 + 1.8 = 4.1 $$

$$ 4.1 + 2.1 = 6.2 $$

$$ 6.2 + 1.9 = 8.1 $$

$$ 8.1 + 2.4 = 10.5 \text{ seconds} $$

Therefore:

$$ MTTR = \dfrac{10.5}{5} = 2.1 \text{ seconds} $$

**Step 2 — Compute MTBF in compatible units (1.5 marks):**

Convert MTBF from hours to seconds:

$$ MTBF = 720 \times 3600 = 2{,}592{,}000 \text{ seconds} $$

**Step 3 — Compute Availability (2 marks):**

$$ A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} $$

$$ A = \dfrac{2{,}592{,}000}{2{,}592{,}000 + 2.1} $$

$$ A = \dfrac{2{,}592{,}000}{2{,}592{,}002.1} $$

$$ A \approx 0.99999919 $$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):**

This corresponds to approximately **"six nines" of availability**, meaning roughly 31.5 seconds of allowable downtime per year. This is **comparable to industry-grade cloud storage systems** such as Amazon S3 (99.999999999% — eleven nines).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Internal Choice]

**Q: As part of your PECPT752 project review, you must present a Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) based fault-tolerant controller for a real-time embedded system.**
**(a)** Draw the block-level architecture of your TMR system and explain the role of the majority voter. List TWO scenarios where TMR outperforms simplex (single-module) systems and ONE scenario where it underperforms. **[7 marks]**
**(b)** Given individual module reliability $R_1 = 0.85$, calculate the system reliability of: (i) a single module, (ii) a 3-parallel system without a voter, and (iii) a TMR system with voter. Comment on the results. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **Block Diagram (Verbal description, 2 marks):** Three identical modules A, B, C receive the same input $X$. Their outputs $Y_A$, $Y_B$, $Y_C$ feed into a **majority voter** that produces the system output $Y_{sys}$. Disagreement detectors flag faulty modules for isolation.

2. **Role of Majority Voter (2 marks):** The voter implements a **median or bit-by-bit majority function**. It continuously masks the output of any single faulty module by selecting the agreement of the other two. This provides **fault masking without system interruption** — a key property for safety-critical real-time control.

3. **TMR Outperforms Simplex — Scenario 1 (1 mark):** In an **avionics flight controller**, transient radiation-induced bit flips (SEUs) must be masked in real time. A simplex system would output the corrupted value, while TMR masks it via voting.

4. **TMR Outperforms Simplex — Scenario 2 (1 mark):** In a **spacecraft command processor**, a permanent hardware failure in one module must not interrupt operation. TMR continues with 2-of-3 voting while a backup is activated.

5. **TMR Underperforms — Scenario (1 mark):** In **mass-market IoT devices** where cost and power are critical, TMR triples hardware cost and energy consumption. A simplex system with periodic self-test is more economical.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

Given $R_1 = 0.85$, compute the three reliability values.

**(i) Single Module (1.5 marks):**

$$ R_{single} = R_1 = 0.85 $$

**(ii) 3-Parallel Without Voter (2.5 marks):**

$$ R_{parallel,3} = 1 - (1 - R_1)^3 $$

$$ R_{parallel,3} = 1 - (1 - 0.85)^3 $$

$$ R_{parallel,3} = 1 - (0.15)^3 $$

$$ R_{parallel,3} = 1 - 0.003375 $$

$$ R_{parallel,3} = 0.996625 $$

**(iii) TMR With Voter (2.5 marks):**

$$ R_{TMR} = R_1^3 + 3 R_1^2 (1 - R_1) $$

Substitute:

$$ R_{TMR} = (0.85)^3 + 3 (0.85)^2 (1 - 0.85) $$

Compute intermediate values:

$$ (0.85)^3 = 0.614125 $$

$$ (0.85)^2 = 0.7225 $$

$$ 1 - 0.85 = 0.15 $$

$$ 3 \times 0.7225 \times 0.15 = 0.325125 $$

Sum:

$$ R_{TMR} = 0.614125 + 0.325125 = 0.93925 $$

**(iv) Comment on Results (0.5 mark):**

The reliability ranking is:
- $R_{parallel,3} = 0.9966$ (best) — but requires **all three** modules to be switched.
- $R_{TMR} = 0.9393$ (middle) — provides **fault masking** with continuous operation.
- $R_{single} = 0.85$ (worst) — no fault tolerance.

TMR's trade-off: lower reliability than full 3-parallel, but **continuous fault masking** is critical for real-time systems.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PECPT752 Project Presentations (Costing up to 5–7 marks per student):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to state the Fault Model explicitly (–1 to –2 marks):** Examiners ALWAYS ask "What is your fault model?" Students who omit it on the slide and cannot answer verbally lose easy marks.
> 
> 2. **Confusing Reliability with Availability (–2 marks):** Reliability $R(t)$ is a probability function of time; Availability $A$ is a long-run fraction. Never use the terms interchangeably.
> 
> 3. **No baseline comparison (–1 to –2 marks):** Every experiment MUST compare the fault-tolerant system against a non-fault-tolerant baseline. Presenting only one graph is insufficient.
> 
> 4. **Skipping the MTTR calculation (–1 mark):** Even if not asked, including an MTTR bar chart in the results slide demonstrates quantitative depth.
> 
> 5. **Citing Wikipedia or unrefereed sources in literature survey (–1 mark):** Always use IEEE, ACM, Springer, or Elsevier journal/conference papers from 2018 onwards.
> 
> 6. **Running over the time limit (–1 mark):** Going beyond 20 minutes triggers automatic marks deduction. **Practice with a stopwatch.**
> 
> 7. **Not preparing for "Why not use X?" questions (–2 marks):** Always have a one-sentence "non-choices" slide in the appendix justifying why alternative architectures (Paxos vs. Raft, BFT vs. CFT, TMR vs. Dual Modular) were rejected.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU Project Presentation & Review (PECPT752 Module 4):**
> 
> - **Definition:** Project presentation = structured 15–20 min oral defense of a self-designed fault-tolerant networked system mapped to CO4 and CO5 of PECPT752.
> - **The 8-Slide Mandate:** Title → Problem → Literature → Architecture → Fault Model → Implementation → Results → Conclusion. Memorize this order.
> - **Three Pillars of Every Project:** (1) What can break? (2) How do we detect/recover? (3) How do we prove it works?
> - **Five-Phase Lifecycle:** Conceptualization → Design → Implementation → Evaluation → Communication/Defense.
> - **Headline Metric:** Availability $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$ is the single most important number on your results slide.
> - **MTTR Formula:** Arithmetic mean of recovery times across all fault-injection trials. Always report in seconds, not abstract units.
> - **TMR Reliability:** $R_{TMR} = R_1^3 + 3 R_1^2 (1 - R_1)$ — voted 2-out-of-3 majority. TMR provides **fault masking**, not just redundancy.
> - **Byzantine Threshold:** $n \geq 3f + 1$ for $f$ Byzantine failures. Cite Lamport-Shostak-Pease 1982 in literature survey.
> - **Raft Crash Fault Limit:** Tolerates up to $\lfloor (n-1)/2 \rfloor$ simultaneous node crashes in an $n$-node cluster.
> - **The 9's Reference:** 3 nines = 8.76 hr/yr downtime; 4 nines = 52.6 min/yr; 5 nines = 5.26 min/yr. Memorize the table.
> - **Color-Code Your Architecture:** Blue (input) + Yellow (detection) + Green (data) + Pink (recovery) — consistent across all slides.
> - **Always include a Baseline:** Compare fault-tolerant vs. non-fault-tolerant; otherwise, the examiner will deduct 1–2 marks.
> - **Time Discipline:** Practice with a stopwatch. Going beyond 20 minutes = automatic penalty.
> - **Q&A Preparation:** Prepare one-sentence answers for "Why not Paxos / Raft / BFT / TMR / Dual Modular?" questions.
> - **Documentation:** Use IEEE-style references; avoid Wikipedia; use 10–15 quality papers from 2018 onwards.
> - **Re-submission Rule:** KTU allows **only one** re-attempt of external review before grade penalty.
> - **Rubric Awareness:** Show the evaluation rubric as an appendix slide — earns 2–3 bonus marks in "presentation skills."
> - **Bloom's Cognitive Levels:** Module 4 sits at **Evaluate** level — your defense must demonstrate critical analysis, not just description.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Review of Key Concepts and Course Wrap-Up
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT752 - FAULT TOLERANCE IN NETWORKED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Review of Key Concepts and Course Wrap-Up # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Fault Tolerance? (Formal KTU Definition) > [!NOTE] > **Fault Tolerance** is the ability of a system (hardware, software, or networked) to continue performing its intended functions in the presence of **faults**, **errors**, or **failures**, possibly at a reduced level of service, rather than failing completely. In the context of **Networked Systems**, fault tolerance is the discipline of designing distributed protocols, replicated services, and communication layers that **mask**, **detect**, **isolate**, and **recover** from faults arising from: - Hardware crashes (node/link failures) - Software bugs - Network partitions - Malicious/Byzantine behavior - Timing violations ## 1.2 The Dependability Umbrella Fault tolerance is a **sub-discipline of Dependability**. Per the **Laprie Dependability Model (IEEE/IFIP)**: | Attribute | Meaning | Key Metric | |---|---|---| | **Reliability** | Continues correct service without interruption | $R(t)$ | | **Availability** | Ready for correct service at a given instant | $A(t)$ | | **Safety** | No catastrophic consequence to users/users | Failure rate of unsafe state | | **Integrity** | No improper system state alterations | Mean Time To Data Corruption | | **Maintainability** | Ability to undergo repairs/modifications | MTTR | | **Confidentiality** | No unauthorized information disclosure | Probability of breach | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Reliability and Availability are the two most-asked attributes in KTU 2024 ESE questions. Memorize the difference: **Reliability = over an interval**; **Availability = at an instant**. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Redundancy Principle Think of a **commercial airliner**: - A single-engine failure does NOT crash the plane — it flies on the other engine (**Spatial Redundancy**). - Pilots can re-attempt an approach if the first fails (**Temporal / Retry Redundancy**). - Three independent flight computers vote on every command to avoid single-point logic errors (**Information / N-Version Redundancy**). - The black box records state for post-crash forensic analysis (**Checkpointing + Rollback Recovery**). The same four principles — **Spatial, Temporal, Information, Recovery** — form the backbone of every fault tolerance strategy taught across **Modules 1–4** of PECPT752. ## 1.4 Fault-Error-Failure Chain (Cause-Effect Hierarchy) The **fundamental chain** of dependability: $$\text{Fault} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Error} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Failure}$$ - **Fault (Defect):** The *root cause* — a wrong assumption, a broken wire, a bug. - **Error:** The *manifestation* — incorrect state in the system (e.g., a flipped bit). - **Failure:** The *visible deviation* from required service to the user. > [!TIP] > **Prevention** stops faults from entering. **Removal** reduces faults already present. **Tolerance** provides service despite faults. **Forecasting** estimates fault consequences. — These are the **four means of dependability**. ## 1.5 Course-Scope Map (What We Covered in Modules 1–4) | Module | Core Focus | |---|---| | **Module 1** | Dependability attributes, fault models, fault-error-failure chain, terminologies | | **Module 2** | Hardware fault tolerance, error detection/correction codes, modular & TMR redundancy | | **Module 3** | Software fault tolerance, N-version programming, recovery blocks, exception handling, defensive programming | | **Module 4** | Networked/distributed fault tolerance, replication, consensus, checkpointing, transaction recovery | This final review ties all four modules together through a unified, exam-ready lens. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Reliability and Availability — The Heart of the Course ### 2.1.1 Reliability Function $R(t)$ For a system with **constant failure rate** $\lambda$ (the exponential failure model, valid for the "useful life" of most digital systems per the **Bathtub Curve**): $$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$ Where: - $\lambda$ = constant failure rate (failures per hour) - $t$ = mission time - $1/\lambda$ = **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures) ### 2.1.2 Availability $A(t)$ The **steady-state (long-run) availability** of a repairable system is: $$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$ Where **MTTR** = Mean Time To Repair, and $\mu = 1/\text{MTTR}$ is the repair rate. ### 2.1.3 MTTF, MTBF, MTTR — Don't Confuse Them - **MTTF** (Mean Time To Failure) — for **non-repairable** systems - **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures) — for **repairable** systems, $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ - **MTTR** (Mean Time To Repair) — repair time only - **MTTD** (Mean Time To Detect) — for fault detection - **MTTI** (Mean Time To Isolate) — for diagnostics ## 2.2 Series and Parallel Reliability (Network Reliability Composition) ### 2.2.1 Series Configuration (Chain) For $n$ **independent** components in series (any single failure kills the system): $$R_{\text{series}}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t} = e^{-\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i\right) t}$$ System failure rate: $\lambda_{\text{sys}} = \sum \lambda_i$ ### 2.2.2 Parallel Configuration (Redundancy) For $n$ components in parallel, the system fails **only if all** fail: $$R_{\text{parallel}}(t) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left(1 - R_i(t)\right)$$ For 2 identical components with failure rate $\lambda$: $$R_{\text{parallel}}(t) = 2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Trap:** A **parallel system has higher reliability** but **lower availability during single repair**. A **series system has higher availability during normal operation** but no failure tolerance. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Reliability (constant $\lambda$) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential model | | 2 | Failure rate from data | $\lambda = \text{failures / total test hours}$ | Constant rate assumption | | 3 | Steady-state availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Repairable system | | 4 | Series reliability | $R_s = \prod R_i$ | Any failure = system failure | | 5 | Parallel reliability | $R_p = 1 - \prod (1 - R_i)$ | All must fail | | 6 | TMR reliability (voter perfect) | $R_{\text{TMR}} = 3R^2 - 2R^3$ | Triple Modular Redundancy | | 7 | Hamming single-error correction | $2^r \geq m + r + 1$ | $m$ data bits, $r$ check bits | | 8 | MTTF of duplex w/ repair | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{3}{2\lambda}$ | Standby redundancy | | 9 | N-version programming agreement | $P(\text{agree}) = \sum_{k=\lceil N/2 \rceil}^{N} \binom{N}{k} p^k (1-p)^{N-k}$ | $p$ = per-version prob. correct | | 10 | Quorum size (read + write) | $N \geq R + W$ | $R, W$ read/write quorums | | 11 | Byzantine fault tolerance bound | $N \geq 3f + 1$ | $f$ = Byzantine nodes | | 12 | Checkpoint overhead bound | $T_{\text{chk}} \leq \delta$ | Optimal period (Chandy–Lamport related) | > [!WARNING] > **TMR Trap:** $R_{\text{TMR}} = 3R^2 - 2R^3$ is derived assuming the **voter is perfect and never fails**. In KTU papers, if the question says "unreliable voter," you must apply $R_{\text{TMR}} = R_v \cdot (3R^2 - 2R^3)$ where $R_v$ is voter reliability. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Telecommunications:** Five-nines availability ($A = 0.99999$) is the **telecom-grade SLA target**, corresponding to ~5.26 minutes of downtime/year. - **Avionics (DO-178C):** Fault tolerance levels A–E mandate specific redundancy and voting strategies. - **Cloud / Datacenters:** Google Spanner, Amazon DynamoDB, and Apache ZooKeeper all apply **quorum replication + Paxos-style consensus** to survive datacenter failures. - **Blockchain (Bitcoin / Ethereum):** Asynchronous **Byzantine Fault Tolerance (aBFT)** underpins proof-of-stake finality. - **Aerospace Satellites:** SEU (Single-Event Upset) tolerance via **Hamming + Reed-Solomon** codes with hardware scrubbing. ## 2.5 Layered Defence: Defence in Depth A fault-tolerant networked system stacks **four complementary layers**: 1. **Fault Avoidance** — rigorous design, formal methods, testing. 2. **Fault Detection** — watchdogs, parity, CRCs, heartbeat protocols. 3. **Fault Isolation** — partitioning, sandboxing, fencing (STONITH). 4. **Fault Recovery** — retries, replication, checkpoint-restart, consensus-based reconfiguration. > [!TIP] > **Kerala Board Examiner Tip:** Any 14-mark "design a fault-tolerant X" question expects the student to name **at least 2 layers** explicitly and justify the choice. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Reliability of Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) **Setup:** Three identical modules of reliability $R$ feed a majority voter. The system is correct if **at least 2 of 3** modules produce correct outputs (voter is assumed perfect). **Step 1 — Identify the favourable cases.** - Exactly 2 modules correct: $\binom{3}{2} = 3$ ways - Exactly 3 modules correct: $\binom{3}{3} = 1$ way **Step 2 — Write the probability of each case.** - $P(\text{exactly 2 correct}) = \binom{3}{2} R^2 (1-R)^1 = 3R^2(1-R)$ - $P(\text{exactly 3 correct}) = \binom{3}{3} R^3 (1-R)^0 = R^3$ **Step 3 — Add the disjoint cases.** $$R_{\text{TMR}} = 3R^2(1-R) + R^3$$ **Step 4 — Simplify algebraically.** $$R_{\text{TMR}} = 3R^2 - 3R^3 + R^3 = 3R^2 - 2R^3$$ **Step 5 — Sanity check at boundary values.** - If $R = 1$ (perfect modules): $R_{\text{TMR}} = 3 - 2 = 1$ ✓ - If $R = 0$ (useless modules): $R_{\text{TMR}} = 0$ ✓ - If $R = 0.9$: $R_{\text{TMR}} = 3(0.81) - 2(0.729) = 2.43 - 1.458 = 0.972$ ✓ (better than 0.9) ## 3.2 Derivation: Steady-State Availability of a Repairable Duplex **Setup:** A duplex system has two identical modules with failure rate $\lambda$ each. **One is active**, the other is **hot-standby**. There is a single repair crew with repair rate $\mu$. Repairs are to the **whole duplex** when both modules are down (or to the failed module when only one is down — we model the simpler case here). **Step 1 — Define the Markov states.** - State 0: Both modules working - State 1: One module working, one failed (degraded but functional) - State 2: Both modules failed (system down) **Step 2 — Steady-state balance equations.** $$P_0 \cdot \lambda = P_1 \cdot \mu$$ $$P_1 \cdot (\lambda + \mu) = P_0 \cdot \lambda + P_2 \cdot 0$$ (We assume both fail before repair completes for the simplified model.) **Step 3 — Apply normalization.** $$P_0 + P_1 + P_2 = 1$$ **Step 4 — Solve the system.** Solving (for the simplified case where repair only returns State 1 to State 0): $$P_0 = \frac{\mu^2}{\lambda^2 + \lambda\mu + \mu^2}, \quad P_1 = \frac{\lambda\mu}{\lambda^2 + \lambda\mu + \mu^2}, \quad P_2 = \frac{\lambda^2}{\lambda^2 + \lambda\mu + \mu^2}$$ **Step 5 — Compute availability.** The system is up in states 0 and 1: $$A = P_0 + P_1 = 1 - P_2 = 1 - \frac{\lambda^2}{\lambda^2 + \lambda\mu + \mu^2} = \frac{\mu^2 + \lambda\mu}{\lambda^2 + \lambda\mu + \mu^2}$$ **Step 6 — Compare with simplex.** $$A_{\text{simplex}} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$ For typical values $\lambda = 0.001$/h, $\mu = 1$/h: - $A_{\text{simplex}} = 0.999$ - $A_{\text{duplex}} = \frac{1 + 0.001}{0.000001 + 0.001 + 1} \approx 0.999999$ — **three extra nines**. ## 3.3 Derivation: Hamming Code Parity-Bit Requirement **Problem:** Find the number of parity bits $r$ for a Hamming code on $m$ data bits. **Step 1 — A single-error-correcting code must place each of the $m + r$ bit positions into a unique syndrome (non-zero parity-check outcome).** **Step 2 — There are $2^r - 1$ non-zero $r$-bit syndromes available (the all-zero syndrome means no error).** **Step 3 — We need at least $m + r$ distinguishable syndromes (one per bit position).** $$2^r - 1 \geq m + r \;\Longleftrightarrow\; 2^r \geq m + r + 1$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $r$ by trial** (since $r$ is integer): | $m$ | Min $r$ | Total bits $n = m+r$ | |---|---|---| | 4 | 3 | 7 | | 8 | 4 | 12 | | 16 | 5 | 21 | | 32 | 5 | 37 | | 64 | 7 | 71 | | 128 | 8 | 136 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Direct derivations of $2^r \geq m + r + 1$ appear in **Part A 3-mark questions** and as the opening of a Part B 14-mark question. Memorize the inequality. ## 3.4 Derivation: N-Version Programming Agreement Probability **Setup:** $N$ independently developed software versions. Each version produces the correct answer with probability $p$. The system outputs the **majority answer** (or fails if no majority). **Step 1 — Probability of exactly $k$ correct versions out of $N$:** $$P(k) = \binom{N}{k} p^k (1-p)^{N-k}$$ **Step 2 — A correct majority means $k \geq \lceil N/2 \rceil$ (and the majority of *incorrect* versions coincidentally agree on the wrong answer — assumed negligible for different bugs).** **Step 3 — Sum over the majority range:** $$P(\text{agree}) = \sum_{k=\lceil N/2 \rceil}^{N} \binom{N}{k} p^k (1-p)^{N-k}$$ **Step 4 — Worked example with $N=3$, $p=0.9$:** $$P(\text{agree}) = \binom{3}{2}(0.9)^2(0.1)^1 + \binom{3}{3}(0.9)^3(0.1)^0 = 3(0.081)(0.1) + 0.729 = 0.0243 + 0.729 = 0.7533$$ ## 3.5 Derivation: Quorum Intersection Property (Distributed Replication) **Setup:** Read quorum size $R$, write quorum size $W$, total replicas $N$. For correctness (a read always sees the latest write), any read quorum and write quorum must **intersect** in at least one node. **Step 1 — The intersection is non-empty iff:** $$R + W > N$$ **Step 2 — For the common "majority" choice $R = W = \lceil (N+1)/2 \rceil$:** $$R + W = 2 \lceil (N+1)/2 \rceil \geq N + 1 > N \;\checkmark$$ **Step 3 — Example: Dynamo-style "sloppy quorum"** with $N=3, R=2, W=2$ satisfies $R+W=4 > 3$ ✓. If a coordinator can contact only 2 of 3 replicas during a network partition, reads and writes still overlap. ## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Most-Asked in KTU) | Strategy | Tolerates | Cost | Latency | Used In | |---|---|---|---|---| | **TMR (Hardware)** | 1 faulty module | 3× hardware | Voter delay | Aerospace, safety-critical | | **N-Version Programming** | 1 buggy version | $N$× dev cost | Voter/comparator | Avionics (DO-178C) | | **Recovery Blocks** | 1 failed try | Time | Sequential | Control software | | **Active Replication** | Node crashes | $N$× messages | Low (multicast) | Google Spanner | | **Passive (Primary-Backup)** | Primary crash | 1× + backup | Failover delay | PostgreSQL streaming repl. | | **Paxos / Raft** | Minority crash | $2F+1$ nodes | Rounds | ZooKeeper, etcd | | **PBFT** | $f$ Byzantine | $3f+1$ nodes | Multi-round | Hyperledger Fabric | | **Checkpointing** | Process crash | Disk I/O | Save overhead | MPI, long-running sims | | **ECC (Hamming)** | 1-bit SEU | Parity bits | None | Memory, storage | ## 3.7 Quick Numerical: Compare 3 Strategies at $p = 0.9$ | Strategy | Correctness probability | |---|---| | Single module | 0.900 | | TMR (voter perfect) | 0.972 | | N-Version ($N=3$) | 0.753 (assumes independent failures) | | TMR + 1 spare (NMR) | 0.972 + standby benefit | > [!WARNING] > The N-Version result (0.753) is **lower** than TMR because the problem's $p=0.9$ assumption makes even the *correct* versions fail with probability 0.1. In practice, $p \gg 0.9$ for well-tested software, and N-Version gives substantial diversity benefit. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchical Layered Architecture of Fault-Tolerant Networked Systems ```mermaid flowchart TB AppLayer["APPLICATION LAYER
Replication Aware APIs"] Middleware["MIDDLEWARE LAYER
Replication Manager and Naming Service"] Consensus["CONSENSUS LAYER
Paxos or Raft or PBFT Protocol"] Checkpoint["CHECKPOINT RECOVERY LAYER
Chandy Lamport Algorithm and Logs"] CommLayer["COMMUNICATION LAYER
Group Communication and Heartbeats"] Detection["DETECTION LAYER
Watchdog Timer and CRC Checks"] HWLayer["HARDWARE LAYER
TMR and ECC Memory and ECC Bus"] AppLayer --> Middleware Middleware --> Consensus Middleware --> Checkpoint Consensus --> CommLayer Checkpoint --> CommLayer CommLayer --> Detection Detection --> HWLayer ``` ## 4.2 Fault-Error-Failure Propagation Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A["FAULT
Root Cause: Bug, Hardware Defect,
Human Error, Environmental Event"] --> B["ACTIVATION
Fault Becomes Active
When Triggered"] B --> C["ERROR
Incorrect System State
Data Corruption"] C --> D{"DAMAGE PROPAGATION
Is Error Contained?"} D -- Yes --> E["ERROR CONTAINED
Fault Masked"] D -- No --> F["FAILURE
Service Violation to User"] E --> G["SERVICE DELIVERED
Correctly"] F --> H["FAULT TOLERANCE TRIGGERED
Recovery Initiated"] H --> I["SERVICE RESTORED
State Rollback and Replica Promotion"] ``` ## 4.3 Decision Tree — Which Fault Tolerance Strategy? ```mermaid flowchart TB Start["START: Choose Fault Tolerance Strategy"] Q1{"Fault Type?"} Crash["Crash Fault Only"] Omission["Omission or Performance"] Byzantine["Byzantine Arbitrary"] Q2{"Repair Possible?"} Repairable["Yes: Repairable System"] NonRepairable["No: One Shot System"] Q3{"Latency Budget?"} LowLatency["Low Latency OK"] HighLatency["High Latency Tolerable"] Q4{"Cost Budget?"} LowCost["Low Cost Allowed"] HighCost["High Cost Allowed"] Start --> Q1 Q1 --> Crash Q1 --> Omission Q1 --> Byzantine Crash --> Q2 Omission --> Q2 Q2 --> Repairable Q2 --> NonRepairable Byzantine --> Q3 Q3 --> LowLatency Q3 --> HighLatency Q4 --> LowCost Q4 --> HighCost Repairable --> A["Primary Backup + Checkpointing
Paxos Raft"] NonRepairable --> B["Triple Modular Redundancy
or NMR with Voting"] LowLatency --> C["Active Replication
State Machine Approach"] HighLatency --> D["Passive Replication
Quorum Based Storage"] LowCost --> E["Software Fault Tolerance
Recovery Blocks"] HighCost --> F["Hardware Fault Tolerance
TMR and ECC Memory"] ``` ## 4.4 Replicated State Machine — Consensus Round (Paxos/Raft View) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant C as Client participant L as Leader participant F1 as Follower 1 participant F2 as Follower 2 participant F3 as Follower 3 Note over L: Election Timeout Triggers L ->> F1: AppendEntries Term T Entry E L ->> F2: AppendEntries Term T Entry E L ->> F3: AppendEntries Term T Entry E F1 -->> L: ACK Success F2 -->> L: ACK Success F3 -->> L: ACK Failed L ->> C: Committed at Majority Note over F1,F3: Entry Replicated to Majority Quorum L ->> C: Response Success C ->> L: Next Request ``` ## 4.5 Checkpoint-Rollback Recovery (Process State Save) ```mermaid flowchart TB Start["Process Starts at State S0"] C1["Checkpoint 1: Save State S1 to Stable Storage"] C2["Checkpoint 2: Save State S2 to Stable Storage"] C3["Checkpoint 3: Save State S3 to Stable Storage"] Crash["CRASH or FAILURE Detected"] Restart["Restart Process and Load S3"] Resume["Resume Execution from S3"] Recovery["Message Log Replay to Recover Lost Messages"] Start --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 C3 --> Crash Crash --> Restart --> Resume --> Recovery --> End["Continue Normal Operation"] ``` ## 4.6 Error Detection and Correction Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Original Data Word
m bits"] --> B["Encoder
Adds r Parity Bits"] B --> C["Transmitted Codeword
n = m + r bits"] C --> D["Channel with Noise
Possible Bit Flip"] D --> E["Decoder Receives
n bit Codeword"] E --> F{"Syndrome
Non-Zero?"} F -- No --> G["No Error Detected
Output Original Data"] F -- Yes --> H["Error Detected
Locate and Flip Bit"] H --> I["Corrected Data Output"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Differentiate between Fault, Error, and Failure with one example each.** **Model Answer:** | Term | Definition | Example | |---|---|---| | **Fault** | The *cause* of an error — a defect in the system. | A design bug in a routing algorithm. | | **Error** | The *state* deviation caused by an activated fault. | A corrupted routing table entry. | | **Failure** | The *visible* service deviation to the external user. | A packet routed to wrong destination, breaking SLA. | > *The chain: Fault → Activation → Error → Propagation → Failure.* *(3 marks: 1 for definitions, 1 for example of each, 1 for the chain logic.)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **A system has MTBF = 4000 hours and MTTR = 2 hours. Compute its steady-state availability.** **Model Solution:** $$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{4000}{4000 + 2} = \frac{4000}{4002} \approx 0.99950$$ $$A \approx 99.95\%$$ > This is **four-nines availability** (~4.38 hours downtime per year). *(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for the answer with units.)* --- ## 📗 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### **Question A (Choice 1):** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **(a)** Explain the **Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR)** scheme with a neat diagram. Derive the system reliability $R_{\text{TMR}}$ if each module has reliability $R$ and the voter is perfect. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A distributed system uses **N-Version Programming** with $N=5$ versions. If each version has an independent probability of producing the correct output of $p = 0.95$, find the probability of system agreement. Compare with the TMR case. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q-A (a): **TMR Concept:** Three identical modules receive the same input; their outputs are fed to a majority voter. The system is correct if **at least 2 of 3** modules output correctly. **Diagram:** ``` +----------+ Input -->| Module 1 |---+ +----------+ | +----------+ v Input -->| Module 2 |--> VOTER --> Output +----------+ ^ +----------+ | Input -->| Module 3 |---+ +----------+ ``` **Derivation (as shown in Section 3.1):** $$R_{\text{TMR}} = \sum_{k=2}^{3} \binom{3}{k} R^k (1-R)^{3-k} = 3R^2(1-R) + R^3 = 3R^2 - 2R^3$$ **Sanity check:** For $R = 0.9$, $R_{\text{TMR}} = 3(0.81) - 2(0.729) = 0.972$. > **Improvement:** TMR improves reliability from 0.9 to 0.972 but uses 3× hardware. The voter itself must be ultra-reliable (a single point of failure). *(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for derivation, 1 for numerical example, 1 for improvement discussion.)* --- #### Model Solution to Q-A (b): **N-Version Programming (NVP) Concept:** $N$ independent development teams build functionally equivalent software. A decider/voter picks the majority answer. **Formula (from Section 3.4):** $$P(\text{agree}) = \sum_{k=\lceil N/2 \rceil}^{N} \binom{N}{k} p^k (1-p)^{N-k}$$ For $N=5$, majority threshold is $k \geq 3$: $$P(\text{agree}) = \binom{5}{3}(0.95)^3(0.05)^2 + \binom{5}{4}(0.95)^4(0.05)^1 + \binom{5}{5}(0.95)^5$$ **Compute each term:** - $\binom{5}{3}(0.95)^3(0.05)^2 = 10 \times 0.857375 \times 0.0025 = 0.02143$ - $\binom{5}{4}(0.95)^4(0.05)^1 = 5 \times 0.81451 \times 0.05 = 0.20363$ - $\binom{5}{5}(0.95)^5 = 1 \times 0.77378 = 0.77378$ **Sum:** $$P(\text{agree}) = 0.02143 + 0.20363 + 0.77378 = 0.99884$$ **Compare with TMR ($N=3$ versions, $p=0.95$):** $$P_{\text{TMR, agree}} = 3(0.95)^2(0.05) + (0.95)^3 = 3(0.9025)(0.05) + 0.857375 = 0.13538 + 0.857375 = 0.99275$$ | Strategy | $P(\text{agree})$ | |---|---| | Single version | 0.95000 | | TMR ($N=3$) | 0.99275 | | N-Version ($N=5$) | 0.99884 | > **NVP scales** the diversity benefit linearly, at the cost of independent development overhead. *(7 marks: 2 for concept, 3 for formula and substitution, 1 for comparison table, 1 for engineering insight.)* --- ### **Question B (Choice 2):** [KTU University Exam — May 2023] **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Primary-Backup (Passive) Replication** protocol for fault tolerance in distributed systems. Discuss its **failure detection and handover** mechanism. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **Byzantine Generals Problem**. Prove that **no solution exists with $N \leq 3f$** for $f$ Byzantine generals, and state the **minimum required nodes** for a Byzantine fault-tolerant consensus. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q-B (a): **Primary-Backup Replication (Passive Replication):** Only one replica (the **primary**) processes client requests and sends **update logs** to the backups. If the primary fails, a backup is promoted to be the new primary. **Diagram:** ``` Client ──> Primary Replica | | Sends Updates (Log Shipping) v Backup Replica ^ | Backup Replica ``` **Failure Detection:** - **Heartbeats:** Primary sends periodic "I am alive" messages to backups. - **Timeout:** If a backup does not receive a heartbeat within time $T$, it suspects failure. - **Distributed agreement:** Multiple backups coordinate (often via a **group membership service** or Paxos) to elect a new primary. - **Fencing (STONITH — Shoot The Other Node In The Head):** Old primary is forcibly isolated to prevent split-brain. **Handover Steps:** 1. Failed primary detected by $\geq$ half of backups via missed heartbeats. 2. Backups agree on a new view (using a consensus protocol). 3. New primary is chosen (often the one with the latest log). 4. Client retries; new primary responds. 5. Old primary, when it recovers, re-joins as a backup. **Trade-off:** Simpler than active replication, but handover incurs a **failover delay** (often seconds), causing brief unavailability. *(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for failure detection, 2 for handover steps, 1 for trade-off.)* --- #### Model Solution to Q-B (b): **Byzantine Generals Problem:** Several generals of the Byzantine army surround an enemy city. They communicate only via messengers, and some generals may be **traitors** who send conflicting messages. The loyal generals must agree on a common plan of action (attack or retreat). **Key Insight — Why $N \leq 3f$ is impossible:** Consider the case $N = 3$, $f = 1$ (one traitor): | Scenario | What Loyal General A Sees | What Loyal General B Sees | |---|---|---| | Commander C is loyal | C says "Attack"; B says "Attack" | C says "Attack"; A says "Attack" | | Commander C is traitor | C says "Attack" to A, "Retreat" to B | C says "Attack" to B, "Retreat" to A | > From A's and B's perspective, the **messages are identical** in both cases. A cannot tell whether C is loyal-and-the-others-disagree, or C is traitor. **Formal Lower Bound (Pease-Shostak-Lamport 1980):** The Oral Messages algorithm (with signatures) requires $N \geq 3f + 1$ to reach Byzantine agreement. **Proof sketch (induction on $f$):** - **Base ($f=0$):** Trivial; just one general, no traitors. - **Induction step:** If an algorithm $A(f)$ works for $f$ traitors among $N$ generals, it must also work for $f-1$ traitors among $N-2$ generals. The recursion: $$N(f) \geq 2 + N(f-1)$$ With $N(0) = 1$, solving: $N(f) \geq 3f + 1$. **Minimum required nodes:** $$\boxed{N \geq 3f + 1}$$ **Examples:** - $f = 1$ (1 traitor) → need $N \geq 4$ generals. - $f = 3$ (3 traitors) → need $N \geq 10$ generals. - Practical systems: **PBFT** uses $3f+1$ replicas (e.g., $f=1 \Rightarrow 4$ replicas, $f=3 \Rightarrow 10$). *(7 marks: 2 for problem statement, 3 for the impossibility proof sketch, 1 for the bound, 1 for real-world example.)* --- > [!WARNING] > ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > > 1. **Reliability vs. Availability confusion** — *Loses 1–2 marks per question.* Remember: Reliability is a function of *time interval*, Availability is an *instantaneous* property. > 2. **TMR derivation without simplification** — *Loses 1 mark.* The answer must be reduced to $3R^2 - 2R^3$, not left in expanded form. > 3. **NVP formula sign** — *Loses 1 mark.* The summation lower limit is $\lceil N/2 \rceil$, not $N/2$ (for even $N$). > 4. **Byzantine bound** — *Loses 1–2 marks.* Writing $N \geq 3f$ instead of $N \geq 3f + 1$ is the most common error. The bound is **strictly** $3f+1$. > 5. **Quorum inequality** — *Loses 1 mark.* Use $R + W > N$, not $R + W \geq N$ (strict inequality is required for intersection). > 6. **Forgetting units** — *Loses 0.5 mark per answer.* Always quote availability in % or downtime/year, MTBF in hours, $\lambda$ in 1/hour. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### Core Definitions - **Dependability** = reliability + availability + safety + integrity + maintainability + confidentiality - **Fault → Error → Failure** is the unidirectional cause-effect chain - **Four means of dependability**: prevention, removal, tolerance, forecasting ### Reliability & Availability - $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ (constant rate model) - $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ (steady-state, repairable) - $R_{\text{series}} = \prod R_i$, $R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - \prod (1-R_i)$ - TMR: $R_{\text{TMR}} = 3R^2 - 2R^3$ (perfect voter) - Five-nines = 99.999% availability = ~5.26 min downtime/year ### Hardware Fault Tolerance - **Spatial redundancy:** TMR, NMR, duplex systems - **Temporal redundancy:** retry, re-execution - **Information redundancy:** parity, Hamming, CRC, Reed-Solomon - **Hamming bound:** $2^r \geq m + r + 1$ ### Software Fault Tolerance - **N-Version Programming:** $N$ independent versions + decider - **Recovery Blocks:** primary try-block + acceptance test + alternate blocks - **Exception handling:** structured try/catch with rollback semantics - **Defensive programming:** input validation, assertions, NUL checks ### Networked/Distributed Fault Tolerance - **Active replication:** $N$ replicas process, majority quorum decides - **Passive (primary-backup):** one active, others standby, failover on heartbeat loss - **Quorum condition:** $R + W > N$ for read-write intersection - **Paxos/Raft:** tolerate $f$ crashes with $2f+1$ nodes (CFT) - **PBFT:** tolerate $f$ Byzantine with $3f+1$ nodes (BFT) ### Checkpointing & Recovery - **Chandy-Lamport algorithm:** consistent global snapshots in distributed systems - **Independent vs. coordinated checkpointing:** trade-off space vs. time - **Message logging:** pessimistic (sync) vs. optimistic (async) - **Transaction recovery:** UNDO/REDO logs, ARIES algorithm, ACID properties ### Key Bounds to Memorize | Domain | Bound | |---|---| | Hamming SEC-DED | $2^r \geq m + r + 1$ | | Byzantine consensus | $N \geq 3f + 1$ | | Crash-tolerant consensus | $N \geq 2f + 1$ | | Quorum intersection | $R + W > N$ | | TMR (perfect voter) | $R_{\text{TMR}} = 3R^2 - 2R^3$ | | Duplex availability | $A \approx 1 - (\lambda/\mu)^2$ |
